docs.title: Get Your Game Plan
docs.text:
PrescriptionA Few QuestionsYour Next Games, In Order

Stop guessing what to train. Answer a few questions, get an ordered plan from the library: which games, in what order, and how to fit them in your sessions.

This tool needs JavaScript. Browse the full games library instead.

Loading the library…

Plans use field-tested games only, work-in-progress games stay out until confirmed. Prefer to browse? All games · Learning paths · Game finder

docs.title: Project Context & Goals
docs.text:
AboutThe Project StoryIntent · Philosophy · Structure

The big idea: we are building a constraints-led, ecological training framework for MMA, not a catalogue of moves, but a library of repeating fight problems and the environments that shape the solutions.

What We Are Building

📚A knowledge base

Structured games and concepts, authored in Markdown.

🗺️A navigable site

Rendered as a documentation site you can move through.

🥋Built for the mat

Organized for practical use in real training environments.

Key distinction: the system is game-based, not technique-based. We are not cataloging moves, we are organizing repeating MMA problems and the environments that shape them.

Core Training Philosophy

🧠MMA is a decision system

The system is built around recurring decision states, not named techniques.Fights repeat predictable patterns: accessing an opponent, stabilizing position, exploiting with strikes or submissions, and countering escapes or reversals.

🌿The environment teaches

Using Representative Design, behavior emerges from constraints, the coach designs the environment, not the solution.Athletes learn by solving problems, not following instructions. Games are constructed so correct behavior is rewarded naturally.

📍Control precedes damage

Across all environments: control before striking, stability before exploitation.Strikes are allowed in most games, but they are informational and carry positional trade-offs.

⚖️Every offense needs a defense

The system is built symmetrically, no domination layer exists without resistance.If we teach pinning, we teach escaping. If we teach takedowns, we teach scrambles.

Design Rule: Games vs Levels

Environment or dominant constraint changes→New game Difficulty or completion increases→New level 🎮 GameA distinct learning problem with a stable environment, clear goal, defined invariants, and clear win conditions. 📈 LevelA progression inside the same game with stricter success criteria, same problem, higher standard.

Full breakdown → Levels vs Games

End State Vision

  • Comprehensive ecological MMA curriculum
  • Navigable via visual map
  • Usable by coaches as a reference and planning tool
  • Complete coverage of all MMA environments and decision states (in progress)

System Evolution Philosophy

This framework is intentionally non-dogmatic. As new information, competitive insight, or safety considerations arise, concepts may be refined, definitions updated, and games reorganized.

The only non-negotiables

🌍Ecological validity

It must hold up in real fighting contexts.

🧩Internal consistency

The pieces must agree with one another.

🦺Safety

No game compromises participant safety.

🎯Clarity of objectives

Every game states what it develops.

System evolution notice

This context document reflects the current best understanding of the system's intent. It may be revised as the framework evolves.

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text:
AboutRead Before TrainingSafety & Responsibility

Train under qualified supervision. Choose your coaches carefully. Accept responsibility for your decisions. Stay safe.

Coach Supervision Required

All gameplay must be supervised by a qualified coach or experienced practitionerThese games involve physical contact, striking, grappling, and submissions, activities that carry inherent risk of injury. Supervision is not optional.

Proper supervision is essential to

🎚️Set intensity

Ensure appropriate intensity levels.

👀Monitor safety

Watch for danger during play.

🧭Guide technically

Provide technical guidance.

🛑Intervene

Step in when necessary.

⚙️Adapt games

Scale to participant skill levels.

What Makes a "Qualified Coach"?

This system does not certify coaches or define qualification standards. Whether someone is qualified is subjective, contextual, and ultimately your responsibility, you choose who you trust to guide your training.

Factors to weigh

⏳ ExperienceHow long have they trained? Competed? Coached? 📖 KnowledgeDo they understand the techniques and the safety considerations behind them? 🏅 Track recordWhat is their history with student safety and development? 💬 CommunicationCan they explain concepts clearly and adapt to different learners? 🏟️ EnvironmentIs the training space safe and appropriately equipped? Your responsibilityIf you choose to learn from someone, you accept responsibility for that choice. This system provides the games, it does not evaluate or endorse any particular coach or gym.

Assumption of Risk

By using this system, you acknowledge:

⚠️Combat sports are dangerous

Injury can occur even with proper technique and supervision.

🙋You participate voluntarily

No one is forcing you to train.

🤝You accept responsibility

For your choice of partners, coaches, and facilities.

🎓This is educational material

Not a substitute for professional instruction.

Not Medical Advice

This system provides no medical guidanceNo medical advice, no injury treatment protocols, no rehabilitation guidance, no concussion protocols. If you are injured, consult medical professionalsDo not rely on training partners, coaches, or this documentation for medical decisions.

Age & Experience Considerations

Games here are designed for adults with foundational martial arts experience. Consider carefully before applying them to higher-risk participants.

Minors→Require additional supervision and modified intensity Complete beginners→May need prerequisite skill development first Those with injuries or conditions→Medical clearance may be necessary

Liability

This system is provided as-is for educational purposes. The creators and contributors:

  • Make no guarantees about safety or effectiveness
  • Are not responsible for injuries incurred during training
  • Do not endorse any specific gym, coach, or methodology
  • Provide this material freely, without warranty

The Bottom Line

Combat sports can be practiced safely and beneficially when approached with proper respect for the risks involved. This system provides structured games that develop real skills, but the responsibility for safe implementation lies with you and your training community.

Questions?

If you have questions about implementing this system safely, consult with qualified professionals in your area. This documentation cannot replace hands-on guidance from experienced practitioners. For the system's intent and design rules, see Project Context & Goals.

docs.title: Levels vs Games
docs.text:
AboutDesign RuleA Key Distinction

The distinction in one line: change the problem and you have a new game; raise the standard on the same problem and you have a new level.

The Two Definitions, Side by Side

🎮Game

A distinct learning problem: stable environment, clear goal, defined invariants, clear win conditions.Each game is its own learning unit, a self-contained problem the environment poses to the athlete.

📈Level

Increased completion inside the same problem: same environment, same decision state, stricter success criteria.A level turns up the difficulty without changing what is being learned, the problem stays fixed.

The Decision Framework

Environment changed?→Yes → New game Dominant constraint changed?→Yes → New game Win condition fundamentally different?→Yes → New game None of the above, just harder?→New level

When to Make Each

📈 New Level

Same core problem, higher standard.

Environment unchanged · decision state unchanged · difficulty increased · same problem, stricter criteria. VS 🎮 New Game

A different problem to solve.

Environment changes (e.g., wall → ground) · dominant constraint changes · win condition fundamentally different · roles change significantly.

Rule of Thumb

If you change ≥2 of these, it's probably a new game: environment · entry condition · win condition · roles · primary decision state.

Example 1: One Game, Three Levels

The Wall Stand-Up progression is a single game with three levels, the environment, roles, and core decision (escape vs maintain) stay constant.

1Stand-up onlyThe core problem.Establish the base skill: getting back up off the wall. 2Stand-up + disengageHigher completion.Same problem with a stricter standard, stand and break free cleanly. 3Stand-up + reversalHighest difficulty.Same problem, top standard, stand, escape, and turn the tables.

Example 2: Two Different Games

Wall Control and Wall Escape are two different games, not levels, the roles, goals, and decision states are inverted.

🔒 Wall Control

Attacker · establish the pin · hold 5 seconds.

Stage: stabilize. The problem is building and keeping the pin. VS 🗝️ Wall Escape

Defender · break the pin · create space or reverse.

Stage: counter. The problem is undoing the pin, an inverted role and win condition.

Why This Matters

🧱For curriculum design

Games are distinct learning units; levels are progressive mastery of one unit.Mixing them creates confusion about what's actually being learned.

📊For assessment

Level progression shows mastery; a new game shows breadth.The clear distinction is what makes athlete tracking possible.

🗣️For coach communication

"Run Wall Control Level 2" is unambiguous. "Run some wall stuff" is not.Clear naming enables precise session planning.

Edge Cases

Transition games (Wall → Ground)→Single game, the transition is the problem Loop games (Stand-Up Loop)→Single game, the loop is the learning unit

See also → Project Context & Goals

System evolution notice

These design rules may be refined as the system grows.

docs.title: Confidence Rating
docs.text:
Core Concept · Perceptual FoundationConfidence Rating Core ConceptPerceptual FoundationOffense + DefenseThe Information War

The longer you engage, the more you read, and the more you read, the more you can commit. Confidence is information, not emotion.

Confidence isn't a feeling, it's sufficient information to commit.

It links exposure, pattern recognition, and commitment quality. Enough data for a high-probability prediction, then commit.

Exposure→Pattern recognition Pattern recognition→Confidence Confidence→Commitment

Critical insight: confidence applies to BOTH sides at once. The fight is an information war, whoever builds accurate confidence faster gains the advantage.

🥊Offense builds confidence in…

Which attack will work against this opponent's defense.

🛡️Defense builds confidence in…

Which trap to set based on this opponent's offense.

The Confidence Cycle

1Low, SurvivalMinimal data. Absorb & gather.You don't know their attacks, timing, or tells. Appropriate response: survive, absorb, gather information. Do NOT commit to high-risk defenses (slip, parry) or counters, the chance of reading wrong is too high. 2Building, RecognitionPatterns emerge."They jab before the cross." "They change levels before shooting." "They have a tell before the hook." Each exchange adds data points to your mental model of the opponent. 3High, CommitmentAnticipate, don't react.Reliable patterns identified. You can anticipate rather than react, commit to specific defenses, and time counters with precision. The probability is now high enough to justify the risk, commit.

Offensive Confidence: Pick the Right Attack

When on offense, you read the opponent's defensive patterns:

Guard position→What's open, what's protected Defensive habits→What attack will catch them Reaction speed→How much you can get away with Movement patterns→When they're stationary / vulnerable Recovery time→When to chain attacks Building offensive confidence: probe → recognize → commit

Probe (low confidence)

  • Throw single strikes to test reactions
  • Use feints to see how they respond
  • Try different entries to see what they defend
  • Gather data before committing to combinations

Recognize (building)

  • "They always parry the jab to the same side"
  • "They drop their hands after blocking the hook"
  • "They back up straight instead of circling"
  • "They overreact to level changes"

Commit (high confidence)

  • Select the combination that exploits THEIR specific habits
  • Time the entry to THEIR specific rhythm
  • Commit to the takedown knowing THEIR defensive tendency

Two worked examples

Striking→Probe jabs → "they always parry right" → jab → left hook (right hand is busy parrying) Takedowns→Feint level change → "they back up, don't sprawl" → forward pressure → catch mid-step → takedown

You don't throw the same combinations against every opponent. You BUILD confidence in which attacks work against THIS opponent's defense.

Defensive Confidence: Set the Trap

Defensive confidence is NOT just surviving, it's setting traps based on the opponent's offensive patterns.

When defending, you read the opponent's offensive patterns:

Preferred combinations→What's coming after the first strike Timing & rhythm→When to counter Commitment level→How exposed they'll be Recovery patterns→When they're vulnerable Tells before big attacks→When to set the trap Building defensive confidence & the trap concept

Survival → Recognition → Trap

Low, survival→Block, absorb, don't counter, learn their game Building→"Always jab-cross-hook" · "loads up before the power shot" · "shoots after the right hand" · "overcommits when they smell blood" High, trap→You KNOW what's coming: prepare the counter, bait it, exploit the commitment

A trap needs HIGH confidence: you commit before the attack arrives

Low confidence→See attack → react → defend High confidence→Predict attack → prepare trap → bait → counter

Two worked examples

Counter striking→"Right hand after the jab" → bait the jab, prep the slip → give a target → slip the right you KNEW was coming → counter Counter wrestling→"Shoots off the right hand" → prep sprawl + guillotine grip → give the look → sprawl the known shot → guillotine

Defensive response by confidence level

1Very LowShell up, survive, retreat. 2LowBlock / absorb, no counters. 3MediumDefend, look for a single counter. 4HighSet a specific trap for the predicted attack. 5Very HighBait, trap, counter-combination.

The Two-Way Game

Both players build confidence at once, the interaction creates a dynamic:

Low ATK · Low DEF→Tentative exchange, both probing High ATK · Low DEF→Attacker lands, defender survives Low ATK · High DEF→Defender sets traps, attacker cautious High ATK · High DEF→Technical exchange, best read wins

The meta-game: while building YOUR confidence, you also deny the opponent information (vary your patterns), feed them FALSE information (set up traps), and disrupt their confidence (break their rhythm).

Commitment Risk: Confidence Justifies Risk

Every commitment carries risk. Higher-commitment actions demand higher confidence.

🛡️ Tight block, lowDefensive commitment: low. Risk if wrong, absorb impact, but safe. ↗️ Parry, mediumDefensive commitment: medium. Risk if wrong, miss the deflection, get hit. 💨 Slip, highDefensive commitment: high. Risk if wrong, move into the punch, get hit harder. 🔁 Counter, very highDefensive commitment: very high. Risk if wrong, get caught while attacking. 👆 Jab / probe, lowOffensive commitment: low. Risk if wrong, miss, but recover easily. 🥊 Combination, mediumOffensive commitment: medium. Risk if wrong, miss, potentially off-balance. 💥 Power shot, highOffensive commitment: high. Risk if wrong, miss, exposed to counter. 🤼 Takedown entry, very highOffensive commitment: very high. Risk if wrong, stuffed, potentially guillotined. 🦅 Flying / jumping attack, extremeOffensive commitment: extreme. Risk if wrong, miss, completely out of position. The confidence–commitment equation

Low conf · High commit→Getting caught / stuffed High conf · High commit→Successful execution Low conf · Low commit→Appropriate, gathering information High conf · Low commit→Missed opportunity, commit more

This is why blocking exists though it's "passive" (appropriate when defensive confidence is low), why probing jabs exist (low offensive confidence), and why fighters feel each other out early, both building confidence before committing.

How Constraints Manipulate Confidence

The game system uses constraints to set confidence levels for learning:

🔒High constraint: given

Parry the Straight: attacker can ONLY throw straights, so "what's coming" is ~100%. The defender focuses purely on timing and mechanics, no pattern-recognition load.

🔓Low constraint: earned

Close-Range Defense: straights, hooks, body shots all live. The defender must READ what's coming, running the full cycle: exposure → recognition → confidence → commitment.

High constraint→Mechanics focus, confidence GIVEN Low constraint→Recognition focus, confidence EARNED Open play→Full integration, confidence VARIABLE

Reading Signals Across Domains

The confidence principle applies everywhere, not just striking, each signal predicts something.

Striking signals

Weight shift backward→Rear hand loading (cross or kick) Shoulder rotation→Hook incoming Eyes drop to body→Body shot or level change Rhythm break→Attack coming

Takedown signals

Level change→Shot incoming Grip fighting intensifies→Clinch entry or throw attempt Head position drops→Penetration step Weight commits forward→Driving takedown

Submission signals

Grip placement→Specific submission path Hip angle change→Sweep or submission attempt Breathing pattern→Explosion coming Incremental position gain→Setup sequence

Ground-control signals

Hip escape attempt→Direction of escape Frame placement→Escape route Breathing / tension→Explosive movement coming

Feints: Weaponising the System

Feints work by exploiting the confidence system against the defender:

1EstablishSet a pattern: jab–cross–jab–cross. 2BuildDefender gains confidence in the pattern. 3BreakFeint jab → hook instead. 4CommitDefender acts on false confidence. 5CaughtDefender gets hit.

Counter-strategy: don't commit until the signal is confirmed. Real attacks differ from feints by commitment level (weight transfer), follow-through, and recovery position. This is why higher-level games add feints, they teach defenders to verify confidence before committing.

Confidence vs. Time Pressure

Confidence building needs time. Aggressive pressure is an attempt to deny you that time:

Constant forward pressure = less time to read High output = more signals, less processing time Overwhelming volume = forced into survival mode

Defending Against Pressure

  • Accept survival mode initially
  • Use low-commitment defenses (blocking, footwork)
  • Look for ONE reliable pattern
  • Exploit that pattern when confidence is sufficient

You don't need to read everything, you need to read ONE thing with high confidence.

Training Implications

Defensive skill-isolation games

The win condition (successful parry/slip = role switch) teaches:

  • You don't need to defend everything
  • Wait for confidence to build
  • Commit when probability is high
  • One good read beats ten reactive blocks

Offensive development

  • Probe before committing, single strikes to test reactions
  • Read defensive patterns, what do they do vs. the jab? the level change?
  • Select strategy from reads, not the same combos against everyone
  • Build to commitment, low-risk probes → medium combos → high-commitment attacks

Trap setting (counter-offense)

  • Survival buys time, blocking gathers information
  • Patterns reveal traps, their offense tells you what trap to set
  • Baiting is active, give them the look they want to attack
  • Commit to the trap, when confidence is high, trust your read

Scaling games

As constraints decrease, athletes learn:

  • Confidence must be earned, not given
  • More variables = more exposure needed
  • Pattern recognition is trainable, both offensive and defensive reads
  • Reading speed improves with exposure

Coaching: what to watch for

Defensive struggles

Committing too early, not waiting for confidence Never committing, not recognising when confidence IS sufficient Caught by feints, committing to false signals

Offensive struggles

Same attacks every time, not reading patterns Never committing, stuck in probe mode Overcommitting early, not building confidence first

Counter-offense struggles

Only surviving, never trapping, missing patterns to exploit Springing traps too early, low confidence = trap fails Telegraphing the trap, opponent reads your setup

Adjust constraint level to match pattern-recognition development on BOTH sides.

The Summary in Four Beats

Exposure builds data→Every exchange gives you information Data builds patterns→Repeated signals become predictable Patterns build confidence→High-probability predictions become possible Confidence enables commitment→Risk becomes justified 🥊Offense

Confidence in what attack works against their defense → committed combinations & entries.

🛡️Defense

Confidence in what attack is coming → trap setting & counter-offense.

Skill-isolation games give confidence artificially through constraints so you can learn mechanics; integration games make you earn it through pattern recognition. The ultimate skill: building accurate confidence faster than your opponent while disrupting theirs.

System evolution notice

This concept may be refined as pattern recognition training methods develop.

Related · Defensive Solutions, each solution needs a different confidence level · Hand Controls, gather tactile info to build confidence · Games · Parry the Straight (given) · Close-Range Defense (earned) · Counter Striking

docs.title: Decision States
docs.text:
Universal Framework · All EnvironmentsDecision States Universal FrameworkAll EnvironmentsFour Phases

Every moment in a fight is one of four decision states. Knowing which one you're in determines your immediate priority, and the cycle never stops turning.

You're always in one of four states, know it, and you know your next move.

Access · Stabilize · Exploit · Counter, then the loop begins again. The cycle never stops turning.

1Access"Can I get to them?" 2Stabilize"Can I hold this?" 3Exploit"Can I finish from here?" 4Counter"Can I escape or reverse?"

Counter returns you to Access, the loop never breaks

🚪Access

"Can I get to them?", establish connection or position.The entry phase: closing distance, establishing clinch, completing a takedown, passing guard, achieving mount.

🔗Stabilize

"Can I hold this?", secure and control the position.The control phase: establishing grips/hooks, killing hips, controlling posture, preventing frames. Without it, access is temporary.

💥Exploit

"Can I finish from here?", deal damage or submit.The finish phase: striking from dominance, submission attempts, ground and pound, TKO accumulation. The payoff of Access and Stabilize.

🔄Counter

"Can I escape or reverse?", deny the opponent's exploitation.The defensive phase: defending strikes, escaping positions, reversing control, standing up. Success returns you to Access.

Access: The Entry Phase

"Can I get to them?" Getting where you need to be: closing striking distance, establishing clinch, completing a takedown, passing guard, achieving mount.

Key skills→Distance management · timing entries · reading reactions · feinting and setup Games→Touch & Don't Get Touched · Pressure to Clinch · Ground Access

Stabilize: The Control Phase

"Can I hold this?" Making your position stick. Without stabilization, access is temporary and exploitation is impossible: establish grips/hooks, kill hips, control posture, prevent frames.

Key skills→Pressure distribution · anticipating escapes · grip fighting · weight placement Games→Wall Control · Ground Control · position maintenance in any game

Exploit: The Finish Phase

"Can I finish from here?" Where control becomes damage or finish, the purpose of Access and Stabilize: striking from dominant position, submission attempts, ground and pound, TKO accumulation.

Key skills→Recognizing finishing windows · committing to finish · holding position while attacking · chaining attacks Games→Sustained Offense · Wall Grinding · ground finishing via Ground Control + Submission

Counter: The Defensive Phase

"Can I escape or reverse?" What happens on the wrong side of the exchange, defending strikes, escaping positions, reversing control, standing up from bottom. Success returns you to Access (your own, or neutral).

Key skills→Survival · recognizing escape windows · defensive submissions · technical stand-up Games→Close-Range Defense · Wall Escape · Ground Escape · Leg Reclaim · all defensive games

How the States Flow

⚔️Offensive flow

Access → Stabilize → Exploit → (finish or lose position) → Access.

🛡️Defensive flow

Counter → (escape) → Access (your turn) or Counter (still defending).

↩️Position loss

Exploit → (opponent escapes) → Access (chase) or Counter (they reversed).

Application to Games

Every game maps onto the four states across its environment:

Striking→Touch Game · Sustained Offense · KO/TKO · Close-Range Defense Wrestling→Pressure to Clinch / Takedowns · Wall Control · Ground transitions · Takedown Defense Wall→Pressure to Wall · Wall Control · Wall Grinding · Wall Escape Ground→Ground Access · Ground Control · Ground Control + Submission · Ground Escape / Leg Reclaim

Read across each row as Access · Stabilize · Exploit · Counter.

Training Implications

  • Know your state, at any moment, know which state you're in; it sets your priority.
  • Don't skip states, exploiting without stabilizing leads to scrambles. Stabilize before you attack.
  • Transitions are vulnerable, moving between states creates openings, for you and the opponent.
  • Counter resets the game, a successful Counter returns to neutral or gives you Access. Not just survival, opportunity.
Summary & system integration

Access→Get there Stabilize→Hold it Exploit→Finish it Counter→Escape it

Every technique, position, and game fits somewhere in this cycle. When a game description mentions "Access phase" or "Counter state, " it refers to this framework, understanding where you are determines what you should do next.

docs.title: Defensive Solutions
docs.text:
Core Concept · StrikingDefensive Solutions in Striking Core ConceptStriking4 SolutionsContext-Driven

There are four functionally distinct ways to defend a strike, and the skill is selecting, not memorizing, the right one.

Defense is any action that meaningfully reduces the opponent's ability to land effective strikes, nothing more, nothing less.

Four ways to defend a strike, the skill is choosing, not memorizing.

Block, parry, evade, enter. No solution is universally best, context decides.

The Four Core Solutions

🛡️Block

Use the arms or legs as a physical barrier between the body and the strike, forearm shields, leg checks, elbow blocks, tight guards.

↗️Parry

Redirect a strike off the centre line before it lands cleanly, pushing straights off line, slapping or guiding shots away.

💨Evade

Avoid the strike entirely through movement, head movement, footwork, angle changes, distance management.

🤝Enter

Neutralise by removing the opponent's ability to strike, clinching, hand control, level changes, takedown entries.

How Each One Behaves

🛡️ Block, reliable but costlyForce: absorbs or redirects. Timing: reactive. Movement: often static. Cost: builds fatigue over time. Reliable, but expensive if overused. ↗️ Parry, efficient but timing-dependentForce: redirects. Timing: requires precision. Movement: light, efficient. Cost: low if timed well, but timing-dependent. 💨 Evade, cheapest, needs spaceForce: none, no contact. Timing: anticipatory. Movement: dynamic. Cost: lowest impact cost. Ideal when space is available. 🤝 Enter, proactive, high commitmentForce: negates the threat entirely. Timing: proactive. Movement: high commitment. Cost: trades striking range for grappling control.

Combination Patterns

The four categories are functionally distinct but not mutually exclusive, most real defensive actions chain two together. This is expected and desired.

Evade → Enter→Slip the punch, initiate the clinch Parry → Hand control→Redirect the jab, trap the hand Block → Clinch→Absorb the hook, close the distance Evade → Counter→Slip the punch, return a strike

Why Not a Fifth Category?

What about countering?

Countering is sometimes proposed as a fifth defensive solution. In this system it is not: it's an offensive response, not a defence in itself.

A defence succeeds→block / parry / evade / enter lands first Only then→a counter can exist

A counter only exists after one of the four defences has succeeded, so countering is not listed as a primary defensive category.

No Universal Best: Context Decides

No defensive solution is universally superior. The system teaches athletes to select, not memorise, their defence.

Effectiveness depends on the live situation:

📐 Available space 🔋 Fatigue level 🎯 Skill level 🧱 Wall vs. open space 🤜 Proximity to clinch

How Games Use This

Games deliberately constrain which solutions are allowed, emphasised, or punished, this is intentional and context-driven.

👆Touch Game

Evasion & counter-touch.

🤜Pressure to Clinch

Evasion (footwork) → Enter.

🧱Wall Escape

Frames (blocking) → Enter (DNS).

System evolution notice

Defensive categories may be revised if new functional distinctions emerge.

Related · Hand Controls, a defence-and-entry tool · Confidence Rating, how much each solution dares to commit · Games · Tight Block · Parry the Straight · Evade the Punch

docs.title: Fight Philosophy
docs.text:
System Foundation · Guides Every GameFight Philosophy

Finish the fight, take as little damage as possible.

Not time, not rounds, not points. Efficient finish, minimal harm. This one goal governs every game in the system.

All Paths Lead to the Finish

The Finish 💥 KnockoutA clean strike with power and precision drops the opponent. 🥊 TKOAccumulated damage until they can no longer intelligently defend. 🔒 SubmissionA control position enables a choke or joint lock, tap or sleep.

Every pathway in the system leads to one of these three, everything else (points, decisions, position) is secondary.

Efficiency Is the Measure

Efficiency = the damage ratio, how much you gave versus how much you took. A 5-second KO and a 15-minute submission are both efficient if you took minimal damage. A clean finish beats a sloppy one where both fighters are hurt.

Strategy Drives Efficiency

The most efficient path depends on the opponent. Read their strengths and weaknesses, then exploit the opening.

Vulnerable on the ground→Take them down Weak cardio→Extend the fight and pressure Can't handle body shots→Attack the body Poor takedown defense→Wrestle

Efficiency isn't a fixed formula, it's a strategic calculation across three inputs:

🧰Your skills

What finishes are available to you?

🎯Their weaknesses

Where are they most vulnerable?

⚖️Risk assessment

What's the safest path to exploit those weaknesses?

The goal stays constant; the strategy adapts to the opponent in front of you.

Implications for Training

🛡️Defense is not passive

Defense exists to prevent damage, it's winning the damage exchange, not avoiding the fight.Strong defense directly serves the goal: every hit you don't take is a win.

⚔️Offense must be purposeful

Every action should create a finishing opportunity, improve position toward finish, or prevent the opponent from finishing.Striking and grappling should move toward finish, not just accumulate activity.

📍Position serves finish

Dominance matters because it enables finishing and prevents being finished.Top control lets you strike and submit; guard means you can't be struck as easily.

🎲Risk vs. reward

Calculated aggression serves the principle; reckless aggression violates it.Take risks when the potential reward (finish) justifies the potential cost (damage taken).

Application to Games

Defensive games→You're learning to take less damage Offensive games→You're learning to create finishing opportunities Combined games→You're learning the damage exchange, giving more than you take

At the highest level (Full MMA Expression), every game tests whether you can pursue your objective while holding the damage principle.

Common Mistakes

Activity over outcomeThrowing strikes that don't hurt, holding positions without advancing, "winning" exchanges that don't move toward finish. Fix: if an action doesn't create damage, position, or opportunity, why do it? Ignoring damage takenCelebrating a knockdown while ignoring the three clean shots you ate to get there. Fix: assess the trade honestly, could you have gotten the same outcome with less damage? Confusing aggression with effectivenessBeing first, loud, and busy ≠ being effective. Fix: measure by the principle, did you move toward finish while minimizing damage?

The Philosophy in Three Lines

  • Finish the fight, KO, TKO, or Submission.
  • Take minimal damage, every hit you don't take is a win.
  • Everything serves this goal, defense, offense, position, timing.
System integration

This concept underpins the entire game system. Every game, at its core, develops skills that help you finish fights while taking less damage. When evaluating any technique, position, or strategy, ask: "Does this serve the philosophy?" This isn't a style preference, it's the fundamental logic of fighting.

docs.title: The Finishing Sequence
docs.text:
Tactical · All DomainsThe Finishing Sequence TacticalAll DomainsFoundationalThe Closing Phase

The finishing sequence is the closing phase of a fight, the moves that actually end it. The goal of every exchange before it is to arrive here: a moment where the opponent is compromised and a finish becomes available. The skill is recognizing that moment and taking the finish it affords.

The fight has stages. Know which one you're in.

Like chess: an opening, a mid-board, and a finish. The finishing sequence is its own state, with its own attunement, and it can arrive in an instant.

On the term

We credit UFC referee John McCarthy for the framing of the "finishing sequence", the green light that opens once an opponent is hurt or compromised. We adopt the term and build games around reaching it and recognizing it.

The Three Stages

♟️Opening

Probe, engage cautiously, download information.Enter the fight and read the opponent. Low-risk exchanges that gather information about range, timing, reactions, and weaknesses, without overcommitting.

⚔️Mid-board

Significant exchanges where the damage ratio tilts one way.Press the engagements where you do more damage than you take. This is where the opponent gets compromised, a wobbled base, a hurt body, a broken posture.

🎯Finishing

The green light: a finish is now available, take it.The opponent is compromised and a finish is afforded, a strike on a hurt target, a neck left open, a posted arm to isolate. A different state, with a different attunement.

Calm Is Not a Decision, It Is an Attunement

The common mistake is to see the moment and then react, an adrenaline dump, the berserker, the bull charging in. Over-arousal mis-attunes you: you rush, you lunge, you miss the finish. The fighters who finish cleanly stay calm, not because they summon a mental state and then calculate, but because they are attuned to the right information and not over-aroused. Calm is what lets you keep perceiving while the finish is live.

The opponent's state crosses a threshold→Wobbled base, exposed neck, posted hands, broken posture The position's layout changes→New finishes open that were not there a moment ago You perceive the shift and act→Take the afforded finish, you don't compute a memorized one

In ecological terms, the finishing sequence is a nonlinear phase transition. When a control parameter (damage, balance, posture) crosses a critical value, the layout of available finishes reorganizes. The skill is not a calculation, it is educated attention: learning to perceive that the layout has shifted and a finish is now afforded.

Don't Jump the Guillotine

A clean example of finishing well versus badly. Many fighters "jump the guillotine", sacrificing position to their back to chase the choke, with no backup if it fails, and end up fatigued in a worse spot. The better expression keeps the finish as a threat: use the guillotine to climb to top mount, where you either get the finish or keep a dominant position. One commits to a single prescribed solution; the other stays attuned to the evolving layout and takes the better afforded path.

❌Jumping the finish

Commit everything to one move, sacrifice position, no backup. If it fails, you are worse off and gassed.

✅Threatening the finish

Use the threat to improve position. Stay attuned to what opens next, finish or dominate, never gamble the position away.

Developed Through Games

Mount Submission Hunt→Convert a held position into an afforded finish, the sequence in miniature Ground-and-Pound Defense→The defender's side: deny the finishing sequence, recover before it lands

Related Concepts

  • TKO Pin, the positional finish that ends a fight without a submission
  • The Three Zones, the stages of range where exchanges build toward the finish

docs.title: Full MMA Expression
docs.text:
Highest Level · All GamesFull MMA Expression Highest LevelAll GamesMulti-Threat Pressure

Every game can be played at a Full MMA Expression level, the highest level, where complications from other domains are introduced while the game keeps its core focus.

Master the skill in isolation, then stress it under every threat.

The game keeps its core focus, but other domains complicate it. The ceiling, not the default.

The Pattern

Lower levels (1–3)→Primary skill in isolation, single domain only Mid levels (3–4)→Primary skill with rising complexity, single domain, more variables Highest level→Primary skill under full MMA pressure, multiple domains, realistic threats

Why This Matters

MMA is not a collection of isolated skills. Train only in isolation and you miss the reality; introduce everything at once and you overwhelm beginners. The fix: add complications only after the foundation is set.

🛡️Defending strikes

...while staying aware of takedown threats.

🤼Working takedowns

...while respecting striking counters.

📍Controlling position

...while managing submissions and escapes.

What Full MMA Expression Looks Like

🛡️Striking defense games

Adds grappling threats.Lower levels focus on the defensive solution (block, parry, slip, evade). At full expression the attacker can shoot or clinch, and the defender must defend strikes AND deny grappling entry, shelling up without posture awareness invites takedowns.

⚔️Striking offense games

Adds counter threats and clinch responses.Lower levels focus on landing targets and reading defense. At full expression the defender can counter-strike or clinch as denial, and the attacker must land while respecting those responses.

🤼Wrestling / grappling games

Adds striking threats.Lower levels focus on control, escapes, or takedowns. At full expression light strikes become available, position must account for damage potential, and control without strike awareness is incomplete.

Implementation Across Games

Every game's highest level should ask: "What other threats would exist in a real fight, and how does that change the skill?"

Blocking games→Absorb strikes + grappling threat Parrying / slipping games→Deflect/evade strikes + grappling threat Offensive striking games→Land targets + counter threat, clinch denial Space control games→Manage distance + striking threat Wrestling games→Control/escape + striking threat Clinch games→Establish/break connection + strikes, takedowns

The Teaching Logic

1Beginners, lower levelsLearn the primary skill without distraction.Build correct movement patterns and develop confidence in the core mechanic. 2Intermediate, progress through levelsAdd tempo, combinations, feints.Increase complexity within the domain and prepare for multi-threat environments. 3Advanced, Full MMA ExpressionPrimary skill under realistic pressure.Account for all relevant threats, the closest approximation to fight conditions within the game's constraints.

Use It Sparingly

This level exists for athletes who have mastered the lower levels. Most training will occur at levels 1–4, the skill must be solid before adding complications.

  • Athletes are proficient at lower levels.
  • You want to stress-test skills under realistic pressure.
  • You're preparing for competition.
Rushing here before the core skill is solid Treating it as the default rather than the ceiling

Relationship to Other Concepts

Confidence Rating→Higher confidence needed before committing under multi-threat pressure Defensive Solutions→Each solution has different vulnerability to MMA complications Three Zones→Zone awareness becomes more critical when grappling threats exist System evolution notice

This concept applies across all games and should be referenced when designing new games or levels.

docs.title: Guard Recovery
docs.text:
Ground Control · GrapplingGuard Recovery Ground ControlGrapplingFoundationalGraded Outcome

Guard recovery is not a single moment, it is a spectrum of how much of your lower body you have reinserted between you and the opponent. The more leg you get back across the line, the more secure the guard and the cleaner the escape.

Guard is not a switch, it's a ladder.

Every leg you get back between you is a rung climbed. Score the rung, not a yes or no.

The Recovery Ladder

Ordered least-to-most recovered. Each rung is observable across the mat, which is what makes it a usable win condition.

🦶One foot back

The first point reinserted, the pass is stalled but no real guard yet. Minimal.

🦵One knee between

Knee shield or half guard, a leg now traps the opponent. The floor for a recovered guard.

🧎Both knees between

Double knee shield, both legs frame the hips. Controlling.

🦿Both feet between

Feet on the hips or hooks in, you own the distance. Full open guard.

🔒Closed loop

Legs wrapped and locked behind the opponent, closing the guard. Most secure.

It Mirrors Passing

The passer and the guard player work the same three lines in opposite directions. The passer wins by getting past the hip, knee, and foot lines; the guard player wins by reinserting across them. Defining recovery this way makes the two win conditions two ends of one ruler.

Passer→Past the line of feet → past the knees → past the hips → pin Guard player←Reinsert across the hips → the knees → the feet → closed loop

How We Score It

Recovery is a graded win, not pass or fail. A game sets a floor (the minimum that counts as escaped) and then rewards getting further up the ladder.

Floor→One knee in (half guard), the realistic recovery from a flattened position Cleaner→Both knees → both feet → closed loop Full escape→A reversal to top, or standing up, out entirely

A game may set its floor lower (one foot back, for beginners) or higher (full guard only) to match the position it trains. From mount, the honest floor is half guard, you rarely recover straight to a closed guard.

Common Errors

⏸️Surviving, not recovering

Holding still under pressure feels safe but climbs no rung. Recovery means reinserting a leg, not waiting.

🪜Skipping rungs

Reaching for closed guard before a knee is even in. Climb the ladder in order, each rung makes the next reachable.

❎Calling it binary

Treating "guard or no guard" loses the feedback. Half guard recovered is real progress and should score as such.

Developed Through Games

Mount Escape→Recover from under mount, floor at half guard Ground Escape→Recover across the major pinning positions Leg Reclaim→The first rung in isolation, get a leg back between

The fighter who recovers guard does not just survive, they climb back into options. Each rung returns a little more control over the distance and the next exchange.

docs.title: Hand Controls
docs.text:
Core Concept · Bridge SkillHand Controls Core ConceptBridge SkillAll Ranges

Hand control is the bridge between striking and grappling, it stops the tool before the strike, not the strike after it leaves.

Hand control is any intentional use of the hands or arms to interfere with an opponent's ability to strike, frame, or maneuver effectively. It is not a position, it is a functional interaction.

Stop the tool, not the strike after it leaves.

A functional interaction, not a position. It's the bridge from striking into grappling.

What It Buys You

🚫Deny initiation

Stop the strike before it starts.

🎵Disrupt rhythm

Break timing and tempo.

✋Gather info

Read intent through tactile contact.

🌉Bridge ranges

Connect striking to grappling.

🚪Create entries

Open opportunities without absorbing damage.

Common Forms

↗️ Parrying & trappingRedirecting a strike and momentarily holding the limb that threw it. ✌️ Two-on-one controlControlling one of the opponent's arms with both of your hands, temporary, not a held position. 🤚 Wrist / forearm interferenceBlocking the striking tool before it is used. 🧹 Clearing framesRemoving the opponent's defensive structures so you can advance. 🔄 Redirecting guard positionChanging where the opponent's hands point, opening lines of attack. 📡 Tactile monitoringFeeling the opponent's intent through sustained contact, these interactions may be brief or sustained depending on context.

Hand Control vs. Blocking

Blocking reacts to strikes. Hand control prevents strikes from occurring cleanly.

Focus→Block targets the strike · Hand control targets the striking tool Timing→Block is reactive · Hand control is proactive or simultaneous Result→Block absorbs or deflects force · Hand control prevents the strike from occurring

One Tool, Four Jobs

🛡️Defense

Denying strikes.

🚪Entry

Bridging to the clinch.

⏬Pressure

Disrupting rhythm.

🚫Denial

Preventing the counter-attack.

It matters most for advanced striking exchanges, clinch initiation, and minimising damage while closing distance.

Where It Lives in the System

Touch Game · Lv 4→Advanced expression of "don't get touched" Pressure to Clinch→Pressure tool and clinch entry All grappling games→Controlling the defender's frames

It Emerges: It Isn't Taught

Design philosophy: hand control should emerge naturally through constraints, not be forced as a technique. When games constrain options appropriately, hand control appears as a solution, because it solves the problem the game presents.

Integration map & evolution notice

The bridge skill between domains

Striking range→Hand control Hand control→Clinch entry Clinch entry→Wall / ground control

Hand control is the bridge skill between the striking and grappling domains.

This concept may be refined as additional interaction patterns are identified.

Related · Defensive Solutions, hand control as Enter & Parry · Confidence Rating, tactile reads build confidence · Games · Pressure to Clinch · Clinch Denial

docs.title: Three Zones of Attack
docs.text:
Striking Concept · Offense + DefenseThree Zones of Attack Striking ConceptOffense + DefenseMisdirection

There are only three zones you can attack. Every strike targets one; every defense protects one or more. You cannot fully protect all three at once, and that is the whole game.

No guard covers all three, hit them where they're not.

Head, body, legs. Every stance is a trade; their guard shows you their holes.

The Three Zones

🧠 HighHead ChinTempleNoseJawEar 1 🫁 MidBody LiverSolar plexusRibsFloating ribs 2 🦵 LowLegs & Base ThighKneeCalfBelow the kneeFoot sweeps 3

Three zones is the high-level map. The low zone runs deeper than thigh kicks: the lower leg below the knee and the feet are live targets too, and sweeping the base is its own threat.

The Misdirection Principle

"Hit them where they're not." The opponent can defend more than one area at a time, but never everywhere at once. Pull their guard and attention onto the zones you threaten, and the zone they leave open is the one you take. This isn't a gimmick, it's the fundamental reality of striking.

1Threaten one zoneMake them believe an attack is coming to Zone A.A real, committed-looking threat, a feint or a partial strike that reads as the real thing. 2They respondGuard, attention, or body position shifts to protect Zone A.The defensive reaction is what creates the opening, you're shaping it on purpose. 3Attack another zoneZone B or C is now more exposed.They cannot cover what they just left to protect Zone A. 4Land the targetStrike where their defense isn't.The last strike was often planned from the start, everything before it was setup.

When defense commits to one zone, the others open

Protect head (hands high)→Body and legs open Protect body (guard low, elbows tight)→Head opens Protect legs (checking, weight shifted)→Head and body open

Guard Positions: Every One Is a Trade

There is no guard that protects all three zones. Every stance is a choice about what to prioritize and what to leave vulnerable. When you see an opponent's guard, you're seeing their priorities, and their holes.

⬆️ High GuardHands at temples/forehead. Protects head from hooks and straights; exposes body and legs. Used when expecting head attacks or shelling up. ↔️ Mid GuardHands at chin level. Protects head with small movement, can drop to body, but nothing is fully covered without movement. The neutral, ready position. ⬇️ Low GuardHands at chest/belly. Protects body and solar plexus; head is completely open. Used against body punchers or to bait head shots. 🤚 Extended GuardHands reaching out. Protects nothing directly, instead intercepts strikes before they arrive, controls range, disrupts the opponent. Risk: if they get past the hands, everything is exposed. 🛞 Philly Shell / Shoulder RollLead shoulder and rear hand. Protects lead side of head and body; exposes rear side of head, body if the shoulder rolls late, and legs. A counter-punching style.

Building Combinations Across Zones

The best combinations don't just throw multiple strikes, early strikes shape the defensive response to open the target you actually want. The last strike is the plan; everything before it is setup.

When you attack a zone, the defender reacts: opening another

Attack head→Hands up, chin tucked → body, legs open Attack body→Elbows drop, hunch forward → head opens Attack legs→Check or shift weight → head and body open (hands busy with balance) 🥊Jab → Cross → Left Hook to Body

Head shots raise the guard; the body opens for the hook.Jab and cross attack the head, the guard rises, the open body takes the hook.

🫁Jab to Body → Cross to Head

The body jab drops the guard; the head opens for the cross.The body jab pulls elbows/guard down, the cross lands high on the exposed head.

🦵1-2 → Low Kick

Hands occupied high; the legs are unguarded.Jab-cross occupies the hands high, the low kick lands while they focus on the hands.

🎭Head Kick Feint → Low Kick

The high feint lifts the hands; the lead leg is available.The feint sends hands up and weight high to check; the low kick lands on the exposed lead leg.

Found vs. Created Openings

High-level striking is about creating openings, not just finding them. You make the hole, then you hit it.

🔍Found openings

Exist from the opponent's habits or current position, observe, recognize, attack.Guard naturally sits low, hands drop after throwing, no leg-kick checks, looks away at certain moments. The attacker's job: spot the pattern, hit the available zone.

🛠️Created openings

Don't exist until you manufacture them with misdirection.Feint to head → body opens; attack body repeatedly → they guard it → head opens; threaten the lead leg → weight shifts → rear leg planted. The attacker's job: force the response that creates the opening you want.

Defensive Implications

You cannot protect all three zones simultaneously. Sound defense runs a loop:

1ReadWhat zone are they likely to attack? 2PrioritizeProtect the most threatened zone. 3RecoverReset before they attack another. 4VaryDon't be predictable about which zone you prioritize. Always protecting the same zone, they'll find the hole Showing a consistent guard pattern Not knowing which zone you're currently exposing

Application to Confidence Rating

Zone reading is how you build offensive confidence, it scales with your Confidence Rating.

Low→Probe all three zones to see how they react Building→Identify which zone they protect most, which they neglect High→Attack the neglected zone with commitment Very High→Create openings by manipulating their zone defense Summary & related concepts

  • Three zones, Head, Body, Legs are the only targets.
  • Misdirection, make them commit to one zone, attack another.
  • Guard reality, every position protects some zones, exposes others. No perfect defense.
  • Combination logic, early strikes shape the response to open the real target.
  • Found vs. created, high-level striking manufactures openings.

Confidence Rating→Zone reading is how you build offensive confidence Defensive Solutions→Each solution (block, parry, slip) works differently across zones

This concept may be refined as striking game patterns develop.

docs.title: TKO Pin
docs.text:
Control Concept · Wall + GroundTKO Pin Control ConceptWall + GroundFleeting State

A TKO Pin is a control state, not a position. It exists the moment you control the opponent's frame while keeping a limb free to strike, and it can vanish just as fast.

Control the frame, free a limb, strike before it slips away.

It's a fleeting state, not a held position. Create it, capture it, stabilize it, re-acquire it.

When a TKO Pin Exists

All four conditions must hold at once:

  • The defender's hips and shoulders are meaningfully controlled.
  • Posture and mobility are compromised.
  • At least one limb is free to strike effectively.
  • Defender options are high-cost, damage, exposure, reversal risk.
Control + a limb free to strike→TKO Pin, exploitation is possible Control, no limb free→Standard Pin, control only

Key Properties

⏳Often fleeting

Unlike a stable mount or back control, it's frequently a momentary state.It must be created, captured, stabilized if possible, and re-acquired when lost.

⚖️Control vs. strike access

To strike, you must release some control, skilled attackers oscillate between the two.Maximum control = minimal strike access. Maximum strike access = reduced control. The oscillation is the skill.

📍Wall or ground

Emerges in multiple environments.Wall pin positions (Level 3), ground control (from Wall → Ground transition), and the Stand-Up Loop after a successful re-pin.

Quality Spectrum

Threat→Available but not stabilized, fleeting Stable→Held for multiple seconds, strikes landing Dominant→No viable escape, fight-ending territory

Training Focus

The system trains the TKO pin as a four-phase loop:

1CreateOpen the pin 2CaptureSeize the control 3StabilizeHold it if you can 4Re-acquireRecover it when lost

A fleeting state: lose it, re-acquire, repeat

1CreateEarn the position through control and transitions. 2CaptureRecognize when the TKO pin is available. 3StabilizeMaintain the state while striking. 4Re-acquireRecover the pin after defender movement.

Where TKO Pin Appears

Wall Control→Level 3, threaten TKO pin access Wall → Ground→Level 3, establish TKO pin threat Stand-Up Loop→Attacker win condition

Related Concept: DNS

Defensive Neck Submissions (DNS) are the primary defensive counter to TKO pin attempts, they keep attackers honest, and are allowed in all games where TKO pin is relevant.

When an attacker reaches for the TKO pin and their posture breaks:

🪢Guillotine threats

Become available as posture breaks forward.

🤼Front headlock entries

Open up against the broken posture.

🔀Forced choice

Attacker must choose between TKO pin pursuit and neck safety.

System evolution notice

TKO Pin definitions may evolve as control models refine.

docs.title: Winning the Circle
docs.text:
Spatial Control · All DomainsWinning the Circle Spatial ControlAll DomainsFoundationalContinuous Battle

Winning the Circle is the outcome of a spatial exchange where one fighter holds the positional advantage. The outside fighter wins by cutting off escape and corralling the opponent to the boundary; the inside fighter wins by escaping to open space and regaining freedom.

Whoever owns the space, owns the options.

Every moment, one fighter is winning the circle: shrinking space by pressure, or keeping it open by escape.

Key Positions

⚖️ 50/50 clinchEach fighter has one overhook and one underhook, diagonally matched (one per side), starting chest to chest, symmetric and neutral. Whoever first wins inside control or the angle gains the advantage. 🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

The Two Perspectives

🎯Outside: Pressuring

Escape routes shrinking · their movement turns predictable · you hold centre while they near the boundary · you dictate range & timing.Success: opponent reaches the boundary with limited options → clinch → wall pin.

🌀Inside: Escaping

You keep access to open space · you move laterally without being cut off · you control when & where engagements happen · they chase instead of cutting angles.Success: return to centre, reset distance, regain freedom.

The "Circle" Is Available Space

The circle doesn't need an actual circular boundary, it represents the available space around a fighter at any moment. Winning it means controlling that space: taking it away (attacker) or maintaining it (defender).

Cage→The space between you and the fence Ring→The space between you and the ropes Open mat→The imaginary space defined by your movement options Training drill→The marked perimeter (cones, tape, etc.)

Why It Matters

Fighters who consistently win the circle control the fight's geography, tactical advantage in every domain.

🎯Win it attacking

You dictate range & timing · clinch and takedown entries open · their offense turns predictable · you accumulate control advantage.

🌀Win it defending

You choose when to engage · you strike and move freely · you avoid being trapped · you deny their game plan.

Winning vs. Losing: A Continuous Battle

The circle can be won and lost many times in a single exchange. It is a continuous battle, not a single event.

Options increasingvsOptions decreasing Space availablevsSpace shrinking Movement proactivevsMovement reactive You set the termsvsOpponent sets the terms Key insight: winning the fight ≠ winning the circle

You can be "winning" a fight (landing strikes, scoring points) while losing the circle. If the opponent is steadily taking space, your options shrink even as you land shots. Eventually it catches up, you end up pinned to the cage or taken down.

How to Win It

🎯Outside: Pressure

Cut angles, don't chase, move where they're going · use feints & strikes as pressure without overcommitting · keep composure & structure · recognise when clinch is available, then convert.

🌀Inside: Escape

Move early, before space closes · circle laterally instead of backing up · strike to create space · recognise early when you're losing, awareness beats panic.

Three Ways to Move Forward

Pressure has a temperament. The same forward intent can be reckless, evasive-and-sharp, or patient, and only one of them reliably wins the circle.

🐂The Bull

Charges forward recklessly, overcommits, and crashes.Covers ground fast but gives up balance and structure. Easy to redirect, counter, or pull into a takedown. Pressure without composure.

🩸The Matador

Lets the opponent commit, then redirects and makes them miss.Sharp and efficient, uses the opponent's momentum against them. Wins exchanges, but on its own it does not take space, it waits for mistakes rather than herding.

🪖The Soldier

Advances methodically, takes a little ground, and does not give it back.Patient, structured forward pressure. Cuts angles, takes distance away a step at a time, and corrals to the boundary. This is the temperament that wins the circle.

Take your time. You have no rush. As the opponent moves back, take the distance away slowly and meet them where they're going. You don't need to lunge for the finish, herd patiently and the boundary does the work.

Developed Through Games

Touch & Don't Get Touched→Range and timing foundation Pressure to Clinch→Full pressure-to-pin sequence Wall Control→What to do after winning the circle

Common Errors

Attacker errors

Chasing, not cutting→Opponent escapes, you lose position Over-pressuring (crashing)→Loss of composure, vulnerable to counters Clinching too early→No setup, easy to defend or reverse

Defender errors

Backing up straight→Runs directly into the boundary Moving too late→Space already gone Only using hands→Movement is the primary tool

The fighter who wins the circle doesn't just accumulate position, they accumulate options. And options win fights.

Related Concepts

  • Wall Control, Establish the Pin, The completion of winning the circle for the attacker
  • Pressure to Clinch, The primary game for developing this skill

Games where the attacker is the primary learner:

Skill Isolation: - Lead Hand Offense · Lead Leg Offense · Rear Hand Offense · Rear Leg Offense

Open Space Striking: - Land the Target · Sustained Offense

Open Space Wrestling: - Body Lock to Ground (WIP) · Pressure to Takedown · Pressure to Wall

Wall: - Wall Control - Establish Pin · Wall Grinding · Wall Pin to Ground

Ground: - Back Maintenance (WIP) · Closed Guard Pass (WIP) · Ground Access · Ground Control · Half-Guard Pass (WIP) · Knee-on-Belly Control (WIP) · Mount Maintenance (WIP) · Mount Submission Hunt (WIP) · North-South Control (WIP) · Open Guard Pass (WIP) · Side-Control Ride (WIP)

Games where both sides have distinct objectives (A vs B roles):

Open Space Striking: - One Shot Discovery (WIP) · Pressure to Clinch · Touch and Don't Get Touched

Open Space Wrestling: - 50/50 Clinch (WIP) · Open Space Takedown

Wall: - Stand-Up and Re-Pin Loop

Ground: - Closed Guard Bottom (WIP) · Half-Guard Bottom (WIP) · Positional Sparring · Win Ladder (WIP)

docs.title: Games by Decision State
docs.text:
AccessStabilizeExploitCounter

Games focused on connection and engagement:

  • All Skill Isolation games
  • Touch and Don't Get Touched
  • Pressure to Clinch
  • Ground Access

Games focused on control establishment:

  • Wall Control
  • Wall Pin to Ground
  • Open Space Takedown
  • Ground Control

Games that progress to damage or finish:

  • Sustained Offense → KO/TKO
  • Wall Grinding → TKO
  • Ground Control → TKO/Submission
  • Stand-Up Loop (TKO Pin access)

Games focused on defensive response:

  • Close-Range Defense, Evasive Movement
  • Counter-Striking, Counter-Wrestling, Clinch Denial
  • Takedown Defense
  • Wall Escape, Wall Defensive Submission
  • Ground Escape, Leg Reclaim, Ground to Standing, Ground Defensive Submission

docs.title: End States
docs.text:
All pathways lead to three fight-ending outcomes:

End State Definition Primary Paths Knockout (KO) Single strike renders opponent unconscious Sustained Offense TKO Accumulated damage until cannot defend Wall Grinding, Ground Control Submission Choke or joint lock forces tap Ground Control, Defensive Submissions

See Fight Philosophy for the underlying principle: Finish while taking minimal damage.

docs.title: Full MMA Expression
docs.text:
Every game has a highest level called Full MMA Expression where cross-domain threats are added:

Game Type MMA Complication Added Striking games Grappling threats (shoot, clinch) Wrestling games Striking threats Ground games Strikes from position

This ensures skills are tested under realistic MMA pressure. See Full MMA Expression for details.

docs.title: Striking Track
docs.text:
Defensive Skill Isolation → Close-Range Defense → Evasive Movement
Offensive Skill Isolation → Land the Target → Sustained Offense → KO/TKO
Both → Touch Game → Pressure to Clinch

docs.title: Transition Track
docs.text:
Pressure to Clinch → Wall Control / Open Space Takedown
Close-Range Defense → Counter-Striking / Counter-Wrestling

docs.title: Wall Track
docs.text:
Pressure to Wall → Wall Control → Wall Grinding → TKO
                                → Wall to Ground → Stand-Up Loop

docs.title: Ground Track
docs.text:
Offensive: Ground Access → Ground Control → TKO/Submission
Defensive: Ground Escape → Leg Reclaim → Ground to Standing
Combined: Positional Sparring (role reversal possible)

System Evolution Notice

This library will expand as new games are developed and tested. See Change Log for version history.

docs.title: Back Escape
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingBack Escape GroundGrapplingDefensiveAdvancedCounter

Turn to face before the choking arm finds your neck.

StartBottom with the back taken, top in a seatbelt with both hooks in, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom defends the neck, strips a hook, and turns in to face; top keeps the back and works the choking arm under the chin. → FinishTurn in to face, reverse, or stand → bottom · Get the choking arm under the chin → top · Hold the back the full count, no finish → reset · Out of bounds → loss.

Don't out-muscle the choke, outrun it to the front.

Hands defend the neck, hips turn into the choke. Every time they hunt the chin, a hook comes light.

What to Read

Attune to the tension in the seatbelt and the path of the choking arm, the moment the top swims a hand toward your throat or shifts weight to climb. That specifies which way to turn and when a hook is light enough to strip, not the top's voice or a memorized sequence. When the choke arm climbs high, turn low into it.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom (defender), one top (on the back). PositionTop chest-to-back in a seatbelt (one arm over the shoulder, one under the armpit, hands clasped), both hooks in; bottom seated or on the loaded side. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesBottom defends the neck and turns to face; top keeps the back and works the choke. Start & resetBegin from seatbelt and hooks; reset on escape, the arm under the chin, or the count.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to defend the neck, strip a hook, and turn in to face (or reverse, or stand).

Two hands on the choking arm, chin tucked to your chest. Slide your hips down, strip a hook, then turn toward the choking-arm side, never away from it. Survival first, then escape, exposing the neck to rush the turn hands the top the finish. The turn is graded: chest-to-chest is the floor, on top is better. VS 🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to keep the back and slide the choking arm across the throat, under the chin.

Stay chest-to-back, follow the hips, keep the seatbelt tight. Bait the hand-fight, then swim the choking arm under the chin. Control is proven by closing on the neck, not by riding the back for time.

The Rules

🤜 Bottom defends and turns, no subsThe bottom hand-fights, strips hooks, and turns to face continuously. No submissions from the bottom, the task is the escape, not the counter-attack. 🎯 Top wins by getting the arm under the chinThe top proves control by sliding the choking arm across the throat and under the chin (the closing position every rear-naked choke shares), not by sitting on the back. This gives the top an active, observable target instead of a stall. ⏱️ Hold the count or escapeIf the top keeps back control for the set count (start at 20 seconds) without getting the arm under the chin, the round resets. If the bottom turns to face, reverses, or stands first, the bottom wins. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are control only, so the bottom can read the seatbelt and hand-fight without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Use striking as a disincentivization tool, the cost that punishes stalling and lazy structure. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Switch Bottom turns in to face, reverses, or stands → bottom wins, switch roles.The turn is graded by how far you escape: turning chest-to-chest into the top's guard is the floor, coming out on top is better, standing clear is best. Stripping a hook and facing kills the back position, the choke can no longer be closed from the front. Win Top slides the choking arm under the chin → top wins.The choking hand crossed the throat and under the jaw, the shared closing of the rear-naked choke. The observable proof that back control beat the hand-fight. Stop at the closing position, before any squeeze. Reset Top holds the back the full count, no arm under the chin → reset, same roles.The top kept the back but never got under the chin before the count expired. The round resets from seatbelt and hooks for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Hands on the choke armDefend the neck only.Bottom starts with two hands gripping the choking arm and must keep the chin tucked while the top tries to climb the hand toward the throat. Isolates neck defense and the chin-down habit. 2Strip one hookFree a leg.Bottom adds the goal of clearing one hook off the hip while still defending the neck. Reading which hook is light becomes the task. 3Turn the cornerHips down, face in.With a hook cleared, the bottom slides the hips to the mat and turns toward the choking-arm side to face. Forces the top to choose between chasing the choke and keeping the back. 4Break the seatbeltWin the 2-on-1.Bottom peels the clasped hands or wins a 2-on-1 on the choking arm to break the seatbelt, then turns. The hand-fight-to-turn mechanic is now the focus. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Continuous from seatbelt and hooks until the bottom faces or the top gets under the chin, with light strikes on. The choke and strike threat make hesitation costly, escape under real urgency.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What tells you a hook is ready to strip? When the top swims the choking arm high for the chin or shifts to climb, the base and a hook go light. Choke-arm high, turn low into it. Strip the light hook and turn toward the choking-arm side. Q Why does the top win by getting the arm under the chin rather than just riding the back? The arm under the chin is the shared closing of the rear-naked choke, and it is observable. It gives the top an active target instead of a stall, and it keeps the bottom's chin honest. Q What is the bottom's first job, before escaping? Defend the neck, two hands on the choking arm, chin tucked. Rushing the turn with the neck exposed hands the top the finish. Hands first, then turn. Q Which way do you turn to escape the back, and why? Toward the choking-arm side, into the choke, never away. Turning away feeds your neck to the choke; turning in chases the choking arm off your throat and brings you to face.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Two hands on the choke arm, chin down, strip a light hook, slide the hips to the mat, turn toward the choke side, face.

🥋Top (Attacker)

Stay chest-to-back, follow the hips, keep the seatbelt tight, swim the choking arm under the chin.

Coaching cues

⚓Where is the choke arm?

Ask the bottom: "Which way is the choking arm climbing?" Direct attention to the seatbelt tension, not to a named escape.

🫷Chin or the choke?

Ask the top: "Did you get under the chin, or just ride?" Keeps the top hunting the finish instead of stalling for the count.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Defense restricted per level (hands → strip hook → turn → seatbelt)→Isolates one escape variable at a time Top wins by getting the arm under the chin→Keeps the bottom's chin tucked, gives the top an active target Hold the count or escape→Urgency for the bottom, no stalling for the top No strikes until the top level→Frees the bottom to read the seatbelt before strike defense Live, resisting top→Keeps the tension-reading perception intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the defense ladder isolates one escape variable per level while the bottom keeps reading seatbelt tension and timing from a real, resisting opponent. The top's arm-under-chin win keeps the representativeness, this is the actual danger of the back.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Seatbelt tension and hook pressure → which way the choke is coming, which hook is light, when to turn.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own chin and hip position → whether the neck is safe and whether the corner can turn yet.

👁️Visual

The choking arm swimming high → the timing window to strip a hook and turn into it.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom strips a hook and turns to face faster than the top can slide the arm under the chin. Track escapes to face vs. chokes closed, and whether the chin stays tucked as the top swims. The hand-fight-and-turn versus swim-and-close race is the order parameter; when the bottom consistently turns into the lightened choke arm, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: being on the back under a seatbelt and turning to face before the choke closes, the exact problem of back defense in grappling and in MMA.

Simplified: defense ladderno strikes L1-4reset on the count

Deepens the back-control danger behind Ground Escape; the hand-fighting transfers into Ground Defensive Submission and Ground to Standing.

Readiness to progress

  • Keeps the chin tucked and two hands on the choke arm under pressure
  • Strips a hook before turning the corner
  • Turns toward the choking-arm side, not away
  • Breaks the seatbelt or wins the 2-on-1 when the first turn fails

Warning signs

Chin lifts, neck exposed Turns away from the choke Pulls the arm without stripping a hook Panics when the first turn fails

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Stop on a closed choke or neck crank 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Escape Follow-on→Ground Defensive Submission · Ground to Standing Related→Decision States · Guard Recovery

docs.title: Back Maintenance
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingBack Maintenance

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

The top-side mirror of Back Escape. Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveAdvancedControl → Finish

Keep the back and slide the choking arm under the chin before the bottom turns to face.

StartTop chest-to-back in a seatbelt, both hooks in; bottom seated against the chest, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop follows the hips, keeps the seatbelt, and works the choking arm under the chin; bottom defends and turns. → FinishChoking arm under the chin 3s → top · Turn in to face, reverse, or stand → bottom · Out of bounds → loss.

The back is a race, not a resting place.

Stay glued chest-to-back, follow the hips, and swim the arm to the throat. If you ride for time, they turn to the front.

What to Read

Attune to the bottom's hands and the side they turn toward, the moment their hand-fight opens a lane to the throat or their shoulders rotate to escape. That specifies when to swim the choking arm and which hook to weight, not a memorized sequence. When they commit to turning one way, the chin opens on the other.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top (attacker, on the back), one bottom (defender). PositionTop chest-to-back in a seatbelt (one arm over the shoulder, one under the armpit, hands clasped), both hooks in; bottom seated against the chest. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesTop keeps the back and works the choke; bottom defends the neck and turns to face. Start & resetBegin from seatbelt and hooks; reset on the arm under the chin, an escape, or the count.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to keep the back and slide the choking arm across the throat, under the chin.

Stay chest-to-back, follow the hips, keep the seatbelt tight, weight the bottom hook. Bait the hand-fight, then swim the choking arm under the chin. Control is proven by closing on the neck, not by riding for time. VS 🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to defend the neck, strip a hook, and turn in to face (or reverse, or stand).

Two hands on the choking arm, chin tucked. Slide the hips down, strip a hook, then turn toward the choking-arm side. Survival first, then escape.

The Rules

🎯 Top wins by the arm under the chinThe top proves control by sliding the choking arm across the throat and under the chin (the closing position every rear-naked choke shares), held 3 seconds, not by sitting on the back. Stop at the closing position, before any squeeze. 🤜 Bottom wins by facingThe bottom wins by turning in to face, reversing, or standing clear. Stripping a hook and facing kills the back, the choke can no longer be closed from the front. Mirrors Back Escape. ⏱️ Hold the count or finishIf the top keeps the back for the set count (start at 20 seconds) without getting the arm under the chin, the round resets. If the bottom turns to face first, the bottom wins. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are control only, so both players read the seatbelt and the hand-fight before strikes are added. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound from the back: strikes are the disincentivization tool that punishes a passive defense. Ground games train smashing, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round.

How to Win

Win Top slides the choking arm under the chin (3s) → top wins.The choking hand crossed the throat and under the jaw, the shared closing of the rear-naked choke, held three seconds. The observable proof that back control beat the hand-fight. Stop at the closing position, before any squeeze. Switch Bottom turns in to face, reverses, or stands → bottom wins, switch roles.The turn is graded: chest-to-chest is the floor, on top is better, standing clear is best. Mirrors Back Escape. Reset Top holds the back the full count, no arm under the chin → reset, same roles.The top kept the back but never got under the chin before the count expired. Resets from seatbelt and hooks. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position.

The Levels

1Chest-to-back, no hands linkedStay glued.Top keeps chest-to-back contact and follows the hips with no seatbelt or hooks yet, just connection. Builds the glued-to-the-back base that makes everything else possible. 2Connect hips and shouldersSeatbelt on.Top adds the seatbelt and weights the bottom hook, controlling the upper body and hips together.
