Reading the bottom's turn becomes the task. 3Seatbelt and both hooksMaintain through the scramble.Full back control: seatbelt and both hooks, maintained as the bottom hand-fights and slides the hips. Staying on the back through the escape is the focus. 4One hook, hunt the chinTrade a hook for the choke.Top drops to one hook and commits to swimming the choking arm under the chin, accepting the lower control to chase the closing. The control-to-finish trade becomes the skill. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Continuous from seatbelt and hooks, strikes live, until the top gets under the chin or the bottom turns to face. Finish slow, tap early. The bottom's turn is now urgent.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why is the back a race rather than a resting place? If the top rides for time, the bottom strips a hook and turns to face, killing the position. The top has to close on the neck before the turn, the win is the arm under the chin, not the seconds held. Q What opens the lane to the throat? The bottom's commitment to turning one way, or an over-eager hand-fight. When they turn one way, the chin opens on the other, swim the choking arm then. Q What does the top trade at Level 4, and why? Drops to one hook to free the body to chase the choke. Less positional control, but the closing is the win, so the trade is worth it once the chin is there.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Attacker)

Stay chest-to-back, follow the hips, keep the seatbelt tight, weight the bottom hook, bait the hand-fight, swim the choking arm under the chin.

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Two hands on the choking arm, chin tucked, slide the hips down, strip a hook, turn toward the choking-arm side.

Coaching cues

🏁Chin or just riding?

Ask the top: "Did you close on the chin, or ride for time?" Keeps the back a race for the finish, not a stall.

🧲Glue and follow

Ask the top: "When did your chest peel off the back?" The instant the connection breaks is the instant the bottom turns.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top wins by the arm under the chin→Forces the chase to the finish, no stalling Under the chin, 3s, no squeeze→A clean, observable closing short of the choke Hold the count or finish→Urgency for the top, a real turn window for the bottom Live, hand-fighting bottom→Keeps the hand-fight and hip-reading perception intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): each level adds one control layer (connection, seatbelt, hooks, then the chin chase) while the top keeps reading the bottom's hands and hips from a live opponent. The arm-under-the-chin win keeps the representativeness, back control only matters if it closes on the neck.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

Seatbelt tension and the bottom's hand-fight → the lane to the throat and the hook to weight.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own chest connection and hooks → whether the back is still controlled, when to trade a hook for the chase.

👁️Visual

The side the bottom turns toward → the open chin and which way to swim.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top gets the arm under the chin faster than the bottom strips a hook and turns. Track chin-closings vs. turns to face, and whether the top stays glued chest-to-back through the scramble. The close-the-chin versus strip-and-turn race is the order parameter.

Representativeness

Models: keeping the back and finishing the strangle before the opponent turns to face, the exact problem on the back in MMA and grappling.

Simplified: control ladderno strikes L1-4reset on the count

Mirror of Back Escape; deepens the top side of Ground Control; the chin-closing transfers to the rear-naked-choke finish game.

Readiness to progress

  • Stays glued chest-to-back through the bottom's hip-slides
  • Weights the bottom hook to deny the turn
  • Swims the chin when the bottom commits to a side
  • Trades a hook for the closing only when the chin is there

Warning signs

Rides for time instead of chasing the chin Chest peels off and the bottom turns Both hooks lost in the scramble Squeezes before the closing position

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling, GnP by coach permission 🛑 Stop at the closing position, no squeeze, tap early 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Control · Back Escape Follow-on→Body Lock to Ground Related→TKO Pin · Decision States

docs.title: Body Lock to Ground
docs.text:
Open Space · GrapplingBody Lock to Ground

Provisional (WIP): invented 2026-06-15, re-validated on the mat 2026-06-20, pending final coach sign-off

Built on the mat on 2026-06-15. Alex flagged it as a standard, "we're going to play that one a lot." It trains the standing back-and-body-lock grounding battle, the offensive mirror of Back Escape. Re-validated on 2026-06-20 (the R5 mat pass), which refined the defender's escape to a 50/50 Clinch reset and added a wall-proximity start. Awaiting a final pass before the WIP tag comes off.

Open SpaceGrapplingOffensiveIntermediateGrounding Battle

Put a standing opponent's hips on the ground from the body lock or the back, before they break the grip and turn.

StartAttacker has a body lock or chest-to-back control; defender sets their posture first, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalAttacker off-balances the hips to the ground; defender breaks back to the 50/50 clinch, or threatens the neck. → FinishHips down on top 1-2s → attacker · Break back to the 50/50 clinch or a neck strangle → defender · Out of bounds → loss.

Don't hold and wait. The grip that waits, loses.

Off-balance the instant it starts, side to side, until the hips touch down. Especially against someone stronger, move first.

What to Read

Attune to the defender's posts and weight shifts, where their base is loaded and which post is holding them up. Drag them to the weak side, take a post away, and the hips fall. Read the shifting base, not a memorized throw. When their hands go to the mat, they're no longer fighting your hands.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one attacker (behind or around), one defender (held). PositionTwo starts: a body lock, or chest-to-back standing control. Defender sets overhooks / whizzer / posture before "go". BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. Start one body-length off the wall, the wall is live but not automatic. RolesAttacker grounds the hips; defender breaks free, off-balances out, or threatens the neck. Start & resetLock in tight, then "go"; reset on a grounding, a full separation, or a neck strangle.

The Matchup

🤼 Attacker (Grounding)

Trying to put one or both of the defender's hips on the ground and end on top, from the body lock or the back.

Off-balance immediately, don't hold and wait. Use feet-to-feet contact, your head as a fulcrum, and side-to-side drags. From the body lock you may switch to a single leg. Get their hands to the mat, then they're posting, not fighting your grip. VS 🛡️ Defender (Surviving)

Trying to break back to the 50/50 clinch, off-balance the attacker to break free, or threaten a top neck strangle.

Set a whizzer or overhook before the start. Split the grip down the line, put a hip in as a blade so you're not square, then turn and face. Keep one hand back to stop the re-shot. A guillotine or front headlock is a live win.

The Rules

🎯 Attacker wins by hips down 1-2sOne or both of the defender's hips put on the ground with the attacker on top, held one to two seconds to clear a scramble. Knees, shoulders, and turtle do not count, the hips have to touch. 🐢 Turtle is not a winIf the defender drops to turtle, that is not a grounding. The attacker still needs the hips down. A belly-down grapevine with the hips flattened counts as hips down. ⛰️ Must end on topThe attacker has to finish with weight on top of the grounded hips. Ending underneath, even in a strong jiu-jitsu bottom position, does not score, the game rewards top control, not pulling guard. 🔁 Redemption shot on re-connectIf the defender breaks off and the attacker re-connects (for example re-shoots a single leg), it is on the defender to push all the way to a full separation. The attacker gets that redemption shot before the round is called for the defender. ⚖️ No falling body weightNo dropping your full weight on a downed partner. Off-balance and follow to top under control, this is a grounding game, not a slam. 🪢 Defender's neck strangle is liveA top neck strangle (guillotine or front headlock) that submits the attacker is a defender win, the same threat that keeps a real grounding attempt honest. 🤼 Defender resets to the 50/50 clinchThe defender's separation win is to break back to a neutral 50/50 over-under clinch, not just to flail free. Handing the defender the 50/50 as the escape route is deliberate, it incentivizes the attacker to keep pummeling and re-grounding rather than stalling on a stalemate grip. 🥊 Strikes on, GnP by permissionStrikes are live as a disincentivization tool. Use realistic strikes with space, not pitter-patter, by coach permission. Ground games train smashing, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player leaves it, that player loses the round.

How to Win

Attacker Hips on the ground, on top, held 1-2s → attacker wins.One or both hips down with the attacker's weight on top, held one to two seconds to prove it past the scramble. A belly-down grapevine with flat hips counts; turtle, knees, and shoulders do not. Defender Break back to the 50/50 clinch, off-balance break, or a neck strangle → defender wins.Break the grip back to a neutral 50/50 over-under clinch, off-balance the attacker to force the break, or finish a top neck strangle (guillotine / front headlock). The redemption rule means a half-break that gets re-connected is not yet a win. Loss Leave the perimeter, or drop falling body weight → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round. Dropping full weight on a downed partner is a safety foul.

The Levels

1Body lock, off-balance onlyNo strikes, find the weak post.Start in a body lock, defender sets their posture first. Attacker works feet-to-feet and side-to-side off-balancing to put the hips down, no strikes. Finds the drag-to-the-weak-side mechanic. 2Body lock, defender breaksGrounding vs separation.Same start, the defender now actively breaks grips and separates, and may switch to a single. The redemption shot is live. The grounding-versus-escape race becomes the task. 3Chest-to-back startThe tougher position.Start from standing back control, chest-to-back. Harder for the attacker to finish, harder for the defender to turn. Remove a post, drag to the weak side, hips down. Defender hand-fights the front-headlock and looks to turn and face. 4Add the neckDefender's strangle is live.From either start, the defender's top neck strangle (guillotine / front headlock) is now a win, the cost that punishes a reckless, posture-broken grounding attempt. 5Full expressionRandom start, wall live, strikes on.Coach calls body lock or chest-to-back at random, strikes live, play starts one body-length off the wall. Lock in tight before "go", off-balance immediately, finish the hips down or break back to the 50/50.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q On offense, why is holding the grip and waiting the worst thing you can do? A static grip loses eventually, especially against someone stronger. Off-balance immediately, side to side, so the defender is always reacting and never gets to settle and break free. Q What does getting the defender's hands to the mat buy you? If their hands are posting to hold themselves up, they are no longer fighting your hands. A posted hand is a committed base you can drag away to put the hips down. Q Why lock in tight before "go" instead of starting loose? Like arm-wrestlers fighting for the grip before the start, you claim your strongest position first. Starting loose hands the defender a free chance to break. Unless there's a clear skill or power gap to scaffold, take all the claim you can. Q As the defender, how do you break the grip without getting taken straight back? Split the grip down the line (the cigar-cutter), put a hip in as a blade so you're not square and can't be dragged straight back, then turn and face, keeping one hand back to stop the re-shot.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤼Attacker (Grounding)

Lock in tight, off-balance immediately and continuously, feet-to-feet and head as a fulcrum, remove the weak-side post, get the hands to the mat, finish hips down on top.

🛡️Defender (Surviving)

Set a whizzer first, split the grip down the line, hip in as a blade, turn and face, keep one hand back against the re-shot, threaten the neck when the posture breaks.

Coaching cues

⚡Move first, especially vs stronger

Ask the attacker: "Did you off-balance on go, or hold and wait?" The grip that waits loses. Against a stronger partner, motion beats strength.

🎚️Scaffold only on a gap

Start locked into your strongest position. Only give a softer start when there's a real skill or power discrepancy worth scaffolding, otherwise take full claim.

🔄The 50/50 reset shapes the game

Handing the defender the 50/50 clinch as the escape incentivizes pummeling and re-grounding instead of a stalemate. The reward you give the defender shapes the behavior that emerges, that is the dial.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Hips-down win, 1-2s hold→A clean, observable grounding past the scramble Turtle is not a win, must end on top→Rewards real top control, not stalling or pulling guard Redemption shot on re-connect→Trains finishing the separation, not a half-escape Defender's neck strangle is live→Punishes a reckless, posture-broken grounding attempt Defender sets posture before "go"→A representative, fightable start, not a gifted back Defender's escape is the 50/50 clinch→A representative re-neutralization, and a reward that incentivizes pummeling over stalling

Implements Representative Learning Design (Renshaw et al., 2019): the body-lock and chest-to-back starts model the two most common standing rear-control battles, and the hips-down win keeps the real goal, grounding the opponent, intact while the neck threat preserves the cost of overcommitting.

What athletes read

✋Haptic

Load through the grip and the defender's posts → which base is committed, which way to drag, when the grip is breaking.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hips square or bladed → whether you can be dragged straight back, and when the turn-to-face is open.

👁️Visual

Hands going to the mat, a broken posture → the post to drag and the neck-strangle window.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the attacker off-balances the hips to the ground faster than the defender can break the grip and turn. Track groundings vs. full separations, and whether the attacker stays in motion instead of holding and waiting. The off-balance versus break-and-turn race is the order parameter; when the attacker reliably grounds the hips by moving first, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the standing back-and-body-lock battle, grounding a turtled or standing opponent and finishing on top, the exact problem after a clinch or back-take in MMA.

Two starts: body lock + backhips down, on top, 1-2sstrikes by permission

Offensive mirror of Back Escape; follows Open Space Takedown; the top finish feeds Ground Control and Mount Maintenance.

Readiness to progress

  • Off-balances immediately, never holds and waits
  • Drags to the weak-side post to drop the hips
  • Gets the defender's hands to the mat
  • Finishes on top, not underneath

Warning signs

Holds the grip and waits Counts turtle or a knee-down as a finish Ends underneath and calls it a win Drops falling body weight

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling, no falling body weight 🛑 Tap early on neck strangles, slow finishes only 🔁 Reset on a grounding, a full separation, or a stall

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Open Space Takedown · Back Escape Follow-on→Ground Control · Mount Maintenance Related→50/50 Clinch · Winning the Circle · Win Ladder

docs.title: Clinch Denial
docs.text:
Transition · WrestlingClinch Denial TransitionWrestlingDefensiveIntermediateOpen Space

Deny the clinch before it forms, frames, hand fighting, and movement keep open space.

StartTwo fighters at mid-range in open space, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalAttacker locks a clinch; defender denies connection & keeps space. → FinishDeny for the round → switch · Clinch locks → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Don't escape the clinch, never let it form.

Frames, hand fighting, and movement keep open space. Denying connection is far easier than escaping it once locked.

What to Read

Attune to the felt frames, the inertial array at the contact: how the attacker's reach and posture load against your hands and forearms as they close, not their eyes or their feet. That haptic information specifies which grip is coming and which way to frame before connection sets.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off at mid-range. RangeMid-range, open space. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne attacker, one defender. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset after a clinch locks or space is created.

The Matchup

🤼 Attacker

Trying to close distance and lock a meaningful clinch, collar tie, underhook, or body lock with control.

Swim hands to connection; chain attempts when denied. Brief contact isn't a clinch, you need a meaningful, controlling grip. Scale pressure to the defender's success. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to deny connection and keep open space, frames, hand fighting, and movement, not escape.

Post and frame, hand-fight the grips, circle and angle off entries. No striking at Level 1, pure denial tools. Stop them before they connect, not after.

The Rules

🤼 Attacker must attempt clinchThe attacker must seek a clinch entry, no striking-only pressure. This keeps the defender's reading problem focused on connection denial. 🛡️ Denial tools only, no strikes (L1)At Level 1 the defender uses frames, hand fighting, and movement only, no strikes. This isolates the pure denial solution before striking is integrated. 🔗 Meaningful connection onlyBrief hand contact is not a clinch. The attacker must establish a controlling grip, collar tie, underhook, or body lock, for it to count. 🏁 Game ends on clinchPlay ends the moment a meaningful clinch is established, this game trains denial, not escape from a locked clinch (a different game). ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly.

How to Win

Switch Deny for the round or create space → switch roles.When the defender denies the clinch for the set time or creates significant space, the players switch roles. The defender kept open space for the full round or broke clean, and earns the attacking role. Reset Clinch locks → reset, same roles.When the attacker locks a meaningful clinch, reset to mid-range, same roles. Both hands connected with control, collar tie, underhook, or body lock. Then begin again from mid-range. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.When both of a player's feet leave the perimeter, that player loses, whoever they are. Crossing the marked perimeter loses the game instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the cage-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1No strikesPure entry vs. denial.Pure clinch entry against pure denial, no strikes either side. Build hand fighting and framing with no other pressure. 2Attacker pressuresForward drive to close space.The attacker adds forward pressure to close space. The defender must deny while being backed up. 3Add movementFootwork and angles.The defender uses footwork and angles to deny; the attacker must cut off and close. Movement-based denial. 4Full MMAStrike to create space.The defender can strike to create space; the attacker must close through the strike threat. Clinch denial under full MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to know which grip is coming and which way to frame? The felt frames, the inertial array at the contact: how the attacker's reach and posture load against your hands and forearms as they close. Not their eyes or their feet. That haptic information arrives before connection sets. Q Why does this game end the moment a clinch locks rather than letting you escape? It trains denial, not escape. Denying connection is far easier than escaping a locked clinch, so the whole skill lives in stopping them before they connect. Q What counts as a real clinch here, and what doesn't? A meaningful, controlling grip: a collar tie, underhook, or body lock. Brief hand contact is not a clinch. The attacker has to establish control for it to count. Q What is the difference between framing and just pushing? A frame redirects and creates an angle; a shove only delays the entry. Feel which grip is coming and meet it, don't just brace against forward pressure.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤼Attacker

Close distance; swim hands to connection; use collar ties, underhooks, body lock attempts; chain when denied.

🛡️Defender

Post and frame to create distance; hand-fight grips; circle and angle off entries; push off and disengage.

Coaching cues

⏱️Stop them before they connect

"Are you stopping them before they connect or after?" Early denial, frames and hand fighting before the clinch sets. Late denial becomes escape, which is harder.

✋Frame, don't just push

"Am I framing effectively or just pushing?" A frame redirects and creates an angle; a shove only delays. Feel which grip is coming and meet it.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Attacker must attempt clinch→Consistent clinch attempts to defend Defender can't strike (L1)→Isolates hand fighting & framing skill Game ends on clinch (not escape)→Focuses on denial, not escape after connection Progressive tools (frames → movement → strikes)→Scaffolded learning of denial options Mid-range start→Time to read and react before contact

Develops clinch-denial affordances, perceiving entry attempts and selecting denial tools. Denial is easier than escape; this game exploits that principle (Renshaw et al., 2019).

What the defender reads

👁️Visual

Entry angle, hand targeting, distance closure, head position → frame direction & movement timing.

✋Haptic

Grip attempts & reach loading on the frames → which hand to fight.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own frame position & strength → denial effectiveness.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender meets the entry in time, frame and hand-fight arriving before the grip sets, so they stay free rather than getting clinched. Track clean denials vs. clinches locked, and whether the frame re-sets between attempts. That timing relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: a striker keeping a wrestler or clinch-fighter from establishing connection to keep the fight at distance.

Simplified: no strikes (L1)ends on clinchdenial not escape

Hand fighting and framing here transfer to any clinch-denial situation, opposite perspective to Wall Control.

Readiness to progress

  • Denies ~70%+ vs. varied attackers
  • Uses frames, hand fighting & push-offs
  • Can describe: "I stopped their underhook by…"
  • Composed under forward pressure

Warning signs

Gets clinched consistently Only uses one denial tool Backs up without framing Panics when grips are attempted

Safety & related games

🤝 Light contact on frames & hand fighting 🛑 Stop on excessive force or frustration 🔁 Reset if neither player is progressing

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Pressure to Clinch Follow-on→Wall Control (opposite perspective) Related→Hand Controls

docs.title: Close-Range Defense
docs.text:
Integration · StrikingClose-Range Defense IntegrationStrikingDefensiveIntermediateClose Quarters

Layer block, parry, and dodge in the pocket, learning when to use which.

StartTwo fighters at close range inside a marked perimeter; attacker pressing. → The GoalAttacker finds openings; defender picks the right defense without exiting. → FinishNo winner, reset each rep. Leave the perimeter → loss.

Don't pick one defense, pick the one the shot calls for.

Stay in the pocket and layer block, parry, and dodge under pressure. The skill is knowing when to use which.

What to Read

Attune to the rate of expansion (τ) of each incoming strike in the combination, how fast it grows and along which path, read via shoulder–hip motion at center mass, not the opponent's eyes or their absolute distance. Shoulder motion is the punch line; hip motion is power and kicks, "the little X" holds both at once and tells you which defense to pick.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, attacker slightly ahead, defender behind. RangeInside striking range. BoundaryA marked perimeter, defender can't exit freely. RolesOne attacker, one defender, coach rotates. Start & resetAttacker presses; reset after a defended sequence or a clean strike.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to compromise the defender's openings and land strikes through the guard.

Throw combinations and allow resets, don't crowd or overwhelm. Start narrow (head only, or straights only) and scale variety, speed, and combo length only as the defender succeeds. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to defend every strike without exiting, mixing tight blocking, parrying, and static dodging.

Can't exit the pocket. The toolkit opens up level by level, block first, then parry, then dodge, then counter. Pick the defense the strike calls for.

The Rules

🚫 Defender cannot exitThe defender must stay and defend in the pocket, no backing up continuously. This prevents the footwork-escape habit and forces real defensive skill. 🥊 Combinations with resetThe attacker throws combinations but must allow a reset between them, no continuous overwhelming pressure, so the defender has processing time to learn. 📏 Reasonable distanceThe attacker must strike from reasonable range and can't crowd the defender, keeping the defensive problem readable. 🧰 Tools open progressivelyThe defender's available solutions unlock by level, block, then parry, then dodge, then counter, so each is layered in rather than dumped at once. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly, training cage-edge awareness.

How to Win

Diagnostic Scored, not won, % defended + selection quality.No winner, the point is learning when to use which solution, not beating the partner. The coach reads selection quality, not a tally of points. Reset Sequence defended or strike lands → reset, same roles.Either outcome ends the rep; begin again from the close-range start so the next combination can be read fresh. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.In the confined-perimeter variant, crossing the line ends it instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the boundary awareness a fighter needs near the cage.

The Levels

1Tight blocking onlyShell up; straights to the head.The defender can only block; the attacker throws straight head punches only. Absorb and reset. 2Add parryingBlock or parry; add body shots.The defender can now block or parry; the attacker adds body strikes. Choose the appropriate defense for each strike. 3Add dodgingBlock, parry, or dodge; add hooks.Static head movement joins the toolkit; the attacker adds hooks and varied combinations. Full defensive integration. 4Add counter opportunityOne counter after defense.After a successful defense the defender may throw one counter; the attacker must respect the threat. Defense as a setup for offense. 5Turn the cornerReposition for angle.The defender can work to turn the corner on the attacker, creating an angle for counter-attack or escape, defensive footwork integration. 6Full MMAAdd shot / clinch threat.The attacker can now shoot or clinch too, defend strikes while denying the grappling entry. Shelling up without posture invites takedowns.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What tells you which defense the strike calls for? The rate of expansion (τ) and path of each strike, read via shoulder and hip motion at center mass: shoulder is the punch line, hip is power and kicks. Not the eyes, not absolute distance. Q Why is the defender barred from exiting the pocket? It kills the footwork-escape habit and forces real defensive selection: block, parry, or dodge in place instead of running. Q Why do the tools (block, parry, dodge, counter) unlock one level at a time? So each is layered in, not dumped at once. The defender builds degeneracy: multiple solutions for similar problems, picking the one the shot calls for. Q What is the trade-off when you dodge instead of block? Going offline with the head can open a counter, but more offline means more exposed to significant strikes. Hands close defend better; hands out counter better.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Compromise openings and land strikes; start narrow (head or straights only); scale difficulty to the partner's success.

🛡️Defender

Mix defenses as the toolkit opens; block first, then layer parrying and dodging; stay in the pocket.

Coaching cues

👁️See center mass

Keep eye contact on center mass, not the head, the head carries no information and the eyes lie. Center mass holds shoulder (punch line) and hip (power, kicks) at once: "the little X."

⚓Base under fire

Strike force off-balances you, that's what defeats the counter, not the damage. Bend the knees, create suspension "like an ATV over bumps, " and keep energy to drive forward.

Trade-offs to feel

🤲Hands close

Easier to defend strikes, harder to counter.

👐Hands out

More compromised, but can disrupt the attacker or counter.

🌀Dodging

More offline head = more exposed to significant strikes.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Cannot exit→Forces defensive engagement, no escape option Progressive tool unlock→Scaffolds learning of defensive integration Reset between combos→Processing time for learning Close-quarters start→Must defend immediately, no distance buffer All solutions viable at range→Defensive selection actually matters

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), progressively unlocking solutions lets the defender discover which tool fits which attack. Degeneracy develops: multiple solutions for similar problems.

What the defender reads

👁️Visual

Punch type, trajectory & combination rhythm → select block, parry, or dodge and anticipate follow-ups.

✋Haptic

Strike impact location → adjust guard position.

🧭Proprioceptive

Guard position & coverage → gaps in defense.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender's chosen defense lands in time with each strike, track strikes defended vs. shots eaten, whether the right solution was selected, and whether the guard re-sets between combinations. That timing-and-selection relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: being pressured in close quarters and defending multiple strikes while looking to counter or escape.

Simplified: progressive unlockresets between combosstay in range

Integrates the isolated solutions, the confined-perimeter variant extends into clinch off-balancing and takedowns, see The Square.

Readiness to progress

  • Right defense for the right strike (~70%+)
  • Smooth transitions between defensive tools
  • Composure held under combination pressure
  • Can articulate why they chose a defense

Warning signs

Only uses one defensive tool Panics under combinations Backs up despite the constraint

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact, felt, not damaging 🛑 Stop on overwhelming pressure or lost composure 🔁 Reset if the attacker isn't allowing defensive opportunities

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Parry the Straight (and other skill-isolation games) Follow-on→Touch and Don't Get Touched · Pressure to Takedown Related→Defensive Solutions · Hand Controls

docs.title: Closed Guard Bottom
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingClosed Guard Bottom

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper (prerequisite chain corrected per the pressure-test); awaits validation against a live grappling class. Details may change.

GroundGrapplingCombinedIntermediateBreak & Attack

Break their posture, then take the back, the sweep, or the top.

StartBottom in closed guard controlling posture; top working to posture up, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom keeps the posture broken and converts it; top rebuilds and opens the guard. → FinishSweep, climb to the back (chest-to-back, 3s), or a controlled finish threat → bottom · Posture rebuilt and guard opened → top · Out of bounds → loss.

A closed guard that only holds on is just a slow way to lose.

Broken posture is currency, spend it. Every second their head is down, something is available.

What to Read

Attune to the top's weight through your legs and grips, where their hands are based and when their head crosses their hands. A head pulled past the hands has no base under it: that's the climb. Hands on your chest are posts to trap; hands on the mat are doors to the back. The guard tells you which attack is open, listen with the legs.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom (closed guard), one top (posturing). PositionBottom's ankles locked behind the top, grips on; top kneeling, posture contested. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesBottom breaks posture and converts; top rebuilds posture and opens the guard. Start & resetBegin locked with grips contested; reset on a conversion, an open, or the round cap.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Closed Guard)

Trying to keep the top's posture broken and convert it into a sweep, a back-climb, or a controlled finish threat.

Arms and legs pull together, the lock is an engine, not a belt. Trap a hand when it posts on your chest, climb the hips when the head comes down. Pick the attack the posture break offers, don't force a favorite. VS 🥋 Top (Posturing)

Trying to rebuild posture, win the grips, and open the guard.

Head up, spine stacked, hands fighting grips, never both hands on the mat. Posture is rebuilt in pieces: win the head first, then the grips, then the hips. Opening the guard from a broken posture isn't possible, stop trying.

The Rules

🔒 Posture is the resourceThe round revolves around the top's posture: broken, the bottom attacks; rebuilt, the top opens. Both sides should always know who owns it right now. 🧗 The back is climbed, not jumpedThe back-take counts when the bottom's hips climb outside the top's arms and reach chest-to-back, held 3 seconds. The climb route (hips to shoulder line) is the skill being trained. 🛑 Finish threats are positionsA finish threat wins when the controlling position is locked (arm trapped and extended, or a closed loop on the neck), then everyone stops. We prove the position, never the crank. Tap early, finish nothing fast. ⏱️ Round cap, no stallingRun a set round cap (start at 60 seconds). If neither side wins by the cap, reset and switch roles. A locked guard that attacks nothing is a stall, the cap punishes it. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are grappling only, so the bottom can read posture and base without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. A postured top now strikes down, breaking posture becomes strike defense too. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Win Bottom sweeps to top → bottom wins.A reversal that lands on top. The broken posture converted into position the most direct way. Win Bottom climbs to the back, chest-to-back held 3s → bottom wins.Hips outside the arms, chest to the top's back, held. The highest-value conversion and the one the climb levels build toward. Win Bottom locks a controlled finish threat → bottom wins, stop there.An arm trapped and extended or a closed loop on the neck, position proven, nothing cranked. Play stops on the lock. Switch Top rebuilds posture and opens the guard → top wins.Head up, grips won, ankles unlocked. From here the position is the closed-guard-pass game, that's the next page. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Own the postureKeep them broken, nothing else.The round is only the posture war: bottom keeps the top's head down with arms and legs together, top rebuilds. Whoever owns the posture at the bell owns the round. The engine of every closed-guard attack, isolated. 2Trap the postsCapture the basing hand.When the top bases a hand on your chest or the mat, trap it (wrist plus elbow, or 2-on-1) before it pulls back. A trapped post means the next posture break has nothing to catch it. 3Hips to the shoulderClimb outside the arms.From the broken posture and a trapped arm, the bottom climbs the hips outside the top's arms toward the shoulder line. The doorway position to the back, learned as its own destination first. 4ConvertBack, sweep, or finish threat.The full task, grappling only: break, trap, climb, and convert to whichever win the top's reaction offers. Forcing one favorite attack into every posture break is the tell this level exists to remove. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Light strikes on. The top strikes from posture, so breaking posture now disarms as well as attacks. Closed guard the way MMA prices it.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why is a holding-only closed guard a slow loss? Because the top gets unlimited posture-rebuild attempts against a passive lock, and in MMA, a postured top strikes. Broken posture is currency: spend it on an attack or lose it. Q What do the top's hands tell you? Hands based on your chest are posts to trap; hands on the mat are doors to the back. And a head pulled past the hands has no base under it, that's the climb. Q What makes the back-take count? Hips climbed outside the arms, chest-to-back, held 3 seconds. The climb route is the skill; jumping past it into a scramble proves nothing. Q In what order does the top rebuild posture? Head first, then grips, then hips. Trying to open the guard from a broken posture feeds every attack the bottom has.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Closed Guard)

Break posture with arms and legs together, trap the posts, climb the hips outside the arms, convert to whichever win the reaction offers.

🥋Top (Posturing)

Rebuild head-grips-hips in order, never base both hands on the mat, open the guard only from a won posture.

Coaching cues

💱What did you buy?

Ask the bottom: "You broke the posture three times, what did it buy you?" Posture breaks that purchase nothing are the stall in disguise.

🧗Which door opened?

Ask after each conversion: "What did the top do that made that the right attack?" Trains attack selection from the reaction, not the repertoire.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Posture-war opening level→Makes the round's central resource explicit before attacks exist Trap-the-post level→Same post-punishing grammar as the other guard games, compounding transfer Climb proven by chest-to-back 3s→Rewards the route, not the scramble Three win doors for the bottom→Attack selection becomes the trained skill Round cap→Punishes the hold-and-wait guard directly

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the break → trap → climb → convert ladder sequences closed-guard offense as perceptual stages, and the three-door win structure keeps the attack choice coupled to the top's live reaction instead of a rehearsed sequence.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Top's weight through the legs and grips → posture owned or rebuilding, hips light or heavy.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hip height relative to the top's arms → whether the climb doorway is open.

👁️Visual

Head past the hands, hands on chest or mat → which of the three doors just opened.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom converts posture breaks before the top rebuilds. Track conversions (sweep / back / threat) per posture break, and which door each conversion used. When the attack consistently matches the reaction, three different doors across a session, the selection skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the closed guard as MMA actually prices it, a position you must attack from immediately, because a postured top turns it into a striking platform.

Simplified: door ladderno strikes L1-4round cap

Builds on Seated Guard Retention's post-punishing; mirrors Closed Guard Pass.

Readiness to progress

  • Keeps posture broken with arms and legs together
  • Traps posts before they recover
  • Climbs the hips outside the arms on the route
  • Picks the door the reaction offers, all three across a session

Warning signs

Holds the lock and waits Forces the same attack into every break Climbs inside the arms, gives the head back Top bases both hands on the mat

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Finish threats stop on the lock, nothing cranked, tap early 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Leg Reclaim · Seated Guard Retention Follow-on→Guard Submission Hunt (planned) Mirror→Closed Guard Pass Related→Guard Recovery · Decision States

docs.title: Closed Guard Pass
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingClosed Guard Pass

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class. Details may change.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediatePassing

Win your posture, open the legs, pass to chest-to-chest.

StartTop kneeling inside a closed guard, posture contested, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop breaks the grips, opens the feet, and passes; bottom breaks posture and attacks. → FinishChest-to-chest past the legs, held 3s → top · Sweep, climb to the back, or break posture to a finish threat → bottom · Out of bounds → loss.

The guard doesn't open from the legs, it opens from your posture.

Win the head and the grips first. A postured spine makes the locked feet cost more than they hold.

What to Read

Attune to the pull on your posture, where the bottom's grips and legs are dragging your head and hips. Every pull commits the bottom's hips to one side and tells you which side to pressure and when the ankles unlock. Posture regained is space created; pass into that space, not against the lock.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top (passer), one bottom (closed guard). PositionTop kneeling inside the bottom's closed guard, posture contested by grips. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesTop breaks grips, opens the guard, and passes; bottom controls posture and attacks. Start & resetBegin from locked guard; reset on a pass, a sweep, a back-take, or the round cap.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Passer)

Trying to win posture, open the locked feet, and pass to a chest-to-chest pin past the legs.

Strip the grips before you stand. Keep the spine stacked over the hips, a pulled-down head is a sweep waiting to happen. Open the feet with posture and a stand, not by digging at the ankles from a broken position. VS 🤸 Bottom (Guard)

Trying to break the top's posture and convert it: sweep, climb to the back, or threaten a finish.

Grips plus legs work together, pull the head down as the hips climb. When the top stands, follow the hips, don't hug the ankles. A broken posture is your window: attack it before it rebuilds.

The Rules

🧍 Posture before passingThe top's first job is winning the grips and the spine. Trying to force a pass from a broken posture feeds sweeps, that's the lesson, not a rule violation. 🎯 Pass proven by the holdA pass counts when the top reaches chest-to-chest past the legs and holds it for 3 seconds. Flying past into a scramble proves nothing yet. ⏱️ Round cap, no stallingRun a set round cap (start at 60 seconds). If neither side wins by the cap, reset and switch roles. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are grappling only, so both sides can read posture and grips without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Use striking as a disincentivization tool, the cost that punishes a lazy posture. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Win Top passes to chest-to-chest past the legs, held 3s → top wins.Both legs cleared and a chest-to-chest connection held for 3 seconds, the observable proof the guard is beaten, not just briefly evaded. Switch Bottom sweeps to top, climbs to the back (chest-to-back, 3s), or breaks posture into a finish threat → bottom wins.Any of the three converts the broken posture into something real: a reversal, the back, or a controlled finish position. The threat must be held, not just touched. Reset Round cap expires with the guard intact → reset, switch roles.Neither the pass nor the attack landed before the clock. Switch roles so both sides get the passing problem. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Win the gripsStrip and deny, posture only.No passing yet. Top works only to break the bottom's grips and keep a stacked spine; bottom works to keep posture broken. Whoever owns the posture at the bell owns the round. Isolates the grip-and-spine battle every pass starts from. 2Open the feetMake the ankles unlock.Top adds the next goal: force the locked feet open, by posture, knee position, or standing pressure. The round ends when the feet open or the cap expires. The bottom learns what an opening guard feels like before it costs them. 3Stand to openThe standing break.Top must open the guard by standing. Standing changes every read: the bottom hunts the hips and ankles on the way up, the top learns to stand inside the guard without giving the sweep. 4Open to passPast the knee line to chest.The full task, grappling only: break, open, and pass to chest-to-chest held 3 seconds. Bottom fights every stage and wins by sweep or back-climb. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Light strikes on for both sides. Posture now protects your jaw as well as your base, and the bottom's broken-posture attacks carry real urgency. Pass under fire.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What has to be won before the feet will open? The grips and the posture. A stacked spine over the hips makes the locked feet cost more than they hold. Digging at the ankles from a broken posture feeds the sweep. Q Why does the pass require a 3-second chest-to-chest hold? Because flying past the legs into a scramble proves nothing. The held connection is the observable proof the guard is actually beaten. Q What does every pull on your posture tell you? It tells you where the bottom's hips committed. A pull is information: pressure the side it came from, and pass into the space posture creates. Q When the top stands, what should the bottom chase? The hips, not the ankles. Hugging the ankles surrenders the connection that powers sweeps and back-climbs. Follow the hips up and attack the base.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Passer)

Strip grips, stack the spine, open the feet with posture or a stand, pass past the knee line to chest-to-chest and hold it.

🤸Bottom (Guard)

Break posture with grips and legs together, follow the hips when the top stands, convert every broken posture into a sweep, a back-climb, or a finish threat.

Coaching cues

🧍Where is your head?

Ask the top: "Was your head over your hips when you tried to open?" Directs attention to posture as the opener, not hand strength.

⏳What did the break buy?

Ask the bottom: "You broke the posture, what did you do with it?" A broken posture that isn't attacked rebuilds for free.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Posture-only and open-only levels→Isolates the grip battle and the opening before the pass exists Pass proven by a 3s chest-to-chest hold→Rewards control over scramble-luck, observable for the coach Bottom wins by sweep, back, or finish threat→Keeps the bottom dangerous, the top's posture honest Round cap→Urgency for the top, no guard-sitting for the bottom Live, resisting guard→Keeps the posture-reading perception intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): each level holds one slice of the passing problem (grips → opening → standing → passing) against a fully resisting opponent, so the perception-action loop never gets replaced by a drilled sequence.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

Pull on the collar ties and sleeves, the drag on the spine → which side the bottom's hips committed to.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own head-over-hips alignment → whether the posture is actually won or about to fold.

👁️Visual

The bottom's hip angle and knee line → which pass lane is opening as the feet unlock.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top rebuilds posture faster than the bottom can convert a break. Track passes held vs. sweeps and back-climbs conceded, and whether the guard opens from posture or from ankle-digging. When passes start arriving through the stand-and-open route under a live guard, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: being locked inside a competent closed guard, the canonical top-side problem in grappling exchanges and in MMA once strikes stall.

Simplified: staged openingno strikes L1-4round cap

Deepens the passing side of Ground Access; mirrors Closed Guard Bottom.

Readiness to progress

  • Strips grips before attempting to open
  • Opens the guard from a stacked posture, standing when needed
  • Passes to chest-to-chest and holds, not through, the position
  • Survives broken posture without conceding the sweep

Warning signs

Digs at the ankles with a bent spine Stands without controlling the grips first Sprints past the legs without securing the chest Stalls inside the guard waiting for the cap

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Finish threats are positions, not cranks, stop on the catch 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Access Follow-on→Open Guard Pass · Half-Guard Pass Mirror→Closed Guard Bottom Related→Decision States

docs.title: Counter-Striking
docs.text:
Counter · StrikingCounter-Striking CounterStrikingDefensiveIntermediateTransition Zone

Offense as defense, wait, read the lead, punish it with a timed counter.

StartTwo fighters at striking range inside a marked perimeter; initiator leads, counter-striker waits. → The GoalInitiator lands clean offense; counter-striker times a reactive counter. → FinishClean counter → switch · Clean offense, uncountered → reset.

Don't lead, make them pay for committing.

The counter-striker can't initiate; they wait, read the lead, and punish it. Offense becomes the defense.

What to Read

Attune to the rate of expansion (τ) of the initiator's strike, how fast it commits and travels, read via shoulder–hip motion at center mass, not the opponent's eyes or their absolute distance. That information specifies when the strike commits and which counter window is open, intercept, simultaneous, or punish.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, neutral stance, one leads, one waits. RangeStriking range, not clinch. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne initiator, one counter-striker. Start & resetInitiator leads; reset on a clean exchange or clean lead.

The Matchup

🥊 Initiator

Trying to land clean offense despite the counter threat.

You must initiate, can't just stand there. Vary timing to avoid predictability, use feints to draw counters, and set traps to find clean entries. VS ⚡ Counter-Striker

Trying to interrupt or punish the initiation with a well-timed, reactive counter.

Wait for the initiation, don't lead. Counters must be reactive, not preemptive offense. Read their favorite initiation, then make them pay for committing.

The Rules

⏳ Counter-striker waitsThe counter-striker may not lead, they must wait for the initiation. This eliminates preemptive offense and isolates the reactive skill. 🥊 Initiator must initiateThe initiator can't just stand there, they must lead with offense, providing reliable attacks to counter. ↩️ Counters must be reactiveA counter is a response to a committed strike, not anticipatory offense thrown on a guess. Reading and responding, not predicting. 🥋 Stay in the striking domainBoth players keep it striking, no wrestling, until Level 4, isolating counter-timing without grappling complexity. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly.

How to Win

Switch Land a clean counter → switch roles.A reactive counter that interrupted or punished the offense, landing clean off a real initiation, the counter-striker earns the initiating role. Reset Clean offense, uncountered → reset, same roles.The initiation got through the counter threat, or a clean exchange occurred. Begin again from a neutral stance. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the game instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the cage-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Single counterOne counter vs. single strikes.The counter-striker can throw only one counter; the initiator throws single strikes. Pure basic counter timing. 2Counter combinationsFollow up off the counter.The counter-striker can follow up; the initiator uses 2–3 strike combinations. Chaining off the counter. 3Feints and trapsReal vs. fake.The initiator can feint to draw counters; the counter-striker must read real vs. fake. Counter-intelligence. 4Full MMAAdd shot / clinch threat.The initiator can shoot or clinch if countered; the counter-striker must manage the wrestling threat. Counter-striking under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to time the counter? The rate of expansion (τ) of the initiator's strike via shoulder and hip motion at center mass. It tells you when the strike commits and which counter window is open. Q The counter-striker can't lead. Why strip that away? It eliminates preemptive offense and isolates the reactive skill: counters must be responses to a committed strike, not anticipatory guesses. Q Name the three counter windows. Intercept (before their strike lands), simultaneous (as it extends), and punish (after it misses). The read picks which one the moment affords. Q What is the hardest part of the counter-striker's job? Staying calm and ready while waiting, not leading. Patience is the skill: see it, don't guess.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Initiator

Vary timing to avoid predictability; use feints to draw counters; set traps and find clean entries despite the threat.

⚡Counter-Striker

Read initiation cues and timing patterns; time counters to intercept or punish; explore which counter the moment affords.

Counter timing windows

⏱️Intercept

Before their strike lands, e.g. a jab over their jab.

⚔️Simultaneous

As their strike extends, e.g. a check hook vs. the cross.

🎯Punish

After their strike misses, e.g. counter after a pull.

Coaching cues

👁️See, don't guess

Ask: "Did you wait to see it, or guess?" Reactive countering is skilled; anticipatory gambling is risky. Read and respond, don't predict.

😌Compose while waiting

The hardest part is staying calm and ready without leading. Patience is the skill.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Counter-striker must wait→Forces reactive timing development Initiator must initiate→Creates reliable attacks to counter Striking-only (L1–3)→Isolates counter-timing without wrestling complexity Feints allowed (L3)→Develops real-vs-fake discrimination Striking range→Counter timing is viable (not too close, not too far)

Develops counter-timing affordance perception (Hristovski et al., 2006), the three timing windows emerge through exploration, not instruction, embodying self-organization under constraints.

What the counter-striker reads

👁️Visual: initiation

Weight shift & shoulder rotation → attack initiation detection.

🎯Visual: window

Trajectory, timing & recovery position → select counter type and timing.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance and positioning → counter execution readiness.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the counter-striker's counter lands in time with the initiation, track clean counters that interrupt vs. getting hit or missing the window, and whether they stayed reactive rather than guessing. That timing relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: a counter-fighter waiting for the opponent to lead, then timing strikes to punish the initiation.

Simplified: striking-only L1–3single counter at L1clear role split

Counter-timing transfers directly to live striking, and into the wrestling threat if the opponent shoots, see Takedown Defense.

Readiness to progress

  • Lands clean counters consistently (~50%+ of initiations)
  • Uses multiple counter timings (intercept, punish, etc.)
  • Doesn't get drawn by feints at the current level
  • Can articulate: "I saw them load, so I countered"

Warning signs

Leads instead of waiting Gets countered by the initiator Drawn by every feint

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on excessive force or head hunting 🔁 Reset if it becomes a reactive brawl

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Close-Range Defense · Land the Target Follow-on→Takedown Defense (if the opponent shoots) Related→Confidence Rating · Defensive Solutions

docs.title: Counter-Wrestling
docs.text:
Transition · WrestlingCounter-Wrestling TransitionWrestlingDefensiveIntermediateStriking → Wrestling

Change the domain to defend, time reactive clinch entries and level changes that turn the striker's offense into wrestling.

StartTwo fighters at striking range, inside a marked perimeter; the striker leads. → The GoalStriker keeps the striking domain; counter-wrestler clinches or takes down off the initiation. → FinishWrestling established → transition · Striker keeps the domain → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Don't trade strikes, change the domain.

Time the entry off the striker's commitment, not your own count. Turn their offense into your wrestling.

What to Read

Attune to the rate of closure (τ) of the striker's committed strike, the forward commitment and balance shift as they extend on a shot, not their eyes or a count. That information specifies the window to time a clinch or level-change entry.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off at striking range. RangeStriking range into clinch. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne striker, one counter-wrestler. Start & resetStriker leads; reset when wrestling is established or the striker keeps the domain.

The Matchup

🥊 Striker

Trying to land clean offense and keep the fight in the striking domain.

Maintain distance to prevent wrestling; punish level-change attempts; disengage from clinch attempts. You must keep striking, you can't just defend the wrestling. VS 🤼 Counter-Wrestler

Trying to neutralize striking by establishing a clinch or completing a takedown, not by striking.

Read the striking initiation; time clinch entries or level changes; use their commitment against them. Wait for the initiation, the entry must be reactive, not preemptive.

The Rules

⏳ Counter-wrestler waits for initiationThe counter-wrestler doesn't lead, they react to the striker's offense. This forces genuine reading of when to change domains rather than preplanned shooting. 🥊 Striker must strikeThe striker can't just defend the wrestling, they must lead with offense. This provides reliable attacks for the counter-wrestler to time entries against. 🔄 Entry must be reactiveThe wrestling entry must be timed off the striker's commitment, not a preemptive shoot. The skill is recognizing when wrestling beats staying in the exchange. 🤼 Wrestling is the goal, not strikesThe counter-wrestler aims for a clinch or takedown, not strikes of their own. Domain change is the explicit objective. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly.

How to Win

Transition Clinch or takedown established → move to wrestling games.When the counter-wrestler establishes a clinch or completes a takedown, move on to wrestling games. The counter-wrestler changed the domain, clinch locked or takedown finished. Transition to Open Space Takedown and related wrestling games. Reset Striker keeps the domain → reset, same roles.When the striker lands clean offense and keeps the striking domain, reset, same roles. The striker punished the entry or kept distance and stayed striking. Then begin again at striking range. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.When both of a player's feet leave the perimeter, that player loses, whoever they are. Crossing the marked perimeter loses the game instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the cage-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Clinch onlyNo level changes or takedowns.The counter-wrestler can only clinch, no level changes or takedowns. Build the timing of clinch entries off the strike. 2Add level changesShoot or clinch.The counter-wrestler can shoot or clinch; the striker must defend both. Reading which entry is coming. 3Chain wrestlingClinch → takedown, shot → clinch.The counter-wrestler can chain entries, clinch into takedown, shot into clinch. More dynamic exchanges, wrestling chains as defense. 4Full MMAStriker sprawls and continues.The striker can sprawl and keep striking; the counter-wrestler must complete the wrestling. Domain change under full MMA resistance.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What opens the window to enter, and what are you reading to find it? The rate of closure (τ) of the striker's committed strike: the forward commitment and balance shift as they extend on a shot. Not their eyes, not a count of exchanges. Q Why must the entry be reactive, never a preemptive shoot? The skill is recognizing when wrestling beats staying in the exchange. Timing the entry off the opponent's commitment uses their offense against them; a preplanned shoot trains nothing about reading. Q The whole point is not to trade strikes, so what are you trying to do instead? Change the domain. Establish a clinch or complete a takedown, turning the striker's offense into your wrestling, rather than striking back. Q When do you favor a clinch entry over a level change? Forward pressure invites a clinch; a committed power shot opens a level change underneath. Read which the striker gives you.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Striker

Maintain distance to prevent wrestling; punish level-change attempts; disengage from clinch attempts; keep the striking domain.

🤼Counter-Wrestler

Read striking initiation; time clinch entries or level changes; use the opponent's commitment against them.

Coaching cues

👁️Enter on what you saw

"When did you decide to shoot or clinch?" The answer should be about what they saw, "after they threw the cross", not "on my third exchange."

🎯Clinch or shot?

"Is a clinch or level change better here?" Forward pressure invites a clinch; a committed power shot opens a level change underneath.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Counter-wrestler must wait→Forces reactive wrestling entry Striker must strike→Reliable attacks to counter-wrestle Aims for clinch/takedown (not strikes)→Domain change is the explicit goal Progressive entries (clinch → shot → chain)→Scaffolded learning of wrestling options Striking range → clinch→Entry timing is critical

Develops domain-switching as defense, recognizing when wrestling beats striking defense. Forcing reactive entry (not preemptive shooting) builds genuine perception of when to change domains, a key degeneracy in MMA (Renshaw et al., 2019).

What the counter-wrestler reads

👁️Visual

Forward commitment, balance during the strike, combination rhythm, head position → clinch/level-change timing & entry window.

✋Haptic

Contact during the clinch entry → grip confirmation.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own level & balance → entry execution.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the counter-wrestler times the entry to the striker's commitment, the clinch or level change arriving in the window the committed strike opens, establishing wrestling rather than getting struck. Track entries landed clean vs. punished or stuffed. That timing relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: using wrestling to escape striking pressure when being out-struck and needing to change domains.

Striker must strikeentry must be reactivewrestling is the goal

The timing perception, when to shoot under a cross, when to clinch on pressure, transfers directly to live MMA; feeds Open Space Takedown.

Readiness to progress

  • Entry timing matches the striker's commitment
  • Uses multiple entry types (clinch, level change)
  • Doesn't get hit cleanly during entry
  • Can describe: "I shot when they threw because…"

Warning signs

Shoots randomly (not reactive) Gets hit during entries Only uses one entry Entries feel panicked

Safety & related games

🤝 Light striking, controlled wrestling 🛑 Stop on slamming or uncontrolled takedowns 🔁 Reset if it becomes a pure wrestling match

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Close-Range Defense · Pressure to Clinch Follow-on→Open Space Takedown Related→Hand Controls

docs.title: Evade the Punch
docs.text:
Skill Integration · StrikingEvade the Punch Skill IntegrationStrikingDefensiveIntermediateOpen Space

Match the evasion to the punch, slip straights, roll under hooks. Head movement only.

StartTwo fighters at close range, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalAttacker lands a clean head punch; defender reads its type and evades to match. → FinishCorrect evasion → switch · Land clean → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Read the path first, slip straights, roll under hooks.

Head movement only, so the evasion must match the punch type. One right evasion beats ten wrong ones.

What to Read

Attune to the rate of expansion (τ) and the path of the incoming punch, straight line vs. arcing, read via shoulder–hip motion and the bending elbow at center mass, not the opponent's eyes or their absolute distance. That information specifies when it arrives and whether to slip or roll under.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, neutral stance. RangeInside punching distance. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne attacker, one defender. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset after each exchange.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to land a clean punch, straight or hook, to the defender's head.

Straights and hooks to the head only, scaled to level, no uppercuts, kicks, or body shots. Mix punch types to force recognition; vary timing and rhythm; ramp up only as the defender succeeds. VS 🌀 Defender

Trying to evade each punch with the matching movement, slip straights, roll under hooks.

No blocking, no parrying, no backing up, head movement only. Using the wrong evasion gets you hit; read the path early and commit when confident.

The Rules

🥊 Straights & hooks, head onlyThe attacker throws straights and/or hooks to the head, scaled to the level, no uppercuts, kicks, or body shots. The mix forces the defender to recognize punch type, not just react. 🌀 Evade only, no block, no parryThe defender may only use head movement, slip or roll under. Blocking, parrying, and backing up are off-limits, so the evasion solutions explore with no fallback. 🚫 No continuous backing upThe defender can't keep retreating out of range to avoid exchanges, they must stay and solve the problem in the pocket. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly. ⏱️ Reset between strikesAt early levels the attacker pauses between strikes so the defender can reorganize. Pressure becomes continuous as the levels rise.

How to Win

Switch Correct evasion, full miss → switch roles.The right evasion for the punch type, slip a straight or roll under a hook, fully missing. The defender earns the attacking role. Reset Clean head shot lands → reset, same roles.A clean head shot that got through, often the wrong evasion for the punch type. Begin again from a neutral stance. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the game instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the cage-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Predictable patternAlternating straight, hook.The attacker alternates straight then hook so the defender knows what's coming, pure focus on correct evasion mechanics for each type. 2Random selectionStraights or hooks, single punches.The attacker throws straights or hooks at random, single punches with reset between, now it's recognition and selection. 3Variable tempoRandom type, no fixed reset.Random punch type with no guaranteed reset, the defender stays ready for either path as timing shifts. 4Add feintsReal vs. fake.The attacker can feint, fake a jab then throw a hook. Patience and commitment timing now matter. 5Two-punch combinationsMixed in one combo.Two-punch combinations that may mix straights and hooks, chain evasions together. 6Full MMAAdd shot / clinch threat.The attacker can now shoot or clinch too, evade while denying the grappling entry, staying aware of all threats.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What tells you whether to slip or roll under? The path of the punch read via the elbow at center mass: arm stays straight means slip, elbow bends and the arm arcs means roll under. Not the eyes. Q Why is the defender held to head movement only, no block or parry? With no fallback, the evasion solutions have to explore and develop on their own instead of leaning on the guard. Q Why does using the wrong evasion get you hit? Each evasion only beats one path: a slip won't beat a hook. The skill is recognizing punch type first, then committing. One correct evasion beats ten wrong ones.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Land clean head punches; mix straights and hooks to force recognition; vary timing and rhythm.

🌀Defender

Slip straights (head laterally offline), roll/weave under hooks (under the arc); read the path early and select.

Coaching cues

👁️Read the path

Arm stays straight → slip. Elbow bends and the arm arcs → roll under. The bending elbow at center mass is the tell, not the eyes.

🎯Right tool, full miss

The wrong evasion gets you hit, a slip won't beat a hook. Don't evade everything; recognize type first, then commit. One correct evasion beats ten wrong ones.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Mixed straights & hooks→Forces recognition and selection, not just reaction Evade-only→Explores evasion solutions with no fallback Predictable → random progression→Scaffolds from mechanics to recognition Role switch on success→Develops both attacking and defending Close range→Evasion timing must be precise (no distance buffer)

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019), the defender develops degeneracy: multiple evasion solutions matched to different attack types, not one fixed response.

What the defender reads

👁️Visual: path

Straight vs. arcing trajectory; extended vs. bent elbow → slip or roll under.

🧭Visual: timing

Shoulder rotation & weight shift → when to move and which hand.

⚖️Proprioceptive

Own head position & balance → execution and recovery readiness.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender's evasion lands in time with the punch, track correct, clean evasions vs. shots eaten, and whether the head re-sets between strikes. That timing relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: reading punch type and matching the right head-movement evasion under pressure.

Simplified: no kicks/uppercutshead onlyevade only

Integrates two evasion solutions before full defense, transfers into Close-Range Defense.

Readiness to progress

  • Correct evasion selection ~70%+
  • Can articulate the tell ("I saw the elbow bend")
  • Smooth, not jerky or panicked movements
  • Handles both lead and rear punches

Warning signs

Uses same evasion for all punches Freezes when uncertain Gets hit by feints

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🥊 Hooks at controlled speed until recognition is shown 🛑 Stop on freezing, lost composure, or excessive force 🔁 Reset if the defender starts blocking instead of evading

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Slip the Straight (recommended) Follow-on→Close-Range Defense Related→Defensive Solutions

docs.title: Evasive Movement
docs.text:
Open Space · StrikingEvasive Movement Open SpaceStrikingDefensiveIntermediateFootwork

Prevent engagement entirely through footwork and ring generalship, "getting on your bike."

StartTwo fighters at medium range inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalEvader keeps distance; pursuer closes it and corners them. → FinishKeep distance for the duration → switch · Establish range or corner the evader → reset.

The best defense, is the fight that never starts.

Avoid engagement through footwork and ring generalship, not by absorbing strikes. Win the space, don't get cornered.

What to Read

Attune to the rate the open space is closing (global optic flow) and the pursuer's preferred distance, far affords your escape, near affords their engagement. Read their angle of approach and weight commitment, plus the gap to the perimeter behind you, not their eyes. That information specifies where your space is and when to pivot vs. circle.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one evader, one pursuer. RangeMedium range, outside engagement. BoundaryA marked perimeter, prevents endless retreat. RolesOne evader, one pursuer. Start & resetPursuer closes; reset when range is established or time elapses.

The Matchup

🦅 Evader

Trying to avoid engagement and maintain distance for the set duration, pure movement, no striking at Level 1.

Keep moving, static defense doesn't count. Use lateral movement, angles, and pivots. Conserve energy, and above all don't get cornered against the perimeter. VS 🥊 Pursuer

Trying to close distance, cut off angles, and herd the evader toward the perimeter.

Actively close, not just walk forward. Cut angles, pressure without overcommitting, and herd toward the boundary. Recognize when the evader is trapped, and keep composure through the frustration.

The Rules

🦅 Evader must keep movingStatic defense doesn't count, the evader's whole job is movement. Standing and blocking is not a solution here. 🥊 Pursuer actively closesThe pursuer must genuinely close and cut angles, not just walk forward passively. This creates real pressure to move efficiently. 🚫 No striking at Level 1At Level 1 the evader cannot strike, pure evasion. Single counters are introduced only at Level 3. ⬛ A marked perimeter forces decisionsPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (ring, cage, taped lines). The boundary prevents infinite retreat and creates decision points. ⏱️ Win by duration or by corneringThe evader wins by keeping distance for a set time; the pursuer wins by establishing range or cornering. Clear objectives for both roles.

How to Win

Switch Keep distance for the duration → switch roles.The evader avoided engagement and kept the space open the whole round, ring generalship demonstrated, and earns the pursuing role. Reset Pursuer establishes range or corners → reset, same roles.The space closed: the pursuer cut the angle and trapped the evader against the perimeter, or closed to engagement. Begin again at medium range. Diagnostic Watch movement quality, angles and pivots, not gassing.Beyond the win, the coach reads movement quality: economy, use of angles, and composure under pursuit, not frantic circling or gassing.

The Levels

1Pure movementEvader can only move.Evader moves only, no strikes. Pursuer walks/jogs forward with pressure. Footwork fundamentals. 2Add feintsRead real vs. fake.The pursuer can feint to test reactions; the evader must read real vs. fake. Movement under pressure. 3Evader can counterSingle counters while moving.The evader can throw single counters while moving, punishing pursuer overcommitment. Movement + counter integration. 4Full MMA expressionShot / clinch threat.The pursuer can shoot or clinch; the evader must manage all threats while moving. Evasion under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to know when to pivot vs. circle? The rate the open space is closing (global optic flow), the pursuer's angle and weight commitment, and the gap to the perimeter behind you. Not their eyes. Q Why must the evader keep moving, with static defense not counting? The whole job is movement and ring generalship, winning the space, not absorbing strikes. Standing and blocking is not a solution here. Q Why does the marked perimeter exist if it limits the evader? It prevents endless retreat and forces decision points: evasion must be purposeful, and getting cornered against it is the loss. Q What is the pursuer actually trying to do? Not chase, but cut angles and herd the evader toward the boundary, closing the space without overcommitting until they are trapped.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🦅Evader

Lateral movement and angles; distance management; ring awareness; time pivots; conserve energy.

🥊Pursuer

Cut off angles; pressure without overcommitting; herd toward the boundary; recognize the trap.

Coaching cues

🗺️Where is your space?
