Ask evaders: "Where is your space? Where is the wall?" This develops spatial awareness, the foundation of ring generalship.

✂️Where do you want them?

Ask pursuers: "Where do you want them to go?" This develops cutting strategy over chasing.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Evader can only move (L1)→Forces development of movement solutions Pursuer actively closes→Creates real pressure to move efficiently Marked perimeter exists→Decision points and consequences for poor positioning Win by duration or cornering→Clear objectives for both roles Bounded space→Evasion must be purposeful, not endless running

Develops ring generalship, the perception of spatial affordances, through the dyadic system interaction (Renshaw et al., 2019).

What the players read

👁️Visual

Pursuer's angle and weight commitment; distance to the perimeter → escape direction, pivot vs. circle.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance and foot position → pivot and direction-change execution.

🫁Fatigue

Own fatigue level → energy-conservation decisions.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the open space is opening or closing over time, is the evader keeping distance and using angles, or is the gap collapsing toward the perimeter? Track distance kept, cornering events, and movement economy. When the evader consistently keeps the space open without gassing, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: avoiding a pressure fighter while maintaining positioning and energy efficiency.

Simplified: no strikes at L1pure pursuit earlyclear duration win

Movement patterns transfer to any distance-management situation, leads into Pressure to Clinch when evasion reaches the boundary.

Readiness to progress

  • Maintains distance for the duration consistently
  • Movement is economical, not gassing prematurely
  • Uses pivots and angles, not just circling
  • Stays calm under pursuit pressure
  • Can verbalize: "I saw them cutting, so I changed direction"

Warning signs

Gets cornered frequently Gasses quickly Only circles one direction Panics near the boundary

Safety & related games

🤝 Light contact on any counters 🛑 Stop on boundary collisions or lost composure 🔁 Coach resets if the evader is truly stuck

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Close-Range Defense Follow-on→Pressure to Clinch Related→Three Zones · Winning the Circle

docs.title: 50/50 Clinch
docs.text:
Open Space · Wrestling50/50 Clinch

Provisional (WIP): invented and run in class 2026-06-20, pending final coach sign-off

Built on the mat on 2026-06-20 as the class's first true typed center-clinch game. It trains the neutral over-under battle that opens every clinch exchange, the moment before anyone has an edge. Awaiting a final pass before the WIP tag comes off.

Open SpaceWrestlingCombinedIntermediateCenter Clinch

Win a dominant connection from a neutral 50/50 clinch, double-unders, a leg, or the back, or put them down, before they get theirs.

StartBoth fighters in a neutral 50/50 clinch (one overhook, one underhook, diagonally matched), chest to chest, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBeat your partner to a dominant connection: double-unders locked, a hand to a leg above the knee, or the back, or off-balance them down while you stay standing. → FinishAny clean connection or grounding → reset to 50/50. Strikes are live to tax the player who only defends.

Neutral doesn't mean wait. Whoever attacks first owns the tie.

The 50/50 is even by design. Flush your offense so they have to defend, and the connection is yours. Especially against someone stronger, move first.

What to Read

Attune to the felt load through the ties (the inertial array), which way your partner's weight is committed and which post is holding them up. Drag to the unloaded side and the leg, the back, or the ground opens. Read the shifting base through your hands, not a memorized entry. When their hand drops to a leg or their head dips, the level change is coming.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, symmetric and neutral, neither one dominant to start. PositionA 50/50 clinch: one overhook and one underhook each, diagonally matched (one per side), chest to chest. Lock in even before "go". BoundaryA marked perimeter. Start one body-length off the wall, the wall is live but not automatic, it will help you or hurt you. RolesBoth fighters have the same goals, it is a neutral race to the first dominant connection. Start & resetLock the 50/50, then "go"; reset to 50/50 on any clean connection or grounding. A simultaneous connection is a reset, no score.

The Matchup

🤼 Fighter A (Connecting)

Trying to beat your partner to a dominant connection: double-unders locked, a hand to a leg above the knee, the back, or an off-balance to the ground while you stay standing.

Don't hold the neutral tie and wait. Feel which post is loaded, drag to the light side, and pummel for the under. Blunted knees off the inside of the thigh and shown punches tax a partner who only defends. From a deep under you can lock the body, dip to a leg above the knee, or spin to the back. VS 🤼 Fighter B (Connecting)

Same goals, the mirror task: get to the same dominant connection first, and don't let your partner settle into theirs.

Defending alone has no path to a win here. Flush your own offense so your partner has to react, then take the under, the leg, the back, or the off-balance. Keep your hips back off the body lock, and use feet-to-feet contact to off-balance rather than out-muscle.

The Rules

🎯 Double-unders + hands locked is a winBoth arms under, hands connected behind the back around the waist (a body lock). A momentary under without the lock does not count, the hands have to connect. 🦵 A hand to a leg above the knee is a winHands connected around a leg above the knee. A grab below the knee is an exposed low single, not a connection, it does not count. This is the hand-connection law. 🔄 The back is the gold-star winGet chest-to-back behind your partner. It is the best connection and the hardest to reach, score it whenever it is there. ⬇️ Off-balance only scores if you stay standingPut your partner's hips to the ground while you stay on your feet or end on top. If you both fall, it does not count, the game rewards staying over the grounded opponent. 🥊 Strikes are the stalling taxBlunted Thai knees off the inside of the thigh and shown punches to antagonize. No elbows. The player who only defends gets antagonized, the strikes punish passivity so neutral never means waiting. 🤝 Reset on connectionAny clean connection or grounding resets both fighters to the neutral 50/50. A simultaneous connection is a reset with no score. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player leaves it, that player loses the round.

How to Win

Either fighter Double-unders locked, a leg above the knee, the back, or an off-balance to the ground while you stay standing → win and reset.Four paths to the same neutral win. The connection has to be real (hands locked, leg above the knee) or the grounding has to leave you on top. Gold star The back (chest-to-back) is the highest win, and the hardest.Behind your partner is always the best place to be. It is the hardest connection to earn, so it is the gold star whenever you can spin to it. Loss Leave the perimeter, or get put down while your partner stays on top → that round to them.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round. Getting off-balanced to your back while your partner stays standing is their grounding win.

The Levels

1Grips onlyNo strikes, find the weak post.Start in the 50/50, no strikes. Pummel for the under and drag to the unloaded side. Connections only, no grounding yet. Finds the felt-load read. 2Add the groundOff-balance is now live.The off-balance to the ground becomes a win, but only if you stay standing or end on top. Feet-to-feet contact and side-to-side drags, not slams. 3Strikes onThe anti-stall tax.Blunted knees off the thigh and shown punches come on. The player who only defends now pays for it. Neutral becomes urgent. 4Wall liveStart a step off the wall.Begin one body-length off the wall. Drag your partner onto it to pin and connect, or get dragged onto it yourself. The wall helps or hurts. 5Full expressionAll wins live, short rounds.Neutral 50/50, every win path live, strikes on, the wall available. Lock in even, then move first, connect, reset, go again.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why is "neutral" not the same as "wait"? The 50/50 is even by design. The fighter who attacks first forces the other to defend, and a pure defender has no path to the connection. Flush your offense first so the opening is yours. Q What does the felt load through the ties tell you? Which post is loaded and which is light. Drag to the light side and the base falls, opening the under, the leg, or the back. You read the shifting base through your hands, not a memorized throw. Q Why does a leg grab below the knee not count? It is the hand-connection law: a connection is around the waist or a leg above the knee. Diving below the knee is an exposed low single, not control, so it does not score. Q What stops two defensive players from stalling forever? The strikes. A player who only defends eats blunted knees and shown punches, the constraint that taxes passivity. It is a built-in cost, not a "stop stalling" instruction from the coach.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each fighter's job

🤼Connecting (both sides)

Move first from neutral, feel the loaded post, drag to the light side, pummel to the under, then lock the body, dip to a leg above the knee, spin to the back, or off-balance to the ground while staying on top.

⚖️Don't out-muscle, off-balance

Use feet-to-feet contact and side-to-side motion to break the base. Against a stronger partner, motion beats strength, the grip that waits loses.

Coaching cues

⚡Flush your offense

Ask: "Are you attacking, or just stopping their attack?" A defender has no win here. Make your partner react first.

🥊Strikes are the tax, not decoration

The blunted knees and shown punches exist to punish stalling. If nobody is attacking, the antagonizing strikes should make that uncomfortable, fast.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Neutral 50/50 start, no edge→The skill is creating the first connection, not exploiting a gift Reset on any connection→Repeated reps of the highest-value clinch moment, the first exchange Leg connection only above the knee→Trains real control, not a desperate low dive Off-balance scores only if you stay standing→Rewards top control, not mutual falling Strikes live as the stalling tax→Makes the neutral tie urgent, a constraint that punishes passivity instead of a cue

Implements Representative Learning Design (Renshaw et al., 2019): the neutral over-under start models the most common clinch moment in MMA, the 50/50 before anyone has an edge, and the multiple win paths let the fighter self-organize the entry that the felt load affords rather than rehearsing one prescribed throw.

What athletes read

✋Haptic

Load through the ties (the inertial array) → which post is committed, which way to drag, when the under is winning.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hips square or back, base loaded or light → whether you can be body-locked, and when the spin to the back is open.

👁️Visual

A dropped hand or a dipped head → the leg or the level change coming, and the post to attack.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the fighter creates the first dominant connection by moving first and dragging to the unloaded side, rather than holding the neutral tie and waiting. Track first-connection wins versus stalls, and whether the fighter stays in motion. When the connection reliably comes from moving first, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the neutral over-under (50/50) clinch exchange that opens almost every MMA clinch, the moment before anyone owns an underhook, the body, the back, or the takedown.

Neutral 50/50 startfour win pathsstrikes as the tax

Follows Pressure to Clinch; the off-balance and body-lock wins feed Body Lock to Ground; the back win feeds Back Maintenance.

Readiness to progress

  • Attacks first from neutral, never just defends
  • Drags to the weak-side post to win the under
  • Connects only above the knee, no low dives
  • Stays standing on the off-balance, ends on top

Warning signs

Holds the neutral tie and waits Dives below the knee for a leg Both fall and claims the grounding Only defends, never attacks

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling, no falling body weight 🦵 Blunted knees off the thigh only, no elbows 🔁 Reset on any connection or grounding

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Pressure to Clinch Follow-on→Body Lock to Ground · Back Maintenance Related→Pressure to Wall · Winning the Circle

docs.title: Ground Access
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingGround Access GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediateAccess

Pass the guard to a dominant position against an active bottom.

StartTop inside the bottom's guard, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop passes to a dominant position; bottom retains or sweeps. → FinishEstablish dominant position → switch · Bottom sweeps or retains → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Get past the legs first, finishing comes later.

The goal is the pass, not the submission. Access comes before control.

What to Read

Attune to the felt weight distribution & base through contact, your posts loading and unloading, the guard grips going light or tight, your own balance over the legs, plus the bottom player's hip and frame motion. That inertial information specifies where the guard opens before the pass is available, not the opponent's limbs in isolation.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top, one bottom. PositionTop inside the bottom's guard. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne top (attacker), one bottom (defender). Start & resetBegin from the guard; reset after each round.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to pass the guard and establish a dominant position, not submission.

Break grips and controls, create a passing angle, complete the pass, and stabilize. Position must be established, not just touched. VS 🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to retain guard or sweep to top.

Actively defend, never go passive. Maintain guard structure, create frames and distance, and threaten sweeps to discourage the pass.

The Rules

🪨 Top must advance positionThe top player must advance position, just holding inside the guard doesn't count. This develops active passing, not stalling. 🏃 Bottom actively defendsThe bottom player works guard retention and sweep threats continuously, no passive waiting. This develops passing against real resistance. 🚫 Submissions are not the goalAt this stage the objective is position advancement, not finishing. Access comes before control and exploitation. 📌 Position must be establishedThe pass only counts when the dominant position is stabilized, not just achieved momentarily. This ensures complete passes. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses instantly, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Switch Top establishes a dominant position → switch roles.Side control, mount, or back, stabilized past the guard, top wins and earns the bottom's retention role. Win Bottom sweeps or retains → bottom wins.Reverse to top, or keep the guard intact long enough that the pass is denied, the bottom defends successfully. Reset Bottom sweeps or retains → reset, same roles.The round ends and resets from the guard, keeping the same roles for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Closed GuardStart in closed guard; top opens and passes.Begin in closed guard. Focus on breaking the closed guard open, the first barrier to access. 2Open GuardStart in open guard of the bottom's choice.Begin in open guard. More dynamic passing required, focus on passing open guard variations. 3Half GuardStart in half guard; top clears and advances.Begin in half guard. Top works to clear the trapped leg and advance, focus on half guard passing. 4Live Positional SparringFrom guard, both compete, no fixed roles.The live Positional Sparring layer: from the starting guard, top passes while bottom sweeps or recovers and both fight for the better position, rather than fixed pass-vs-recover roles. 5Full MMA ExpressionAdd ground strikes for both.Top can use ground strikes to open the guard; bottom can strike from guard. Guard passing under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What information tells you the guard is about to open? The felt weight distribution and base through contact: your posts loading and unloading, grips going light or tight, plus the bottom's hip and frame motion. Not the opponent's limbs in isolation. Q The goal is the pass, not the submission. Why draw that line? Access comes before control. Isolating the pass develops active, complete passing instead of rushing a finish before the position is even secured. Q When does a pass actually count? When the dominant position is established and stabilized, not just touched momentarily. You must re-load weight to hold it before the guard recovers. Q What gets the top swept mid-pass, and how do you prevent it? Losing the connection to the bottom's hips. Control the hips before passing so the pass completes rather than feeding a sweep.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Attacker)

Break guard grips and controls; create passing opportunities; complete the pass to a dominant position; stabilize after passing.

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Maintain guard structure; create frames and distance; threaten sweeps to discourage passing; recover guard if partially passed.

Coaching cues

🚧Name the obstacle

Ask: "What was preventing your pass?" This develops recognition of guard obstacles rather than forcing a single passing technique.

⚓Hips before pass

Ask: "Did you control their hips before passing?" Hip control perception is the key to a pass that actually completes.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top must advance (not just hold)→Develops active passing, not stalling Position must be established→Ensures complete passes Progressive guard types→Scaffolded complexity (closed → open → half) Bottom actively defends→Develops passing against resistance Guard as obstacle→Creates the passing problem-space

Develops passing affordance perception, recognizing when and where the guard can be passed (Renshaw et al., 2019). Degeneracy emerges as athletes discover multiple passing solutions for the same guard type.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

Guard grip strength & hip control level → when to break grips and where the pass is available.

👁️Visual

Guard structure & the bottom's hand position → pass direction choice and grip prevention.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own base & posture → pass execution stability.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top stays connected to the bottom's hips through the pass, track passes completed vs. swept, and whether the top re-loads weight to stabilize before the guard recovers. That control-connection relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: passing guard to a dominant ground position, the fundamental skill of ground offense in MMA.

Simplified: start in guardno submission focusprogressive guard complexity

Isolates passing from the takedown, passing-opportunity perception and hip control transfer directly into competition.

Readiness to progress

  • Passes current guard type consistently
  • Recognizes passing opportunities
  • Maintains control through pass completion
  • Multiple passing solutions per guard

Warning signs

Gets swept during pass attempts Can't break grips Passes but can't stabilize Only one pass works

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling, no slamming 🛑 Stop on joint locks or neck cranks 🔁 Reset if positions stall

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Open Space Takedown · Wall to Ground Follow-on→Ground Control · Positional Sparring (live) Related→Decision States

docs.title: Ground-and-Pound Defense (WIP)
docs.text:
Ground · MixedGround-and-Pound Defense GroundMixedDefensiveAdvancedWIP

Escape the open-camped top before the damage stacks up.

StartBottom supine; top past the hips but NOT closed in, off to the side, no chest-to-chest, no knee-on-belly. → The GoalBottom stands clean, reverses, or closes a leg loop on head-and-arm; top stays open-camped and lands ground-and-pound. → FinishStand clean, reverse, or closed loop → bottom · Open-camped 20s, no escape → reset · Top closes the position → reset (rule break) · Out of bounds → loss.

Top can't pin, so bottom has to move.

Three observable escapes: stand, reverse, closed loop. Closing the loop is enough, the finish is post-game.

Key Positions

🔓 Open campTop past the hips but NOT closed in, no chest-to-chest, no knee-on-belly, no body lock. Top stays off to the side, leaving the space open as the defining constraint of the game. 🪢 Closed loopBottom's legs closed around top's head and arm, the structural geometry of triangle/armbar entries. The closed loop wins regardless of submission progress, the finish is post-game.

What to Read

Attune to the load shifts in the open camp, the moment top's hips drop to land a heavier shot, the hand that commits to a strike and leaves an arm or head reachable. That weight shift specifies which escape is open, the lightened side stands, the committed arm closes the loop. Read weight, not eyes.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom (defender, the protagonist), one top (open-camped). PositionBottom supine, hips on the mat. Top kneeling or posting past the hips, off to the side, past the line of the hips but not past the line of the knees. No chest-to-chest, no knee-on-belly. BoundaryA marked perimeter on mats, both stay inside. RolesBottom escapes by stand, reverse, or closed loop. Top stays open-camped and lands ground-and-pound, top cannot win. Start & resetBegin from open camp; reset on any bottom escape, the 20-second cap, a rule break, or out of bounds.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to stand clean, reverse to top, or close a leg loop around top's head and arm.

Three observable terminal escapes. Stand-clean requires full separation, if top connects on a leg or hip on the way up, not over yet. Reverse means ending on top. Closed loop is the structural geometry of triangle or armbar around head-and-arm, the finish is post-game. VS 🥊 Top (Open Camp)

Trying to stay open-camped past the hips and land significant ground-and-pound.

No chest-to-chest, no knee-on-belly, no body lock. Stay off to the side, past the hips not past the knees. Damage is the only output, top cannot win the game, the constraint isolates the open-top ground-and-pound problem rather than letting it collapse into a regular pin.

The Rules

🔓 Top stays OPENNo chest-to-chest, no knee-on-belly, no body lock. Top camps off to the side, past the hips, leaving the space open. Closing the position is a rule break, the round resets. 🥊 Strikes scaled per levelNo strikes at L1 (pure position). Light strikes from L2. Light-to-moderate ground-and-pound at L4+. Always mimic ground-and-pound, never deliver it for real. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionAt the striking levels, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial within the per-level caps above: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Use striking as a disincentivization tool, the cost that punishes stalling and lazy structure. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⏱️ 20-second cap, top has no winIf top stays open-camped for 20 seconds without bottom escaping, the round resets, same roles. Top has no positive win, the clock is bottom's threat window, not top's score. 🦶 Three observable escapesBottom wins by standing cleanly, reversing to top, or closing a leg loop around top's head and arm. The closed loop is the structural geometry, the submission finish is not required. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens on mats inside a marked perimeter. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

How to Win

Stand Bottom stands clean → bottom wins, switch roles.Full separation to feet, top has no connection on bottom's legs or hips. If top connects on the way up, the stand is not clean, continue. Reverse Bottom reverses to top → bottom wins, switch roles.Bottom gets above the opponent, on top of them or standing over them in control. Reversal beats damage, the position has flipped. Loop Bottom closes a leg loop on head and arm → bottom wins.Legs closed around top's head and one of top's arms, the closed structural geometry of triangle or armbar. The closed loop is the win, the finish is post-game and not required for scoring. Reset Top open-camps the full 20s, no escape → reset, same roles.Top stayed open and bottom failed all three escapes before the cap. The round resets from open camp for another rep. Top still does not score, the clock is the threat window. Reset Top closes the position → reset, rule break.If top achieves chest-to-chest, knee-on-belly, or a body lock, the open-camp constraint is broken and the round resets to the start. Top does not score from a closure, the game is not for that. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Position onlyNo strikes.Top stays open-camped without strikes. Bottom drills the three escape paths against a positional-only top. Calibrates the open-camp shape and the closed-loop geometry. 2Light strikesDamage threat on.Top adds light strikes from the open camp. Bottom must defend strikes AND find one of the three escapes. The strike threat changes which escape is available moment to moment. 3Side switchingTop swaps the camp.Top may switch sides past the hips, increasing the scramble demand on bottom. Reading which side is loaded becomes the task. 4Sustained ground-and-poundLight-to-moderate.Top stays open-camped and lands sustained light-to-moderate strikes, simulating real ground-and-pound. Bottom must time the escape under continuous damage threat. 5Full expressionLive, open hunting.Top stays open-camped and hunts damage with everything they have inside the rules. Bottom finds the escape under live pressure. Most representative of the real ground-and-pound problem.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why is the top NOT allowed to close the position? The constraint isolates the open-camped ground-and-pound problem. Closing to chest-to-chest, knee-on-belly, or a body lock collapses it into a regular pin, a different game (mount, side, KOB). Open camp is the variable being trained here. Q Why does the closed loop count without finishing the submission? The closed structural geometry (legs around head-and-arm) is the observable terminal event. The finish is post-game, requires control speed and a tap, which is not what's being trained here. Closing the loop proves the escape via attack. Q What counts as a "clean stand," and what doesn't? A clean stand is full separation to feet with no top connection. If top connects on a leg or hip on the way up, the stand is not clean, continue. The standard is the same as a wrestling stand-up, hands and hips off, range restored. Q Why does the top have no win condition? Bottom is the protagonist. Top's job is to BE the threat, not to score against it. The 20-second clock is bottom's threat window, not top's score, this keeps top hunting damage rather than stalling for time.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Three escape paths active at all times. Read the load, time the stand, hunt the closed loop on a committed arm, look for the reverse when top's base lightens.

🥊Top (Open Camp)

Stay off to the side past the hips. Hunt damage with hands and feet inside the rules. Do not close the position, it breaks the game. Keep moving, side-switch as openings appear.

Coaching cues

⚓Which path opened?

Ask bottom after each rep: "Stand, reverse, or loop?" Direct attention to which one was available, not to a memorised escape.

🔓Did you stay open?

Ask top: "Were you off to the side, or did you slip into a pin?" Keeps the open-camp constraint honest.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top must stay open→Isolates the open-camped GnP problem, not a regular pin Three observable escapes→Multiple solutions, develops degeneracy Top has no win, 20s cap→Urgency for the bottom, no stalling for the top Closed loop is enough→Observable terminal proxy, no tap required for scoring Live, light-to-moderate strikes→Keeps the strike-threat perception intact

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019). The open-camp constraint creates the affordance of escape-by-attack (closed loop) and escape-by-movement (stand, reverse), which the regular pin geometry would not allow. The closed-loop-as-win is a sharp affordance, the geometry itself is the proof.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Load through top's hands and base → which side is committed, when an arm leaves range, when the camp lightens for a stand.

👁️Visual

Top's hand committing to a strike → the arm that closes the loop. Top's base on one side → the lightened side to stand toward.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hips and feet → whether a clean stand is set up or a connection on the leg will catch the rise.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom matches the escape to the moment, the stand to the lightened side, the loop to the committed arm, the reverse to the over-extended base. Track which of the three paths is taken across reps and whether each is matched to the actual opening. When the bottom consistently picks the right escape for the moment, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the open-camped ground-and-pound problem in MMA, where the top fighter has space to strike but cannot or will not commit to a pin, and the bottom must escape via attack or movement before damage stacks up.

Simplified: top stays open20s threat windowthree observable escapes

Builds on Ground Escape, Ground to Standing, and Leg Reclaim; transfers into Full MMA Expression as the ground link of the open-space → wall → ground cycle.

Readiness to progress

  • Reads the open-camp load and picks the matching escape
  • Stands cleanly when the lightened side appears
  • Closes the loop on a committed arm, not on a stalled one
  • Continues escaping under sustained light-to-moderate strikes (L4)

Warning signs

Waits for a pin to escape from (game is open-camp only) Tries to finish the submission instead of closing the loop Stands without checking top's connection Top drifts into chest-to-chest (rule break, reset)

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate ground-and-pound 🛑 Stop on closed-loop control, no completed submissions 🔁 Reset on rule break (top closes the position) or 20s cap

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Escape · Ground to Standing · Leg Reclaim Follow-on→Full-MMA integration games (open → wall → ground) Related→Full MMA Expression · Decision States · Three Zones

Source: original to Alex Calienes (2026-05-30 class). Marked WIP pending live platform review. Maps to coverage-map M #67 (Ground-and-Pound Defense).

docs.title: Ground Control
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingGround Control GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediateStabilize

Hold a dominant ground position against live escape attempts.

StartTop in a dominant ground position, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop holds through escapes; bottom escapes to guard or stands up. → FinishHold for the duration → switch · Bottom escapes → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Control is weight, not muscle, feel the escape before it leaves.

Hold the position without rushing to finish. Anticipate the hips, not the limbs.

What to Read

Attune to the felt resistance & weight distribution through contact, your base, your posts, the points of connection going light or loading up, plus the bottom player's hip and frame motion. That inertial information specifies the escape before it fully develops, not the opponent's limbs in isolation.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top, one bottom. PositionA dominant ground position. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne top (attacker), one bottom (defender). Start & resetBegin from the dominant position; reset after each round.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to maintain the dominant position through escape attempts, not submission.

You must actively control, not just lie on top. Distribute weight, anticipate escapes, and transition between control positions as needed. VS 🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to escape to guard or stand up.

Actively escape, never go passive. Create frames and space, bridge and shrimp, and threaten reversals to break the control.

The Rules

🪨 Top must actively controlThe top player must actively control, not just lie on top as dead weight. This develops dynamic, adjustable control. 🏃 Bottom actively escapesThe bottom player works escape sequences continuously, no passive waiting. This creates a realistic control challenge. 🚫 Submissions are not the goalAt this stage the objective is position maintenance, not finishing. Control comes before exploitation. 🔄 Brief loss with recovery still countsA brief position loss with immediate recovery still counts as control, modeling realistic control recovery. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses instantly, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Switch Top holds for the set duration → switch roles.Hold the dominant position for the set duration (e.g., 30 seconds), top wins and earns the bottom's escape role. Win Bottom escapes, stands, or reverses → bottom wins.Break free to guard, get back to your feet, or reverse the position, the bottom breaks the control. Reset Bottom escapes or top holds the duration → reset, same roles.The round ends and resets from the dominant position, keeping the same roles for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Side ControlStart in side control; top maintains, bottom escapes.Begin in side control. Focus on side control maintenance, top holds while bottom works to escape. 2MountStart in mount, higher stakes for both.Begin in mount. Higher stakes for both players, focus on mount control and escape. 3Back ControlStart with the back taken, hooks in.Begin with the back taken (hooks in). Top maintains back, bottom escapes, focus on back control retention. 4Live Positional SparringFrom the starting position, both compete, no fixed roles.The live Positional Sparring layer: from the established position, both players fight for the better spot and it can change hands, rather than fixed top-holds and bottom-escapes. 5Full MMA ExpressionAdd strike threats for both.Top can threaten strikes to maintain control; bottom can strike to create escape opportunities. Position control under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What do you read to feel an escape before it leaves? The felt resistance and weight distribution through contact, connection points going light or loading, plus the bottom's hip and frame motion. The escape shows in the hips before it shows in the limbs. Q Control is weight, not muscle. Why does that matter? Weight-based control is sustainable; strength-based control gasses you out. Distribute weight and anticipate, do not grip and squeeze. Q If you briefly lose the position but recover instantly, did you fail? No. A brief loss with immediate recovery still counts as control, modeling realistic control recovery rather than a single fixed pin. Q What is being measured to know the skill has formed? Whether you stay connected to the bottom's hips through each escape attempt and re-load weight before the escape completes. When that connection stabilizes, control has formed.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Attacker)

Distribute weight effectively; anticipate and shut down escapes; transition between controls; maintain pressure without exhausting.

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Create frames and space; execute escape sequences; bridge and shrimp effectively; threaten reversals.

Coaching cues

⚓Weight, not strength

Ask: "Are you using weight or strength to control?" Weight-based control is sustainable; strength-based control leads to fatigue.

👁️Feel it early

Ask: "Did you feel their escape before it happened?" Controlling the hips, not just the upper body, develops anticipation.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top must actively control→Develops dynamic control, not dead weight Bottom actively escapes→Creates a realistic control challenge Duration requirement (30s)→Ensures sustained control skill Brief loss with recovery counts→Realistic control recovery Ground positions→Full position vocabulary development

Develops control maintenance perception, anticipating escapes before they fully develop (Renshaw et al., 2019). Active control builds efficient, adjustable weight distribution.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

Bottom's hip movement and frame attempts → escape prediction and counter-adjustment.

👁️Visual

Bottom's bridge setup → anticipate the reversal.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own weight distribution & connection points → control adjustment.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top stays connected to the bottom's hips through each escape attempt, track time controlled vs. position lost, and whether the top re-loads weight before the escape completes. That control-connection relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: holding dominant ground position through escape attempts, essential for ground-and-pound or submission setup.

Simplified: start in dominant positionno submission focusset duration

Isolates control from access, escape anticipation and weight distribution transfer directly into competition.

Readiness to progress

  • Controls current position for full duration
  • Anticipates escapes before they develop
  • Uses weight efficiently (not gassing)
  • Transitions between control positions

Warning signs

Gets reversed consistently Loses position immediately Exhausts quickly Only controls one position

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Stop on submission attempts or excessive force 🔁 Reset if control becomes stalling

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Access Follow-on→Positional Sparring · Ground Escape Related→Decision States · TKO Pin

docs.title: Ground Defensive Submission
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingGround Defensive Submission GroundGrapplingDefensiveAdvancedCounter

Turn the bottom into a threat, submit, escape, or deter the top from a disadvantaged position.

StartBottom under established top control, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom threatens or completes a submission; top controls while avoiding danger. → FinishBottom finishes or threatens to escape/reverse → switch · Top controls & defends the duration → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

A bad position isn't a safe one, make the top pay to stay there.

Even from underneath, the threat is real. The threat itself buys the escape.

What to Read

Attune to the felt weight distribution & pressure through contact, where the top's posture breaks, which limb extends or posts, where the connection goes light enough to attack, plus the top player's head and hip motion. That inertial information specifies when a submission opens before the top re-postures, not the opponent's limbs in isolation.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom, one top. PositionBottom under established top control. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne bottom (defender), one top (controller). Start & resetBegin from top control; reset after each round.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to threaten or complete a submission from bottom, not just escape.

Identify position-appropriate submission opportunities, create openings through movement and frames, use the threat to improve position, and finish if the top makes a mistake. VS 🪨 Top (Controller)

Trying to maintain control while avoiding submission danger.

Maintain position while protecting limbs and neck, recognize submission setups, posture to deny attacks, and continue offense while staying safe.

The Rules

🎯 Bottom must threaten submissionsThe bottom player must threaten submissions, not just escape. This develops an offensive bottom game. 🪨 Top maintains control intentThe top player maintains the intent to control, not just defending. This creates a realistic control situation to attack against. 📍 Submissions must fit the positionAttacks must be available from the position, guard has the most options; under mount has very few. This keeps technique selection reality-based. ⚖️ Top is still generally advantageousThe position hierarchy holds, top is still generally advantageous. The bottom is creating risk, not flipping the value of the position. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses instantly, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Switch Bottom finishes or threatens to escape/reverse → switch roles.A finish, or a threat that buys the escape/reversal, bottom wins and earns the top's control role. Win Top controls & defends the duration → top wins.Hold position and stay safe from submissions for the duration, the top wins. Reset Submission defended or position shifts significantly → reset, same roles.The round ends and resets from the top-control position, keeping the same roles for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1From Guard OnlyStart in guard, bottom has the most options.Begin in guard, where the bottom has the most options. Focus on guard submissions, triangle, armbar, and more. 2From Half GuardStart in half guard, fewer options, more timing.Begin in half guard. Fewer options and more about timing, focus on half guard submissions and sweeps. 3From Bad PositionsStart under side control or mount, high risk.Begin under side control or mount. Very limited options, high risk, focus on recognizing rare opportunities. 4Full MMA ExpressionTop can use ground strikes.Top can use ground strikes; bottom must time submissions around the ground-and-pound. Bottom submissions under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q From the bottom, what tells you a submission has opened? The felt weight and pressure through contact: where the top's posture breaks, which limb extends or posts, where connection goes light, plus the top's head and hip motion. It opens before the top re-postures. Q A bad position is not a safe one. What does the bottom actually do with that? Make the top pay to stay there. Even when it does not finish, the threat itself buys the escape or improves position. Q The position hierarchy still holds, so what is the real decision? The risk-reward of submission vs. escape. Top stays generally advantageous, so prioritize escape unless the sub is high percentage, you are confident, escapes are exhausted, or the position is about to get worse anyway. Q Why must attacks fit the position? To keep selection reality-based: guard offers the most options, under mount offers very few. No fantasy techniques, only what the position genuinely affords.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Identify submission opportunities from the position; create openings through movement and frames; use the threat to improve position; finish if the top makes a mistake.

🪨Top (Controller)

Maintain position while protecting limbs and neck; recognize submission setups; posture to deny attacks; continue offense while staying safe.

Coaching cues

⚖️Submission or escape?

Ask: "Was submission higher percentage than escape there?" This develops the risk-reward perception at the heart of the bottom game.

🪤Threat as tool

Ask: "Did you use the threat to improve position?" Submission threats often create escape openings even when not finished.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Bottom must threaten submissions→Develops an offensive bottom game Top maintains control intent→Creates a realistic control situation Position-appropriate submissions→Reality-based technique selection Progressive positions→Scaffolded difficulty (guard → half → bad positions) Various ground positions→Full defensive-submission vocabulary

Develops defensive submission perception, recognizing when submission opportunities exist from bottom (Renshaw et al., 2019). The requirement to threaten (not just escape) develops counter-offense from defense: athletes learn the risk-reward calculation of when submission beats escape.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Top's posture breaks & limb position → submission opportunity and target selection.

👁️Visual

Top's head position & arm extension → guillotine/choke or armbar/kimura availability.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hip position & grip quality → submission setup and attack commitment.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom stays connected to the top's extended limb or broken posture long enough to commit, track threats that force a top reaction (finish, escape, or reversal) vs. attacks shut down, and whether the bottom keeps the threat live before the top re-postures. That threat-connection relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: using submission threats from bottom to deter top control, create escapes, or finish, crucial despite bottom being generally disadvantaged.

Simplified: progressive positionsclear threat requirementcontrolled submissions

Perception of when a submission is available and worth pursuing is identical to competition, threats that create space feed back into Leg Reclaim.

Readiness to progress

  • Threatens submissions from the current position
  • Top feels genuine danger
  • Finishes when openings appear
  • Uses threats to improve position

Warning signs

Only escapes (no submissions) Top doesn't feel danger Gives up position for failed attacks Only one submission works

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled submissions, tap immediately 🛑 Stop on cranking submissions or dangerous stacking 🔁 Reset if bottom is taking damage without progressing

MMA reality check

In MMA, bottom is generally disadvantaged, the top can strike, judges favor top, energy favors top, and a failed submission often worsens position. Prioritize escape over submission UNLESS the submission is very high percentage, you are highly confident, escape routes are exhausted, or the position is about to get much worse anyway. This game teaches when and how to attack from bottom, not that you should always attack from bottom.

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Escape (understand escape first) Follow-on→End state (Submission) if completed · Leg Reclaim if the threat creates space Related→Decision States

docs.title: Ground Escape
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingGround Escape GroundGrapplingDefensiveIntermediateCounter

Escape from under a dominant position before damage accumulates.

StartBottom under a dominant position, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom escapes to guard, neutral, or standing; top holds and prevents it. → FinishBottom escapes → switch · Top holds the duration → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Escapes start in the space, go where the weight isn't.

Read the pressure, then move. Frames and knees go where the connection is light.

What to Read

Attune to the felt weight distribution & pressure through contact, where the top's base loads and unloads, which limbs are pinned, where the connection goes light, plus the top player's posture and frame motion. That inertial information specifies which way the space opens before the escape window closes, not the opponent's limbs in isolation.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom, one top. PositionA dominant top position. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne bottom (defender), one top (attacker). Start & resetBegin from the dominant position; reset after each round.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to escape to guard, neutral, or standing, not submission.

Create frames and space, execute bridges, shrimps, and turns, and chain attempts. Escape means guard recovery, reversal, or standing up. VS 🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to maintain the dominant position and prevent the escape, not submission.

Maintain weight distribution, anticipate and counter escapes, and stay heavy without exhausting. Control only, no finishing.

The Rules

🏃 Bottom must actively escapeThe bottom player works escape sequences continuously, not just surviving or waiting. This forces exploration of escape solutions. 🪨 Top maintains, does not attackThe top player maintains control without submissions, so the bottom can focus on escape rather than submission defense. 🎯 Escape = guard, reversal, or standingA successful escape is guard recovery, a reversal to top, or standing up. This defines clear, hierarchical success. 🚫 No submissions from bottomSubmissions from bottom are not the focus here, the task is escape mechanics. Submission threats live in a separate game. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionAt Level 4, when strike threats are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Use striking as a disincentivization tool, the cost that punishes stalling and lazy structure. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses instantly, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Switch Bottom escapes, reverses, or stands → switch roles.The bottom breaks free to a safer position, bottom wins and earns the top's maintenance role. Win Top holds the position for the set duration → top wins.Hold the dominant position for the set duration, the top denies the escape. Reset Top holds for the set duration → reset, same roles.The round ends and resets from the dominant position, keeping the same roles for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Side Control EscapesStart under side control.Begin under side control, the most fundamental escape position. Focus on frames, shrimp to guard, and the sit-up escape. 2Mount EscapesStart under mount, higher urgency.Begin under mount. Higher urgency and fewer options, focus on bridge and roll and the elbow-knee escape. 3Back EscapesStart with the opponent on the back, hooks in.Begin with the back taken (hooks in), the highest-danger position. Focus on hand fighting and escaping to guard. 4Full MMA ExpressionTop can threaten strikes.Top can threaten strikes; bottom must escape while defending them. Escape under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Escapes start in the space. What are you reading to find it? The felt weight distribution and pressure through contact: where the top's base loads and unloads, which limbs are pinned, where the connection goes light. Frames and knees go where the weight isn't. Q Why does the top maintain only, with no submissions, at Levels 1 to 3? So the bottom can focus on escape mechanics, not submission defense. It isolates the escape task while keeping the live weight-reading loop intact. Q What three outcomes count as a successful escape? Guard recovery, a reversal to top, or standing up. A clear, hierarchical definition of success, not just surviving in place. Q What is being measured to know the escape skill has formed? Whether you recover space and frames faster than the top can re-load weight, and whether frames stay live as the top adjusts. When that space-recovery relationship stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Create frames and space; execute bridges, shrimps, and turns; chain escape attempts; recover to guard or standing.

🥋Top (Attacker)

Maintain weight distribution; anticipate and counter escapes; stay heavy without exhausting; transition to maintain control.

Coaching cues

⚓Where is the weight?

Ask: "Where is their weight?" This directs attention to the haptic information that matters, rather than prescribing a technique.

🕳️Find the space

Ask: "Where is the space?" Escapes start from felt space, frames and knees go where the connection is light.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Bottom must actively escape→Forces exploration of escape solutions Top maintains (no submissions)→Bottom focuses on escape, not submission defense Specific starting positions→Isolates escape patterns per position Win by escape OR duration→Urgency for bottom, patience for top Live, resisting opponent→Keeps weight-reading perception intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019), isolates the escape task while keeping the essential perception-action loop intact. The bottom still reads weight distribution and timing from a real, resisting opponent.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Pressure distribution & grip location → which direction to escape and which limbs are controlled.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hip position & frame strength → available escape directions and when space exists.

👁️Visual

Top's posture changes & space between bodies → timing windows and where to insert frames or knees.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom recovers space and frames faster than the top can re-load weight, track escapes completed vs. position held, and whether frames stay live as the top adjusts. That space-recovery relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: being controlled in side control, mount, or back and escaping before damage accumulates or a submission occurs.

Simplified: no strikes (L1–3)no submissions by topreset on time limit

Haptic perception of weight and pressure is identical to competition, transfers into Leg Reclaim and Ground to Standing.

Readiness to progress

  • Escapes ~60%+ against resisting partners
  • Uses multiple escape techniques
  • Chains escapes when the first fails
  • Stays calm under pressure

Warning signs

Only one escape works Escapes only vs lighter partners Panic / flailing when escape fails Can't feel weight distribution

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Stop on submission attempts or neck cranks 🔁 Reset if positions stall completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is a fundamental defensive game Follow-on→Leg Reclaim · Ground to Standing Related→Decision States · Guard Recovery

docs.title: Ground to Standing
docs.text:
Ground → Open Space · GrapplingGround to Standing Ground → Open SpaceGrapplingDefensiveIntermediateCounter

Get back to your feet from the bottom before they drag you down.

StartOne on bottom, one on top, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom stands and disengages; top keeps them down. → FinishBottom stands & disengages → switch · Top keeps them down → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Standing while connected gets dragged down, clear the grips first.

Separation, not a sweep, is the win. Both feet up and clear of control.

What to Read

Attune to the felt weight distribution & connection through contact, which grips, hooks, or body-locks are keeping you tied to them, where the top's base loads and goes light, plus the top player's posture and hip motion. That inertial information specifies when separation is sufficient to stand before they re-engage, not the opponent's limbs in isolation.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, on the mat, one on bottom, one on top. PositionBottom in guard, under side control, or turtle, set by the level. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne bottom (defender), one top (attacker). Start & resetBegin from the ground; reset there each round.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to stand up and disengage completely, both feet, clear of control.

Create space, build base and posture, manage the grips while rising, and complete the standup cleanly. Sweeping to top is progress but not the win. VS 🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to keep the opponent on the ground, following if they rise.

Maintain connection and weight, collapse standup attempts, drag them back down when they rise, and transition to a takedown if needed.

The Rules

🧍 The goal is standing, not topThe bottom player's goal is standing, not the top position. A sweep is progress but doesn't end the round, this develops the distinct "wrestling up" skill. 🪨 Top keeps them groundedThe top player keeps the opponent down and follows if they rise, realistic resistance, not a passive partner. 🎯 Standing = both feet, clear of controlA complete standup is both feet up and clear of the opponent's control. Standing while still connected gets dragged back down. 🔗 Clear control before standingGrips, hooks, and body-locks must be cleared before rising, standing while connected invites the drag-down. This forces grip clearing. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses instantly, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Switch Bottom stands & disengages → switch roles.A clean standup, both feet, no opponent control, bottom wins and earns the top's keep-down role. Win Top denies the standup for the duration → top wins.Keep the opponent grounded for the set duration, or advance to a dominant control, the top denies the standup. Reset Top wins → reset, same roles.When the top prevents the standup or advances control, the round resets from the ground position, keeping the same roles for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → loss.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1From GuardStart in open guard; bottom works the technical standup.Begin in open guard. Bottom works the technical standup, focus on creating space to stand. 2From TurtleStart in turtle; bottom bases and stands.Begin in turtle. Bottom works to base and stand, focus on standing up from a defensive position. 3From UnderStart under side control or mount; escape THEN stand.Begin under side control or mount. Bottom must escape first, then stand, focus on chaining escape to standup. 4Full MMA ExpressionAdd strikes for both.Top can strike to keep the bottom down; bottom can strike to create separation. Standup under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Standing while connected gets you dragged down. What must happen first? Clear the grips, hooks, and body-locks before rising. Name what is tying you to them, then break it, then stand. Q What exactly counts as a win here? Both feet up and clear of control. Separation, not a sweep. Sweeping to top is progress but does not end the round. Q What are you reading to know separation is enough to stand? The felt connection through contact: which grips keep you tied, where the top's base loads and goes light, plus the top's posture and hip motion. It specifies the window before they re-engage. Q Why is this trained as its own skill instead of as a sweep? Because "wrestling up" is distinct from guard work: getting back to the feet is often preferable to staying down, so the standup affordance gets its own development.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Create space from the opponent; build base and posture; manage grips while rising; complete the standup cleanly.

🥋Top (Attacker)

Maintain connection and weight; collapse standup attempts; drag back down when they rise; transition to a takedown if needed.

Coaching cues

🔗What's connecting you?

Ask: "What was keeping you connected?" This develops grip awareness, naming the tie before trying to break it.

🧹Clear, then rise

Ask: "Did you clear their control before standing?" Clearing before standing is essential, standing while connected gets dragged back down.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Goal is standing, not top position→Develops standup-specific "wrestling up" skill Standing = both feet, clear of control→Clear success definition Top follows and attempts to keep down→Realistic resistance Progressive starting positions→Scaffolded difficulty (guard → turtle → under) Ground → standing transition→Environment-change practice

Develops standup affordance perception, recognizing when separation is sufficient to stand (Renshaw et al., 2019). The constraint distinguishing standup from sweeping develops the specific "wrestling up" skill, separate from guard work.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Connection points & top's weight distribution → what to clear and when to time the standup.

👁️Visual

Space available → base-building opportunity.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own base position & posture quality → standup method and readiness to rise.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom recovers base and clears connection faster than the top can re-load weight, track standups completed vs. drag-downs, and whether the grip is cleared before rising. That separation-recovery relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: "wrestling up", getting back to feet from bottom, where standing is often preferable to staying down.

Simplified: focus on standing, not sweepingstart from various positionsclear success criteria

Grip-clearing and base-building perception is identical to competition, returns to the Open Space games.

Readiness to progress

  • Stands from current level consistently
  • Clears grips before standing
  • Completes standup without being dragged back
  • Timing is appropriate (not forced)

Warning signs

Gets dragged back immediately Can't build base Only sweeps (doesn't stand) Stands into a takedown

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Stop on slamming during standup attempts 🔁 Reset if positions stall

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Escape Follow-on→Returns to the Open Space games Related→Decision States

docs.title: Half-Guard Bottom
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingHalf-Guard Bottom

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu, Modern Defensive Guard material), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class. Details may change.

GroundGrapplingCombinedIntermediateShield & Sweep

Hold the shield, break their base, take something better.

StartBottom on the side with a knee shield framed in; top working to clear it, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom causes posts, punishes them, and elevates; top clears the shield to chest-to-chest. → FinishSweep, full guard, the back, or top elevated fully off the mat → bottom · Shield cleared to chest-to-chest, held 3s → top · Out of bounds → loss.

The shield isn't a wall, it's a lever.

It holds them off only long enough to tilt them. A shield that only blocks eventually gets crushed.

What to Read

Attune to the pressure coming through the knee shield. Light pressure means the top is basing back, safe but passive, attack their posture. Crushing pressure means their weight has committed past their knees, that's the elevation window. The shield is a pressure gauge: read it, don't just brace it.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom (half guard, knee shield), one top (passer). PositionBottom on the side, knee shield across the top's chest or hip, one leg hooked; top driving to clear it. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesBottom maintains the shield and converts pressure into offense; top removes the shield and flattens. Start & resetBegin with the shield set; reset on a sweep, a recovery, a clear, or the round cap.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Knee Shield)

Trying to keep the shield framed, force and punish posts, and convert: sweep, full guard, the back, or elevate the top clean off the mat.

Stay on your side, shield angled, not flat. Use it as a lever: when their pressure commits, lift and turn. When they base back, sit up into them. Four wins, the shield is the start of all of them. VS 🥋 Top (Passer)

Trying to clear the knee shield and reach chest-to-chest, held 3 seconds.

Don't wrestle the shield head-on, change the angle until it points at nothing. Control the bottom knee, drop your hip below the shield line, and keep your weight behind your own knees while you do it.

The Rules

🛡️ The shield is the resourceThe round revolves around the knee shield: the bottom converts it into offense, the top works to remove it. Losing the shield isn't losing the round, but it's losing the lever. ⬆️ Elevation countsLifting the top fully off the mat (both feet and hands clear) is a bottom win, the proof that the shield-lever and underhooks beat the base, even without finishing the sweep. 🎯 Clear proven by the holdThe top wins by removing the shield and reaching chest-to-chest, held 3 seconds. Brushing the knee aside into a scramble proves nothing yet. ⏱️ Round cap, no stallingRun a set round cap (start at 60 seconds). If neither side wins by the cap, reset and switch roles. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are grappling only, so the bottom can read pressure through the shield without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. The shield must now manage punches as well as pressure, exactly the MMA half guard. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Win Bottom sweeps, recovers full guard, or takes the back → bottom wins.Three conversions, each observable: a reversal to top, both legs back around (full guard), or chest-to-back. The shield did its job as a lever. Win Bottom elevates the top fully off the mat → bottom wins.Feet and hands clear of the floor at once. The base is beaten even if the sweep isn't finished, an honest, observable proxy that scales safely. Switch Top clears the shield to chest-to-chest, held 3s → top wins.The shield removed and the chest connection held. From here the position is the half-guard-pass game's flatten-and-free problem. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Hold the shieldMaintain the frame only.The round is only maintenance: bottom keeps the shield framed and the side position, top works to clear it. Whoever owns the shield at the bell owns the round. Learn the angles that survive pressure. 2Cause postsTilt them onto a hand.Bottom adds push-pull through the shield and grips: the round ends when the top posts a hand or knee wide. The shield becomes a steering tool, not a wall. 3Punish postsHips to the captured side.The post is the start: bottom captures the posted arm (2-on-1) and moves the hips toward it, the doorway to the back and the sweep. The window after the post is the whole game. 4ElevateLift the base away.Bottom adds the elevation win: underhook plus shield-lever lifts the top fully off the mat, or converts to the sweep, the recovery, or the back. The complete bottom game, grappling only. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Light strikes on. The shield manages punches and pressure together, and a lazy shield gets hit through, not just passed. The MMA half guard, complete.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What does pressure through the shield tell you? Light = they're based back, attack the posture. Crushing = weight committed past their knees, elevate. The shield is a pressure gauge, not just a barrier. Q Why does a blocking-only shield eventually fail? Because the top gets unlimited attempts against a static frame. The shield earns its keep as a lever, every clear attempt is weight you can redirect. Q What does full elevation prove, and why is it a win? Feet and hands clear of the mat means the base is completely beaten. It's the sweep's proof without the slam risk, observable and safe at every size difference. Q How does the top beat the shield without wrestling it? Change the angle until the shield points at nothing. Control the bottom knee, drop the hip below the shield line, keep weight behind the knees while doing it.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Knee Shield)

Stay on the side, angle the shield, read the pressure, cause and punish posts, elevate or convert when the weight commits.

🥋Top (Passer)

Change angles on the shield, control the bottom knee, keep weight behind the knees, clear to chest-to-chest and hold it.

Coaching cues

🛡️Gauge or wall?

Ask the bottom: "What was the pressure telling you before the clear?" Builds the read that turns the shield from a brace into a sensor.

⬆️Where did the weight go?

Ask both after an elevation: "When did the top's weight pass the knees?" Marks the commit moment both sides need to feel.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Maintenance-only opening level→Builds shield angles before offense exists Cause-then-punish post ladder→Same off-balancing grammar as Seated Guard Retention, new position Elevation as a win→Rewards beating the base without slam risk Clear proven by a 3s hold→The top must control, not just brush past Live, resisting passer→Keeps the pressure-gauge read intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the maintain → cause → punish → elevate ladder grows the bottom's role from survival to offense one perceptual layer at a time, against a top whose passing pressure is the very signal being learned.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Pressure through the shield → based-back passive, or committed past the knees: the elevation window.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own torso angle and shield angle → on the side and levered, or flattening toward the back.

👁️Visual

The top's hands and knee width → posts landing, angles changing on the shield.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom converts pressure into offense faster than the top changes angles. Track sweeps, recoveries, back-takes, and elevations vs. shield-clears conceded, and whether the bottom's attacks launch at the commit moment or after it has passed. When the elevation arrives on the commit, the read has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the half-guard bottom every MMA fighter actually ends up in after a half-stuffed takedown, where staying flat means eating punches and the shield is the only structure that buys offense back.

Simplified: shield ladderno strikes L1-4round cap

Deepens the recovery side of Leg Reclaim; mirrors Half-Guard Pass.

Readiness to progress

  • Keeps the side position and shield angle under pressure
  • Causes posts through the shield, not despite it
  • Launches the elevation at the commit moment
  • Converts elevations into sweeps, guard, or the back

Warning signs

Goes flat and braces the shield straight on Blocks every clear but never attacks Elevates without an underhook, gives the pass Top wrestles the shield head-on, feeds the lever

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Elevations lower with control, no dumping the elevated player 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Leg Reclaim Follow-on→Closed Guard Bottom Mirror→Half-Guard Pass Related→Guard Recovery · Decision States

docs.title: Half-Guard Pass
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingHalf-Guard Pass

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class. Details may change.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediatePassing

Win the underhook, flatten them, free the trapped leg.

StartTop in half guard, one leg trapped, underhook and cross-face contested, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop wins the chest battle and frees the leg; bottom builds the shield or hook and recovers. → FinishLeg freed to a chest-to-chest pin (side or mount), held 3s → top · Knee shield in, inside hook in, sweep, or full guard → bottom · Out of bounds → loss.

Half guard is one battle, who owns the chest.

Underhook and cross-face decide it. The trapped leg frees itself once the bottom is flat.

What to Read

Attune to the angle of the bottom's torso against your chest. On their side, they're alive: the underhook, the shield, and the sweep all live there. Flat on the back, they're done. Every inch of shoulder you press to the mat shortens their options, the leg comes free when the hips can't follow the knee.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top (passer), one bottom (half guard). PositionTop's leg trapped between the bottom's; underhook and cross-face contested, nobody owns the chest yet. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesTop flattens and frees the leg; bottom fights for the underhook, the shield, and the recovery. Start & resetBegin from contested half guard; reset on a pass, a recovery, a sweep, or the round cap.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Passer)

Trying to win the underhook and cross-face, flatten the bottom, and free the trapped leg into a held pin.

Chest before leg, always. Yanking the knee while the bottom is on their side feeds the sweep. Flatten the shoulders, kill the inside frames, and the leg slides out almost on its own. VS 🤸 Bottom (Half Guard)

Trying to stay on the side, win the underhook or build the knee shield, and recover full guard, sweep, or stand.

The side position is everything, the moment your back touches flat, start framing back to it. Underhook beats shield when you can get it; the shield buys time when you can't.

The Rules

🫁 The chest battle decidesUnderhook and cross-face are the contested resources. Whoever owns them owns the round's direction, the leg is a consequence, not the fight. 🎯 Pass proven by the holdThe pass counts when the trapped leg is free AND the top holds a chest-to-chest pin (side control or mount) for 3 seconds. Freeing the leg into a scramble proves nothing yet. 🛡️ Recovery is gradedKnee shield in, inside hook in, full guard recovered, a sweep, or standing all win for the bottom, each a cleaner answer than the last. Name which one you got. ⏱️ Round cap, no stallingRun a set round cap (start at 60 seconds). If neither side wins by the cap, reset and switch roles. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are grappling only, so both sides can read the chest battle without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. The cross-face hand becomes a striking hand, exactly as it does in MMA. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Win Top frees the leg to a chest-to-chest pin (side or mount), held 3s → top wins.The trapped leg cleared and the pin held. Freeing the leg but losing the chest in the scramble doesn't count, the hold is the proof. Switch Bottom recovers full guard, sweeps, or stands → bottom wins.Graded ladder: knee shield and inside hook are progress markers; full guard, a sweep to top, or standing fully wins the round. Reset Round cap expires in contested half guard → reset, switch roles.Neither the pass nor the recovery landed before the clock. Switch roles so both sides get both problems. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Win the hip accessFlatten them, nothing else.The round is only about torso angle: top works to press the bottom's shoulders flat, bottom works to stay on the side. Whoever owns the angle at the bell owns the round. The fight underneath every half-guard exchange, isolated. 2Single underhookChest-to-chest, one wing in.Top adds the underhook on one side plus the cross-face, and must keep both while flattening. Bottom hand-fights for the underhook back. The chest battle becomes explicit. 3Double undersBoth wings, total chest control.Top works to double underhooks before any leg work. Heavier control, but the bottom's hips get freer, feel the tradeoff between arm control and hip control. 4Free the legThe full pass, held.The complete task, grappling only: win the chest, flatten, free the trapped leg to side or mount held 3 seconds, against a bottom playing the full recovery ladder. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Light strikes on. The cross-face hand now strikes, the bottom's shield now manages punches too. Half guard the way MMA actually plays it.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What's the one battle half guard reduces to? Who owns the chest, the underhook and cross-face. The trapped leg is a consequence of that battle, not the battle itself. Q Why is yanking the knee out early a mistake? Because while the bottom is on their side, their underhook and sweep are live. Pulling the leg from there trades your base for their best moment. Flatten first. Q What does the bottom's torso angle tell the top? On the side = alive, flat = finished. Every inch of shoulder pressed to the mat removes options.
