The leg slides free when the hips can't follow the knee. Q When should the bottom take the shield over the underhook? When the underhook is lost or being smashed. The underhook wins position; the shield buys time to angle back and re-fight for it.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Passer)

Win underhook and cross-face, press the shoulders flat, kill the inside frames, then free the leg into a held pin.

🤸Bottom (Half Guard)

Fight to the side, hand-fight the underhook, build the shield when losing, climb the recovery ladder: hook, full guard, sweep, stand.

Coaching cues

🫁Who had the chest?

Ask both: "When the pass came, who owned the underhook?" Ties the outcome back to the battle that decided it.

📐Side or flat?

Ask the bottom: "How long were you flat before the leg came out?" Builds the alarm bell that flat = the clock is running.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Angle-only opening level→Isolates the flatten-vs-side war before any grips exist Underhook levels (single → double)→Makes the chest battle explicit and felt Pass proven by leg-free + 3s pin→Rewards control sequence over knee-yanking Graded recovery ladder→The bottom always has a next rung, no dead rounds Live, resisting opponent→Keeps the torso-angle read intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the level ladder peels the half-guard problem into its perceptual layers (angle → chest → leg) while the opponent stays live, so the read that matters, torso angle under pressure, never leaves the game.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

The bottom's torso angle against the chest → flat enough to start freeing the leg, or still alive on the side.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own underhook depth and cross-face pressure → whether the chest is actually won.

👁️Visual

The bottom's free knee → shield coming, hook hunting, or recovery starting.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top wins the chest before working the leg. Track passes held vs. sweeps conceded, and what the bottom's torso angle was at each pass. When passes consistently come from a flattened bottom rather than a knee-yank race, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the most common contested ground position in MMA, half guard after an imperfect takedown or a partial recovery, where GnP and passing share the same chest battle.

Simplified: chest-battle ladderno strikes L1-4round cap

Deepens the passing side of Ground Access; mirrors Half-Guard Bottom; the recovery ladder connects to Guard Recovery.

Readiness to progress

  • Wins the underhook before touching the trapped leg
  • Flattens the shoulders and keeps them flat
  • Frees the leg into a held pin, not a scramble
  • As bottom: fights back to the side the moment the back touches

Warning signs

Yanks the knee while the bottom is on their side Trades the cross-face away to hunt the leg Bottom accepts flat and waits Recovery skips rungs and gives the back

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 No neck cranks from the cross-face 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Access Follow-on→Ground Control Mirror→Half-Guard Bottom Related→Guard Recovery · Decision States

docs.title: Kick Defense Isolation
docs.text:
Skill Isolation · StrikingKick Defense Isolation

Provisional (WIP): invented and run in class 2026-06-20, pending final coach sign-off

Built on the mat on 2026-06-20 to isolate the kick-defense read, the first dedicated kick-defense game in the library. It trains checking, blocking, and catching the roundhouse, one kick at a time, then layers a jab to make the kicker tricky. Awaiting a final pass before the WIP tag comes off.

Skill IsolationStrikingDefensiveIntermediateKick Defense

Read the kick and kill it, check, block, or catch, before it lands, one kick at a time.

StartOne kicker, one defender, inside a marked perimeter; roundhouses only, one at a time. → The GoalDefender checks, blocks, or catches the kick; kicker lands clean and earns the next one. → FinishA clean defense → roles switch. A clean landed kick → kicker continues. Catch and keep → gold star.

You can't block what you read late. Time the kick, don't chase it.

Attune to the rising shin and the hip that throws it, the time-to-contact, not the foot. Catch only when it's really there, reach for everything and you get faked to the head.

What to Read

Attune to τ (time-to-contact), the closing rate of the rising shin, and the hip and shoulder pre-rotation that says which kick is coming and where. Defend on the read, not the impact. The catch is a higher-risk, higher-reward read than the block, take it only when τ gives you the room.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one kicker (offense), one defender. PositionStriking range, both on their feet, roundhouses only. Switch stance freely. BoundaryA marked perimeter, the wall is live, use it to corner the kicker or get walked onto it yourself. RolesKicker lands clean roundhouses one at a time; defender checks, blocks, or catches. Start & reset"Go"; roles switch the moment the defender successfully checks, blocks, or catches. A clean, undefended kick earns the kicker the next one.

The Matchup

🦵 Kicker (Landing)

Trying to land a clean roundhouse to the leg or body, one at a time, and earn the right to throw the next one.

One kick at a time, no spamming. Use feints and stance switches to disguise the kick, and gauge your own commitment, a heavy kick that gets checked hurts you. In the jab variant, use a lead jab to open the kick. Don't leave a caught leg hanging, pull it free. VS 🛡️ Defender (Reading)

Trying to check, block, or catch the kick on the early read, and take over the offense.

Load your weight on the back leg, ready to check. Read τ and the hip, not the foot. Check with the shin, block with two points of contact, and catch only when the time is really there. Keep your fingers curled. In the later levels, return fire the moment you defend.

The Rules

🦵 Roundhouses only, one at a timeLeg or body roundhouses; head only with full control. No spamming, throw one, read the result, then throw again. The kicker continues only by landing clean. 🛡️ Check, block, or catch wins the exchangeCheck = the shin, above the ankle and below the knee. Block = two points of contact absorbing the kick. Catch = secure and keep the leg, the gold star. Any of the three switches the offense to the defender. 🔁 Land clean and continueA clean, undefended kick earns the kicker the next one. A successful defense switches the roles, the defender becomes the kicker. 👊 Jab variantThe kicker may add a lead jab as a probe. It scores no points, it only opens the kick. The defender may return fire or counter for a bonus. 🚫 No head kicks, no calf kicksThe coordination-stage nerf, it makes the catch safe to learn. The mastery level puts the head-kick threat back, with gear, so the catch carries its real risk. ✋ Curl the fingersOpen, splayed fingers get jammed and broken, and they foul. Keep a soft curl on every block and catch. A real safety habit, learned here so it holds in a fight. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter. Leaving it loses the exchange.

How to Win

Defender Check, block, or catch the kick → win the exchange and take the offense.Any clean defense wins the exchange and hands you the offense. Catch and keep the leg for the gold star, and a returned counter on top of the defense is the full expression. Kicker Land a clean, undefended roundhouse → continue and earn the next kick.A clean kick to the leg or body that is not checked, blocked, or caught keeps you on offense. One at a time, read each result before the next. Switch / loss A defended kick hands over the offense; a caught and kept leg can be punished.Getting checked, blocked, or caught switches the roles. A leg left hanging in a catch can be swept or countered, so pull it free.

The Levels

1Check and blockRead τ, no jab.Roundhouses only, no jab, no head kicks. The defender reads τ and the hip, and checks or blocks. Finds the early read. 2Add the catchThe gold star, but risky.Catching and keeping the leg becomes a win. But don't reach for everything, an over-reach against a tricky kicker gets faked. Take the catch only when τ affords it. 3Add the jabThe kicker gets tricky.The kicker adds a lead jab to probe and open the kick. The defender has to read through the misdirection. 4Return fireDefend and punish.A defense plus an immediate counter is the full win. Check and return, or catch and sweep, the defender stops being a wall and starts punishing. 5Mastery: the head kick returnsCalibrate the real risk.With control and gear, head kicks are live again. Now the catch carries its real risk and the read has to be honest, this calibrates the safe habit from the earlier levels to a real fight.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you actually reading, the foot or something earlier? τ, the time-to-contact of the rising shin, and the hip and shoulder that throw it. Defend on the early read, not at the impact, by the time you see the foot it is too late. Q Why not reach to catch every kick? The catch is a high-risk read. Reach for everything and a good kicker feints and goes to the head. Catch only when τ gives you the room, otherwise block safely. Q Why does the no-head-kick rule come off at the mastery level? Nerfing the head kick makes the catch artificially safe. To calibrate the catch's real risk you have to put the head-kick threat back, or the safe habit won't hold in a real fight. Q Did your kick defense carry over from your grappling or boxing? No. Attunement does not transfer across action modes, reading a kick is its own skill, learned from your own stance. That is exactly why this game isolates it instead of assuming it comes free from another base.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🦵Kicker (Landing)

Land clean roundhouses one at a time, feint and switch stance to disguise them, gauge your own commitment, and pull a caught leg free. In the variant, use the jab to open the kick.

🛡️Defender (Reading)

Weight on the back leg ready to check, read τ and the hip, check or block on the early read, catch only when it's there, keep the fingers curled, return fire in the later levels.

Coaching cues

👁️Read the hip, not the foot

The kick is specified by the hip and shoulder before the shin arrives. A defender who watches the foot is always late.

⚖️Gauge your own kick

Ask the kicker: "How sure were you?" A heavy kick that gets checked hurts the kicker. Commit hard only when the kick is really there, the same risk read the catcher makes.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Roundhouses only, one at a time→Isolates the kick-defense read without spam noise (task simplified, loop intact) Win = check / block / catch→Realized defensive affordances, not a cue merely noticed Catch is the gold star, but risky→Rewards the higher read only when τ affords it No head kicks, then back at mastery→A safe coordination stage, then calibration of the real risk Jab variant adds option-space→Repeats the problem with more variability, not a new movement

Implements per-mode learning (the non-transfer rule): kick-defense attunement does not carry over from a grappling or boxing base, so it gets its own isolated path. The kicker stays live and adaptive (feints, stance switches), so this is a task-simplified game with the perception-action loop intact, not a decomposed feed.

What athletes read

👁️Visual

τ of the rising shin and the hip / shoulder pre-rotation → which kick, where, and when, the early read that beats the impact.

✋Haptic

The kick's weight on the block, the leg in the catch → how committed the kick was, whether the catch will hold.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own stance loaded on the back leg → whether you are positioned to check, or stuck flat and late.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender defends on the early τ read, checking and blocking in time and catching only when afforded, versus reacting at impact and reaching for catches. Track defenses on the read versus late reactions, and faked-catch losses. When the defender reliably reads the hip and the closing shin, the kick-defense skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the live kick-defense problem in striking and MMA, the check / block / catch decision under feints. A per-mode skill that does not transfer in from grappling, so it earns its own isolated game.

Roundhouses, one at a timecheck / block / catchτ read, calibrated risk

Follows Close-Range Defense; the return-fire levels feed Counter-Striking.

Readiness to progress

  • Checks and blocks on the early read, not at impact
  • Catches only when τ affords it, doesn't reach
  • Keeps a curled hand on every block and catch
  • Stays composed under feints and stance switches

Warning signs

Reacts at impact, always late Reaches for every catch and gets faked Splays the fingers on the block Over-commits a heavy kick that gets checked (kicker)

Safety & related games

🦵 Shin guards on, light control, no calf kicks ✋ Curl the fingers, slow on the catch 🔁 Switch roles on a clean defense

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Close-Range Defense Follow-on→Counter-Striking Related→Evasive Movement

docs.title: Knee-on-Belly Control
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingKnee-on-Belly Control

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

Fills the empty knee-on-belly bucket in the top-control grid. Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediateControl → Finish

Keep the knee-on-belly alive through the frames, then attack an arm or step to mount.

StartTop in knee-on-belly, posted, opposite leg out; bottom flat, framing the knee, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop stays light and balanced, switches sides on the frame, and starts an arm attack; bottom clears the knee and recovers. → FinishArm attack started, held 3s, or step to mount → top · Clear the knee and recover, or reverse → bottom · Out of bounds → loss.

Knee-on-belly is balance, not weight.

Ride light and tall, switch with the frame, attack the arm it leaves. Settle your weight and you'll get bumped off.

What to Read

Attune to the bottom's framing hands and hip turn, the moment they push the knee to make space or turn to recover. That shift specifies when to switch sides and which arm extends into an attack, not a memorized pin. When they push the knee hard, the other side and their pushing arm both open.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top (attacker, knee-on-belly), one bottom (defender). PositionTop with one knee on the belly, posted on the far foot, hips tall; bottom flat, framing the knee and the hip. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesTop keeps knee-on-belly alive and attacks; bottom clears the knee and recovers. Start & resetBegin from settled knee-on-belly; reset on an arm attack, an advance, an escape, or the count.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to keep knee-on-belly live (switching sides as needed) and start an arm attack, or step to mount.

Ride light and tall, balance over the knee, don't dump your weight. When the bottom frames hard, switch to the far side or step to mount. Trap the arm they push with. Control is proven by an attack or an advance, not by perching. VS 🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to clear the knee, recover a guard, or reverse.

Frame the knee and the hip, turn to your side, and pull the knee across to recover. Don't push with a straight arm, that's the arm the top traps.

The Rules

🎯 Top wins by an attack or an advanceThe top proves control by starting an isolated-arm attack and holding it 3 seconds, or by stepping to mount. Keeping knee-on-belly without a threat is a stall, not a win. Switching sides to stay on top is allowed and encouraged. 🤜 Bottom wins by clearing the kneeThe bottom wins by clearing the knee and recovering a guard (half guard or better), or reversing. A clean, observable bottom goal. ⏱️ Hold the count or finishIf the top keeps knee-on-belly for the set count (start at 20 seconds) without an attack or an advance, the round resets. If the bottom clears the knee first, the bottom wins. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are control only, so both players read balance and frames before strikes are added. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound. Knee-on-belly is a classic strike-and-advance perch; strikes are the disincentivization tool that punishes a passive bottom. Ground games train smashing, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round.

How to Win

Win Top starts an arm attack (3s) or steps to mount → top wins.An isolated-arm attack begun and controlled three seconds, or a clean step over to mount. Either is the observable proof that knee-on-belly is doing work, not just perching. Switch Bottom clears the knee and recovers, or reverses → bottom wins, switch roles.Clearing the knee and getting a knee back in (half guard or better), or reversing, is the escape. See Guard Recovery. Reset Top holds knee-on-belly the full count, no threat → reset, same roles.The top perched but never attacked or advanced before the count expired. Resets from settled knee-on-belly. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position.

The Levels

1No-hands balanceRide light and tall.Top keeps knee-on-belly with no hands, balancing over the knee while the bottom bumps and frames. Builds the light, balanced ride that resists the bump. 2Hands connected, maintainAdd the grips.Top adds collar-and-hip or near-arm grips and maintains through the frames. Reading the bottom's hip turn becomes the task. 3Switch sides, re-pinFollow the escape.When the bottom frames the knee hard, the top switches to the far side or re-pins, staying on top through the turn. Mobility, not weight, keeps the position. 4Attack the arm, step to mountConvert the perch.Top traps the pushing arm for an isolated-arm attack, or steps over to mount as the bottom turns. The conversion from perch to finish becomes the focus. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Continuous from settled knee-on-belly, strikes live, until the top attacks or advances, or the bottom clears the knee. The strike threat makes the perch genuinely costly to escape.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why is knee-on-belly balance, not weight? Dumping weight onto the knee makes you top-heavy and easy to bump off. Riding light and tall keeps your base mobile, so you can switch sides instead of getting tipped. Q What do you do when the bottom frames the knee hard? Switch to the far side or re-pin, and trap the pushing arm. A hard frame both opens the other side and exposes the arm. Q How does the top prove control here? By starting an arm attack (3s) or stepping to mount, not by perching. The perch is the means; the attack or advance is the win.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Attacker)

Ride light and tall, balance over the knee, switch sides on a hard frame, trap the pushing arm, step to mount when the bottom turns.

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Frame the knee and hip, turn to your side, pull the knee across, avoid pushing with a straight arm.

Coaching cues

🪶Light or heavy?

Ask the top: "Were you balancing or dumping weight?" A heavy knee gets bumped; a light ride switches and survives.

🔗Attack or perch?

Ask the top: "Did you start an attack or just sit up there?" Keeps the position converting to a finish.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top wins by an attack or an advance→Forces a finish, no perching No-hands level→Isolates the balance the position lives on Switching sides allowed→Rewards mobility over dead weight Hold the count or finish→Urgency for the top, a real window for the bottom

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the no-hands level isolates the balance the whole position depends on, then grips, mobility, and attacks are layered on against a live, framing opponent. The attack-or-advance win keeps the representativeness, knee-on-belly is a strike-and-pass perch, not a hold.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

The bottom's frame pressure → when to switch sides and which arm is exposed.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance over the knee → whether you're light enough to ride or about to be bumped.

👁️Visual

The bottom's hip turn → the step-to-mount window and the arm to attack.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top starts an attack or advances faster than the bottom clears the knee. Track attacks and advances vs. knee-clears, and whether the top rides light and switches rather than getting bumped. The attack-or-advance versus clear-the-knee race is the order parameter.

Representativeness

Models: the knee-on-belly perch in MMA, ride for strikes, switch with the frame, and step to mount or attack an arm before the bottom recovers.

Simplified: balance firstno strikes L1-4reset on the count

Deepens the top side of Ground Control; the step-over feeds Mount Maintenance; the arm trap feeds Side-Control Ride.

Readiness to progress

  • Rides light and tall, balanced over the knee
  • Switches sides on a hard frame instead of getting bumped
  • Traps the arm the bottom pushes with
  • Steps to mount when the bottom turns

Warning signs

Dumps weight and gets tipped off Perches without attacking Never switches sides on the frame Reaches and loses the balance

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling, GnP by coach permission 🛑 Arm attacks slow, tap early, no cranks 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Control Follow-on→Mount Maintenance · Side-Control Ride Related→TKO Pin · Decision States

docs.title: Land the Target
docs.text:
Open Space · StrikingLand the Target Open SpaceStrikingOffensiveIntermediateAccess

Land one declared target by reading the defense and opening it with setup and misdirection, while the defender resists without countering.

StartTwo fighters at striking range inside a marked perimeter; attacker has one target in mind. → The GoalAttacker opens the defense to land the declared target; defender protects all three zones. → FinishLand the target → switch · Whiffed or blocked → reset · Defender has no win condition (diagnostic only).

Pick the shot first, then make the defense open it.

A declared target turns offense into a read-and-setup problem, not volume. You only win when your one chosen shot lands clean.

What to Read

Attune to the defender's guard reactions to your probes, how their shell shifts when you threaten a zone, together with the rate of expansion (τ) of your own committing shot toward the opening at center mass. The opening you read isn't where the guard is; it's where it goes when you make it move.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off in a neutral fighting stance. RangeStriking range, with room to create angles. BoundaryA marked perimeter. Both stay inside it. RolesOne attacker, one defender, switch when the target lands. Start & resetAttacker picks a target and initiates; reset to neutral after each exchange.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to land a predetermined target shot by reading the defense and creating openings.

Probe, identify, set up, then commit. You may attack other zones as setup, but you only win when you land your declared target. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to defend all three zones, head, body, legs, effectively. No countering.

Defend naturally with the full toolkit (block, parry, slip, move). You have no win condition, your success is making the attacker work for every opening.

The Rules

🚫 Defender cannot counterThe defender plays pure defense. Removing the counter-threat lets the attacker focus entirely on reading patterns and building setups. 🎯 Attacker has a specific targetBefore engaging, the attacker picks one predetermined target shot, not just throwing volume. Everything serves landing that shot. 🛡️ Defend all three zonesThe defender must protect head, body, and legs naturally, no artificial bias toward one zone, so the attacker has to earn the opening with misdirection. 🔍 Probes & setups allowedJabs, feints, and setup combinations are encouraged to shape the defense, but they aren't the goal. Landing the declared target is.

How to Win

Switch Land the declared target clean → switch roles.A clean landing means solid contact reaching the target zone with real energy transfer, not a whiff, graze, or blocked shot. Land it and you become the defender. Reset Whiffed, grazed, or blocked → reset, read again.A failed attempt isn't a loss; it's information, same roles. Reset to a neutral stance and re-enter the probe → read → setup → commit cycle. No score Defender denies every opening → diagnostic success.The defender has no win condition. Success is measured by maintaining solid defense across all zones and forcing the attacker to read, set up, and commit. The attacker calls their own clean landing on the honor system, this is an academic exercise.

The Levels

1Private targetTarget in mind, not announced.The standard mode. Read → setup → land, then claim the win on the honor system. The defender defends all zones naturally. 2Announced targetDeclare it first, harder, not easier.You announce the target ("left hook to body") so the defender focuses there. You must pull their defense away and still land it. 3Defender countersCounter strikes after good defense.The defender can fire counters once they defend successfully. You pursue the target while respecting the counter risk. 4Full MMA expressionCounters plus clinch denial.The defender adds clinch to deny your offense. Land the target under realistic MMA pressure, counters and clinch both live.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What opening are you actually reading? Not where the guard is, but where it goes when you make it move. Read the defender's guard reactions to your probes, then the τ of your committing shot. Q Why does a single declared target make this harder, not easier? You only win on that one chosen shot, so offense becomes a read-and-setup problem, not volume. You must earn the opening with misdirection. Q Why can the defender not counter, and why do they have no win condition? Removing the counter-threat lets the attacker focus on reading patterns and building setups. The defender succeeds by denying openings and forcing the work. Q What is the cycle that should precede every committed shot? Probe, read, setup, commit. Probes gather info; the target is the goal. Intent separates the two, not the technique itself.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Probe to read, identify the protected zone, set up combinations to expose the target, then commit and land it.

🛡️Defender

Defend all zones naturally; use every defensive solution; make the attacker work; never counter.

Coaching cues

👁️Probe → read → setup → commit

"What happens to their guard when you go to the body? What opens?" Build the cycle instead of mindless combinations.

🎯Intent, not technique

A jab can be the target if that's what you set out to land. The distinction is intent, probes gather info, the target is the goal.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Predetermined target→Develops intentional, strategic offense, not volume Defender can't counter (L1–2)→Explore setup patterns without counter-threat distraction Must land a specific shot to win→Forces reading defense and creating openings Defender protects all three zones→Requires misdirection, not just power Open space, striking range→Full movement and angle creation available

Implements the Co-Adaptation Principle (Renshaw et al., 2019), constraining the defender shapes what affordances the attacker perceives and explores.

What the attacker reads

👁️Visual

Guard position, reactions to probes, weight distribution, eye focus → which zones are protected and what opens.

✋Haptic

Contact on setup strikes → confirms defensive commitment.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own distance and angle → when in range for the target shot.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the committing shot lands on the opening the probe created, track target-landed vs. attempts, and whether each landing followed a genuine setup (read → open → commit) rather than a repeated guess. That coupling of opening-read to committed strike is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, intentional offense has formed.

Representativeness

Models: sustained offensive striking with a target in mind, setting up the finish, attacking the body to slow them, chopping the lead leg.

Simplified: no counter-threat (L1–2)single targetrole switch on success

Pattern recognition and misdirection transfer directly into Touch and Don't Get Touched and live sparring.

Readiness to progress

  • Lands varied targets vs. varied defenders
  • Uses setups, not just the target shot repeatedly
  • Can explain "I went to X to open Y"
  • Shows probe → read → setup → commit
  • Success not dependent on one defender

Warning signs

Just throws the target shot repeatedly Can't verbalize the setup logic Only succeeds vs. one defender Frustrated when it doesn't land fast

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate, controlled contact 🛑 Stop on excessive force or lost composure 🔁 Reset if attacker throws volume or defender counters

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Three Zones · Confidence Rating Follow-on→Touch and Don't Get Touched Related→Misdirection Principle · Full MMA Expression

docs.title: Lead Hand Offense
docs.text:
Skill Isolation · StrikingLead Hand Offense Skill IsolationStrikingOffensiveBeginnerAccess

Develop the lead hand as a weapon. The attacker may only use the lead hand, jab, lead hook, lead uppercut, forcing variety and timing within the constraint while the defender resists without countering.

StartTwo fighters at striking range inside a marked perimeter; attacker restricted to the lead hand. → The GoalAttacker opens the defense to land a clean lead hand strike; defender defends without countering. → FinishLand clean → switch · Whiffed or blocked → reset · Defender has no win condition (diagnostic only).

One hand, endless problems, the lead hand isn't just a jab.

Locking the attacker to a single tool forces timing, feints, and target variety to emerge. Creativity grows inside the constraint.

What to Read

Attune to the defender's guard reactions to your lead-hand probes & feints, how their shell shifts when you threaten a zone, together with the rate of expansion (τ) of your committing jab or lead hook toward the opening at center mass. The opening you read isn't where the guard is; it's where it goes when you make it move.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off in a neutral fighting stance. RangeLead-hand range, with room to create angles. BoundaryA marked perimeter. Both stay inside it. RolesOne attacker, one defender, switch on a clean lead hand strike. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset to neutral after each exchange.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to land a clean lead hand strike, jab, lead hook, or lead uppercut, by reading the defense and creating openings.

Lead hand only. Find variety within the constraint: vary targets head/body, feint, double up, change rhythm. The lead hand isn't just a jab. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to defend all lead hand attacks effectively. No countering.

Defend naturally with the full toolkit (block, parry, slip, move). You have no win condition, your success is making the attacker work for every opening.

The Rules

🥊 Lead hand onlyThe attacker uses only the lead hand, jab, lead hook, lead uppercut. No rear hand, no kicks. This forces creativity and timing within a single tool. 🚫 Defender cannot counterAt Level 1 the defender plays pure defense. Removing the counter-threat lets the attacker focus entirely on reading patterns and building openings. 🎯 All targets allowedHead and body are both fair game. Target variation invites level changes and misdirection within the lead-hand constraint. 📏 Maintain striking rangeBoth players hold striking range so the lead hand stays viable and necessary, no drifting out to where it can't reach. ⏱️ Reset after each attackReset to a neutral stance after a clean strike lands or after a set time, so each rep begins from the same problem.

How to Win

Switch Land a clean lead hand strike → switch roles.A clean landing means solid contact reaching the target with real energy transfer, not a whiff, graze, or blocked shot. Land it and you become the defender. Reset Whiffed, grazed, or blocked → reset, read again.A failed attempt isn't a loss; it's information, same roles. Reset to a neutral stance and re-enter the probe → read → setup → commit cycle. No score Defender denies every opening → diagnostic success.The defender has no win condition. Success is measured by quality of defense, not points. Forcing the attacker to read, set up, and commit is the goal.

The Levels

1Defense onlyDefender can't counter.Pure offensive development, lead hand only against a defender who can't counter. Build timing and variety with zero counter-threat. 2Add counter threatCounters after good defense.The defender can fire counters once they defend successfully. You land the lead hand while managing the counter risk. 3Full MMA expressionCounters plus shot / clinch.The defender can counter-strike, shoot, or clinch. Land the lead hand under realistic MMA pressure with all threats live. See Full MMA Expression.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What opening are you reading off your lead-hand probes? Not where the guard is, but where it goes when you threaten a zone. Read the shell's reactions to probes and feints, then the τ of your committing jab or lead hook. Q Why lock the attacker to a single tool? The constraint forces timing, feints, and target variety to emerge: the lead hand isn't just a jab. Creativity grows inside the limit. Q What does variety within the lead hand actually look like? Jab head, jab body, lead hook, lead uppercut, feint any of them, double or triple up, change rhythm. Different tools and levels, one hand.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Find openings with the lead hand; vary targets head/body; vary timing with feints, doubles, rhythm changes; create opportunities through movement and misdirection.

🛡️Defender

Defend all lead hand attacks with appropriate solutions (parry, slip, block); provide realistic resistance; never counter at Level 1.

Coaching cues

👁️"What opening did you see?"

Ask after each rep. This develops perception of defensive gaps rather than throwing blindly.

🎯The lead hand isn't just a jab

Jab head, jab body, lead hook, lead uppercut, feint any of them, double or triple up. The constraint forces creativity within limited tools.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Lead hand only→Forces exploration of lead hand variety Defender can't counter (L1)→Focus on offense without defensive concern All lead-hand tools available→Invites discovery of jab, hook, uppercut options Target variation allowed→Encourages level changes and misdirection Striking range maintained→Lead hand is viable and necessary

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), different fighters develop different lead hand preferences based on reach, speed, and stance.

What the attacker reads

👁️Visual

Guard position, weight distribution, gaps, reaction patterns → target selection and when to feint vs. commit.

✋Haptic

Contact quality → whether the strike landed clean.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance and range → strike selection and power generation.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the committing lead-hand shot lands on the opening a probe created, track target-landed vs. attempts, and whether each landing followed a genuine read → open → commit cycle rather than a repeated guess. That coupling of opening-read to committed strike is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, intentional lead-hand offense has formed.

Representativeness

Models: using the lead hand to establish range, set up combinations, and find openings, the most frequently used tool in striking.

Simplified: lead hand onlyno counter (L1)role switch on success

Timing and target selection transfer directly into Land the Target, where all weapons integrate.

Readiness to progress

  • Lands lead hand consistently (~50%+)
  • Uses 3+ tools (jab, hook, uppercut)
  • Varies targets without prompting
  • Creates openings through feints or timing
  • Composed under counter threat (L2)

Warning signs

Only throws the jab Only targets the head Timing is predictable Freezes under counter threat

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate, controlled contact 🛑 Stop on excessive force or lost composure 🔁 Reset if attacker uses non-lead-hand weapons

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is foundational Follow-on→Land the Target (integrates all weapons) Related→Three Zones of Attack

docs.title: Lead Leg Offense
docs.text:
Skill Isolation · StrikingLead Leg Offense Skill IsolationStrikingOffensiveBeginnerAccess

Develop the lead leg as a weapon. The attacker may only use the lead leg, teep, lead round kick, lead side kick, forcing range management and variety with the fastest kicking tool while the defender resists without countering.

StartTwo fighters at kicking range inside a marked perimeter; attacker restricted to the lead leg. → The GoalAttacker opens the defense to land a clean lead leg strike; defender defends without countering. → FinishLand clean → switch · Whiffed or blocked → reset · Defender has no win condition (diagnostic only).

The fastest weapon you own, if you master the range.

Teep, round kick, side kick from the lead leg control distance and rhythm at low commitment. Every tool lives at its own range.

What to Read

Attune to the defender's guard & stance reactions to your lead-leg probes & feints, how their shell and weight shift when you threaten a level, together with the rate of expansion (τ) of your committing teep or round kick toward the opening at center mass. The opening you read isn't where the guard is; it's where it goes when you make it move.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off in a neutral fighting stance. RangeKicking range, with room to adjust distance. BoundaryA marked perimeter. Both stay inside it. RolesOne attacker, one defender, switch on a clean lead leg strike. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset to neutral after each exchange.

The Matchup

🦵 Attacker

Trying to land a clean lead leg strike, teep, lead round kick, or lead side kick, by reading the defense and managing range.

Lead leg only. The fastest kicking weapon, find variety across levels: teep body/face, round kick leg/body/head, side kick. Manage distance for each tool. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to defend all lead leg attacks effectively. No countering.

Defend naturally with the full toolkit (check, catch, evade, move). You have no win condition, your success is making the attacker work for every opening.

The Rules

🦵 Lead leg onlyThe attacker uses only the lead leg, teep, lead round kick, lead side kick. No hands, no rear leg. This forces range management and variety with the fastest kicking tool. 🚫 Defender cannot counterAt Level 1 the defender plays pure defense. Removing the counter-threat lets the attacker focus entirely on range and timing. 🎯 All targets allowedHead, body, and legs are all fair game. Multiple target levels invite level variation within the lead-leg constraint. 📏 Maintain kicking rangeBoth players hold kicking range so the lead leg stays viable and effective, closer for round kicks, further for teeps. ⏱️ Reset after each attackReset to a neutral stance after a clean strike lands or after a set time, so each rep begins from the same problem.

How to Win

Switch Land a clean lead leg strike → switch roles.A clean landing means solid contact reaching the target with real energy transfer, not a whiff, graze, or checked kick. Land it and you become the defender. Reset Whiffed, grazed, or defended → reset, read again.A failed attempt isn't a loss; it's information, same roles. Reset to a neutral stance and re-enter the probe → read → setup → commit cycle. No score Defender denies every opening → diagnostic success.The defender has no win condition. Success is measured by quality of defense, not points. Forcing the attacker to read, set up, and commit is the goal.

The Levels

1Defense onlyDefender can't counter.Pure offensive development, lead leg only against a defender who can't counter. Build timing and range with zero counter-threat. 2Add counter threatCounters after good defense.The defender can fire counters once they defend successfully. You land kicks while managing the counter risk. 3Full MMA expressionCounters plus shot / clinch.The defender can counter-strike, shoot, or clinch. Land the lead leg under realistic MMA pressure with all threats live. See Full MMA Expression.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What opening are you reading off your lead-leg probes? Not where the guard is, but where it goes when you threaten a level. Read the shell and weight shift, then the τ of your committing teep or round kick. Q Why does the lead leg demand range management more than the lead hand? Each tool lives at its own range: closer for round kicks, further for teeps. Wrong distance and the kick whiffs or never reaches. Q Why restrict the attacker to only the lead leg? It forces range management and variety with the fastest, lowest-commitment kicking tool: teep, round kick, side kick across head, body, and legs.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🦵Attacker

Find openings with the lead leg; vary targets head/body/legs; vary kick types teep/round/side; manage distance for effective kicking.

🛡️Defender

Defend all lead leg attacks with appropriate solutions (check, catch, evade); provide realistic resistance; never counter at Level 1.

Coaching cues

👁️"What range were you at?"

Ask after each rep. This develops perception of distance management for different lead leg tools.

🎯The fastest kicking weapon

Teep body, teep face, lead round kick leg/body/head, lead side kick, feint any of them. Quick, non-committal kicking offense.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Lead leg only→Forces exploration of quick, non-committal kicks Defender can't counter (L1)→Focus on range and timing All lead-leg tools available→Invites discovery of teep, round kick, side kick options Multiple target levels allowed→Encourages level variation (head, body, leg) Kicking range maintained→Lead leg is viable and effective

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), different fighters develop different lead leg preferences based on hip flexibility, stance width, and balance.

What the attacker reads

👁️Visual

Distance, stance/weight distribution, guard position, reaction timing → kick selection, target availability, when to throw vs. feint.

✋Haptic

Contact quality → whether the kick landed clean.

🧭Proprioceptive

Hip position and balance, range to target → kick execution, recovery, and selection.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the committing lead-leg kick lands on the opening a probe created, track target-landed vs. attempts, and whether each landing followed a genuine read → open → commit cycle rather than a repeated guess. That coupling of opening-read to committed kick is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, intentional lead-leg offense has formed.

Representativeness

Models: using the lead leg to control distance, disrupt rhythm, and find openings, the quickest kicking tool for range management.

Simplified: lead leg onlyno counter (L1)role switch on success

Range management and quick-fire skills transfer directly into Land the Target, where all weapons integrate.

Readiness to progress

  • Lands lead leg consistently (~50%+)
  • Uses 3+ tools (teep, round, side kick)
  • Varies targets without prompting
  • Maintains balance after kicking
  • Manages distance to set up kicks

Warning signs

Only throws teeps Only targets the body Loses balance after kicks Range is consistently wrong

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate, controlled kicks 🛑 Stop on excessive force or lost composure 🔁 Reset if attacker uses non-lead-leg weapons

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is foundational Follow-on→Land the Target (integrates all weapons) Related→Three Zones of Attack

docs.title: Leg Reclaim
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingLeg Reclaim GroundGrapplingDefensiveIntermediateCounter

Get your legs back between you and the opponent, one level at a time.

StartBottom compromised with legs NOT between the players, one on top, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom gets legs or knees between; top prevents the recovery. → FinishBottom reaches the next level of separation → reset & continue · Top prevents recovery → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Don't chase the full escape, reclaim one level at a time.

Get a knee or foot back between you and them. Each level wins back a little control.

What to Read

Attune to the felt weight distribution & pressure through contact, where the top's base loads, which knee or hip is pinned, where the connection goes light enough to insert a knee, plus the top player's hip and frame motion. That inertial information specifies where the next level of separation opens before the window closes, not the opponent's limbs in isolation.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, on the mat, one on bottom, one on top. PositionCompromised bottom, mounted or side control, legs NOT between the players, set by the level. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne bottom (defender), one top (controller), roles hold. Start & resetBegin compromised; reset at the new level on a bottom win, same position on a top win.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to get legs or knees between self and opponent, incremental recovery, not full escape.

Create frames to make space, hip escape to recover a leg, and use the knee-elbow connection. Progress one level: mount → half → full → feet on hips → distance. VS 🪨 Top (Controller)

Trying to maintain control and prevent leg recovery.

Actively control, not just sit in position. Kill the hips, control the legs and knees, deny effective frames, and advance if the bottom creates an opening.

The Rules

🦵 Bottom prioritizes leg recoveryThe bottom player prioritizes getting feet or knees between the players, not full escape or submission. This develops incremental recovery. 🪨 Top must actively controlThe top player must actively control, not just sit in position. This creates realistic resistance to recover against. ✅ Any leg recovery countsHalf guard, full guard, or feet on hips all count as a recovered level. Multiple success paths keep the focus on separation, not a single technique. 📈 One level at a timeFocus is position, not escape or submission, progress is measured by reaching the next level of leg separation, reset, and continue. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses instantly, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Advance Bottom reaches the next level of separation → reset there & continue.Half guard, full guard, or feet on hips reached (the level defined at the start), the bottom advances one level and the game continues from there. Win Top denies recovery for the duration → top wins.Deny the recovery for the set duration, or advance to a more dominant position, the top wins the rep. Reset Top wins → reset, same position, same roles.The rep ends and resets from the same compromised position, keeping the same roles. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → loss.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the rep instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Mount to HalfStart in mount; bottom works to half guard.Begin in mount. Bottom works to half guard, focus on basic hip escape and knee recovery. 2Side to GuardStart in side control; bottom works to any guard.Begin in side control. Bottom works to any guard, focus on frames, hip escape, and leg recovery. 3Guard to Feet on HipsStart in closed guard; bottom works to feet on hips.Begin in closed guard. Bottom works to feet on hips (open guard), focus on creating distance with the legs. 4Full MMA ExpressionTop can strike from position.Top can strike from position; bottom must time recovery around the ground-and-pound. Leg reclaim under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Don't chase the full escape. What is the actual goal? Get a knee or foot back between you and them, one level at a time. Each level wins back a little control: trapped, half, full, feet on hips, distance. Q What information shows you where the next level opens? The felt weight and pressure through contact: where the top's base loads, which knee or hip is pinned, where the connection goes light enough to insert a knee, plus the top's hip and frame motion. Q Why does any leg recovery count as a win? Multiple success paths keep the focus on separation, not a single technique. Half guard, full guard, or feet on hips all count, so you progress incrementally. Q What is the key mechanic the whole game turns on? Getting the knee inside before the top re-pins, driven by an efficient hip escape. If the knee never gets inside, recovery stalls.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Create frames to make space; hip escape to recover a leg; use the knee-elbow connection; progress mount → half → full → feet on hips → distance.

🪨Top (Controller)

Kill the hips; control legs and knees; deny effective frames; advance if the bottom creates an opening.

Coaching cues

📈Next level only

Ask: "What's the next level of separation you can achieve?" This develops incremental thinking rather than chasing a full escape at once.

🦵Knee inside

Ask: "Can you get your knee inside?" Knee recovery is the key mechanic for leg reclaim.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Goal is leg recovery (not full escape)→Develops incremental recovery Any leg recovery counts→Multiple success paths Top actively controls→Creates realistic resistance Progressive starting positions→Scaffolded recovery levels (mount → side → guard) Various compromised positions→Full recovery vocabulary

Develops incremental recovery perception, recognizing opportunities to progress one level at a time rather than seeking full escape immediately (Renshaw et al., 2019). Athletes learn the recovery hierarchy: trapped → half → full → open → distance.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Top's weight distribution & knee/leg control → hip escape direction and recovery opportunity.

👁️Visual

Space for knee insertion → recovery timing.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hip mobility & knee-elbow connection → escape method and guard recovery.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom recovers each level of leg separation faster than the top can re-load and kill the hips, track levels reclaimed vs. recovery denied, and whether the knee gets inside before the top re-pins. That separation-recovery relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: recovering guard from bad positions, the progressive skill of getting legs back between you and the opponent.

Simplified: leg recovery focus, not escapeclear level progressionwin = next level achieved

Recovery-opportunity perception and the knee-elbow connection are identical to competition, feeds Ground to Standing and Positional Sparring.

Readiness to progress

  • Achieves the target recovery consistently
  • Hip escapes are efficient
  • Knee-elbow connection is automatic
  • Frame creation is effective

Warning signs

Can't hip escape Gets flattened repeatedly Only recovers from one position Knee never gets inside

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled pressure, no striking (except Level 4) 🛑 Stop on neck cranks or knee pressure 🔁 Reset if completely stalled

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Escape Follow-on→Ground to Standing · Positional Sparring · Ground Defensive Submission Related→Decision States · Guard Recovery

docs.title: Mount Escape
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingMount Escape GroundGrapplingDefensiveIntermediateCounter

Get out from under mount before the top isolates an arm.

StartBottom flat under mount, top settled, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom frames, makes hip space, and recovers; top stays mounted and hunts an isolated arm. → FinishKnee back in (half guard) or better, reverse, or stand → bottom · Isolate an arm or hold mount the full count → top · Out of bounds → loss.

You don't escape the weight, you escape the space it leaves.

Frame to make room, bridge into the light side. Every time the top reaches, the base lightens.

What to Read

Attune to the felt load through the top's hips and hands, the moment the base lifts as they posture, climb, or reach for your arm. That weight shift specifies which way to bridge and when the hip space opens, not the top's eyes or a memorized sequence. When they grab high, the base goes light low.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom (defender), one top (mounted). PositionBottom flat on the back, top in a settled mount, hips low. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesBottom frames and escapes; top maintains mount and hunts an isolated arm. Start & resetBegin from settled mount; reset on escape, arm isolation, or the count.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to frame, make hip space, and recover a guard (half guard or better), a reversal, or standing.

Keep elbows tight to deny the arm. Bridge into the side the top loads, then shrimp into the space. Protect the arms first, then escape, panic exposes the arm the top wants. Recovery is graded: the more leg you get back between you, the cleaner the escape. VS 🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to keep mount and pull one of the bottom's arms away from the body.

Ride the bridge, stay heavy on the hips, climb when threatened. Bait a frame, then attack the arm it leaves. Control is proven by isolating an arm, not just sitting on top.

The Rules

🤜 Bottom frames and escapes, no subsThe bottom works frames, bridges, and hip escapes continuously. No submissions from the bottom, the task is the escape, not the counter-attack. 🎯 Top wins by isolating an armThe top proves control by pulling one of the bottom's arms clear of the body (the entry every mounted submission shares), not by sitting still. This gives the top an active, observable target instead of a stall. ⏱️ Hold the count or escapeIf the top keeps mount for the set count (start at 20 seconds) without isolating an arm, the round resets. If the bottom recovers guard, reverses, or stands first, the bottom wins. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are control only, so the bottom can read weight and build frames without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Use striking as a disincentivization tool, the cost that punishes stalling and lazy structure. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Switch Bottom gets a knee back between (half guard) or better, reverses, or stands → bottom wins, switch roles.Recovery is graded by how much leg you reinsert: one knee in (half guard) is the floor, then both knees, then both feet, then a closed loop (closed guard), each a cleaner escape. A reversal to top or standing up is a full escape. See Guard Recovery. Win Top isolates an arm clear of the body → top wins.One of the bottom's arms pulled away from the torso and controlled, the shared entry to armbar, arm-triangle, and back-take. The observable proof that mount control has beaten the frames. Reset Top holds mount the full count, no arm → reset, same roles.The top kept the position but never isolated an arm before the count expired. The round resets from settled mount for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Two hands, one hipBoth hands frame one hip.Bottom starts with both hands framing one of the top's hips and must hold that frame while the top works to pull an arm free. Isolates the single strongest frame and the elbow-tight habit that denies the arm. 2One hand each hipSplit the frames.A hand on each of the top's hips. Now the bottom must keep both elbows tight at once and pick which side to bridge. Reading the loaded side becomes the task. 3Frames of choice, hands to matForce the base down.Bottom uses any frames and adds a goal: force the top to put a hand on the mat to keep balance. A posted hand is a committed base, the first crack in the mount. 4Attack the postsOff-balance the committed base.Once the top posts, the bottom bridges hard into that post to break the base and start the recovery. The bridge-into-the-light-side mechanic is now the focus. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Continuous from settled mount until the bottom gets a knee or hips between the players, with light strikes on. The top's strike threat makes hesitation costly, escape under real urgency.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What weight shift tells you the hip space has opened? When the top postures, climbs, or reaches high for your arm, the base lifts and goes light low. Grab high, base light low. Bridge into the side they loaded. Q Why does the top win by isolating an arm rather than just holding? An isolated arm is the shared entry to armbar, arm-triangle, and the back-take, and it is observable. It gives the top an active target instead of a stall, and it keeps the bottom's elbows honest. Q What is the bottom's first job, before escaping? Protect the arms, elbows tight. Panic and flailing expose the exact arm the top is hunting. Frame first, make space, then move. Q What does forcing the top to post a hand actually buy you? A posted hand is a committed base. Bridge hard into that post and the top has no frame to catch the fall, the mount breaks and the recovery starts.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Keep elbows tight, frame the hips, read the loaded side, bridge into it, shrimp into the space, chain the next frame when one fails.

🥋Top (Attacker)

Stay heavy on the hips, ride the bridge, climb when threatened, bait a frame and attack the arm it leaves.

Coaching cues

⚓Where is the base?

Ask the bottom: "When did the base go light?" Direct attention to the load shift, not to a named technique.

🫷Elbows or arm?

Ask the top: "Did you get the arm, or just sit?" Keeps the top hunting the isolated arm instead of stalling for the count.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Frame restricted per level (one hip → split → posts)→Isolates one frame and one read at a time Top wins by isolating an arm→Keeps the bottom's elbows tight, gives the top an active target Hold the count or escape→Urgency for the bottom, no stalling for the top No strikes until the top level→Frees the bottom to read weight before adding strike defense Live, resisting top→Keeps the weight-reading perception intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the frame ladder isolates one escape variable per level while the bottom keeps reading load and timing from a real, resisting opponent. The top's arm-isolation win keeps the representativeness, this is the actual danger of mount.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Load through the top's hips and hands → which side is heavy, when the base lifts, which way to bridge.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own elbow and hip position → whether the arm is safe and whether hip space exists yet.

👁️Visual

Top posturing or reaching high → the timing window to bridge into the lightened base.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom re-frames and opens hip space faster than the top can isolate an arm or re-settle weight. Track escapes recovered vs. arms isolated, and whether the elbows stay tight as the top climbs. The frame-and-bridge versus settle-and-isolate race is the order parameter; when the bottom consistently times the bridge to the lightened base, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: being mounted and recovering before an arm is isolated or strikes accumulate, the exact problem under mount in competition and in MMA.

Simplified: frame ladderno strikes L1-4reset on the count

Deepens the mount level of Ground Escape; the weight-reading transfers into Leg Reclaim and Ground to Standing.

Readiness to progress

  • Keeps both elbows tight under pressure
  • Bridges into the loaded side, not away from it
  • Forces the top to post, then attacks the post
  • Chains a second frame when the first fails

Warning signs

Arms drift away from the body Bridges straight up, not to a side Waits and survives instead of framing Panics when the first escape fails

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Stop on submission attempts or neck cranks 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Escape Follow-on→Leg Reclaim · Ground to Standing Related→Decision States · Guard Recovery

docs.title: Mount Maintenance
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingMount Maintenance

Provisional (WIP): validated in class 2026-06-15, pending final coach sign-off

Taught and drilled live on 2026-06-15 as the top-side mirror of Mount Escape. The win condition is the TKO Pin, control plus real strikes, not a grappling hold. Awaiting a final pass before the WIP tag comes off.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediateControl → Finish

Keep the mount and turn control into damage before the bottom gets an elbow back in.

StartTop settled in mount, shins under the bottom's arms, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop stays heavy, climbs, and frees a hand to strike; bottom fights both elbows back inside. → FinishTKO pin or an isolated arm → top · Both elbows back in (half guard) or reverse → bottom · Out of bounds → loss.

Control isn't sitting on someone, it's the strike they can't stop.

Hold three points, free the fourth to hit. If you're only holding, you're not winning, you're waiting.

What to Read

Attune to the felt load through your own hips and the bottom's elbows, the moment their frame opens or their base bumps to bridge. That shift specifies when a hand is free to strike and which arm is exposed to isolate, not a memorized mount sequence. Hold with three points, hit with the fourth, and re-settle the instant the bump comes.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top (attacker, mounted), one bottom (defender). PositionTop settled in mount, hips low, shins under the bottom's arms so the elbows are accessible. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesTop maintains mount and works to a TKO pin or arm isolation; bottom fights both elbows back in or reverses. Start & resetBegin from settled mount; reset on a TKO pin, arm isolation, escape, or the count.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to keep mount, climb the hips, and free a hand to land real strikes (a TKO pin), or pull an arm clear to a submission entry.

Stay heavy and proactive, a step ahead, don't wait for the bump. Control one side and pin the arms high so they can't post or bump. Trade one point of control for a strike, then re-settle. Climb to a higher mount as the base lightens. VS 🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to get both elbows back inside the knees (half guard or better), reverse, or stand.

Wait for the top to lift weight to strike, then spend energy on the bump. Keep elbows tight to deny the isolate, frame and shrimp into the space the strike leaves.

The Rules

🎯 Top wins by a TKO pin, not by sittingThe top proves control by immobilizing the bottom and landing real, unanswered strikes (the TKO Pin), or by pulling an arm clear to a submission entry. Holding mount without a finish threat is a stall, not a win. 🤜 Bottom wins on both elbows back inThe bottom's task is to recover both elbows inside the knee line (the floor is half guard) or reverse. A clean, observable bottom goal that mirrors Mount Escape. ⛰️ Stay topside, no dropping to the legsThe top may not bail back to the legs to stall. Maintain top control, mount or a topside advance, the whole round. This keeps the position honest as a dominant pin, not a scramble reset. ⏱️ Hold the count or finishIf the top keeps mount for the set count (start at 20 seconds) without a TKO pin or an isolated arm, the round resets. If the bottom recovers or reverses first, the bottom wins. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🥊 GnP realism, not pitter-patterOnce strikes are on, throw realistic strikes with space behind them, not a flurry of light taps and not bashing. A real strike needs room, so it doubles as a negative stimulus that makes the bottom move, and it trains the actual control-to-strike oscillation. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionThe coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Strikes are the disincentivization tool that punishes a lazy bottom structure. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Win Top lands a TKO pin (control + real unanswered strikes), or isolates an arm → top wins.A TKO pin is control of the hips and shoulders with one hand free to land effective strikes, four or five unanswered. An isolated arm pulled clear of the body is the shared entry to armbar, arm-triangle, and the back-take. Either is the observable proof that mount control beat the frames. See TKO Pin. Switch Bottom gets both elbows back inside (half guard) or reverses → bottom wins, switch roles.Both elbows recovered inside the knee line is the floor (half guard); a reversal to top or standing is a full escape. Mirrors the win in Mount Escape. Reset Top holds mount the full count, no TKO pin or arm → reset, same roles.The top kept the position but never freed a hand to strike or isolated an arm before the count expired. Resets from settled mount for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position.

The Levels

1Settle and holdShins under the arms, deny the elbows.Top settles low in mount with shins under the bottom's arms and simply denies the elbows coming back inside. Control only, no strikes. Builds the heavy, balanced base that everything else rests on. 2Win one side, arms highPin a wrist, get the arms up.Top controls one side and works the bottom's hands and elbows up toward the head, the higher the arms, the harder it is to bump. Reading which side is loaded becomes the task. 3Arms overhead startBoth arms isolated above the head.Start with both of the bottom's arms already pinned above the head (the class progression). This is the armbar and arm-triangle entry, the top maintains the isolation while the bottom fights the hands back down. The compromised start makes the bottom's task explicit. 4TKO pin windowsFree a hand, strike, re-settle.Top oscillates: hold with three points, free the fourth for one realistic strike, then re-settle before the bump. GnP realism, real strikes with space, not pitter-patter. The control-to-strike trade becomes the skill. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Continuous from settled mount, strikes live, until the top lands a TKO pin or isolates an arm, or the bottom recovers. The bottom's bridges are now urgent, the top must stay proactive and a step ahead.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why does the top win by a TKO pin instead of just holding mount? Holding is a stall. A TKO pin is control plus a hand free to land real strikes, the observable proof that the position is doing work. It also keeps the top oscillating between control and damage instead of sitting. Q Why pin the bottom's arms high toward the head? The higher the arms, the less leverage the bottom has to bump and bridge, and the closer they are to an arm isolation. High arms = a quiet base and a live submission entry. Q What is the cost of striking, and how do you manage it? Every strike frees a point of control, giving the bottom something to bump. Hold three points, strike with the fourth, and re-settle the instant the bump comes. Don't trade all your control for one shot. Q When is the bottom most dangerous, and what should the top do about it? When the top lifts weight to strike. Be proactive, a step ahead, control the arms first so the strike doesn't hand the bottom a free bump.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Attacker)

Stay heavy and low, pin the arms high, climb as the base lightens, free one hand to strike then re-settle, hunt the isolated arm when the frame opens.

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Keep elbows tight, wait for the weight to lift, then bump with everything, frame and shrimp into the space the strike leaves.

Coaching cues

🏃Proactive, not reactive

Ask the top: "Were you a step ahead, or waiting for the bump?" A reactive top gets tossed; a proactive top controls the arms before the escape starts.

⚖️Holding or hitting?

Ask the top: "Did you free a hand, or just sit?" Keeps the top oscillating into the TKO pin instead of stalling for the count.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top wins by a TKO pin (control + strikes)→Forces the control-to-strike oscillation, no stalling Arms-high / arms-overhead levels→Isolates the bump-denial and the submission entry Stay topside, no dropping to the legs→Keeps the position an honest dominant pin Hold the count or finish→Urgency for the top, a real escape window for the bottom GnP realism, strikes with space→A real negative stimulus that drives the bottom to move

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the arms ladder isolates one control variable per level while the top keeps reading the bottom's load and frame from a live, resisting opponent. The TKO-pin win keeps the representativeness, mount only matters in MMA if it produces damage.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

Load through your hips and the bottom's elbows → which side is heavy, when a hand is free, when the bump is coming.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own base and weight → how much control you can trade for a strike without losing the mount.

👁️Visual

An exposed elbow or an opening frame → the arm to isolate and the timing to climb.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top frees a hand to strike or isolates an arm faster than the bottom can recover an elbow or bridge out. Track TKO pins and arm isolations vs. escapes, and whether the top re-settles after each strike instead of losing the mount. The strike-and-re-settle versus bump-and-recover race is the order parameter; when the top reliably converts control into damage without losing position, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: holding mount and turning it into damage before the bottom recovers an elbow, the exact problem on top in MMA.

Simplified: arms ladderno strikes L1-3reset on the count

Mirror of Mount Escape; deepens the top side of Ground Control; the TKO-pin oscillation transfers to Wall Grinding.

Readiness to progress

  • Stays heavy and proactive, a step ahead of the bump
  • Pins the arms high to kill the bridge
  • Frees a hand to strike, then re-settles without losing mount
  • Climbs to a higher mount as the base lightens

Warning signs

Sits and holds instead of threatening a finish Trades all control for one strike, then loses mount Reacts to the bump instead of pre-empting it Bails to the legs when pressured

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling, GnP by coach permission 🛑 Stop on submission attempts or neck cranks 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Control · Mount Escape Follow-on→Ground-and-Pound Defense Related→TKO Pin · Decision States

docs.title: Mount Submission Hunt
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingMount Submission Hunt

Provisional (WIP): invented in class 2026-06-20, pending final coach sign-off

Built on the mat on 2026-06-20 as the finishing layer of the mount ladder (Mount Escape → Mount Maintenance → this). It trains converting a held mount into an observable submission entry, the first dedicated submission-offense game in the library. Refined from the class version (back-take and fatigue rules clarified). Awaiting a final pass before the WIP tag comes off.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveAdvancedSubmission Entry

Isolate the finish from mount, one arm over the head or the back sealed, before they frame, recover a leg, and get out.

StartAttacker fully mounted; defender sets their own arm frames first, inside the mat. → The GoalAttacker isolates one arm above the head, or seals the back on top; defender denies the isolation and recovers a leg. → FinishArm over the head, hands locked, 1-2s, or back sealed on top 1-2s → attacker · One leg recovered, reversal, or stand → defender.

Mount is not the finish. It is the doorway to it.

Don't lounge in the mount, hunt the isolation. Show the strike to open the frame, then take the arm over the head. The position you hold is only worth the finish it leads to.

What to Read

Attune to where the defender's frames are loaded, the felt resistance of their arms against your hips and chest. A shown strike makes them cover, and a covering arm that crosses the centerline is the arm to chase over the head. Read the committed frame, not a memorized submission. When the layout shifts, a wobble, a turn, an exposed arm, the finish is suddenly afforded, take it.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one attacker (mounted), one defender (bottom). PositionFull mount. The defender sets their own arm frames before "go", they are not pre-pinned. BoundaryThe mat, out of bounds is a reset. RolesAttacker isolates the finish; defender denies the isolation and recovers a leg. Start & resetMount set, defender frames, then "go"; reset on a finish, a leg recovered, a reversal, or a stand. Keep the rounds short.

The Matchup

🥋 Attacker (Finishing)

Trying to isolate one arm above the defender's head with your hands locked (the arm-triangle / armbar entry), or seal the back on top if they turn.

Ride the hips high. Use a shown strike to make them cover, then trap the arm that crosses the centerline and walk it over the head, bicep to the ear. Don't lounge and hold, mount is only worth the finish. If they turn away, take the back, but you must seal it on top. VS 🛡️ Defender (Denying & recovering)

Trying to keep both arms framed and connected, deny the over-the-head isolation, and get one leg back in to recover guard.

Elbows tight, never let an arm cross your own centerline. Frame the hips, shrimp, and fight one leg back between you, that is your win. A reversal or a stand is a bonus, but the first job is to deny the arm, not to escape clean every time.

The Rules

🎯 Arm over the head, hands locked, 1-2s → attackerOne arm isolated fully above the defender's head with your hands connected, bicep visibly to the ear, the arm-triangle / armbar entry. Held one to two seconds to clear the scramble. If you have it, you have already won, you don't need to crank the finish. 🔄 Back sealed on top, 1-2s → attackerIf the defender turns away, chest-to-back with both hooks in and you on top, held one to two seconds. Your own back on the floor is not a seal yet, the defender can still escape, so it does not count. 🛡️ Defender's first win is deny + recoverKeep both arms in and get one leg back between you, that is guard recovered and the defender wins. This is the reachable goal that keeps the round honest, not a full clean escape every time. ⬆️ Reversal or stand → defender bonusReversing to top or standing fully up is also a defender win, the bigger prize, but recovering one leg is enough. 🥊 Shown strikes onlyLegitimate threat, no damage. An arc between strikes and a semi-legitimate reaction, no clean landing. No elbows or knees without pads. The strike opens the frame, it does not concuss a partner. ⏱️ Short rounds, reset oftenThe finish-hunt is high intensity for both players. Keep the rounds short and reset on any outcome, fatigue is a dial we turn down here so the skill, not the gas tank, is the limit. ⬛ Stay on the matPlay happens inside the mat perimeter. Rolling out of bounds is a reset.

How to Win

Attacker Arm over the head, hands locked, 1-2s, or the back sealed on top 1-2s → attacker wins.One arm fully above the head with your hands connected (bicep to ear), or, if they turned, chest-to-back with both hooks in and you on top. Either held one to two seconds. You don't crank the finish, the entry is the win. Defender Both arms kept in and one leg recovered → defender wins (reversal or stand is a bonus).Deny the over-the-head and fight one leg back to guard. That reachable win is the point, it keeps both players working instead of grinding to exhaustion. A reversal or a stand is the bigger bonus. Loss Attacker reversed or a leg let back in; defender's arm isolated over the head → that player loses.For the attacker, losing the mount to a reversal or a recovered leg. For the defender, letting an arm get walked over the head and locked.

The Levels

1Positional, no strikesHunt the crossing arm.Attacker rides high and hunts one arm over the head; defender frames and keeps elbows tight. No strikes yet. Finds the trap-the-crossing-arm mechanic. 2Add the shown strikeOpen the frame.The shown strike comes on. The threat makes the defender cover, the covering arm crosses the centerline, and that is the arm to isolate. Legitimate threat, no damage. 3Recovery liveDeny vs isolate.The defender's recovery is now a win, one leg back in. The isolation-versus-recovery race becomes the task, and the defender finally has a reachable goal. 4Add the backThe turn is punished.If the defender turns away to escape, the attacker may take and seal the back on top. Turning is no longer free, it costs the back. 5Full expressionShort rounds, all wins live.Shown strikes on, the arm, the back, the recovery, the reversal, all live. Short rounds, reset often, hunt the finish without gassing.

Recall Check
