Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why is holding the mount not the goal? Mount is only worth the finish it leads to. Lounging lets the defender rest and recover. The game rewards converting the position into an observable submission entry, not sitting in it. Q How does a shown strike help you isolate the arm? It makes the defender cover. The covering arm crosses their centerline, and a crossed arm is the one you can trap and walk over the head. The threat shifts the layout and opens the finish. Q Why is "took the back" not a win when your own back is on the floor? Because that is not a stable, dominant position, the defender can still escape. A back-take only scores as an observable seal: both hooks in, you on top, held 1-2s. Q As the defender, why is "deny and recover one leg" the goal instead of a full escape? Against a secured mount, escaping clean every time is unreachable and gasses you out. Denying the arm and getting one leg back to guard is the reachable, winnable goal, it keeps you working instead of grinding to exhaustion.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Attacker (Finishing)

Ride the hips high, show the strike to open the frame, trap the crossing arm and walk it over the head to the bicep-to-ear lock, or seal the back on top if they turn. Hunt, don't lounge.

🛡️Defender (Denying & recovering)

Elbows tight, never cross your own centerline, frame the hips, shrimp, fight one leg back to guard. Deny first, escape second.

Coaching cues

🎯Hunt, don't lounge

Ask the attacker: "Did you threaten a finish, or just sit there?" Mount is the doorway, the arm over the head is the room.

⏱️Short rounds, fatigue is a dial

Both players gas in a long mount battle. Keep the rounds short and reset often so the skill is the limit, not the gas tank.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Win = arm over the head, 1-2s→An observable submission entry, not a memorized or cranked finish Back-take = sealed on top, 1-2s→Rewards a real dominant seal, ends the "did he get it?" disputes Defender wins by deny + recover one leg→A reachable goal that breaks the gas-out stalemate Shown strikes open the frame→The finish is afforded by the threat, the finishing sequence in miniature Defender sets frames before "go"→A representative, fightable start, not a gifted isolation

Implements the Finishing Sequence: the shown strike shifts the position's attractor layout so a new affordance, the crossing arm, opens up. The fighter attunes to the shift and takes the afforded finish, rather than computing a memorized submission. The position is trained as a doorway to the finish, not an end in itself.

What athletes read

✋Haptic

Load through the defender's frames against your hips and chest → which arm is committed, when the frame breaks, where the isolation is open.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hips riding high and base wide → whether you are stable enough to attack an arm without being bridged off.

👁️Visual

A covering arm crossing the centerline, a turn away → the arm to chase over the head, or the back to seal.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the attacker converts a held mount into an observable finish-entry by opening the frame and chasing the crossing arm, rather than holding the mount and waiting. Track finish-entries versus lounging, and whether the defender denies-and-recovers versus gets isolated. When mount reliably becomes a finish entry, the submission-offense skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the real mount finishing sequence, the arm-triangle / armbar / back-take fork, the layer above simply holding the mount. The first dedicated submission-offense game, and the top of the mount ladder.

Finish layer of the mount ladderarm over head or back sealed, 1-2sshort rounds

The finish layer above Mount Maintenance; the offensive answer to Mount Escape; operationalizes the Finishing Sequence.

Readiness to progress

  • Hunts the arm, never just lounges in the mount
  • Uses the shown strike to open the frame
  • Seals the back on top, never claims it from underneath
  • Resets and goes again without gassing out

Warning signs

Lounges in mount, never threatens a finish Calls a brushed arm a finish Claims a back-take with his own back on the floor Defender gasses chasing a full escape instead of recovering one leg

Safety & related games

🥊 Shown strikes only, no elbows or knees without pads 🛑 The entry is the win, no need to crank the finish, tap early 🔁 Short rounds, reset on any outcome

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Mount Maintenance · Mount Escape Concept→Finishing Sequence Related→Back Maintenance · Ground-and-Pound Defense

docs.title: North-South Control
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingNorth-South Control

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

Fills the empty north-south bucket in the top-control grid. Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediateControl → Finish

Pin from north-south, then transition to a side or trap an arm before the bottom brings the knees in.

StartTop chest-to-chest north-south, elbows in the bottom's armpits, the bottom's head at the beltline, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop flattens, kills the knees, then transitions to side control or traps a kimura; bottom brings the knees in and sits up. → FinishTransition and hold 3s, or a kimura trap → top · Knees in, sit up, or reverse → bottom · Out of bounds → loss.

North-south is a doorway, not a room.

Flatten the shoulders, then move to a side or trap the arm. Sit in it too long and the knees come back in.

What to Read

Attune to the bottom's shoulders and the gap to their hips, the moment a shoulder lifts to turn or an arm slides up to frame. That shift specifies which way to transition and when a kimura is there to trap, not a memorized pin. When they bridge to bring the knees in, a side opens.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top (attacker, north-south), one bottom (defender). PositionTop chest-to-chest north-south, elbows in the bottom's armpits, the bottom's head near the top's beltline; bottom on the back. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesTop maintains the pin and transitions or traps an arm; bottom brings the knees in and recovers. Start & resetBegin from settled north-south; reset on a held transition, a kimura, an escape, or the count.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to flatten the bottom, then transition to side control (held 3s) or trap a kimura.

Weight on the chest, elbows in the armpits to kill the frames, head light. When a shoulder lifts, switch to a side; when an arm slides up, trap the kimura. Control is proven by the transition or the trap, not by sitting north-south. VS 🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to bring the knees in, sit up, or reverse.

Frame into the hips, bridge and bring a knee across, sit up as the top's weight shifts. Keep the elbows tight so an arm can't be slid up into a kimura.

The Rules

🎯 Top wins by a transition or a kimuraThe top proves control by transitioning to in-line north-south or side control and holding 3 seconds, or by trapping a kimura (figure-four) on one arm. Sitting in north-south without a threat is a stall, not a win. 🤜 Bottom wins by recoveringThe bottom wins by bringing the knees in to recover guard, sitting up to the front, or reversing. A clean, observable bottom goal. ⏱️ Hold the count or finishIf the top keeps north-south for the set count (start at 20 seconds) without a held transition or a kimura, the round resets. If the bottom recovers first, the bottom wins. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are control only, so both players read weight and frames before strikes are added. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound. Strikes are the disincentivization tool that punishes a passive bottom. Ground games train smashing, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round.

How to Win

Win Top transitions to a side (3s) or traps a kimura → top wins.A clean transition to in-line north-south or side control held three seconds, or a figure-four trapped on one arm. Either is the observable proof that the pin led somewhere. Finish the kimura slow, tap early. Switch Bottom brings the knees in, sits up, or reverses → bottom wins, switch roles.Recovering the knees to guard, sitting up to the front, or reversing is the escape. See Guard Recovery. Reset Top holds north-south the full count, no threat → reset, same roles.The top kept the pin but never transitioned or trapped an arm before the count expired. Resets from settled north-south. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position.

The Levels

1Flatten the pinKill the bridge.Top settles chest-to-chest north-south, weight on the sternum, and simply flattens the bottom so they can't bridge or turn. Builds the heavy pin everything rests on. 2Arms-out pinBury the elbows.Top drives the elbows into the armpits and pins the arms out, killing the bottom's frames. Reading the bottom's shoulder lift becomes the task. 3Transition to a sideWalk the pin around.Top moves from north-south to side control or in-line and holds 3 seconds. Maintaining contact through the walk-around is the focus. 4Kimura trapTrap the sliding arm.When the bottom slides an arm up to frame, the top traps it in a figure-four. The kimura entry that north-south sets up becomes the goal. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Continuous from settled north-south, strikes live, until the top transitions or traps an arm, or the bottom recovers. The strike threat makes the pin costly to sit under.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why is north-south a doorway, not a room? Sit in it too long and the bottom brings the knees back in. It is a transition point, the win is moving to a side or trapping an arm, not holding the pin. Q What opens the transition to a side? A lifted shoulder or the bottom bridging to bring a knee in. When they commit to one direction, walk the pin to the open side. Q What sets up the kimura from here? An arm sliding up to frame. Bury the elbows so the only arm that moves is one you can trap in the figure-four.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Attacker)

Weight on the chest, elbows in the armpits, head light, walk to a side when a shoulder lifts, trap the kimura when an arm slides up.

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Frame into the hips, bridge and bring a knee across, sit up on the weight shift, keep the elbows tight.

Coaching cues

🚪Move or sit?

Ask the top: "Did you transition or trap, or just lie there?" Keeps north-south a doorway to the next thing.

⬇️Elbows buried?

Ask the top: "Are the elbows in the armpits?" Buried elbows kill the frames and feed the kimura.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top wins by a transition or a kimura→Forces north-south to lead somewhere Transition held 3s→A clean, observable proof the move stuck Arms-out pin level→Isolates frame-killing and the kimura entry Hold the count or finish→Urgency for the top, a real window for the bottom

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): each level adds one layer (flatten, bury the arms, transition, trap) while the top reads the bottom's shoulders and frames from a live opponent. The transition-or-kimura win keeps the representativeness, north-south is a passage, not a destination.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

The bottom's bridge and frame pressure → which side opens and which arm is sliding up.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own chest weight and head position → whether the pin is heavy enough to walk around.

👁️Visual

A lifted shoulder, a sliding arm → the transition lane and the kimura trap.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top transitions or traps an arm faster than the bottom brings the knees in. Track transitions and kimuras vs. recoveries, and whether the top keeps the elbows buried through the bottom's frames. The move-or-trap versus knees-in race is the order parameter.

Representativeness

Models: using north-south as a transition hub, moving to a side, attacking a kimura, or holding for strikes before the bottom recovers, the way the position is actually used in MMA and grappling.

Simplified: pin then moveno strikes L1-4reset on the count

Deepens the top side of Ground Control; the transition feeds Side-Control Ride and Mount Maintenance.

Readiness to progress

  • Flattens the bottom and kills the bridge
  • Buries the elbows to deny the frames
  • Walks to a side on a lifted shoulder
  • Traps the kimura when an arm slides up

Warning signs

Sits in north-south without moving Head heavy, gets bridged off Elbows out, the bottom frames in Lets the knees come back in

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling, GnP by coach permission 🛑 Kimura slow, tap early, no cranks 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Control Follow-on→Side-Control Ride · Mount Maintenance Related→TKO Pin · Decision States

docs.title: One Shot Discovery (WIP)
docs.text:
Open Space · StrikingOne Shot Discovery Open SpaceStrikingCombinedIntermediateWIP

Discover where you can land your first shot with different tools, in a perimeter that won't let you run.

StartTwo fighters at striking range inside a tight rectangular perimeter; one on offense, one on defense. → The GoalOffense lands one clean shot off a feint; defense reads the path, neutralizes, and at higher levels lands the counter. → FinishOffense lands → keep offense, switch tool · Defense clears → switch roles · Defense counters (L4+) → switch roles · Both feet out → loss.

One shot, four tools, no exit.

Feints are the work. One correct read beats ten thrown shots.

What to Read

Attune to the moment a feint resolves into a shot, the load through the lead foot, the shoulder turning, the elbow committing. That commitment specifies when the strike arrives and which weapon is loading, not their eyes or rhythm. A feint that shows commitment without the shot is a counter opening, not a threat to defend.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, or three on rotation (top, bottom, out, switch halfway). RangeStriking range, neutral stance. BoundaryA marked rectangular perimeter, kept deliberately tight. Both feet out, you lose. RolesOne offense, one defense; roles switch on a clean defense. Start & resetOffense initiates; reset on land, clean defense, counter, or perimeter loss.

The Matchup

🥊 Offense

Trying to land one clean shot with any single weapon (lead hand, rear hand, lead leg, rear leg), set up by feints.

Feints to head, body, legs, or stance (back-fist show, foot-stomp, level-change feint). Once you land a shot, you may NOT throw the same weapon next, you have to vary the tool for at least one cycle. No combinations. The skill is the menu, not one favourite. VS 🛡️ Defense

Trying to neutralize the shot with the full defensive shell, and at higher levels counter or close to clinch.

Close blocking, parry, head-movement evasion, hand controls always available. At L3+ you may clinch as a defensive escape (at your own peril, you can be hit on the way in). At L4+ you also win by landing a clean counter or connecting under the waist on legs, collected above the knee (no body lock, no dives below the knee).

The Rules

🎯 One shot, four toolsOffense throws exactly one strike per exchange, chosen from lead hand, rear hand, lead leg, or rear leg. No combinations. After a landed shot, the same weapon cannot be thrown again until at least one other has been used. 🎭 Feints allowedFeints to head, body, legs, or stance are the primary setup tool. A feint is an invitation to counter at L4+, the defender can act off the feint without waiting for physical contact. 🛡️ Defensive shell allowedBlock, parry, head movement, footwork inside the perimeter, hand controls. Defense may strike to break up hand-control attempts. 🤼 Grapple-as-defense (L3+)At L3 and above the defender may clinch to negate strikes, at their own peril. Offense does NOT grapple, offense throws strikes only. If offense lands before the clinch closes, offense wins the exchange. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterA marked rectangle, kept deliberately tight. If both feet leave the perimeter, that player loses the exchange regardless of who had the advantage.

How to Win

Stay Offense lands cleanly → keep offense, switch tool.A clean single shot to the target zone. Offense keeps the role for another exchange, but cannot reuse the same weapon until at least one other has been thrown. Switch Defense clears the shot → switch roles.Block, parry, evasion, or grapple-clinch (L3+) that prevents the clean shot. Defender earns the offensive role. Counter Defense lands a counter (L4+) → switch roles.A clean counter strike that lands, or a connection on legs under the waist, above the knee (no body lock, no grabs below the knee). A counter that MISSES is not a defense, defender loses that exchange (you spent the moment without a return). Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.The tight perimeter is the constraint that forces you to solve in the pocket. Stepping out loses the exchange regardless of position.

The Levels

1Pure shellDefense uses shell only.Offense throws single shots with feints. Defense uses block, parry, head movement, and hand controls. No clinch, no counter. Pure defensive-shell calibration in tight space. 2Vary the toolNo-repeat rule.After offense lands, the same weapon cannot be thrown again until at least one other weapon has been used. Forces the menu of four to actually develop, not just one favourite. 3Clinch as defenseGrapple to escape.Defender may clinch (and hand-control) to negate strikes. At your own peril, offense can hit you on the way in. Offense still throws strikes only, never grapples. Perimeter enforcement tightens, both feet out = loss. 4Defense countersLand or lose.Defense also wins by landing a clean counter, OR by connecting under the waist on legs, above the knee (no body lock). The counter must LAND, a missed counter loses the exchange. Feints become counter openings, defender can act on the feint without waiting for the shot. 5Body-kick counterSkill isolation.Defender's counter must be a kick to the body (front kick, round kick, lead or rear leg, switch stance allowed) OR a leg connection. Forces the body-kick into the menu, since open-space games tend to under-represent it.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What role do feints play in this game? Feints draw responses you can exploit on offense. At L4+ they become a counter opening for the defender too, a feint shows commitment without the shot, so defender can act on it without waiting for physical contact. Q Why can't you repeat the weapon you just landed? It forces the menu of four to develop, not just one favourite. Discovery of where each tool works in which moment is the game; one favourite weapon is the failure mode the rule prevents. Q Why must the defender's counter LAND, not just be thrown? A missed strike is exposure without reward. You spent your moment without a return. The rule punishes throwing for free and aligns defense with the same standard as offense, one clean shot or nothing. Q How does the L3 clinch work as defense and not as offense? Clinch closes range to neutralize strikes, that is its job in this game. Offense throws strikes only, the defender's clinch is an escape, not a takedown attack. If offense lands before the clinch closes, offense wins, the clinch is at your own peril.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Offense

Pick the moment with a feint, vary the menu, commit on the read. One shot, four tools, set traps with feints.

🛡️Defense

Defensive shell first, then add tools as levels open. At L4+, treat feints as counter openings, not threats to absorb.

Coaching cues

🎭What feint set up that shot?

Ask offense: "What did you show before the shot landed?" Direct attention to the setup, not the strike itself.

🎯Did you land or just throw?

Ask defense (L4+): "Did your counter land, or did you just throw it?" Keeps the land-or-lose standard honest, no free strikes.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

One-shot constraint→Forces commitment + discovery, not volume Tight perimeter→No retreat, solve in the pocket Tool-variation rule→Develops the four-weapon menu, not a favourite Role switch on success→Both sides develop, no one specialises in one role Counter-must-land (L4+)→Punishes throwing for free, equal standard both sides

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019) and Representative Learning Design, the one-shot + perimeter constraints isolate the read-and-commit problem, while the role switch and L3+ layers add representativeness back in. The skill develops as degeneracy, multiple weapons matched to multiple feints.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether each landed shot is preceded by a feint that drew a defensive response, vs. cold commits. Track the feint-to-land ratio and the variation of weapons used over a round. When commits start to ride on read feints rather than cold timing, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the read-feint-commit cycle that decides exchanges at every level of striking, in a perimeter tight enough to enforce solving rather than escaping.

Simplified: one shottight perimeterrole switch

Builds on Counter-Striking and Land the Target; transfers into Pressure to Clinch when the L3 clinch escape is upgraded into an offensive entry.

Readiness to progress

  • Lands at least 2 different weapons per round
  • Sets up shots with at least one feint
  • At L4+, lands counters from feints, not only from shots
  • Does not chase out of the perimeter

Warning signs

One favourite weapon repeated Cold commits with no feint Counter thrown but not landed Steps out of the perimeter to reset

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on lost composure or unsafe exchange 🔁 Reset if the perimeter is being repeatedly broken

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Counter-Striking · Land the Target Follow-on→Pressure to Clinch · Close-Range Defense Related→Defensive Solutions · Three Zones of Attack

Source: original to Alex Calienes (2026-05-30 class). Marked WIP pending live platform review.

docs.title: Open Guard Pass
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingOpen Guard Pass

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu; shin-surf leg ride after Craig Jones), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class. Details may change.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediatePassing

Clear the feet, beat the knee line, get chest-to-chest.

StartTop standing or kneeling at the bottom's feet; bottom on the back, knees retracted, framing inside the legs. → The GoalTop clears the feet and passes the knee line; bottom off-balances and recovers. → FinishChest-to-chest past the knee line, held 3s → top · Top dumped to hands or seat → bottom · Out of bounds → loss.

The guard is two lines, the feet and the knees.

Beat them in order. A pass that skips a line gets pulled back through it.

What to Read

Attune to the contact of the bottom's feet and shins on your legs and hips. Every connection the bottom makes is a steering wheel; every connection broken is a lane opening. The pass lives where their feet are not, when both feet chase one side, the other side is the pass.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top (passer), one bottom (open guard). PositionTop standing or kneeling at the feet; bottom supine, knees retracted, feet and frames inside. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesTop breaks connections and passes; bottom steers, off-balances, and recovers. Start & resetBegin at the feet; reset on a pass, a dump, or the round cap.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Passer)

Trying to clear the feet, beat the knee line, and land chest-to-chest past the legs.

Strip a connection, then move before the bottom replaces it. Pass on the side the feet abandoned. Heavy hips, low hands: posts get punished. VS 🤸 Bottom (Guard)

Trying to keep feet and frames connected, off-balance the top to hands or seat, and recover line by line.

Feet on hips and biceps steer the top. When their weight commits forward, lift and turn it. Retreat is graded: lose the foot line, rebuild at the knee line, never give both at once.

The Rules

🦶 Lines fall in orderThe pass works feet line first, then knee line, then chest. Jumping the lines into a scramble doesn't count, the held finish does. Order is the pedagogy. 🎯 Pass proven by the holdA pass counts when the top is chest-to-chest past the knee line, held 3 seconds. The hold is the observable proof, not a moment of daylight. ⚖️ Bottom wins by the dumpOff-balancing the top to hands or seat is the bottom's win, fully observable, no judgment call. Sweep mechanics without needing to come up. ⏱️ Round cap, no stallingRun a set round cap (start at 60 seconds). If neither side wins by the cap, reset and switch roles. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are grappling only, so both sides read connections without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Striking is the cost that punishes lazy frames and dead feet. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Win Top reaches chest-to-chest past the knee line, held 3s → top wins.Both lines beaten and the connection held. The 3-second hold separates a pass from a fly-by. Switch Bottom dumps the top to hands or seat, or recovers a closed loop → bottom wins.The top touching hands or seat to the mat is the observable off-balance win. Recovering closed guard around a passer who over-committed counts the same. Reset Round cap expires with the guard alive → reset, switch roles.Neither the pass nor the dump landed before the clock. Switch roles so both sides get the passing problem. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Clear the feetWin the first line only.The round is only about the feet: top works to clear them off hips and biceps, bottom works to keep them connected. Whoever owns the foot line at the bell owns the round. The steering battle, isolated. 2Beat the knee lineInside or outside.Feet cleared, the top now passes the knees, inside lane or around the outside. Bottom retreats line by line and rebuilds. The decision of which lane comes from where the bottom's knees point. 3Shin surfRide the legs down.Top adds the leg ride: shin across the bottom's thigh, weight settling, surfing the legs flat before advancing. Teaches passing as pressure over time, not a sprint. (After Craig Jones.) 4To the chestThe full pass, held.The complete task, grappling only: feet, knees, chest-to-chest held 3 seconds, against a bottom winning by the dump. Both reads at full speed. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Light strikes on. The bottom's feet now also manage strike range, and the top pays for floating hands. Pass under fire.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are the two lines, and why does order matter? The feet line, then the knee line. A pass that skips a line gets pulled back through it, the bottom's abandoned connections re-attach behind you. Q Where does the pass lane open? Where the feet are not. When both of the bottom's feet chase one side, the opposite side is the pass. Connections broken are lanes opened. Q What is the bottom's observable win? Dumping the top to hands or seat. The sweep mechanic without needing to follow up, the moment the top's weight commits past their base, lift and turn it. Q What does the shin surf teach that a speed pass doesn't? Passing as pressure over time. The leg ride settles weight through the bottom's thighs until the recovery dies, instead of racing the recovery and losing.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Passer)

Strip connections, pass on the abandoned side, beat the lines in order, settle chest-to-chest and hold.

🤸Bottom (Guard)

Keep feet and frames attached, steer the top's weight, dump the over-commit, retreat one line at a time.

Coaching cues

🦶Whose feet won?

Ask both: "Where were the bottom's feet when the pass happened?" Directs attention to the connection battle that decides everything upstream.

⚖️Felt the commit?

Ask the bottom: "When did their weight pass their base?" The dump window is felt through the frames, not seen.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

One line per early level→Isolates the steering battle, then the lane decision Pass proven by a 3s hold→Rewards pressure over scramble-luck Dump-to-hands/seat win for the bottom→Observable sweep mechanics, keeps the top's base honest Round cap→Urgency both ways, no guard-camping Live, resisting guard→Keeps the connection-reading perception intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the line structure (feet → knees → chest) decomposes passing into perceptual stages without ever removing the live opponent, so each stage's read stays attached to real consequences.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

Foot and shin contact on legs and hips → which connections are live, which side was abandoned.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own weight over the base → whether the next step is a pass or a dump waiting to happen.

👁️Visual

The bottom's knee direction → inside or outside lane.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top breaks connections faster than the bottom rebuilds them. Track passes held vs. dumps conceded, and which line each pass died at. When the top consistently passes on the abandoned side instead of fighting live feet, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: standing over an active open guard, the most common passing problem after any takedown that lands clean in MMA and grappling alike.

Simplified: line ladderno strikes L1-4round cap

Deepens the passing side of Ground Access; mirrors Seated Guard Retention.

Readiness to progress

  • Clears the foot line before attacking the knees
  • Passes on the abandoned side, not into live feet
  • Holds chest-to-chest 3s instead of racing past
  • Keeps the base under the weight, survives the dump attempts

Warning signs

Dives past both lines into a scramble Hands post on the mat inside the guard Fights the same connection over and over Bottom holds instead of steering, feet go dead

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🦵 No reaping or heel exposure in the leg ride 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Access Follow-on→Half-Guard Pass Mirror→Seated Guard Retention Related→Decision States

docs.title: Open Space Takedown
docs.text:
Open Space · WrestlingOpen Space Control → Takedown Open SpaceWrestlingCombinedIntermediateClinch → Takedown

Takedowns from a connected position with no wall, more escape routes, and DNS threats at their most dangerous in the system.

StartTwo fighters clinched in open space, hands already connected, no wall. → The GoalAttacker compromises the base and finishes; defender scrambles, reverses, or hits a DNS. → FinishControlled takedown → switch · Scramble up, disengage, reverse, or DNS → reset.

No wall to brace, the neck is always live.

The base must be compromised before descent; force it and you over-commit. In open space, DNS threats are at their most dangerous in the system.

What to Read

Attune to the defender's preferred distance and base within the clinch, near and off-balance affords your finish, recovered base affords their escape. Read their weight displacement and hip position by feel, plus the attacker's head/neck position (DNS risk), not the eyes. That information specifies whether the base is compromised before descent and whether the open space is still closing.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, already clinched. RangeClinch range, hands connected, no shooting from distance. BoundaryA marked perimeter in open space, no wall. RolesOne attacker, one defender. Start & resetBegin connected; reset if the clinch breaks cleanly or the defender disengages.

The Matchup

🤼 Attacker

Trying to compromise the defender's base and bring them to ground with hip and shoulder control, no immediate dumping.

Base must be compromised before descent, displace weight, control the hips, take the line out. Keep your own balance and head position; over-commitment invites a guillotine. VS 🐍 Defender

Trying to maintain balance, then scramble, disengage, reverse, or exploit poor entries with a DNS.

Keep an active base. When the attacker's posture breaks, actively hunt the DNS, front headlock, guillotine. In open space there's no wall to brace, so DNS threats are at their most dangerous.

The Rules

🤝 Hands already connectedPlay starts from the clinch, this is not a shot-entry game. The takedown is isolated from the entry, which is a different game. ⚖️ No immediate dumpingThe base must be compromised before the descent, weight displaced, hips controlled, no easy step-out. Forcing it with strength is not the solution. 🐍 DNS allowed defensivelyDefensive neck submissions are the defender's primary weapon and a win condition. They punish poor head position and over-commitment. 🎯 Controlled takedown requiredThe attacker must control to the ground, hips and shoulders controlled, not merely reach a ground position. Develops follow-through control. ⬛ Open space, no wallInside a marked perimeter with no wall to brace against. Pure body mechanics determine scrambles, and DNS risk is maximal.

How to Win

Switch Controlled takedown → switch roles.When the attacker lands a controlled takedown with hips and shoulders controlled on the ground, the players switch roles. The space closed cleanly: base compromised, descent controlled, top control established. The attacker earns the defending role. Reset Scramble up, disengage, reverse, or DNS → reset.When the defender scrambles to standing, disengages, reverses, or hits a DNS, the position re-opens, reset. The defender re-opened the space, recovered base, broke the clinch, turned the position, or caught the neck. Begin again from the clinch. Diagnostic Was the base compromised, or was the takedown forced?Watch whether the base was truly compromised before the descent, or whether the takedown was forced through strength. DNS is a win condition here, reflecting its importance. Beyond the outcome, the coach reads base-compromise timing and head-position discipline.

The Levels

1Base breakCompromise balance; no strikes.Compromise the defender's balance from the clinch, no strikes. Success = clear weight displacement and controlled descent initiation. 2Deny scrambleFollow the resistance.The defender actively scrambles during descent; the attacker must follow and establish control. Success = ground control despite resistance. 3Follow-through controlSustain 3+ seconds.After the takedown, establish stable control while the defender resists on the ground. Success = sustained hip/shoulder control (3+ seconds); TKO pin threat available. 4Full MMA expressionLight strikes permitted.Light strikes during clinch and ground phases; the attacker can use strikes to set up takedowns, the defender to create scrambles. Control under realistic striking threat.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What must be true before you descend, and what are you reading to know it? The defender's base must be compromised before descent. Read their weight displacement and hip position by feel, near and off-balance affords your finish, recovered base affords their escape. Not the eyes. Q Why are DNS threats at their most dangerous in this game? There is no wall to brace against, so the neck is always live. Over-commitment or poor head position lets the defender hunt a front headlock or guillotine, which is a win condition here. Q What separates a clean takedown from a forced one? A clean finish flows from a compromised base: weight displaced, hips controlled, descent controlled to top control. A forced takedown is muscled through strength, and it over-commits and invites the guillotine. Q As the defender, when do you hunt the DNS? The moment the attacker's posture breaks: actively go for the front headlock or guillotine off poor head position and over-commitment, rather than only defending the base.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤼Attacker

Compromise base before descent; control hips and shoulders to the ground; keep head position to avoid the DNS.

🐍Defender

Keep an active base; scramble, disengage, or reverse; hunt the DNS the moment the attacker's posture breaks.

Coaching cues

⚖️Was the base gone?

Ask attackers: "Was their base compromised before you went?" This develops the key perception, takedown available vs. forced.

🐍See the neck?

Ask defenders: "Did you see the DNS opportunity?" to develop counter-offense awareness from broken posture.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Start from clinch (hands connected)→Isolates the takedown from the entry Base compromised before descent→Prevents forcing/muscling takedowns DNS allowed as primary weapon→Punishes poor head position Controlled takedown required→Develops follow-through control Open space, no wall→Pure body mechanics, maximum DNS risk

Develops takedown affordance perception from connected position, base-compromise reading prevents strength-based solutions, and the DNS constraint builds a "respectful attacker" with safe aggression (Renshaw et al., 2019).

What the players read

✋Haptic

Weight distribution, grip tension, weight shift → takedown direction and control quality; scramble direction.

👁️Visual

Hip position and angle; head/neck position → takedown availability and DNS risk or opportunity.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance, posture, and base → safe execution position and recovery options.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the space closes onto a controlled finish over time, is the defender's base eroding toward the ground, or recovering and re-opening into a scramble? Track base-compromise-before-descent, head-position discipline (DNS avoidance), and whether scrambles end in control. When base compromise reliably precedes the descent, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: completing takedowns from clinch in open space, the highest-risk wrestling environment due to DNS threats and no wall to brace.

Start from clinchno strikes L1–3DNS recognized, not finished

The open-space wrestling game, parallels the wall pathway without environmental assistance. Built on Pressure to Clinch.

Readiness to progress

  • Takedowns emerge from base compromise, not forcing
  • Head position is maintained, DNS-aware
  • Scrambles end in control, not chaos
  • Multiple takedown types available
  • Can articulate: "I felt their weight shift, so I…"

Warning signs

Forces takedowns through strength Gets guillotined frequently Loses position in scrambles Only one takedown works

Safety & related games

🤝 Light strikes; controlled takedowns only 🛑 DNS recognized and stopped before completion, the threat is the tool, not the finish 🔁 Reset on any slam, uncontrolled descent, or neck crank

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Pressure to Clinch Follow-on→Ground Control Related→TKO Pin

docs.title: Parry the Straight
docs.text:
Skill Isolation · StrikingParry the Straight Skill IsolationStrikingDefensiveBeginnerOpen Space

Deflect straight punches with the hands. The defender may only parry, no blocking, no head movement, no backing up, which forces the parry to develop on its own.

StartTwo fighters at close range, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalThe attacker is trying to land a clean straight; the defender is trying to deflect it off the centerline. → FinishClean parry → switch · Land clean → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

No block, no slip, no retreat, only the parry.

Stripping away every other defense forces the deflection to develop on its own. Stay in the pocket and solve the straight with your hands.

What to Read

Attune to the rate of expansion (τ) of the incoming straight, how fast the glove grows as it travels down the centerline, read via shoulder–hip motion at center mass, not the opponent's eyes or their absolute distance. That information specifies when the punch arrives and which way to deflect it.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off in a neutral fighting stance. RangeInside punching distance. BoundaryA marked perimeter. Both stay inside it. RolesOne attacker, one defender, switch on a clean parry. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset to neutral after each exchange.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to land a clean straight punch, jab or cross, to the head or belly.

Straights only, no hooks, uppercuts, or loops. You set the problem: vary targets and rhythm, add feints at higher levels, and ramp up only as the defender succeeds. VS 🖐️ Defender

Trying to deflect each straight off the centerline with a clean parry.

No blocking, no head movement, no backing up, pure parrying. You don't have to parry every punch; absorb while you read the pattern, then commit when confident.

The Rules

🥊 Straights onlyThe attacker throws only straight punches, jab or cross, to head or belly, no hooks, uppercuts, or looping strikes. Parrying only works on straight lines, so this keeps the defender's problem matched to the technique. 🖐️ Parry only, no block, no dodgeThe defender may only deflect with hands or forearms. Blocking, slipping, head movement, and backing up are off-limits, so the parrying solution is forced to develop. 🚫 No continuous backing upThe defender can't keep retreating out of range to avoid exchanges, they must stay and solve the problem in the pocket. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly. ⏱️ Reset between strikesAt early levels the attacker pauses between strikes so the defender can recalibrate. Pressure becomes continuous as the levels rise.

How to Win

Switch Clean parry → switch roles.A full deflection off the centerline, not a graze and not an absorbed shot. The defender earns the attacking role. Reset Land clean to head or body → reset.A clean, significant straight that got through, same roles. Begin again from a neutral stance. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the game instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the cage-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Single punch, fixed tempoOne straight at a time, reset between.Pure timing practice, one straight punch, fully reset between reps, no feints. Build the parry with zero time pressure. 2Variable tempoSingle punches, no fixed reset.The attacker varies timing with no guaranteed reset, the defender has to read rhythm and stay switched on. 3Add feintsReal vs. fake.The attacker can feint before throwing. Parrying a feint is wasted movement, the defender must distinguish real from fake. 4Two-punch combinationsJab-cross, jab-body.Two-punch combos, still straights only, chain parries together while holding structure. 5Counter after parryOne counter allowed.After a successful parry the defender may throw one counter, teaching the parry as a setup, not just survival. 6Full MMAAdd shot / clinch threat.The attacker can now shoot or clinch too, parry while denying the grappling entry, staying aware of all threats.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to time the parry, and what does it tell you? The rate of expansion (τ) of the straight (how fast the glove grows) read via shoulder and hip motion at center mass, not the eyes or absolute distance. It specifies when the punch arrives and which way to deflect it. Q No block, no slip, no retreat: why strip everything down to the parry alone? It forces the deflection to develop on its own. With no other escape, you have to stay in the pocket and solve the straight with your hands. Q Should you try to parry every punch? No. Read the pattern first, then commit. Absorb while you read, and one good parry beats ten bad ones: timing over volume. Q Why is the attacker restricted to straights only? Parrying only works on straight lines. Removing hooks, uppercuts, and loops keeps the defender's problem matched to the parrying solution being trained.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Land clean straights; vary targets (head vs. belly) and which hand; scale difficulty to the partner.

🖐️Defender

Keep hands close to the face; tap the punch left or right to redirect it; read early and reset after each parry.

Coaching cues

👁️See center mass

Don't track the gloves or the eyes. Center mass keeps shoulder & hip motion in view, "the little X", and shows the straight before it fires. The eyes lie.

🎯Read before redirect

You don't need to parry everything. Recognize the pattern first, then commit, one good parry beats ten bad ones. Timing over volume.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Straights only→Defender perceives parry-able attacks Parry-only→Forces exploration of deflection solutions Reset between exchanges→Time to process and recalibrate Role switch on success→Rewards effective parrying Close range→Parrying is viable and necessary

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), different fighters develop different parry solutions based on reach, hand speed, and reaction time.

What the defender reads

👁️Visual

Shoulder rotation & straight-line trajectory → which way to deflect.

✋Haptic

Contact pressure on the parrying hand → confirms the deflection held.

🧭Proprioceptive

Hand position relative to centerline → readiness to parry.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender's parry lands in time with the punch, track clean deflections vs. shots eaten, and whether the hands re-set to centerline between strikes. That timing relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: deflecting straight punches off the centerline in open-space exchanges.

Simplified: straights onlyparry onlyresets between

Isolates the solution before integration, transfers into Close-Range Defense.

Readiness to progress

  • Clean parries ~70%+ against varied attackers
  • Parries both jab and cross
  • Recovers to centerline after each parry
  • Can describe what they read ("I saw the shoulder move")

Warning signs

Only parries jab OR cross Reports "guessing" not "seeing" Only effective vs one partner

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on loss of composure or excessive force 🔁 Reset if the defender starts blocking or dodging

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is foundational Follow-on→Close-Range Defense · Touch and Don't Get Touched Related→Defensive Solutions

docs.title: Positional Sparring
docs.text:
Format · Live SparringPositional Sparring FormatGroundLiveBoth Compete

Positional sparring is a format, not a standalone game. Both players start from a defined ground position and compete live for the dominant one. It is the live layer of the ground games, not its own problem.

There is no symmetric ground start, so define the position, then go live.

The ground is asymmetric: someone is always on top, in guard, or dominant. Pick a real start from a ground game, then both fight to climb the hierarchy. Momentary position doesn't count, establish it and hold it.

Run It On These Games

🗝️Ground Access, live

Start in guard: top passes, bottom sweeps or recovers. Both compete.The "Live Positional Battle" level of Ground Access: from a defined guard, both players fight for the better position rather than playing fixed pass-vs-sweep roles.

📌Ground Control, live

Start in a dominant position: top holds, bottom escapes or reverses. Both compete.The "Live Positional Battle" level of Ground Control: from a defined top position, the exchange runs live and position can change hands.

The Position Hierarchy

This is the value system both players fight toward. Mid-scramble, it tells you which way is "up."

DominantInferior 1Back ControlHooks in behind the opponent. The most dominant position.Dominant 2MountSitting on the torso, full top control.Dominant 3Side ControlPerpendicular top pin.Top 4Top Half GuardOn top, one leg trapped. Working to pass.Top 5GuardOn bottom with legs engaged. Defensive, but live.Bottom 6Bottom / FlattenedPinned with no leg engagement. The most inferior position.Inferior

How to Run the Format

📍 Start from a defined positionNever start from a vague "neutral." Pick a real position from the ground game you are running (guard, side control, mount) so both players know exactly where they begin. ⚔️ Both compete, no fixed rolesUnlike the base ground game's attacker/defender split, here both players fight for the better position at once, a true competitive dyad. 📌 Position must be establishedA position only counts when established and held for the set duration. Momentary advantage earns nothing. 🚫 Submissions optionalKeep it positional to isolate the skill, or allow submissions at the Full MMA layer. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay inside a marked perimeter. Rolling fully out loses the exchange, training mat-edge awareness.

What to Read

Attune to the felt weight distribution and base through contact: the opponent's balance loading and going light, your own base over or under theirs, the control points that strengthen or break, plus the opponent's hip and frame motion mid-scramble. That inertial information specifies the positional advantage before the scramble resolves.

Why this is a format, not a game

Every other ground game has a crisp, representative start: Ground Access begins with the top in guard, Ground Escape begins under control, Ground Control begins from an established dominant position. A standalone "positional battle" tried to start from a symmetric neutral, but the ground has no representative symmetric start, so it could never define where to begin. Positional sparring solves this by running live on top of a defined ground-game position. See Ground Access and Ground Control for the live levels.

docs.title: Pressure to Clinch
docs.text:
Open Space · StrikingPressure to Clinch Open SpaceStrikingCombinedBeginnerAccess → Transition

Manage distance through movement and pressure, then convert pressure into the clinch and wall pin.

StartTwo fighters in open space at neutral distance, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalThe outside player is trying to pressure & clinch; the inside player is trying to keep space & escape. → FinishClinch / wall pin → attacker scores · Escape or reverse → defender scores · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Pressure isn't pushing, it's collapsing their space.

Cut angles to shrink open space, then convert pressure into clinch and clinch into the wall pin. Corral, don't chase.

Key Positions

⚖️ 50/50 clinchEach fighter has one overhook and one underhook, diagonally matched (one per side), starting chest to chest, symmetric and neutral. Whoever first wins inside control or the angle gains the advantage. 🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

What to Read

Attune to the global optic flow of closing pressure, how fast the gap is shrinking and which way space is collapsing, plus the rate of closure (τ) on clinch entries. Near distance affords the clinch and offense; far affords escape and defense. Read the shrinking of open space, not the opponent's hands or feet, that information specifies when the pin is on and where the escape lane still lives.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo at neutral distance, one outside, one inside. RangeStriking distance with room to move. BoundaryA marked perimeter. Both stay inside it. RolesOne outside (attacker), one inside (defender), switch between rounds. Start & resetBoth begin at neutral distance; reset after each exchange or score.

The Matchup

🥊 Outside Player (Attacker)

Trying to apply pressure to control space, close distance, and establish the clinch.

Corral the opponent toward the boundary, cut off lateral escapes, and convert pressure to clinch and clinch to wall pin. Cut angles, don't chase. VS 🦶 Inside Player (Defender)

Trying to maintain access to open space, escape pressure, and prevent the clinch.

Keep space through movement, avoid being flattened against the boundary, and escape or reverse if the clinch is initiated.

The Rules

⭕ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (circle, square, taped lines, cones, or wall). The boundary creates spatial awareness and urgency; if both feet leave it, you lose instantly. 🚫 No takedowns until Level 5Takedowns are off-limits until the final level, keeping the focus on completing the clinch and wall-pin sequence. 🔄 Movement is continuousStatic exchanges reset, both players must keep moving so the spatial battle stays live. 🤼 Clinch from controlling positionThe clinch must be established from a controlling position with the attacker on the outside. If the attacker gets reversed to the wall side, the defender wins the exchange. 👊 No flurriesStrikes are limited to 2–3 strike sequences. They are tools, they create reactions and measure distance, not damage.

How to Win

Attacker Establish the clinch / wall pin per level → attacker scores.Level 2: establish clinch in a controlling position. Level 3: achieve a wall pin. Level 4: hold the pin 3–5 seconds. Level 5: wall pin or takedown. Defender Escape, reverse to the wall side, or prevent the pin → defender scores.Escape the pressure or clinch, reverse the attacker to the wall side, or prevent the pin, scaled by level. At Level 5 a counter-strike to reset also counts. Level 1 Time-based → no winner.The opening level is time-based with no winner. Both players experience pressure and escape patterns before contact is added. Rounds run 60–90 seconds.

The Levels

1Touch-based, pattern recognitionTouch/tag only, time-based, no winner.Touch/tag contact only, no striking; the defender can parry or block tags. Both players learn spatial dynamics before adding contact. 2Striking + clinch entryLight strikes; attacker establishes clinch.Light strikes permitted. Attacker wins by establishing the clinch in a controlling position; defender wins by escaping or reversing to the wall side. Pressure creates clinch opportunity. 3Clinch to wall pinModerate strikes; achieve the wall pin.Moderate strikes permitted. The attacker must achieve a wall pin, not just a clinch; the defender can accept the clinch but fight the pin. Completing the full sequence. 4Pin maintenanceHold the pin for 3–5 seconds.Same as Level 3, but the attacker must maintain the pin for 3–5 seconds while the defender actively works to escape. Controlling position, not just achieving it. 5Full MMA expressionFull contact; takedowns allowed.Full striking contact and takedowns from the clinch. Attacker wins by wall pin or takedown; defender can counter-strike to create escape windows. The complete transition under MMA reality.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What is the attacker reading to drive the pressure? The global optic flow of closing pressure (how fast the gap shrinks and which way space collapses) plus the rate of closure (τ) on clinch entries. Read the shrinking of open space, not the opponent's hands or feet. Q Pressure isn't pushing. What is it, and what's the difference between cutting and chasing? Pressure is collapsing their space. Cutting angles corrals the opponent and shrinks escape lanes; chasing just reacts to their movement. Corral, don't chase. Q What do near vs. far distance each afford? Near affords the clinch and offense; far affords escape and defense. The distance you keep specifies when the pin is on and where the escape lane still lives. Q Why does reversing the attacker to the wall side count as a defender win? The clinch must come from a controlling position. Rewarding the reversal prevents reckless clinch entries: a forced, off-balance entry gets punished.

Variant: Lead-Leg Barrier (WIP, class 2026-06-15)

A scaffolded entry ladder for beginners: the attacker closes the distance with no strikes at all, against a defender whose only job is a lead-leg barrier. Then weapons are added a rung at a time on both sides. Same win, get past the elbows into a clinch (any 50/50, both hands under, or a connection on a leg), under a 20-second shot clock.

1No weapons vs the lead legBlock, catch, time the clinch.Attacker has no strikes, only hands to block and catch kicks plus timing to close. Defender keeps distance with the lead leg only (front, side, or push kick) and the hands. The pure scaffold for someone who has never been punched, all closing, no danger. 2Defender gains the jabAttacker may reach to manipulate.Defender adds the lead jab on top of the teeps. Attacker still has no strikes but may now reach with the hands to post and manipulate, the trade-off is explicit: the more you reach, the more you can be hit. 3Attacker gains the rear crossA threat to herd, not the win.Attacker adds the rear straight (head or body, straight only) to threaten and herd. The cross creates pressure, but the clinch is still the win. Defender can now also win by catching a leg on the level change as the cross is thrown.

Feint volume unlocks the doors. Don't feint one at a time, pump-fake six or seven in a row, top and bottom, until the read breaks. High-level pressure is built on a constant stream of false starts, not single committed shots.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Outside Player (Attacker)

Apply intelligent forward pressure; use footwork, feints, and strikes to corral toward the boundary; cut off lateral escapes; convert pressure to clinch, clinch to wall pin.

🦶Inside Player (Defender)

Maintain space through movement; avoid being crowded or flattened against the boundary; recognize shrinking space; escape or reverse if the clinch is initiated.

Coaching cues

✂️Cut, don't chase

Ask attackers: "Were you cutting angles or chasing?" Pressure makes movement predictable and shrinks escape routes, chasing just reacts to movement.

👁️Feel space early

Ask defenders: "Did you feel space shrinking before it happened?" This develops anticipatory perception of pressure.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Marked perimeter→Creates spatial awareness and urgency Asymmetric roles→Defines distinct movement solutions Clinch as win condition→Gives purpose to pressure (not just pushing) Wall pin as complete success→Teaches full sequence completion Reversal = defender win→Prevents reckless clinch entries

Implements Representative Learning Design (Renshaw et al., 2019), the full pressure → clinch → pin sequence represents how space control works in MMA.

What athletes read

👁️Visual (Attacker)

Defender's position to the boundary, escape routes, weight distribution, and clinch posture → pressure direction, angle to cut, and when to drive the pin.

👁️Visual (Defender)

Available space, the attacker's cutting angles, and clinch-entry timing → urgency of escape and when to frame or reverse.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance, position relative to center, and balance during clinch → composure under pressure, spatial awareness, and reversal opportunity.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether open space is opening or closing over time, track the distance the inside player keeps and the rate the gap shrinks under pressure, then whether the sequence reaches clinch and pin. That distance-closure relationship is the order parameter; when the attacker reliably collapses space and the defender reliably preserves escape lanes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: cage/ring dynamics where one fighter pressures the other against the boundary, converts to clinch, and achieves wall control.

Touch-only at Level 1no takedowns until Level 5time-limited rounds

Athletes who develop the full sequence transfer directly to Wall Control and cage wrestling.

Readiness to progress

  • Attacker cuts angles rather than chases
  • Defender anticipates pressure before space closes
  • Clinch entries are timed, not forced
  • Composure maintained throughout rounds

Warning signs

Attacker chases instead of cutting Clinch entries get reversed Can't maintain the wall pin Defender panics near boundary

Safety & related games

🤝 Touch at Level 1; light-to-moderate strikes higher 🛑 Stop on loss of control or excessive clinch force 🔁 Reset if exchanges go static or pressure turns to aggression

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Touch and Don't Get Touched Follow-on→Wall Control · Open Space Takedown Related→Winning the Circle · Full MMA Expression

docs.title: Pressure to Takedown
docs.text:
Open Space · WrestlingPressure to Takedown Open SpaceWrestlingOffensiveAdvancedStriking → Grappling

A full-sequence offensive game: chain strikes, distance closure, and takedowns into one flow while the defender manages all three threats at once.

StartTwo fighters in neutral standing at close quarters, attacker has initiative. → The GoalAttacker chains strikes to a takedown; defender defends all three layers. → FinishTakedown + meaningful top control → reset · Defender survives strikes, closure & takedown → success (no win state).

The takedown isn't the entry, it's the end of the chain.

Strikes open the closure; closure becomes the shot. The takedown emerges from pressure, never forced.

What to Read

Attune to the rate the distance closes as strikes land (global optic flow) and the defender's preferred distance, near affords your clinch and shot, far affords their escape. Read their guard position and weight, plus the level-change opening (with τ on the shot entry), not their eyes. That information specifies when closure is available and when the takedown is there to take.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one attacker, one defender. RangeNeutral standing at close quarters. BoundaryA marked perimeter, open space, optional wall as a pathway. RolesOne attacker, one defender, defender has no win state. Start & resetAttacker initiates with strikes; reset if the defender escapes cleanly or the attacker establishes top control.

The Matchup

🤼 Attacker

Trying to compromise openings with strikes, close distance, and take the defender down to meaningful top control.

Chain it: strikes → clinch entry → takedown → top control. Strikes must create the closure, aggressive forward motion increases counter vulnerability, so the takedown should emerge from pressure, not be forced. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to defend strikes, prevent closure, break control, defend the takedown, and scramble back to standing.

Manage every layer in turn: block/parry/dodge strikes, use footwork and hand controls to deny closure, break grips if clinched, sprawl/whizzer the shot, and scramble up if taken down.

The Rules

🥊 Strikes must precede the shotThe attacker must use strikes to close distance, not just shoot from outside. This prevents wrestlers from ignoring striking and forces true MMA integration. ⚠️ Forward motion = more open to countersThe more aggressive the forward motion, the more compromised the attacker is for the defender's counter. Closure must be paid for. 🎭 Feints disguise intentionFeints and fakes make it harder for the defender to identify whether a strike, a closure, or a shot is coming. 📈 Layers added progressivelyClosure → takedown → scramble → counters → full expression. Each layer is added in turn so the sequence is built before the defender can fight back fully. ⬛ Open space, optional wallPlay happens inside a marked perimeter in open space, with a wall available as an optional pathway, multiple routes to the takedown.

How to Win

Attacker scores Takedown + meaningful top control → reset.When the attacker executes a takedown AND establishes meaningful top pinning control, the sequence is won, reset. The space fully closed and the chain completed: strikes set up closure, closure became a takedown, and the takedown became control. Then reset. Defender (no win state) Success = threats defended, not a win.The defender has no win condition, success is measured by strikes defended, closure prevented, and takedowns defended. This is an offensive-development game. The defender wins nothing outright; the coach reads how well each layer was defended. Reset Defender escapes or scrambles up → reset to neutral.When the defender escapes cleanly or scrambles back to standing, the space re-opens, reset to neutral. The defender broke the chain, denied closure, defended the shot, or scrambled up. Begin again at close quarters.

The Levels

1Strikes and closure onlyNo takedowns yet.Attacker: strikes and clinch entry only. Defender: defend strikes and prevent the clinch. Managing the first two layers. 2Add takedown threatTakedowns from clinch.The attacker can attempt takedowns from the clinch; the defender must defend clinch AND takedown. Full standing defense. 3Add scramble continuationPlay continues on the ground.If taken down, play continues; the defender scrambles to return to standing. Recovery and continuation. 4Add defender counter strikesTwo-way striking.The defender can throw counter strikes; the attacker must respect the counter threat. Two-way striking with a takedown threat. 5Full expressionBoth strike freely; DNS allowed.Both players strike freely; the attacker chains strikes to takedowns, the defender can counter or disengage. DNS allowed defensively.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Where does the takedown sit in the sequence, and what makes it appear? It is the end of the chain, never the entry: strikes open the closure, closure becomes the shot. The takedown emerges from pressure, it is never forced. Q What are you reading to know when closure and the shot are available? The rate the distance closes as strikes land (global optic flow) and the defender's preferred distance, near affords your clinch and shot, far affords their escape. Plus the level-change opening, with τ on the shot entry. Not the eyes. Q What is the cost of aggressive forward motion? The more aggressive the forward motion, the more open you are to the defender's counter. Closure must be paid for, so it has to be set up with strikes and feints, not bulled through. Q As the defender with no win state, what does success look like, and how do hand position trade-offs work? Success is each layer defended in turn: strikes, closure, clinch, takedown, scramble. Hands closer defend strikes better but make closure harder to stop; hands further out disrupt closure and takedowns but leave you open to strikes.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤼Attacker

Start with only head strikes or only straight punches; use strikes to open closure; chain strikes → clinch → takedown → top control.

🛡️Defender

Defense and distance first; footwork and hand controls to deny closure; break control if clinched; scramble up if taken down.

Coaching cues

🔗Did strikes make it?

Ask attackers: "Did your strikes create the takedown opportunity?" The takedown should emerge from striking pressure, not be forced.

🧱Which layer?

Ask defenders: "What layer were you defending?" to develop threat awareness across strikes, closure, clinch, takedown, and ground.

Defensive trade-offs

✋Hands closer

Easier to defend strikes, but harder to counter or defend closure.

🫳Hands further out

Compromised to strikes, but can disrupt the attacker or defend takedowns. Offline head when dodging is open to significant strikes.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Strikes must close (can't just shoot)→Develops integrated striking-to-grappling Multi-layer sequence→Full MMA offensive chain Defender manages all threats→Develops layered defensive awareness Progressive layers→Scaffolded complexity Open space with optional wall→Multiple pathways available

Develops offensive sequencing, perception-action coupling across multiple domains; requiring strikes before shooting forces true MMA integration (Renshaw et al., 2019).

What the players read

👁️Visual

Guard position, footwork/distance, level-change setup, counter setup → where to strike, when to close, sprawl prep.

✋Haptic

Clinch control quality and pressure → when to attempt the takedown; escape or reversal timing.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance and position → sequence timing; scramble direction if taken down.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the distance closes over time and the chain completes, is the gap-closure rate trending toward the clinch, and does the sequence reach a controlled takedown? Track whether strikes create closure, whether takedowns emerge from clinch control (not forced), and which layer the defender holds. When the chain flows without jumping steps, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the classic "punch to shoot", a wrestler or pressure fighter using striking to set up takedowns.

Progressive defender optionscontrolled contactcontrolled takedowns

The offensive sequence transfers directly to competition, branches into Wall Control and Open Space Takedown.

Readiness to progress

  • Strikes clearly set up the distance closure
  • Takedowns emerge from clinch control, not forced
  • Defender manages the current level's threats
  • The sequence feels integrated, not choppy
  • Can articulate: "I threw the cross then shot because…"

Warning signs

Shoots without strikes Gets countered during closure Sequence feels forced Defender overwhelmed

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate strikes; controlled takedowns 🛑 Stop on uncontrolled takedowns, excessive force, or slams 🔁 Reset if the attacker is just shooting without strikes

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Close-Range Defense · Pressure to Clinch Follow-on→Wall Control · Open Space Takedown Related→Defensive Solutions · Hand Controls · TKO Pin

docs.title: Pressure to Wall
docs.text:
Open Space → Wall · WrestlingPressure to Wall Open Space → WallWrestlingOffensiveAdvancedWall Dominance

Chain strikes to wall control, the destination is the wall itself, for dirty boxing and grinding, not the ground.

StartOpen space, striking range, wall available. → The GoalAttacker herds to the wall & damages; defender escapes or reverses. → FinishWall control + damage → switch · Escape or reverse → reset.

Don't just hit them, move them to the wall.

The wall is the destination, not a takedown. Strikes that herd, then grind.

Key Positions

🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

What to Read

Attune to the rate the open space closes as you cut angles (global optic flow) and the defender's preferred distance, near affords your clinch and grinding, far affords their escape. Read their escape-angle attempts and weight distribution, plus the gap to the wall behind them (with τ on the clinch entry), not their eyes. That information specifies which strike moves them and when wall contact is imminent.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one attacker, one defender. RangeStriking range, open space. BoundaryMarked perimeter with a wall/cage as the destination. RolesAttacker vs. defender, switch on wall control + damage. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset to open space on wall control or clean escape.

The Matchup

🧱 Attacker

Trying to drive the opponent to the wall with strikes and establish control for damage, not a takedown.

Use strikes to back them up, purposeful, not just volume. Cut angles, close distance, establish the clinch at the wall, then work dirty boxing, knees, and grinding while denying escape. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to avoid the wall, escape the pressure, or reverse position, can't back up infinitely.

Circle and create angles before you're trapped. If you reach the wall, escape or reverse. Counter to discourage the pressure, the wall is behind you, so back-up alone won't save you.

The Rules

🥊 Strikes create pressure, not pushingThe attacker must use strikes to create the pressure that moves the defender, not just shove them. This develops striking-based herding. 🧱 Wall is the destination, not the groundThe goal is wall control and damage, not a takedown. Once on the wall, the attacker works grinding and dirty boxing, not a ground transition. This distinguishes it from Pressure to Takedown. 🚫 No infinite back-upThe defender cannot simply retreat forever, the wall exists. This forces real decision points: escape early or face the wall. 📈 Progressive options by levelPressure → wall damage → defender counters → full MMA. Scaffolded so herding is built before counter threats are added. ⬛ Wall / marked perimeter in playA wall or cage is available as the destination, inside a marked perimeter. The environmental constraint shapes the entire game.

How to Win

Switch Wall control + damage → switch roles.When the attacker establishes wall control AND lands damage (knees, dirty boxing), the space fully closed onto the wall and the attacker exploited the position, and earns the defending role. Reset Clean escape or reverse → reset, same roles.The defender re-opened the space, circled out clean or turned the attacker onto the wall. Begin again in open space. Diagnostic Do the strikes move them, or just hit them?Beyond the win, the coach reads whether striking is purposefully herding the defender, not just volume, and whether damage flows from the wall position.

The Levels

1Pressure to wall onlyDrive and pin.Drive to the wall with strikes; establish control once there, no extended damage. Closing and pinning. 2Add wall damageDirty boxing, knees.Once wall control is established, work damage, dirty boxing, knees, grinding. Damage from wall position. 3Defender countersCounter during pressure.The defender can counter-strike during the pressure phase; the attacker must close through counters. Pressure under threat. 4Full MMA expressionCounter, clinch, or shoot.The defender can counter, clinch, or shoot; the attacker must reach the wall while managing all threats. Wall pressure under MMA reality.

Variant: Stalk and Corral (WIP)

Provisional (WIP): derived from class recording 2026-06-06

Captured live from class and pending review on the platform. Same destination as the base game, the wall, but with asymmetric weapons and a touch win instead of a pin.

Both players get a restricted toolset. Attacker: lead hand + rear leg. The lead hand probes and herds, the rear leg makes the pressure respected. Defender: rear hand + footwork (lead-leg teep at Dial 2), looking to win the circle on the outside. The attacker wins when the defender's hip or back touches the wall, a touch, not a pin. The defender wins by a meaningful level-change connection, on one leg or two, under both arms, collected above the knee (a dive below the knee is not a connection).

1Base dialTouch win vs connect win.Attacker: lead hand + rear leg, win on hip/back touching the wall. Defender: rear hand + footwork, win on a level-change connection. First runs feel crashy, that's the point: no lead-hand probes for defense. 2Defender gains the teepLead leg unlocked.The defender adds the lead-leg teep to hold range. Restores defensive capability while keeping the rear-hand-only puzzle alive. 3Connect and destabilizeThe threshold rule.The defender's connection only wins if it moves the attacker's feet from their spot, some destabilization of the current feet pattern. Connection alone is contact; connection that moves them is a takedown forming. 🌀 No moral winsCircling out is survival, not victory: the defender is still in the cage. Defense only escapes the nightmare by connecting. Attacker resets and stays on offense after an exit. 👀 Beware shark eyesThe recurring failure: players hear the win condition, then revert to hunting strikes. If a free push wins the game, take it. The skill being built is getting somebody pressed to the wall, not landing shots. 🔄 Fresh-partner roundOptional: re-run the same dial with switched partners. First-run observation: feint usage jumps against an unfamiliar opponent.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What is the destination of this game, and why does that matter? The wall itself, not the ground. You herd them there for dirty boxing and grinding, not a takedown. This is what separates it from Pressure to Takedown. Q What are you reading to know which strike moves them and when wall contact is imminent? The rate the open space closes as you cut angles (global optic flow), the defender's preferred distance, their escape-angle attempts and weight, plus the gap to the wall behind them (with τ on the clinch entry). Not the eyes. Q What is the difference between strikes that move them and strikes that just hit them? Purposeful strikes herd the defender toward the wall, cutting angles to close space. Just hitting is volume that does not change position. The diagnostic is whether the strikes move them. Q Why can't the defender simply keep backing up, and what should they do instead? The wall exists: back-up alone runs them into it. They must circle and create angles before being trapped, and if they reach the wall, escape or reverse, plus counter to discourage the pressure.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🧱Attacker

Strike to back them toward the wall; cut angles; establish clinch at the wall; dirty box, knee, grind; deny escape.

🛡️Defender

Avoid being backed up; circle and create angles; if on the wall, escape or reverse; counter to discourage pressure.

Coaching cues

➡️Moving or hitting?

Ask attackers: "Are your strikes moving them or just hitting them?" The goal is purposeful striking that herds to the wall.

🧭Know the wall

Ask defenders: "Did you know how close the wall was?" to develop spatial awareness before being trapped.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Strikes must create pressure→Develops striking-based herding Wall control + damage (not takedown)→Distinct from the Pressure to Takedown pathway No infinite back-up→Creates decision points for the defender Progressive options→Scaffolded learning Wall is the destination→Environmental constraint shapes the whole game
