Develops cage-cutting affordance perception, reading angles to herd opponents to the wall and striking purposefully for movement, not just damage (Renshaw et al., 2019).

What the players read

👁️Visual

Distance from the wall, escape-angle attempts, weight distribution → cutting direction and which strike moves them.

✋Haptic

Wall contact (back to the cage) → escape vs. clinch-defense mode; when to establish the clinch.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own pressure angle and positioning relative to the wall → cutting efficiency and available options.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the open space closes onto the wall over time, is the gap-closure rate trending the defender toward the cage, and does the sequence reach wall control with damage? Track wall pins achieved, whether strikes purposefully move the defender, and clean escapes. When herding reliably closes the space, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: cage-cutting and dirty boxing against the fence, a dominant pressure-fighter and wrestler strategy.

Progressive: damage addedcounters added laterclear wall-pin win

Cage-cutting transfers directly to competition, leads into Wall Grinding and Wall Control.

Readiness to progress

  • Consistently herds the defender to the wall
  • Strikes move the defender, purposeful, not just volume
  • Wall control established without excessive effort
  • Damage flows naturally from the wall position
  • Can articulate: "I cut their angle by…"

Warning signs

Chases instead of cuts Pushes instead of strikes Defender escapes repeatedly Wall position doesn't lead to damage

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on excessive force or uncontrolled pressure 🔁 Reset if wall work becomes grinding without purpose

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Pressure to Clinch Follow-on→Wall Grinding · Wall Control Related→Hand Controls

docs.title: Rear Hand Offense
docs.text:
Skill Isolation · StrikingRear Hand Offense Skill IsolationStrikingOffensiveBeginnerAccess

Land the rear hand alone, cross, rear hook, rear uppercut, forcing you to build your own entries.

StartTwo fighters at striking range inside a marked perimeter; attacker restricted to the rear hand. → The GoalAttacker lands a clean rear hand by opening the defense; defender survives without countering. → FinishLand clean → switch · Whiffed or blocked → reset · Defender has no win condition (diagnostic only).

No jab to hide behind, the power shot must build its own opening.

Strip away every weapon but the rear hand and the fighter is forced to discover entries. Read the guard, make it move, commit to where it goes.

What to Read

Attune to the defender's guard reactions to your feints & level changes, how their shell shifts when you threaten a zone, together with the rate of expansion (τ) of your committing cross toward the opening at center mass. The opening you read isn't where the guard is; it's where it goes when you make it move.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off in a neutral fighting stance. RangeStriking range. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne attacker, one defender, switch on a clean rear hand. Start & resetThe attacker initiates; reset to neutral after each exchange.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to land a clean rear hand strike, cross, rear hook, or rear uppercut, by reading the defense and creating openings.

Rear hand only. With no jab to hide behind, you must build entries: feint to draw a reaction, change levels to open targets, manage distance for power. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to defend all rear hand attacks effectively. No countering.

Defend naturally with the full toolkit (block, parry, slip, move). You have no win condition, your success is making the attacker work for every opening.

The Rules

🥊 Rear hand onlyThe attacker uses only the rear hand, cross, rear hook, rear uppercut. No lead hand, no kicks. With the jab removed, entries and setups must be discovered. 🚫 Defender cannot counterAt Level 1 the defender plays pure defense. Removing the counter-threat lets the attacker focus entirely on setup development for the power shot. 🎯 All targets allowedHead and body are both fair game. Target variation invites level changes and misdirection within the rear-hand constraint. 📏 Maintain striking rangeBoth players hold striking range so the rear hand stays viable with a proper entry, no drifting out to where power can't land. ⏱️ Reset after each attackReset to a neutral stance after a clean strike lands or after a set time, so each rep begins from the same problem.

How to Win

Switch Land clean → switch roles.When the attacker lands a clean rear hand strike, the players switch roles. A clean landing means solid contact reaching the target with real energy transfer, not a whiff, graze, or blocked shot. Reset Whiffed or blocked → reset, read again.When the strike is whiffed, grazed, or blocked, reset to neutral, same roles. A failed attempt isn't a loss; it's information. Re-enter the probe → read → setup → commit cycle. No score Defender denies every opening → diagnostic success.The defender has no win condition. Their success is measured by quality of defense, not points, forcing the attacker to set up and commit is the goal.

The Levels

1Defense onlyDefender can't counter.Pure offensive development, rear hand only against a defender who can't counter. Build timing and entries with zero counter-threat. 2Add counter threatCounters after good defense.The defender can fire counters once they defend successfully. You land the power shot while managing the counter risk. 3Full MMA expressionCounters plus shot / clinch.The defender can counter-strike, shoot, or clinch. Land the rear hand under realistic MMA pressure with all threats live. See Full MMA Expression.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q The opening you read isn't where the guard is. Where is it? It's where the guard goes when you make it move. You read the defender's reactions to your feints and level changes, then commit the cross to that opening. Q With no jab to hide behind, how do you create the opening for the rear hand? You build your own entries: feint to draw a reaction, change levels to open targets, and manage distance for power. The power shot must earn its own opening. Q The defender has no win condition. What does their success look like, and what is a whiffed shot? Defender success is quality of defense: making you work for every opening. A whiff or blocked shot is information, not a loss: reset and re-run the probe, read, setup, commit cycle. Q Why must you maintain striking range, not drift out? The rear hand travels further and needs power. Holding striking range keeps it viable with a proper entry; drifting out kills the power and telegraphs the lunge.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Find openings for power; vary targets head/body; develop entries and setups for the rear hand; manage distance for power generation.

🛡️Defender

Defend all rear hand attacks with appropriate solutions; provide realistic resistance; never counter at Level 1.

Coaching cues

👁️"How did you create that opening?"

Ask after each rep. This develops understanding of setup-to-finish sequencing for power shots.

🎯Land power when you need it

Cross head, cross body, rear hook, rear uppercut, feints to draw reactions, level changes to open targets. The rear hand requires setup because it travels further.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Rear hand only→Forces development of power shot entries and setups Defender can't counter (L1)→Focus on setup development No lead hand available→Must create openings without jab setup All rear-hand tools available→Invites discovery of cross, hook, uppercut options Striking range maintained→Rear hand is viable with proper entry

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), removing the lead hand forces discovery of power-shot entries that would normally hide behind a jab.

What the attacker reads

👁️Visual

Distance and stance, defensive posture, reaction to feints, hand position → range for power, where openings exist, target selection.

✋Haptic

Contact quality → whether the power shot landed clean.

🧭Proprioceptive

Weight transfer, rotation, range and balance → power generation, strike selection and commitment.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the committing rear-hand shot lands on the opening a feint or level change created, track target-landed vs. attempts, and whether each landing followed a genuine read → open → commit cycle rather than a telegraphed lunge. That coupling of opening-read to committed strike is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, disguised power offense has formed.

Representativeness

Models: landing power punches, crosses, rear hooks, rear uppercuts, in standing exchanges, the fight-ending shots that require setup and timing.

Simplified: rear hand onlyno counter (L1)role switch on success

Setup and timing transfer directly into Land the Target, where all weapons integrate.

Readiness to progress

  • Lands rear hand without obvious telegraph
  • Uses feints and level changes to open
  • Varies targets head/body
  • Manages distance to generate power
  • Composed under counter threat (L2)

Warning signs

Loads up obviously Only throws the cross Rushes to close distance Power without control

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate, power shots especially controlled 🛑 Stop on excessive force or lost composure 🔁 Reset if attacker uses non-rear-hand weapons

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is foundational Follow-on→Land the Target (integrates all weapons) Related→Three Zones of Attack

docs.title: Rear Leg Offense
docs.text:
Skill Isolation · StrikingRear Leg Offense Skill IsolationStrikingOffensiveBeginnerAccess

Land the rear leg alone, round kick, teep, side kick, forcing you to build your own setups.

StartTwo fighters at kicking range inside a marked perimeter; attacker restricted to the rear leg. → The GoalAttacker lands a clean rear leg by opening the defense; defender survives without countering. → FinishLand clean → switch · Whiffed or blocked → reset · Defender has no win condition (diagnostic only).

The hardest weapon you own, and the one that needs the most setup.

Take away the hands and the lead leg and the fighter must earn every power kick. Feint, manage distance, and commit only when the opening appears.

What to Read

Attune to the defender's guard & stance reactions to your feints & distance changes, how their shell and weight shift when you threaten a level, together with the rate of expansion (τ) of your committing rear round kick toward the opening at center mass. The opening you read isn't where the guard is; it's where it goes when you make it move.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off in a neutral fighting stance. RangeKicking range. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne attacker, one defender, switch on a clean rear leg. Start & resetThe attacker initiates; reset to neutral after each exchange.

The Matchup

🦵 Attacker

Trying to land a clean rear leg strike, rear round kick, rear teep, or rear side kick, by reading the defense and creating openings.

Rear leg only. With no faster tools to hide behind, you must build entries: feint to draw a reaction, control distance, commit to the power kick when the opening appears. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to defend all rear leg attacks effectively. No countering.

Defend naturally with the full toolkit (check, catch, evade, move). You have no win condition, your success is making the attacker work for every opening.

The Rules

🦵 Rear leg onlyThe attacker uses only the rear leg, rear round kick, rear teep, rear side kick. No hands, no lead leg. With faster weapons removed, entries and setups must be discovered. 🚫 Defender cannot counterAt Level 1 the defender plays pure defense. Removing the counter-threat lets the attacker focus entirely on commitment and setup. 🎯 All targets allowedHead, body, and legs are all fair game. Multiple target levels invite level variation within the rear-leg constraint. 📏 Maintain kicking rangeBoth players hold kicking range so rear leg power is viable with a proper setup, no drifting out to where the kick can't land. ⏱️ Reset after each attackReset to a neutral stance after a clean strike lands or after a set time, so each rep begins from the same problem.

How to Win

Switch Land clean → switch roles.When the attacker lands a clean rear leg strike, the players switch roles. A clean landing means solid contact reaching the target with real energy transfer, not a whiff, graze, or checked kick. Reset Whiffed or defended → reset, read again.When the kick is whiffed, grazed, or defended, reset to neutral, same roles. A failed attempt isn't a loss; it's information. Re-enter the probe → read → setup → commit cycle. No score Defender denies every opening → diagnostic success.The defender has no win condition. Their success is measured by quality of defense, not points, forcing the attacker to set up and commit is the goal.

The Levels

1Defense onlyDefender can't counter.Pure offensive development, rear leg only against a defender who can't counter. Build timing and entries with zero counter-threat. 2Add counter threatCounters after good defense.The defender can fire counters once they defend successfully. You land the power kick while managing the counter risk. 3Full MMA expressionCounters plus shot / clinch.The defender can counter-strike, shoot, or clinch. Land the rear leg under realistic MMA pressure with all threats live. See Full MMA Expression.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to set up the rear leg, and where is the real opening? The defender's guard and stance reactions to your feints and distance changes, plus the rate of expansion (τ) of your committing round kick at center mass. The opening is where the guard goes when you make it move, not where it sits. Q Why does the rear leg need more setup than other weapons? It's the power weapon but the slowest: with the hands and lead leg stripped away, you must feint, manage distance, and commit only when the opening appears. You earn every power kick. Q A kick gets checked or whiffs. What does that mean for the rep? It's information, not a loss. Reset to neutral, same roles, and re-enter the probe, read, setup, commit cycle. A telegraphed swing teaches you to disguise the next one. Q The defender has no win condition. What is their job? Defend naturally with the full toolkit (check, catch, evade, move) and provide realistic resistance. Their success is denying every opening so the attacker has to genuinely set up and commit.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🦵Attacker

Find openings for power kicks; vary targets head/body/legs; develop entries and setups for the rear leg; manage distance for power generation.

🛡️Defender

Defend all rear leg attacks with appropriate solutions (check, catch, evade); provide realistic resistance; never counter at Level 1.

Coaching cues

👁️"How did you set that kick up?"

Ask after each rep. This develops understanding of entry-to-finish sequencing for power kicks.

🎯Land fight-changing kicks

Rear round kick to leg/body/head, rear teep, rear side kick, feints to draw reactions. The rear leg is the power weapon but requires more setup.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Rear leg only→Forces development of power kick entries and setups Defender can't counter (L1)→Focus on setup and commitment No lead leg or hands→Must create openings without faster tools All rear-leg kicks available→Invites discovery of round kick, teep, side kick options Kicking range maintained→Rear leg power is viable with proper setup

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), removing the lead leg and hands forces discovery of power-kick entries that would normally hide behind faster weapons.

What the attacker reads

👁️Visual

Distance, stance and weight distribution, defensive posture, reaction to feints → range for power, target selection, where openings exist.

✋Haptic

Contact quality → whether the power kick landed clean.

🧭Proprioceptive

Hip rotation and balance, range to target → power generation, kick selection and commitment.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the committing rear-leg kick lands on the opening a feint or distance change created, track target-landed vs. attempts, and whether each landing followed a genuine read → open → commit cycle rather than a telegraphed swing. That coupling of opening-read to committed kick is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, disguised power kicking has formed.

Representativeness

Models: landing power kicks, leg kicks, body kicks, head kicks, in striking exchanges, the high-damage tools that require setup and timing.

Simplified: rear leg onlyno counter (L1)role switch on success

Setup and timing transfer directly into Land the Target, where all weapons integrate.

Readiness to progress

  • Lands rear leg without obvious telegraph
  • Uses feints and distance control to open
  • Varies targets leg/body/head
  • Maintains balance through power kicks
  • Composed under counter threat (L2)

Warning signs

Telegraphs kicks obviously Only throws leg kicks Rushes to close distance Power without control

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate, power kicks especially controlled 🛑 Stop on excessive force or lost composure 🔁 Reset if attacker uses non-rear-leg weapons

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is foundational Follow-on→Land the Target (integrates all weapons) Related→Three Zones of Attack

docs.title: Seated Guard Retention
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingSeated Guard Retention

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu; seated-vs-standing structure after Justin Mark / Impact Jiu-Jitsu), re-expressed with our threshold rules. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class. Details may change.

GroundGrapplingDefensiveIntermediateRetention

Keep your structure, make them post, punish the post.

StartBottom seated, feet inside the top's knees; top working to flatten or pass, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom keeps structure, causes posts, and punishes them; top flattens or passes. → FinishTop dumped to hands or seat, or shoulders connected for the back entry, held 3s → bottom · Bottom flattened to the back or passed → top · Out of bounds → loss.

A seated guard isn't lying down, it's a fighting stance on the floor.

Structure first, then offense. Every post they make is a handle you can punish.

What to Read

Attune to the moment the top's weight leaves their feet, the hand that touches the mat, the knee that drops, the lean past the toes. A post is committed weight, and committed weight can't defend itself. Your feet and frames exist to cause those posts; your offense lives in the second after one lands.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom (seated guard), one top (passer). PositionBottom seated, feet between the top's knees, hands ready; top kneeling or crouched, working forward. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesBottom retains, causes posts, and punishes them; top flattens the bottom or passes. Start & resetBegin seated and connected; reset on a dump, a back entry, a flatten, a pass, or the round cap.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Seated Guard)

Trying to keep the seated structure, off-balance the top onto hands or seat, or connect to the shoulders for the back entry.

Sit tall, feet live, hands fighting. Don't fall to the back, the seated posture IS the guard. Push and pull until a hand hits the mat, then attack the arm that posted before it recovers. VS 🥋 Top (Passer)

Trying to flatten the bottom to the back or pass around the feet to a held pin.

Drive through the center or beat the feet around the side, but keep the weight over your own base. Every reach is a post the bottom is waiting for, advance with your legs, not your hands.

The Rules

🪑 Stay seated, that's the guardThe bottom plays from the seated posture. Falling flat to the back concedes the round's central resource. Getting put flat is how the top wins, don't volunteer it. ⚖️ Bottom wins by the dump or the shouldersTwo observable wins: off-balance the top to hands or seat, or connect both hands at the top's shoulder line (the back-take entry) held 3 seconds. Both are concrete events, no judgment call. 🎯 Top wins by flatten or passPress the bottom's shoulders flat to the mat, or pass the legs to a chest-to-chest pin held 3 seconds. Hovering over the feet wins nothing. ⏱️ Round cap, no stallingRun a set round cap (start at 60 seconds). If neither side wins by the cap, reset and switch roles. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are grappling only, so the bottom can learn the post-punish rhythm without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. The top's strikes punish a dead seated guard, the bottom's structure must handle punches, not just grips. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Win Bottom dumps the top to hands or seat → bottom wins.The top's hands or seat touching the mat is the observable off-balance. The sweep mechanic, proven without needing to follow up. Win Bottom connects both hands at the shoulder line, held 3s → bottom wins.Hands connected at the top's shoulders with the hips outside the arms, the doorway to the back. Held 3 seconds, it proves the entry was real. Switch Top flattens the bottom or passes to a held pin → top wins.Shoulders pressed flat, or chest-to-chest past the legs held 3 seconds. Either ends the seated guard's round. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Hold the structureFeet in, posture tall.The round is only retention: bottom keeps the seated posture with feet inside, top works to flatten or clear the feet. Whoever owns the structure at the bell owns the round. No offense yet, learn what a strong seat feels like. 2Cause postsMake a hand touch the mat.Bottom adds the goal: push-pull until the top posts a hand or drops a knee wide. The round ends on the post. Teaches that posts are created, not waited for. 3Punish postsOwn the arm that landed.Now the post is the beginning: bottom must capture and hold the posted arm (2-on-1 or wrist-and-elbow) before it recovers. The one-second window after a post becomes the whole game. 4To the shouldersClimb the captured arm.From the captured post, the bottom climbs the connection to the shoulder line (the back entry) or converts to the dump. The full retention-to-offense chain, grappling only. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Light strikes on. The top's punches test the structure, the bottom's feet now manage range and base at once. The seated guard as MMA actually demands it.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why is falling to your back a concession? Because the seated posture is the guard. Flat on the back, your feet lose their steering, your hands lose their fight, and the top's flatten-win is half done. Q What is a post, and why is it your window? A post is committed weight, a hand on the mat, a knee dropped wide. Committed weight can't defend itself. The arm that posted is yours for about a second, take it before it recovers. Q What are the bottom's two observable wins? The dump (top's hands or seat touch the mat) and the shoulder connection (both hands at the shoulder line, 3 seconds, the back entry). Q How should the top advance without feeding posts? With the legs, not the hands. Weight stays over the base; every reach is a handle. Advance in angles the feet can't follow rather than leaning through them.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Seated Guard)

Sit tall, keep the feet live and inside, hand-fight, cause the post, capture it, climb it to the shoulders or convert to the dump.

🥋Top (Passer)

Advance with the base, deny handles, flatten through the center or beat the feet around the side into a held pin.

Coaching cues

🪑Still seated?

Ask the bottom: "When did you end up flat, and who put you there?" Distinguishes being flattened from volunteering.

✋Who made that post?

Ask both: "Did the post happen, or did the bottom cause it?" Directs attention to off-balancing as an action, not luck.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Retention-only opening level→Builds the structure before any offense exists Cause-then-punish post ladder→Turns off-balancing into a created event with a follow-up window Two observable bottom wins→The bottom is the protagonist, never a survivor Round cap→The top must engage, no hovering at the feet Live, resisting passer→Keeps the weight-commit read intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): the cause → capture → climb ladder decomposes guard offense into perceptual stages against a live passer. The post-punish window trains the timing attunement that makes seated guards dangerous rather than merely durable.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Pressure through the feet and grips → which way the top's weight is leaning, when it passes their toes.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own seat and spine → whether the structure is tall or collapsing toward the back.

👁️Visual

The top's hands → the post landing, the one-second window opening.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom creates and converts posts faster than the top can recover them. Track dumps and shoulder connections vs. flattens and passes conceded, and whether posts get punished or just observed. When the bottom's offense reliably starts within a second of a caused post, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: playing guard against a passer who can also strike, the seated structure is the only guard posture that manages both the pass and the punch, which is why MMA guards sit up.

Simplified: post ladderno strikes L1-4round cap

Deepens the retention side of Leg Reclaim; mirrors Open Guard Pass.

Readiness to progress

  • Holds the seated structure under real forward pressure
  • Causes posts on purpose, not by accident
  • Captures the posted arm inside the window
  • Converts captures into the dump or the shoulder connection

Warning signs

Falls to the back at the first pressure Watches posts land without attacking them Feet go passive, stop steering Top hovers out of range to run the clock

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 No jumping passes over the seated player 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Leg Reclaim Follow-on→Closed Guard Bottom Mirror→Open Guard Pass Related→Guard Recovery · Decision States

docs.title: Side-Control Escape
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingSide-Control Escape GroundGrapplingDefensiveIntermediateCounter

Recover a guard or get to your knees before side control advances.

StartBottom flat under side control, top chest-to-chest, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalBottom frames, gets a knee in or turns to the knees; top holds and works toward mount or the back. → FinishKnee back in (half guard) or better, turn up, or reverse → bottom · Advance to mount or back, or hold the full count → top · Out of bounds → loss.

The pin loosens when they reach, that's your frame and your knee.

Make a frame, find the space, drive the knee through it. The moment they climb to advance, the chest goes light.

What to Read

Attune to the chest connection and the cross-face pressure, where the top's weight loads and the instant it lightens as they reach for an underhook, the cross-face, or a climb to mount. That shift specifies when the knee can come through and which way to turn, not the top's hands in isolation. When they reach to advance, the pin opens.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one bottom (defender), one top (side control). PositionBottom flat on the back, top chest-to-chest and perpendicular, hips low. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesBottom frames and recovers; top maintains side control and works toward mount or the back. Start & resetBegin from settled side control; reset on escape, advance, or the count.

The Matchup

🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to frame the cross-face, get a knee back in (half guard or better), turn to the knees, or reverse.

Build the near elbow and forearm frames against the cross-face and hip. Use them to make space, then shrimp the knee through or turn in to the knees. Frames first, then move, never bridge with the arms flat. VS 🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to hold side control and advance to mount or the back.

Stay chest-to-chest and heavy, kill the near frames, switch hips to follow the escape. Control is proven by advancing to mount or taking the back, not by resting on top.

The Rules

🤜 Bottom frames and escapes, no subsThe bottom works frames, hip escapes, and turns continuously. No submissions from the bottom, the task is recovery, not the counter-attack. 🎯 Top wins by advancingThe top proves control by improving to mount or taking the back, not by sitting still. This gives the top an active, observable target instead of a stall, and keeps the pressure realistic. ⏱️ Hold the count or escapeIf the top keeps side control for the set count (start at 20 seconds) without advancing, the round resets. If the bottom recovers a guard, turns up, or reverses first, the bottom wins. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 3 are control only, so the bottom can read pressure and build frames without defending strikes. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Use striking as a disincentivization tool, the cost that punishes stalling and lazy structure. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Switch Bottom gets a knee back between (half guard) or better, turns up to the knees, or reverses → bottom wins, switch roles.Recovery is graded: one knee in (half guard) is the floor, then both knees, both feet, then a closed loop. Turning up to the knees or a reversal is a full escape. See Guard Recovery. Win Top advances to mount or takes the back → top wins.Improving the position past side control is the observable proof that the pin has beaten the frames, and it is the real danger the bottom is racing. Reset Top holds side control the full count, no advance → reset, same roles.The top kept the pin but never improved before the count expired. The round resets from settled side control for another rep. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position, training the mat-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Chest control onlyHands and head, no grips.Top holds with hands and head on the chest, no underhook, no cross-face. The lightest pin, so the bottom can find the near elbow frame and the knee-in without fighting heavy grips first. 2Add the far underhookOne arm threatens.Top adds a far-side underhook, threatening to climb and turn the corner. The bottom must keep the near frame while denying the underhook's lift. Reading the climb becomes the task. 3Add the cross-faceHeaviest pin.Top adds the cross-face, turning the bottom's head away and killing the near frame. Now the bottom must rebuild the frame against the cross-face before any escape, the full-weight problem. 4Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Continuous from settled side control with light strikes on. The strike threat makes a slow frame costly, recover or turn up under real urgency.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q When does the chest connection go light enough to bring the knee through? When the top reaches for the underhook, the cross-face, or climbs to advance. Reaching to improve unloads the chest. When they reach, the pin opens. Q Why does the top win by advancing rather than just holding? Improving to mount or the back is observable and is the real danger of side control. It gives the top an active target instead of a stall and keeps the bottom's frames honest. Q What has to happen before any escape, once the cross-face is in? Rebuild the near frame against the cross-face. The cross-face turns your head and kills the frame, so the frame comes back first, then the space, then the knee. Q What is the floor that counts as a recovered guard here? One knee back between (half guard). More is cleaner (both knees, feet in, closed loop), and turning up to the knees or reversing is a full escape.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Build the near elbow and forearm frames, deny the cross-face and underhook, make space, shrimp the knee in or turn to the knees, chain the next frame when one fails.

🥋Top (Attacker)

Stay chest-to-chest and heavy, kill the near frames, switch hips to follow the escape, climb toward mount or the back.

Coaching cues

🧱Frame before move

Ask the bottom: "Did the frame come first?" Stops the flat-armed bridge that just feeds the cross-face.

🧗Advance or rest?

Ask the top: "Did you improve, or just sit?" Keeps the top climbing to mount or the back instead of stalling for the count.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top control stepped per level (chest → underhook → cross-face)→Isolates one piece of the pin at a time Top wins by advancing→Keeps the pressure realistic, gives the top an active target Hold the count or escape→Urgency for the bottom, no stalling for the top No strikes until the top level→Frees the bottom to read pressure before adding strike defense Live, resisting top→Keeps the pressure-reading perception intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): stepping the top's control isolates one frame problem per level while the bottom keeps reading pressure and timing from a real, resisting opponent. The top's advance condition keeps the representativeness, this is the actual danger of side control.

What the bottom reads

✋Haptic

Chest load and cross-face pressure → when the pin lightens and which way to turn.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own frame strength and hip position → whether space exists yet and which escape is open.

👁️Visual

Top reaching or climbing to advance → the timing window to insert the knee or turn up.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the bottom rebuilds a frame and recovers a knee faster than the top can advance or re-settle the cross-face. Track recoveries and turn-ups vs. advances to mount or the back, and whether the near frame stays live as the top switches hips. The frame-and-recover versus settle-and-advance race is the order parameter; when the bottom consistently times the knee to the lightened pin, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: being pinned in side control and recovering guard or turning up before the top improves to mount, the back, or starts striking, the exact problem under side control in competition and in MMA.

Simplified: stepped controlno strikes L1-3reset on the count

Deepens the side-control level of Ground Escape; pairs with Mount Escape and feeds Leg Reclaim and Ground to Standing.

Readiness to progress

  • Rebuilds the near frame even against the cross-face
  • Times the knee to the top's reach, not randomly
  • Picks knee-in vs. turn-up by where the space is
  • Chains a second frame when the first fails

Warning signs

Bridges with flat arms, no frame Gives up the back turning away Waits and survives instead of framing Lets the cross-face flatten the head

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling 🛑 Stop on submission attempts or neck cranks 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Escape Follow-on→Mount Escape · Leg Reclaim · Ground to Standing Related→Decision States · Guard Recovery

docs.title: Side-Control Ride
docs.text:
Ground · GrapplingSide-Control Ride

Provisional (WIP): built from the ground-wave spec, pending coach review

The top-side mirror of Side-Control Escape. Sourced from the Slime Mold Grappling Club catalog (Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu), re-expressed with our threshold rules and GnP doctrine. Passed the build rubric on paper; awaits validation against a live grappling class.

GroundGrapplingOffensiveIntermediateControl → Finish

Hold side control and turn it into an arm or a better position before the bottom frames a knee back in.

StartTop chest-to-chest in side control, near arm under the head, far under the armpit, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalTop kills the frames and isolates a bent arm, or advances to mount; bottom frames and recovers. → FinishBent-arm control 3s or advance to mount → top · Knee in, turn up, or reverse → bottom · Out of bounds → loss.

A pin that goes nowhere is just a rest.

Stay chest-to-chest, kill the near frames, then climb to the arm or to mount. Prove control by advancing, not by sitting.

What to Read

Attune to the bottom's near elbow and the gap at the hip, where their frames load and the instant one arm drifts off the body. That shift specifies when a bent arm is there to trap and when the hips can switch to mount, not a memorized pin. When they push to frame, the arm they push with is the arm to take.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one top (attacker, side control), one bottom (defender). PositionTop chest-to-chest and perpendicular, near arm under the head, far arm under the armpit, hands connected; bottom flat. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesTop maintains side control and works a bent-arm or an advance; bottom frames and recovers. Start & resetBegin from settled side control; reset on a bent-arm hold, an advance, an escape, or the count.

The Matchup

🥋 Top (Attacker)

Trying to hold side control and isolate a bent arm (kimura or americana), or advance to mount.

Stay chest-to-chest and heavy, kill the near elbow and hip frames, switch your hips to follow the escape. When a frame extends an arm, trap it in a figure-four. Control is proven by the bent arm or the advance, not by resting. VS 🤸 Bottom (Defender)

Trying to frame, get a knee back in, turn to the knees, or escape to seated or full guard.

Keep the near elbow tight so it can't be isolated, frame the cross-face and hip, shrimp the knee through. Don't extend an arm into space, that's the arm they trap.

The Rules

🎯 Top wins by a bent arm or an advanceThe top proves control by isolating one arm in a bent-arm figure-four (kimura / americana) held 3 seconds, or by improving to mount. Holding side control without a threat is a stall, not a win. 🤜 Bottom wins by recoveringThe bottom wins by getting a knee back in (half guard or better), turning up to the knees, escaping to seated or full guard, or reversing. Mirrors Side-Control Escape. ⏱️ Hold the count or finishIf the top keeps side control for the set count (start at 20 seconds) without a bent arm or an advance, the round resets. If the bottom recovers first, the bottom wins. A clock, never "as long as possible". 🚫 No striking until the top levelLevels 1 to 4 are control only, so both players read frames and weight before strikes are added. Strikes enter at the full-expression level. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionOnce strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Strikes are the disincentivization tool that punishes a lazy bottom structure. Ground games train smashing, not grappling for its own sake. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape. If a player rolls fully out of it, that player loses the round, training mat-edge awareness.

How to Win

Win Top isolates a bent arm (3s) or advances to mount → top wins.A figure-four on one arm (kimura or americana) held three seconds is the shared entry to the side-control submission game; advancing to mount is the positional win. Either is the observable proof that the pin beat the frames. Finish slow, tap early. Switch Bottom recovers a knee, turns up, or reverses → bottom wins, switch roles.Recovery is graded: a knee in (half guard) is the floor, then seated or full guard, then a reversal. See Guard Recovery. Reset Top holds side control the full count, no threat → reset, same roles.The top kept the pin but never isolated an arm or advanced before the count expired. Resets from settled side control. Loss Roll fully out of the perimeter → that player loses.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the round instantly, regardless of position.

The Levels

1Chest-to-chest pinDeny the rotation.Top settles chest-to-chest and simply denies the bottom turning in or framing a knee through. Control only. Builds the heavy connection everything else rests on. 2Isolate one armTrap the bent arm.Top reads an extended frame and traps that arm in a figure-four (kimura / americana), held 3 seconds. Reading which arm the bottom commits becomes the task. 3Double-arm threatAttack both sides.Top hunts the bent arm on either side, switching the attack as the bottom defends one. Keeps the bottom's elbows honest on both sides at once. 4Open side to mountSwitch the hips and climb.Top loosens to a knee-on-belly or open side control and advances to mount as the base lightens. Adds the positional advance as a second win path. 5Full expressionContinuous, strikes on.Continuous from settled side control, strikes live, until the top isolates an arm, advances, or the bottom recovers. The bottom's escapes are now urgent.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Why does the top win by a bent arm or an advance, not by holding? Holding is a stall. A bent arm or a climb to mount is the observable proof the pin is doing work, and it keeps the top hunting instead of resting. Q What hands the top the arm to trap? An extended frame. When the bottom pushes with a straightening arm, that arm is exposed, trap it in the figure-four. Q What does staying chest-to-chest buy the top? It kills the bottom's space to turn in and keeps the weight connected, so every frame the bottom makes has to fight your chest first.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥋Top (Attacker)

Stay chest-to-chest and heavy, kill the near frames, switch hips to follow, trap the bent arm when a frame extends, climb to mount as the base lightens.

🤸Bottom (Defender)

Keep the near elbow tight, frame the cross-face and hip, shrimp the knee through, never extend an arm into space.

Coaching cues

🔗Arm or advance?

Ask the top: "Did you take the arm or improve, or just sit?" Keeps the ride hunting a finish instead of stalling for the count.

🧲Stay connected

Ask the top: "Where did your chest go light?" Direct attention to the connection, the moment it lifts is the moment the bottom turns in.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Top wins by a bent arm or an advance→Forces an active finish, no stalling Bent-arm hold, 3s→A clean, observable control proof short of a crank Hold the count or finish→Urgency for the top, a real window for the bottom Live, framing bottom→Keeps the frame-reading perception intact

Implements Task Simplification (Renshaw et al., 2019): each level isolates one control variable (deny rotation, isolate one arm, both arms, advance) while the top keeps reading frames from a live, resisting opponent. The arm-or-advance win keeps the representativeness, side control only matters if it leads somewhere.

What the top reads

✋Haptic

The bottom's frames and the gap at the hip → which arm is exposed, when the hips can switch.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own chest connection and base → whether the pin is still heavy, when a climb is safe.

👁️Visual

An extended arm, a turning shoulder → the figure-four entry and the path to mount.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the top isolates an arm or advances faster than the bottom recovers a knee or turns in. Track arms and advances vs. escapes, and whether the top stays chest-to-chest through the bottom's frames. The trap-or-advance versus frame-and-recover race is the order parameter.

Representativeness

Models: holding side control and converting it to a submission or a better position before the bottom recovers, the exact problem on top in MMA and grappling.

Simplified: control ladderno strikes L1-4reset on the count

Mirror of Side-Control Escape; deepens the top side of Ground Control; the bent-arm transfers toward the side-control submission game.

Readiness to progress

  • Stays chest-to-chest through the bottom's frames
  • Traps the arm a frame extends, not a forced crank
  • Switches hips to follow the escape
  • Climbs to mount when the base lightens

Warning signs

Sits and holds instead of threatening Chest lifts and the bottom turns in Cranks the arm instead of isolating it Never switches hips to follow

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled grappling, GnP by coach permission 🛑 Bent-arm locks slow, tap early, no cranks 🔁 Reset if the position stalls completely

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground Control · Side-Control Escape Follow-on→Mount Maintenance Related→TKO Pin · Decision States

docs.title: Slip the Straight
docs.text:
Skill Isolation · StrikingSlip the Straight Skill IsolationStrikingDefensiveBeginnerOpen Space

Make straight punches miss with head movement, slip only, no block, no duck, no backing up.

StartTwo fighters at close range, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalAttacker lands a clean straight to the head; defender moves the head offline so it misses. → FinishClean slip → switch · Land clean → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

Don't block it. Don't run from it, move your head and make it miss.

Take away every defense but the slip and head movement is forced to develop on its own. Read the rate the glove grows, not the eyes, and move offline in time.

What to Read

Attune to the rate of expansion (τ) of the incoming straight, how fast the glove grows as it travels toward the head, read via shoulder–hip motion at center mass, not the opponent's eyes or their absolute distance. That information specifies when the punch arrives and which way to move the head offline.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off in a neutral fighting stance. RangeInside punching distance. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne attacker, one defender, switch on a clean slip. Start & resetThe attacker initiates; reset to center after each exchange.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to land a clean straight punch, jab or cross, to the defender's head.

Straights to the head only, no hooks, uppercuts, or loops, no body shots. You set the problem: vary timing and which hand, add feints at higher levels, and ramp up only as the defender succeeds. VS 🌀 Defender

Trying to move the head laterally offline so each straight passes by clean.

No blocking, no ducking, no backing up, pure slipping. Small, efficient movements beat large dramatic ones; absorb while you read, then commit when confident.

The Rules

🥊 Straights to the head onlyThe attacker throws only straight punches to the head, no hooks, uppercuts, or loops, and no body shots. Slipping works only on straight lines, and head targets keep the focus on head movement. 🌀 Slip only, no block, no duckThe defender may only move the head laterally offline. Blocking, ducking, parrying, and backing up are off-limits, so the slipping solution is forced to develop. 🚫 No continuous backing upThe defender can't keep retreating out of range to avoid exchanges, they must stay and solve the problem in the pocket. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly. ⏱️ Reset between strikesAt early levels the attacker pauses between strikes so the defender can recenter. Pressure becomes continuous as the levels rise.

How to Win

Switch Slip clean → switch roles.When the defender slips a punch cleanly, a full miss, not a graze, the players switch roles. The punch passed by the head without contact, and the defender earns the attacking role. Reset Land clean → reset to center.When the attacker lands a clean punch to the head, reset to center, same roles. A clean, significant straight that got through. Begin again from a neutral stance, head on center. Loss Leave the perimeter → loss.When both of a player's feet leave the perimeter, that player loses, whoever they are. Crossing the marked perimeter loses the game instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the cage-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Single punch, fixed tempoOne straight at a time, reset between.Pure timing practice, one straight punch, fully reset between reps, no feints. Build the slip with zero time pressure. 2Variable tempoSingle punches, no fixed reset.The attacker varies timing with no guaranteed reset, the defender has to read rhythm and stay switched on. 3Add feintsReal vs. fake.The attacker can feint before throwing. Slipping a feint is wasted movement, the defender must distinguish real from fake. 4Two-punch combinationsJab-cross.Two-punch combos, still straights only, slip both punches and recover center between them. 5Counter after slipOne counter allowed.After a successful slip the defender may throw one counter, teaching the slip as a setup for offense, not just survival. 6Full MMAAdd shot / clinch threat.The attacker can now shoot or clinch too, slip while denying the grappling entry, staying aware of all threats.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to time the slip, and what does it tell you? The rate of expansion (τ) of the straight (how fast the glove grows) read via shoulder and hip motion at center mass, not the eyes or absolute distance. It specifies when the punch arrives and which way to move the head offline. Q Don't block it, don't run from it: so what is the defender's only job? Move the head laterally offline so the straight passes by clean. Stripping away block, duck, and retreat forces the slip to develop on its own. Q What's the difference between a clean slip and a graze, and why does size of movement matter? A clean slip is a full miss, the punch passes completely. A graze doesn't count. Small, efficient slips beat big dramatic ones, and recover to center faster. Q Which way do you slip, and off what cue? Slip to the opposite side of the loading hand: read the shoulder rotation and which hand loads at center mass to pick the direction.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Land clean straights to the head; vary timing and which hand; scale difficulty to the partner.

🌀Defender

Move the head left or right to make the punch miss; slip early; return to center after each slip.

Coaching cues

👁️See center mass

Don't track the gloves or the eyes. Center mass keeps shoulder & hip motion in view, "the little X", and shows which hand is loading. The eyes lie.

🎯Miss, don't graze

The punch should pass by completely, not brush you. Small, efficient slips beat big dramatic ones, and one clean miss beats ten late ones.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Straights only→Defender perceives slip-able attacks Slip-only→Forces exploration of head-movement solutions Head strikes only→Clear target zone for both roles Reset between exchanges→Time to recenter and process Close range→Slipping is necessary and viable

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), different fighters develop different slip patterns based on height, reaction time, and stance.

What the defender reads

👁️Visual

Shoulder rotation & which hand loads → direction to slip (opposite side).

🧭Proprioceptive

Head position relative to centerline → confirming the evasion.

⚖️Balance

Balance during and after the slip → recovery to neutral.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender's slip lands in time with the punch, track clean misses vs. shots eaten, and whether the head re-sets to center between strikes. That timing relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: making straight punches miss with head movement in open-space exchanges.

Simplified: straights onlyhead onlyslip only

Isolates the solution before integration, transfers into Evade the Punch.

Readiness to progress

  • Clean misses ~70%+ (not grazes)
  • Slips both jab and cross
  • Returns to center without being reminded
  • Can describe what they read ("I see the shoulder")

Warning signs

Slips too late and gets grazed Only slips one direction Slips drift into blocking

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on freezing, lost composure, or excessive force 🔁 Reset if the defender starts blocking instead of slipping

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is foundational Follow-on→Evade the Punch · Close-Range Defense Related→Defensive Solutions

docs.title: Stand-Up and Re-Pin Loop
docs.text:
Wall / Ground · WrestlingWall Stand-Up & Re-Pin Loop Wall / GroundWrestlingCombinedAdvancedCounter ↔ Stabilize

Wall positions cycle rather than resolve, the defender stands and disengages while the attacker re-pins or flattens, and the skill is staying in the part of the loop that favors you.

StartDefender seated, back to the wall; attacker in top control with one collar-tie and a near-side underhook. → The GoalDefender stands, faces, disengages or reverses; attacker re-pins, flattens, or pins for TKO. → FinishStand & disengage or reverse → defender · Re-pin, flatten, or TKO pin → attacker.

The position doesn't end, it loops.

Each stand-up forces control to be re-earned. Win by living where the cycle favors you.

Key Positions

🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

What to Read

Attune to the felt resistance to rotation through the attacker's grips and the looseness in their control, the moments the load eases as they adjust their grip or shift weight. That changing resistance specifies when to initiate the stand-up and which way to turn, not the attacker's eyes or a fixed count.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one defender standing up, one attacker preventing it. PositionDefender seated, hips and back to the wall; attacker in top control with one collar-tie and a near-side underhook, a live hold, not a locked pin. (Turtle and elbow-post are start variants, Level 3+.) BoundaryWall or cage within a marked perimeter, on mats. RolesDefender stands, faces, disengages or reverses; attacker re-pins, flattens, or pins for TKO. Start & resetBegin compromised; reset on full disengage or TKO pin.

The Matchup

🛡️ Defender

Trying to stand using the wall, turn to face, and disengage or reverse.

Use the wall to build base and rise. Turn to face before disengaging, disengaging while facing away exposes the back. Time the stand-up to the attacker's adjustment moments. VS 🧱 Attacker

Trying to prevent disengagement, re-pin, flatten, or reach a TKO pin.

Follow the stand-up under control, not desperately. Decide between re-pinning to the wall or flattening back to the ground based on the defender's facing direction. Control is re-earned every cycle.

The Rules

🧱 Wall access always availableThe wall is always reachable for the defender, the stand-up option always exists. It is the core environmental constraint of the game. 🚪 Disengagement ends the exchangeWhen the defender disengages, the exchange is over and the defender wins. This keeps the success criteria clear. 🔁 Control is re-earned each cycleAfter every stand-up attempt the attacker must re-earn control, this is what creates the cyclical, looping dynamic. ↪️ Turn to face before disengagingTurn to face before attempting to disengage, disengaging while facing away exposes the back to re-clinch and striker access. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens against a wall or cage within a marked perimeter, any shape. Both players stay inside it.

How to Win

Defender Stand & disengage facing, or reverse → defender wins.Standing and facing the attacker with space to reset, or a clean reversal that puts the attacker on the defensive. Attacker Re-pin, flatten, or TKO pin → attacker wins.When the attacker re-pins to the wall, flattens back to ground control, or reaches a TKO pin. Multiple win conditions reflect the cyclical nature of the position, the attacker can win at any point in the loop.

The Levels

1Stand-upRise using the wall.No strikes. Success is standing with the wall behind you, the rising-from-compromised mechanic itself. 2Stand-up + disengageTurn, face, create space.Must turn to face and create space; attacker follows aggressively. Success is facing the attacker with space to reset. 3Stand-up + reversalTake the dominant position.Defender attempts to pin the attacker. Highest difficulty, requires reading the attacker's balance. 4Full MMAStrikes both ways.Light strikes, attacker discourages stand-up and re-establishes control, defender creates stand-up openings. Ground-and-pound threat makes cycling urgent.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q As the defender, what are you reading to know when to stand and which way to turn? The felt resistance to rotation through the attacker's grips and the looseness in their control: the moments the load eases as they adjust their grip or shift weight. Not their eyes or a fixed count. Q Why turn to face before disengaging? Disengaging while facing away exposes the back to a re-clinch and striker access. Turn to face first, then create space. Q The position loops rather than ends. What does that mean for how you win? Control is re-earned every cycle: each stand-up resets the contest. You win by living where the cycle favors you, not by expecting a single clean resolution. Q As the attacker, what decides whether you re-pin or flatten? The defender's facing direction: re-pin them to the wall or flatten them back to the ground based on how they have turned. Follow the stand-up under control, not desperately.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🛡️Defender

Build base and stand using the wall, time the rise to the attacker's adjustment, turn to face before disengaging, look for the reversal.

🧱Attacker

Follow under control, choose re-pin vs. flatten by the defender's facing direction, re-earn control each cycle, work toward a TKO pin.

Coaching cues

↪️Turn before disengaging

Ask defenders: "Did you turn before disengaging?" Disengaging while facing away exposes the back, turn to face first.

🔀Re-pin or flatten?

Ask attackers: "Did you re-pin or flatten?" to develop the decision between returning to the wall and returning to the ground.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Start from compromised ground position→Realistic disadvantaged starting point Wall access always available→The stand-up option always exists Disengagement ends the exchange→Clear success criteria Control re-earned each cycle→Creates the cyclical loop dynamic Wall / ground hybrid→Both environmental constraints active at once

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), develops loop perception: positions cycle rather than resolve cleanly, and the wall affords different solutions to each player.

What the players read

✋Haptic

Defender: attacker's adjustment moments and control looseness → stand-up timing. Attacker: stand-up initiation → follow/re-pin timing.

👁️Visual

Defender: wall position → stand-up direction. Attacker: defender's base building and facing direction → re-pin or flatten decision.

🧭Proprioceptive

Defender: own base and facing direction → stand-up method and turn-before-disengage readiness.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the attacker keeps the defender's shoulders and hips pinned flat vs. free to rotate up and away across each cycle, track control time held through the loop and whether the defender turns to face before disengaging. The press→pin-or-stand phase transition is the order parameter, repeating each cycle; the skill has formed when the defender consistently times the rise to the moments control loosens.

Representativeness

Models: the grinding stand-up battle near the cage, positions cycling between ground control, stand-up attempts, and re-clinching, identical to competition.

Simplified: start compromisedclear win conditionsprogressive striking

Builds on Wall to Ground; feeds future ground games as TKO Pin access emerges.

Readiness to progress

  • Stands using the wall efficiently
  • Turns to face before disengaging
  • Manages re-pin attempts
  • Understands loop dynamics
  • Can describe: "I stood when they adjusted…"

Warning signs

Can't stand at all Gets re-pinned immediately Disengages facing away Always gets flattened

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on lost composure or unsafe scramble 🔁 Reset if cycling stalls without progress

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Wall to Ground Follow-on→Future ground games · TKO Pin emerges Related→TKO Pin · Full MMA Expression

docs.title: Sustained Offense
docs.text:
Open Space · StrikingSustained Offense Open SpaceStrikingOffensiveIntermediateAccess → Stabilize

Keep striking through the defender's counters, escapes, and clinches, continue, never reset to one-and-done.

StartAttacker presses forward inside a marked perimeter; defender sits in a defensive posture. → The GoalAttacker sustains offense through resistance; defender breaks the rhythm to force a reset. → FinishSustain the sequence → switch · Rhythm fully broken → reset, same roles · Single shots that reset don't count.

One shot and stop loses, don't break the rhythm.

Every defensive move the opponent makes is the next opening, not a reason to reset. Slip, follow, shed the clinch, and keep striking through it.

What to Read

Attune to the defender's guard reactions to your pressure, their counter, movement, or clinch attempt, how their shell shifts as you threaten, together with the rate of expansion (τ) of your next committing shot toward the opening at center mass. Each defensive move is itself a read that opens the next strike, the rhythm is a chain of openings, not a single one.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, attacker on the front foot, defender in a defensive posture. RangeStriking range, open space. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne attacker, one defender, switch on a sustained sequence. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset to neutral only when the rhythm is fully broken.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to maintain continuous striking offense through the defender's attempts to interrupt.

Chain strikes into combinations. When they counter, slip/absorb and continue; when they move, follow; when they clinch, disengage and continue. The key: continue without a full reset. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to interrupt the attacker's rhythm through counters, movement, or clinch.

Active resistance, not passive defense. Counter after defensive moments, move to create space, clinch to break rhythm, shoot to threaten the takedown. You win by forcing a full reset.

The Rules

🔁 Attacker must continueSingle shots followed by a full reset don't count. The attacker has to chain offense, brief defensive moments are allowed as long as the rhythm continues. ⚡ Defender actively interruptsThe defender is not a passive target, they actively try to break rhythm with counters, movement, or clinch. This creates the realistic resistance the attacker maintains against. 🌊 Brief defensive moments allowedThe attacker can slip, absorb, or frame mid-flow, defensive awareness within offense is expected and desired, as long as it doesn't become a full reset. ⛔ Full reset = defender succeedsIf the attacker's rhythm is fully broken and they re-engage from scratch, the defender has won that exchange. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). The attacker follows movement without chasing out of bounds.

How to Win

Switch Sustain the sequence → switch roles.When the attacker sustains a continuous offensive sequence (e.g., 10+ seconds of unbroken pressure), the players switch roles. A sustained sequence means rhythm maintained through resistance, counters slipped, movement followed, clinches shed, without a full reset. Reset Rhythm fully broken → reset, same roles.When the defender forces the attacker to fully reset and re-engage, reset to neutral, same roles, and the defender keeps trying to break rhythm. A broken rhythm isn't a loss of the game; it's information. Re-enter with pressure, reading where the next opening lives. Diagnostic Continued vs. stopped is the read.Whether each defensive moment was absorbed-and-continued or turned into a stop, "I slipped and continued" vs. "I got hit and reset." The goal is continuity, not perfection. Brief defensive moments within offense are expected; the question is whether the attacker continued through them or broke off.

The Levels

1Counter strikes onlyDefender can only counter.The defender's only interruption is the counter-strike. The attacker learns seamless defense within offense, slip/absorb and continue. 2Add movementCounter AND move to escape.The defender can now move to create space. The attacker follows while maintaining rhythm, pressure without chasing. 3Add clinchCounter, move, AND clinch.The defender can clinch to break rhythm. The attacker must disengage and continue, managing the clinch attempt without resetting. 4Full MMA expressionCounter, move, clinch, OR shoot.The defender adds the takedown threat. Sustain offense under MMA reality with all interruptions live. See Full MMA Expression.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q How should you read the defender's counter, movement, or clinch? Each defensive move is itself a read that opens the next strike. The rhythm is a chain of openings, not a single one: read the guard shift, then commit the next shot to where it goes. Q One shot and stop loses. What actually wins the exchange? Sustaining the sequence through resistance: counters slipped, movement followed, clinches shed, without a full reset. A single shot that resets to one-and-done doesn't count. Q Brief defensive moments are allowed. When does one become a defender win? A slip, absorb, or frame mid-flow is fine: defense within offense is expected. It becomes a defender win only when the rhythm is fully broken and you re-engage from scratch. Q What's the diagnostic question after each exchange? "Did you continue or reset?" ("I slipped and continued" vs. "I got hit and reset"). The goal is continuity, not perfection, and not chasing or throwing blindly through resistance.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Chain strikes into combinations; when the defender counters, evade/absorb and continue; when they move, follow and continue; when they clinch, disengage and continue.

🛡️Defender

Counter after defensive moments; move to create space; clinch to break rhythm; shoot to threaten the takedown. Active interruption, not passive defense.

Coaching cues

👁️"Did you continue or reset?"

Ask after each exchange. This develops awareness of rhythm breaks, the goal is continuity, not perfection.

🌊Seamless defense within offense

Slip a counter, follow movement, shed a clinch, sprawl on a shot, then keep striking. The defensive moment is part of the flow, not a stop.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Attacker must continue (no full reset)→Develops seamless defense-within-offense Defender actively interrupts→Creates realistic resistance to maintain rhythm against Progressive defender tools→Scaffolds learning of pressure maintenance Duration-based win→Rewards continuous pressure, not single moments Open space with movement→Attacker must follow, not just stand and throw

Develops attacking degeneracy, multiple ways to maintain offensive rhythm. The constraints force "adaptability under pressure" (Renshaw et al., 2019): solving the continuous problem of maintaining offense, not running a fixed combination.

What the attacker reads

👁️Visual

Counter initiation, movement direction, clinch setup, level change → slip/absorb, follow, disengage, or sprawl while continuing.

✋Haptic

Contact on blocks/parries → adjust targets without stopping.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own rhythm and balance → maintain flow through defensive moments.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether each committing shot lands on the opening the defender's reaction created, track sustained sequences vs. full resets, and whether each strike followed a genuine read → open → commit chain rather than chasing or throwing through resistance. That continuity of opening-read to committed strike across interruptions is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, sustained pressure has formed.

Representativeness

Models: pressing an opponent with sustained combinations while they try to escape, counter, clinch, or shoot, the pressure-fighting phase of MMA.

Progressive defender toolscontrolled contactclear duration win

Rhythm maintenance transfers directly into Pressure to Takedown and Pressure to Wall and live sparring.

Readiness to progress

  • Maintains rhythm through current tools
  • Defensive moments don't break flow
  • Follows movement without resetting
  • Articulates "slipped and continued" vs. "hit and reset"
  • Rhythm consistent across defenders

Warning signs

Full resets after every exchange Chasing the defender frantically Gets hit cleanly and stops Ignores counters, throws through them

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on reckless chasing or excessive force 🔁 Reset if exchanges become chaotic

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Land the Target Follow-on→Pressure to Takedown · Pressure to Wall Related→Three Zones · Confidence Rating

docs.title: Takedown Defense
docs.text:
Open Space · WrestlingTakedown Defense Open SpaceWrestlingDefensiveIntermediateMats

Stop the shot and get back to neutral, denial, not ground escape.

StartTwo fighters at clinch range, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalAttacker completes a takedown; defender denies it & returns to standing. → FinishDeny & disengage → switch · Takedown completes → reset · Leave perimeter → loss.

Don't escape the takedown, deny it.

Once it completes, the game's over. Read the shot, sprawl in time, get back to neutral.

What to Read

Attune to the rate of closure (τ) of the level change / shot, the hip and level drop as the attacker commits, not their feet or their eyes. That information specifies when the shot arrives and when to sprawl.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, squared off at clinch range. RangeClinch / wrestling distance. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesAttacker vs. defender, switch when the defender denies and disengages. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset on takedown or full disengage.

The Matchup

🤼 Attacker

Trying to complete a takedown to ground control, shots, trips, throws.

Shoot singles and doubles; set up with level changes; chain attempts when the first is defended; follow through on successful entries. Scale to the defender's success. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to deny the takedown and return to standing neutral, not escape from the ground.

Recognize the shot early, sprawl, use whizzer / crossface / frames, then disengage cleanly. No striking at Level 1, wrestling defense only. DNS allowed.

The Rules

🤼 Attacker initiates takedownsThe attacker shoots, no waiting for the defender to attack. This keeps the defender's job reactive: read and defend the shot. 🛡️ Wrestling defense only, no strikes (L1)At Level 1 the defender uses wrestling defense only, no strikes. This isolates sprawl mechanics and recovery before striking is integrated. 🏁 Game ends on takedownIf the defender is taken down with control, play ends, this is not a ground escape game. The skill is denying the takedown, not recovering from it. 🦈 DNS allowedThe defender may use the guillotine / front-headlock (DNS) as a defensive tool, punishing poor head position on the shot. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly.

How to Win

Switch Deny & disengage to standing → switch roles.The defender stopped the shot and got back to neutral standing, and earns the attacking role. Reset Takedown completes with control → reset, same roles.A takedown finished with control on the ground. Then begin again from standing at clinch range. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the game instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the cage-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1No strikesPure wrestling defense.Pure wrestling defense, no strikes either side. Build sprawl mechanics and recovery with no other pressure. 2Attacker sets upFeints and level changes.The attacker adds feints and level changes to set up shots. The defender must read real vs. fake, shot recognition. 3Add DNSGuillotine / front headlock.The defender can threaten the guillotine / front headlock, punishing poor head position. DNS as a defensive weapon. 4Full MMAStrike and sprawl.The defender can strike and sprawl; the attacker must shoot through the strike threat. Takedown defense under full MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to know when the shot arrives and when to sprawl? The rate of closure (τ) of the level change or shot: the hip and level drop as the attacker commits. Not their feet, not their eyes. Q Why does this game end the moment the takedown completes? It trains denial, not ground escape. The skill is stopping the shot and getting back to neutral standing, not recovering from a finished takedown. Q Beyond the sprawl, what tools does the defender have, and what does the DNS punish? Whizzer, crossface, and frames off the sprawl, then a clean disengage. The guillotine or front headlock (DNS) punishes poor head position on the shot. Q What does a good sprawl actually do? Hips back, weight down on the attacker, meeting the shot in time. Then choose: fight the position or disengage to neutral. A late sprawl means you get driven back.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤼Attacker

Shoot singles and doubles; set up with level changes; chain when defended; follow through on entries.

🛡️Defender

Recognize the shot early; sprawl effectively; use whizzer, crossface, frames; disengage cleanly.

Coaching cues

👁️See it before it lands

"What did you see before they shot?" Read the level change early, late recognition means the sprawl is too late.

⚓Hips back, weight down

The sprawl is hips back and weight on the attacker. Then choose: fight the position or disengage to neutral.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Attacker initiates takedowns→Forces development of reactive defense Defender can't strike (L1)→Isolates wrestling defense skill Game ends on takedown (not escape)→Focuses on denial, not ground recovery DNS allowed as defensive tool→Completes the defensive arsenal Open space, wrestling distance→Shot recognition is critical

Develops shot-recognition affordance perception. Forcing the defender to react (not preemptively sprawl) builds genuine perceptual skill, multiple solutions (sprawl, whizzer, crossface, DNS) emerge through self-organization (Renshaw et al., 2019).

What the defender reads

👁️Visual

Level-change initiation, shot angle, head position, drive continuation → sprawl timing & defense selection.

✋Haptic

Grip and pressure of the shot → which counter works.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own hip position & weight → sprawl effectiveness.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender meets the shot in time, sprawl and frame arriving with the level change, so they stay standing rather than getting taken down. Track sprawls landed before being driven back vs. takedowns conceded. That timing relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: defending takedowns in open space to keep the fight standing.

Simplified: no strikes (L1–2)ends on takedowndenial not escape

Sprawl timing, whizzer instinct, and DNS awareness transfer directly to live MMA, feeds Open Space Takedown.

Readiness to progress

  • Sprawls before being driven back ~70%+
  • Uses multiple tools (sprawl, whizzer, DNS)
  • Disengages cleanly after defending
  • Can describe: "I saw them drop so I sprawled"

Warning signs

Always sprawls late (gets driven) Only sprawls, no whizzer/DNS Can't disengage after defense Taken down by feints

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled shots, no slamming 🛑 Stop on uncontrolled takedowns or neck cranks 🔁 Reset if positions stall

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Pressure to Clinch (recommended) Follow-on→Open Space Takedown (combined perspective) Opposite→Pressure to Takedown

docs.title: The Square, Close Defense to Takedown Progression
docs.text:
Progression · Mixed (Striking → Grappling)The Square, Close Defense to Takedown Progression

Provisional (WIP): derived from class recording 2026-05-25

This game was captured live from a class and is pending review on the platform. Name and structure may change. Remove the WIP status once confirmed.

ProgressionMixed (Striking → Grappling)DefensiveIntermediateConfined Square

A single constraint set, layered live one variable at a time, that grows from close-range blocking defense into a full striking-to-takedown game. It is the in-class bridge between isolated striking defense and the clinch and takedown games, survival first, then the right to counter, clinch off-balance, and finish.

StartTwo fighters at close range, inside a marked perimeter with no exit. → The GoalThe offense is trying to land significant strikes (and, later, takedowns); the defense is trying to survive, then counter, off-balance & finish. → FinishSurvive / counter / off-balance / finish → win · Leave the perimeter → loss.

One game, layered live, survive first, then earn the right to finish.

It starts as block-only survival in the pocket and grows, one variable at a time, into a full striking-to-takedown game. Counter, clinch off-balance, finish.

What to Read

What you attune to shifts as the game evolves. Early, in the striking phases, read the rate of expansion (τ) of the incoming punch and the shoulder–hip motion at center mass, that specifies when and where the shot arrives.
