As the game opens into the clinch and becomes grappling, attune instead to the felt resistance, the inertial array of the opponent's weight and directional force through your hand controls. That felt push is no longer something to absorb; it is the information you redirect to off-balance and finish.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, neutral stance. RangeClose quarters, no gap to retreat into. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside; the edge is live. RolesOne offense, one defense, switched on a win. Start & resetOffense initiates; reset after each exchange. Phases layer one variable at a time.

The Matchup

🥊 Offense

Trying to land significant strikes inside the perimeter, and, as phases unlock, leg attacks and takedowns to the ground.

Boxing first, scaled combinations (≤3 punches, 1 significant shot to win), allowing the game to play. Later you may enter on the legs / double-under and must land two significant strikes. The offensive role layers up alongside the defense. VS 🛡️ Defense

Trying to survive behind a tight shell, then, as the game opens, to counter, off-balance in the clinch, and finish.

Starts as pure blocking, no parry, no head movement, no footwork escape. Then earns one counter strike, clinch off-balancing, body-kick counters, and finally a takedown finish. The defensive role grows phase by phase, layering on top of the survival base.

The Rules

⬛ Confined space, no escapePlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). There is no footwork escape; both players stay inside and solve the problem in the pocket. 🚫 Both feet out = lossIf both of a player's feet leave the perimeter, that player loses instantly, whoever they are. The boundary is live, like a cage edge. 🤝 No tying upHand controls are legal to nullify the opponent, but break fast, boxing rules. No stalling tie-ups. ⚖️ Scale to the partnerStrikes scale to the partner; the offense must allow the game to play, no teeing off, no crowding out the defensive opportunity. ➕ One variable at a timeNew phases are added one constraint at a time. Earlier constraints persist unless explicitly lifted, resist enriching the game before the current constraint has done its work.

How to Win

Defense wins Survive, counter, off-balance, or finish → win.The phase-dependent defensive win, survival early, then counter (one strike, later a body kick), then clinch off-balancing, then a takedown / top neck strangle finish as the game layers up. Winning switches roles. Offense wins Land the required strikes, or take it to the ground → win.Significant shots with front-two-knuckle contact (1 → 2 required); later, connect a leg attack / double-under and get the hips to the ground (hands down / feet up = on track), no scrambling once the hips land. A caught kick does not count as landed. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.Crossing the marked perimeter loses instantly, regardless of the exchange, whoever you are. A caught kick used to push or take a player out leaves the kicker liable, training cage-edge awareness.

The Levels

1Confined block + hand controlsBlock-only survival in the pocket.Offense throws a ≤3-punch combination, landing 1 significant shot to win. Defense blocks only, no parry, no head movement, no footwork escape. Hand controls are legal to nullify; no tie-ups, break fast. 2Counter + live boundaryEarn one strike back.Defense may counter with one strike, interrupt the rhythm, then keep defending. The perimeter goes live: both feet out of it = loss. 3Clinch off-balancingRedirect the felt force.Defense can win by off-balancing the opponent in the clinch, redirect the directional force rather than absorb it. Offense must now land two significant strikes, and may enter on the legs / double-under body lock. 4Kick countersBody kicks come online.Defense may counter with kicks above the waist, e.g., a body kick after a block. A caught kick is not a score and leaves the kicker liable. No kicks to head or legs, body only. 5Single-leg takedown finishGrappling to the ground.Start from a single-leg position, inside grip, alternating to outside. Offense gets the hips to the ground (hands down / feet up = on track); no scrambling once hips land. Defense separates and gets out, strikes at any level, redirects to top, or finishes with a neck strangle / guillotine. Back exposure = bonus.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What you attune to shifts as the game evolves. From what to what? Early, the rate of expansion (τ) of the punch and shoulder-hip motion at center mass, when and where the shot arrives. As it opens to the clinch, the felt resistance, the inertial array of the opponent's weight and directional force through your hand controls. Q In the clinch, the opponent's forward push is not a problem. What is it? It is the information you redirect to off-balance and finish. Redirect the directional force, don't absorb it; being bullied forward is the opportunity, not a foul. Q Why is only one variable layered at a time? So each constraint does its work before the next layers on. Earlier constraints persist unless lifted; resist enriching the game before the current one has formed the skill. Q Defense earns the right to finish in what order? Survive first, behind a block-only shell, then counter (one strike, later a body kick), then clinch off-balance, then a takedown / top neck strangle finish. Survival is the base everything layers on.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Offense

Land significant strikes in scaled combinations (≤3, then a 2-significant requirement); land with the front two knuckles; in later phases mix striking with leg attacks / double-under entries to finish.

🛡️Defense

Tight shell, absorb on arms/gloves; use hand controls to interrupt the boxing and redirect directional force; stay conscious of where your feet are relative to the boundary.

Coaching cues

👁️See center mass

Don't go blind behind the gloves. Center mass keeps shoulder & hip motion in view so you can read the incoming strike.

⚓Base under fire

Base down, bend the knees, create suspension so strike force doesn't rob your counters or jostle you out of the square.

↪️Redirect, don't absorb

When the offense bullies forward, that pressure is the opportunity, redirect the directional force to off-balance and win. Being pushed out is yours to solve, not a foul to call.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Confined square, no escape→Forces in-pocket solutions; removes the footwork-escape habit Both feet out = loss→Converts the boundary into a live cage-edge constraint Block-only start→Forces the shell / guard solution to develop first Hand controls, no tie-ups→Invites redirecting directional force over absorbing it One variable at a time→Each constraint does its work before the next layers on

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), one constraint set scaled live so the same game grows from survival to finishing.

What the defender reads

👁️Visual

Shoulder & hip motion at center mass → when and where the strike arrives.

✋Haptic

Felt resistance through hand controls → the inertial array to redirect in the clinch.

🧭Proprioceptive

Base, balance & feet relative to the boundary → staying structured and inside.

What we measure (order parameter)

Early, whether the defender's block lands in time with the punch; as the game opens, whether they can redirect the felt directional force to off-balance rather than absorb it. That shifting timing-then-force relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes through each phase, the skill has formed and the next variable can be added.

Representativeness

Models: surviving and recovering in a confined pocket, then converting pressure into clinch off-balancing and takedown finishes, the bridge from striking defense to grappling.

Confined squarelayered one variable at a timesurvival → finish

The live connective tissue between isolated close-range defense and the clinch/takedown games, feeds into Pressure to Takedown.

Readiness to progress

  • Survives the pocket behind a tight shell
  • Stays based and inside the boundary under fire
  • Redirects directional force instead of just absorbing
  • Half-enjoying / half-struggling, the desensitization is working

Warning signs

Offense crowds & removes the defensive opportunity Tie-ups stall the action Caught-kick scrambles going wrong

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact, felt, not damaging 🛑 Stop on lost control, boundary disputes, or hips hitting the ground 🔁 Reset if the offense crowds out the defensive opportunity

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Tight Block · Close-Range Defense Follow-on→Pressure to Takedown · Wall Control Related→Defensive Solutions · Hand Controls

docs.title: Tight Block
docs.text:
Skill Isolation · StrikingTight Block Skill IsolationStrikingDefensiveBeginnerOpen Space

Absorb strikes behind a tight shell, block only, no parry, no head movement.

StartTwo fighters at close range, inside a marked perimeter. → The GoalAttacker lands clean; defender blocks & survives. → FinishSurvive → switch · Land clean → reset · Leave the perimeter → loss.

You can't avoid every shot, so make them cost nothing.

The defender may only block, no parry, no head movement. The shell has to solve it alone.

What to Read

Attune to the rate of expansion (τ) of the incoming punch, plus the shoulder–hip motion at center mass, not the opponent's absolute distance or their eyes. That information specifies when and where the shot arrives.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, neutral stance. RangeInside punching distance. BoundaryA marked perimeter, both stay inside. RolesOne attacker, one defender. Start & resetAttacker initiates; reset after each exchange.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to land one clean punch, head or body, inside a 1–3 punch combination.

Boxing only, straights and hooks, scaled to level. You set the problem: vary targets and rhythm, and ramp up only as the defender succeeds. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to block everything behind a tight shell and survive the sequence.

No countering, no head movement, no footwork escape, pure blocking. Your only job is to stay structured and composed while reading the attack.

The Rules

🥊 Straights & hooksThe attacker throws only straight punches and hooks, scaled to the level, no kicks or knees. This keeps the defender's reading problem focused on hand defense. 🛡️ Block only, no parry, no dodgeThe defender may only block with a tight guard. Parrying, slipping, head movement, and footwork escapes are all off-limits, so the blocking solution is forced to develop. 🚫 No continuous backing upThe defender can't keep retreating out of range to avoid exchanges, they must stay and solve the problem in the pocket. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens inside a marked perimeter, any shape (square, circle, taped lines). If both feet leave it, you lose instantly. ⏱️ Reset between strikesAt early levels the attacker pauses between strikes so the defender can reorganize the guard. Pressure becomes continuous as the levels rise.

How to Win

Switch Survive clean → switch roles.The defender blocked a full attacking sequence with no clean shot landing, and earns the attacking role. Reset Attacker lands clean → reset, same roles.The required clean shots land (1, then 2 for stronger partners), a clean, significant shot inside a ≤3-punch combo. Then begin again from a neutral stance. Loss Both feet leave the perimeter → loss.Crossing the marked perimeter loses the game instantly, regardless of the exchange, training the cage-edge awareness a fighter needs.

The Levels

1Single straightsOne at a time, reset between.Pure shape practice, one straight punch, fully reset between reps. Build the guard with zero time pressure. 2Add hooksStraights or hooks; adjust the guard.Hooks come in on round angles, so the guard must now cover the sides, not just straight lines. 3Variable tempoNo fixed reset, stay ready.Timing becomes unpredictable with no guaranteed reset, the defender has to read rhythm and stay switched on. 4Combinations2–4 punches on the guard.Full 2–4 punch combinations, hold structure and composure through sustained fire. 5Full MMAAdd shot / clinch threat.The attacker can now shoot or clinch too, block while denying the grappling entry. Shelling up without posture invites takedowns.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to time the block? The rate of expansion (τ) of the incoming punch plus the shoulder and hip motion at center mass. Not the opponent's eyes, and not their absolute distance. Q The defender may only block: no parry, no head movement. Why strip those away? The constraint forces the blocking solution to develop on its own. With no escape route, the shell has to organize and hold. Q You can't avoid every shot, so what is the defender actually trying to do? Make the shots cost nothing: absorb on the guard, stay based, and survive the sequence without getting hit clean. Q What breaks the guard under fire, and how do you prevent it? Getting knocked off balance breaks it, not the damage itself. Bend the knees and create suspension (the "ATV over bumps") so impact is absorbed without getting rocked.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Land clean punches; vary targets and types; scale difficulty to the partner.

🛡️Defender

Keep the guard tight; absorb on arms/gloves, not exposed targets; reset each sequence.

Coaching cues

👁️See center mass

Don't bury your head behind the gloves. Center mass keeps shoulder & hip motion in view, "the little X." The eyes lie.

⚓Base under fire

Strikes knock you off balance, that's what breaks the guard. Bend the knees, create suspension "like an ATV over bumps."

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Straights & hooks→Defender perceives multiple attack angles Block-only→Forces exploration of shell / guard solutions Reset between exchanges→Time to reorganize guard structure Role switch on success→Rewards effective blocking Close range→Blocking is viable and necessary

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), different body types develop different guard structures.

What the defender reads

👁️Visual

Punch type & target → guard adjustment and positioning.

✋Haptic

Impact on arms/gloves → confirms the block held.

🧭Proprioceptive

Guard tightness & balance → maintaining structure.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender's block lands in time with the punch, track clean blocks vs. shots eaten, and whether the guard re-sets between strikes. That timing relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: shelling up under fire while recovering composure.

Simplified: punches onlyblock onlyresets between

Isolates the solution before integration, transfers into Close-Range Defense.

Readiness to progress

  • Absorbs ~70%+ on guard
  • Recovers structure quickly
  • Stays calm under sustained attack
  • Can describe where shots land

Warning signs

Guard collapses on hooks Flinching / turning away Only effective vs one partner

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on guard breakdown or lost composure 🔁 Reset if the defender starts dodging

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is foundational Follow-on→Close-Range Defense · The Square Related→Defensive Solutions

docs.title: Touch and Don't Get Touched
docs.text:
Open Space · StrikingTouch and Don't Get Touched Open SpaceStrikingCombinedBeginnerFoundational

The foundational engagement game, touch, don't get touched, and answer back the instant contact lands.

StartTwo fighters in neutral stance, open space, at a comfortable distance. → The GoalBoth players touch the opponent, avoid being touched, and deny the response. → FinishClean touch → advantage · Touch + counter-touch → neutral · Touched with no answer → lost exchange. Reset.

Touch, don't get touched, and if you do, answer back now.

The most basic engagement game in the system, built on honest range and composure under contact. Recover initiative in the same window, not the next exchange.

What to Read

Attune to the opponent's preferred distance and the rate the gap is closing (τ on the entry), near affords your touch and offense, far affords their escape. Read their weight shift and recovery after contact, not their absolute position or their eyes. That information specifies whether you are truly in range and when you can answer back.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, mirrored, no fixed attacker or defender. RangeOutside touching range, room to enter and exit. BoundaryA marked perimeter, open space, no wall. RolesMirrored, same job for both. Start & resetEither initiates; each exchange resolves fast and resets to neutral.

The Matchup

🤜 Players (mirrored)

Trying to touch the opponent, avoid being touched, and deny their ability to respond.

Both players share one job, this is about initiative management, not damage. Touch/light contact only, single strike or a max two-strike sequence, no flurries. Stay exploratory, not competitive. MIRROR 🛡️ The counter-touch

Trying to recover initiative immediately, one clean touch back inside the same exchange window.

If you get touched, you have one immediate opportunity to touch back within the same window. No chasing once the moment has passed. This is the core mechanic.

The Rules

👋 Touch / light contact onlyAll contact is touch/light, light touches to head or torso. Punches and kicks are allowed by level, but the goal is contact, not power. This removes fear so range can be explored honestly. 2️⃣ Max two-strike sequenceA single strike or at most a two-strike sequence per entry. Probing jabs are encouraged, but no flurries, this rewards precision over volume. ↩️ One immediate counter-touchIf touched, you get exactly one opportunity to touch back, inside the same exchange window. No chasing once the moment passes, this trains composure and initiative recovery. 🚫 No sustained pressure or crashingNo forward crashing, collisions, or sustained pressure. No prolonged clinch unless a level permits it. Each exchange resolves on its own. ⏱️ Resolve quickly and resetEvery exchange resolves fast and resets to neutral distance, giving both players time to perceive and recalibrate. Stay exploratory, not competitive.

How to Win

Advantage Clean touch, no answer → win the exchange.When you land a clean touch and the opponent has no response, you win the exchange, the strongest outcome. It shows honest range and a clean entry. Neutral Touch + counter-touch → even (1-for-1).When a touch is met by an immediate counter-touch, the exchange is even, composure under contact, you recovered initiative inside the window. Reset and go again. Diagnostic No score kept, read from touch and counter quality.Success is read from clean touches, quality of counter-touches, and reduced panic after contact. This game is diagnostic, not score-driven, the coach evaluates range honesty, counter-touch quality, and composure rather than a points tally.

The Levels

1Hands onlyPunches only; jab range.Punches only, focus on jab range and hand timing. The simplest range problem. 2Hands + kicksAdd kicks; layered range.Kicks are introduced, teaching layered range awareness, multiple weapons at multiple distances. 3Counter emphasisPunish poor entries.The counter-touch is strongly emphasized, poor entries are punished immediately. 4Hand control as denialHand fighting allowed.Hand fighting and hand control allowed; brief two-on-one control permitted. Goal stays: touch and deny the response. 5Clinch as denialClinch only as a response.Clinch entry allowed only as an immediate response to touch, connected clinch, level change, or leg connection counts as denial, then the exchange ends. 6Full MMA expressionTakedown threat as denial.Takedown entry allowed as a denial response; brief continuation if clinch is established. Full denial toolkit under MMA reality.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to know whether you can touch, and what does near vs. far afford? The opponent's preferred distance and the rate the gap is closing (τ) on the entry, plus their weight shift and recovery after contact, not their absolute position or eyes. Near affords your touch and offense; far affords their escape. Q If you get touched, what is the one thing you must do, and when? Take your one immediate counter-touch inside the same exchange window. Recover initiative now, not the next exchange. No chasing once the moment passes. Q The three outcomes are advantage, neutral, and lost exchange. What separates them? Clean touch with no answer = advantage (honest range, clean entry). Touch met by an immediate counter-touch = neutral, 1-for-1 (composure under contact). Touched with no answer = lost exchange. Q Why touch contact only and no flurries, and what's the coaching question? It removes fear so range can be explored honestly and rewards precision over volume. The cue: "Were you actually in range, or did you reach?"

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each player's job

🤜Touch

Read distance honestly; time entry and exit cleanly; coordinate eyes, feet, and hands.

🛡️Deny & recover

Maintain composure after contact; recover initiative immediately; take the one counter-touch when touched.

Coaching cues

📏Were you in range?

Ask: "Were you actually in range, or did you reach?" This develops honest range perception, the foundation for all striking.

🧠Answer back

"If you get touched, can you respond immediately?" Composure and initiative recovery matter more than the first touch.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Touch contact only→Removes fear, invites exploration of range No sustained pressure / flurries→Each exchange resolves, allowing perception Counter-touch opportunity→Teaches response composure after being hit Max two-strike sequence→Prevents mindless volume, rewards precision Open space, no boundaries pressure→Pure range and timing without pressure dynamics

Implements Representative Learning Design (Renshaw et al., 2019), touch-without-being-touched mirrors the fundamental striking problem of MMA.

What both players read

👁️Visual

Distance, weight distribution, and recovery after contact → entry/exit and counter-touch timing.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance and range; position after being touched → recovery and counter-response.

✋Haptic

Being touched → triggers the one counter-touch opportunity.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the gap closes or stays open over time, is the player entering at honest range and exiting clean, and does the sequence reach a clean touch or a counter-touch? Track clean touches, counter-touch quality, and reduced panic after contact. When honest range and immediate recovery stabilize, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: open-space striking exchanges where both fighters seek to land without being countered.

Simplified: touch contactno sustained pressureresets between

Builds the perceptual and timing foundation for the system, transfers into Pressure to Clinch, where pressure and space control are added.

Readiness to progress

  • Touches land cleanly, no reaching or lunging
  • Counter-touches occur naturally after being touched
  • Composure maintained after contact
  • Entry and exit are clean, not crashing
  • Can articulate range and timing decisions

Warning signs

Reaching / lunging to touch Freezes after being touched Crashes forward on entry Chases after missed attempts

Safety & related games

👋 Touch contact only 🛑 No forward collisions; coach pauses if structure degrades 🔁 No chasing after missed strikes, reset

Where it sits

Prerequisite→None, this is foundational Follow-on→Pressure to Clinch Related→Full MMA Expression

docs.title: Turtle Breakout
docs.text:
Ground · WrestlingTurtle Breakout

Provisional (WIP): derived from class recording 2026-06-06

Invented live in class and pending review on the platform. Name and structure may change. Remove the WIP status once confirmed.

GroundWrestlingDefensiveBeginnerGet-Up

A pure attachment race. The defender starts in turtle, the attacker starts standing with palms on the small of the back, arms fully extended. The defender says go: stand, turn, face. The attacker wins by one thing only, connecting the hands around the waist or a leg above the knee.

StartDefender in turtle. Attacker standing behind, palms on the small of the back, arms fully extended. → The GoalDefender stands, turns, and faces. Attacker connects hands around waist or leg, above the knee. → FinishHands connected → attacker wins · Clean separation, turned and facing → defender wins.

Connection is the entire win, nothing else matters.

No points for position, no credit for almost. Hands locked, or turned and facing.

What to Read

Attune to the felt pressure through the contact, the inertial array, not vision. The defender reads the attacker's weight and drive direction through the palms on their back: where the pressure commits specifies which way is free to stand. The attacker reads the defender's first motion through the same contact, the hitch before the explosion, and tracks which limb is nearest to encircle as the scramble opens. Lose the contact and you are guessing.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one attacker, one defender. DefenderTurtle. How you build it is yours: nobody said elbows on the ground. AttackerStanding, freestyle/Greco posture, palms on the small of the back, arms fully extended, no bent-arm loading. TriggerThe defender starts the interaction: they say go. Start & resetReset to the same start after every decision. Switch roles on attacker win.

The Matchup

🔗 Attacker

Trying to connect the hands around the waist, or around a leg above the knee, before the defender gets up and faces.

Don't chase one grip. Re-orient the hand connections as the scramble moves, waist, single leg, both legs, whatever the get-up exposes, always collecting above the knee. Use your weight: a heavy chest buys time the hands can use. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to stand, turn, face, and stay away. Separation ends it.

Explode on your own go, you own the timing. Hand-fight the connection attempts, collect the wrists, and break free upward. Your back touching the mat is a loss, the only way out is up.

The Rules

🗣️ Defender starts itThe defender says go. The asymmetric trigger gives the bottom player the timing advantage, the attacker must react to the explosion, not pre-load it. 📏 Full extension at the startThe attacker's arms are fully extended, palms on the small of the back. No bent arms, no pre-wrapped grips. The gap is what makes the race fair. 🔗 Connection wins, above the kneeHands locked around the waist, one leg, or two legs, all count, but a leg only counts collected above the knee. No diving for a low single: a grip below the knee is not a connection. It's all over if the attacker connects, nothing else matters. 🎯 The redemption shotAs the defender escapes, the attacker gets one last connection attempt. Miss it and the defender wins. Punishes a lazy exit, rewards a committed one. 🚫 No supine: back to the mat = lossIf the defender's back touches the mat, they lose the round. Turning to face from the bottom isn't an escape, it's a worse fight. The game wants the explosive get-up. (Locked after the first-run review, 2026-06-06.)

How to Win

Switch Hands connect on waist or leg (above the knee) → attacker wins, switch roles.The attachment is the whole game. A connection during the redemption shot counts the same as one in the opening scramble. Below the knee never counts. Reset Defender stands, turns, faces, and stays away → defender wins.Separation must be clean: up, turned, facing, out of the redemption shot's reach. A scramble that ends face-down or backward isn't a win yet. Loss Defender's back touches the mat → defender loses, switch roles.Supine is not an escape route. Going to the back to face from the bottom ends the round immediately, the only way out is up. Diagnostic Is the first motion upward?The coach watches whether defenders explode up and break contact off the go, or brace in place and hand-fight from static turtle. The game wants the immediate get-up.

The Levels

1Base gameNo-supine rule live.Defender-triggered start, full-extension palms, connection (above the knee) wins, redemption shot live, back to the mat = loss. The no-supine rule was locked after the first run: the supine drift was a flaw, not an affordance. 2Strikes on (proposed)GnP pressure added.Per the system's GnP-first law for ground games: light strikes make hesitation in turtle expensive and reward the immediate explosion. Pending review.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What is the attacker's one and only win condition? Connecting the hands, around the waist, one leg, or two legs, always above the knee. A grab below the knee is not a connection. Position, pressure, and near-misses count for nothing. Q Who starts the interaction, and why does that matter? The defender says go. They own the timing, so the attacker must perceive the explosion through the palm contact and react, not pre-load a grip. Q What is the redemption shot? As the defender escapes, the attacker gets one final connection attempt. Miss it and the defender wins. It keeps the exit honest to the last instant. Q Did anyone say your elbows have to be on the ground? No. The rule said turtle, nothing more. Build the strongest legal version of the position, the game rewards players who test the stated constraints instead of imagining extra ones.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🔗Attacker

React off the contact; chase the connection, not a specific grip; re-orient the hands as the scramble moves; use body weight to slow the get-up.

🛡️Defender

Explode upward on your own go; hand-fight the wrists; turn and face only once free; never settle under the weight.

Coaching cues

⏱️30 seconds first

Before the first go: "What can you genuinely test to solve this problem?" Each player commits to a plan, then revises it from evidence.

⚖️Where's your weight?

Ask attackers stuck hand-chasing: "Are you making them carry you?" Weight on the turtle slows the explosion for free.

⬆️Up, not over

Remind defenders the back touching the mat ends the round. "Did you escape, or did you change which pin you're fighting?"

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Defender-triggered start→Attacker trains reaction off felt contact, not anticipation Full arm extension→A real gap to close, the race stays winnable for both Connection is the only win→Kills point-chasing; trains finishing the attachment Redemption shot→Escapes must be completed, not merely started No supine (back = loss)→Forces the explosive get-up; closes the turn-to-guard exit "Turtle" left undefined→Players explore the position's legal space (staggered base, high hips)

Develops the attachment race both ways: the wrestler's hand connection behind a standing opponent, and the bottom player's explosive get-up under threat, with perception anchored in the inertial array through the contact (Blau & Wagman, 2022).

What the players read

✋Haptic

Pressure through the palms / on the back → drive direction, the hitch before the explosion, which side is free.

👁️Visual

Nearest encircleable limb in the scramble → waist vs single-leg connection choice; the redemption window.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own base through the get-up → can I stand from here, or do I hand-fight first?

What we measure (order parameter)

Does the defender get up and face before the connection closes? Track connection rate by type (waist / single / double), get-up success, supine concessions, and redemption-shot wins. When defenders consistently break contact upward and attackers consistently re-orient hands instead of chasing one grip, the skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: the freestyle/Greco par terre start, turtle breakdowns, and MMA's most common get-up problem, standing up while someone hunts the body lock.

First-run observations (2026-06-06)

  • Players drifted turtle → supine instead of exploding up; ruled a flaw, no-supine locked as a core rule (back to the mat = loss)
  • Nobody used body weight on the turtle until coached; cue added above
  • Player discovery: stop chasing the body lock, re-orient hand connections, pin the defender's hands to the mat first

Readiness to progress

  • Defender explodes on the go instead of bracing in place
  • Attacker re-orients connections through the scramble
  • Escapes finish up-turned-facing, not face-down or supine
  • Can articulate: "I felt the drive commit, so I stood through the other side"

Warning signs

Defender concedes to supine Attacker chases one grip No weight used on top Escapes left half-finished

Safety & related games

🤝 Scramble intensity, controlled landings 🛑 No slamming the turtle flat; weight is pressure, not impact 🔁 Reset if the scramble leaves the mat space

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Ground to Standing Follow-on→Counter-Wrestling · Takedown Defense Related→Hand Controls

docs.title: Wall Control - Establish Pin
docs.text:
Wall · WrestlingWall Control, Establish the Pin WallWrestlingOffensiveIntermediateStabilize

Teach vertical control, the wall becomes the dominant constraint that organizes the pin.

StartTwo grapplers clinched, defender pinned between attacker and wall. → The GoalAttacker organizes a functional pin; defender rotates out or disengages. → FinishHold pin 5s → attacker · Disengage or reverse → defender · Only pressing → no pin.

Pressing isn't pinning, deny the rotation.

A pin is organized structure, not muscle. If their shoulders can turn, it's only pressure.

Key Positions

🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

What to Read

Attune to the felt resistance to rotation through your grips and frames, the inertial array at the shoulders and hips, plus where the defender's weight wants to escape. That resistance (or its sudden absence) specifies whether the pin is organized or merely pressing, not how hard you are pushing or what their face is doing.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo grapplers, clinched with connection but no control yet. Range/positionTied up at the wall, 1–2 feet out, defender pinned against it. BoundaryThe wall (or cage) is the dominant constraint, inside a marked perimeter. RolesOne attacker establishing the pin, one defender resisting and escaping. Start & resetBegin from clinch; reset on significant space or full disengage.

The Matchup

🤼 Attacker

Trying to establish a functional wall pin, hips and shoulders controlled, rotation denied.

You start with initial connection but no control yet. No immediate takedowns, stabilize first, then organize the pin against the wall. VS 🧱 Defender

Trying to prevent the pin or disengage before being flattened.

Active resistance only, no passive drilling. Work rotation, frames, and defensive neck submissions to keep the attacker honest.

The Rules

🧱 Wall is the dominant constraintAll control is relative to the wall. It limits the defender's options and is the structure the attacker organizes the pin around. 🚫 No immediate takedownsStabilize before advancing. The game is about establishing control, not shooting for the takedown right away. ⚡ Defender actively resistsNo passive drilling, the defender works real escapes, frames, and rotation so the control problem is genuine. 🤐 DNS permitted for the defenderDefensive neck submissions, guillotine threats and front headlock entries, are allowed. They punish poor posture, over-commitment, and head-position errors without changing the game's focus. 📍 Start 1–2 feet from the wallBegin from clinch with the defender positioned between attacker and wall. Reset if the defender creates significant space or fully disengages.

How to Win

Attacker wins Hold a functional pin for 5 seconds → attacker wins.A functional pin: shoulders substantially flat to the wall, hips controlled or compromised, no free rotation, with the attacker keeping structure and balance. The 5-second hold ensures the pin is stable, not momentary. Defender wins Disengage or reverse → defender wins.Break free by creating significant space, or turn the position around. Either ends the attacker's control attempt. No pin Shoulders can still rotate → pressing, not a pin.If the defender can still rotate their shoulders, it isn't a pin, only pressure. Organized structure that denies rotation is what counts.

The Levels

1Establish PinFlat shoulders and hip control, no strikes.Focus on achieving flat shoulders and hip control. No strikes. The defender resists but does not counter-attack. 2Deny RotationDefender works rotation; attacker re-pins.The defender actively works rotation escapes and the attacker must adjust and re-pin. Light strikes permitted. 3Threaten TKO Pin AccessRelease a control point to simulate strike access.The attacker may briefly release one control point to simulate strike access, then re-establish the pin. Introduces the trade-off between control and damage. 4Full MMA ExpressionLight strikes for both; knees from control.Light strikes permitted for both players. The attacker softens resistance and maintains the pin; the defender strikes to create escapes. Knee strikes available for the attacker from control.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What is the difference between pressing and a real wall pin? A wall pin is at least two points of pinning control, hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall, with rotation denied. If the shoulders can still rotate, it is only pressure, not a pin. Q What are you reading to know whether the pin is organized or just muscle? The felt resistance to rotation through your grips and frames, the inertial array at the shoulders and hips, plus where the defender's weight wants to escape. Not how hard you push or what their face does. Q Why is denying shoulder rotation the attacker's primary task? Shoulder rotation is the primary escape: it lets the defender frame, generate hip movement, and access angles. Deny it and the position crosses from pressing into a pin. Q Why no immediate takedowns, and what keeps the attacker honest? Stabilize before advancing: the game develops control before the shot. The defender's DNS (guillotine, front headlock) punishes poor posture, over-commitment, and head-position errors.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤼Attacker

Establish a functional pin, shoulders flat, hips controlled, rotation denied, while keeping your own balance and structure.

🧱Defender

Prevent the pin or disengage before being flattened. Resist actively; use frames, rotation, and DNS.

Coaching cues

👁️Are their shoulders flat?

Ask: "Are their shoulders flat? Can they rotate?" This develops pin recognition, control is organized structure, not just pressure.

🔃Deny the rotation

Shoulder rotation is the primary escape, it lets the defender frame, generate hip movement, and access angles. Denying it is the attacker's primary task.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Functional pin required→Develops organized control, not just pressing 5-second hold→Ensures stable control, not momentary DNS allowed for defender→Punishes poor posture, keeps attacker honest No immediate takedowns→Develops control before advancement Wall as dominant constraint→All control relative to wall structure

Develops pinning affordance perception, reading when control is organized vs. merely pressing. The defender develops early escape timing through the scramble window (Renshaw et al., 2019).

What the attacker reads

✋Haptic

Shoulder position (flat vs. rotated), hip movement, and frame attempts → control quality, re-pinning direction, denial method.

👁️Visual

Defender's rotation setup → anticipate and prevent it.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance and structure → maintain position without over-committing.

What the defender reads

✋Haptic

Attacker's control points & weight distribution → where to frame, which way to rotate.

👁️Visual

Attacker's head position → DNS opportunity.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own shoulder and hip position → current escape options.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the defender's shoulders stay rotatable or go flat, track free rotation vs. denied rotation under active resistance, and whether the attacker can hold that flatness for the full 5 seconds. That rotation-denial relationship is the order parameter; when it stabilizes, organized control has formed and the position has crossed from pressing into a pin.

Representativeness

Models: establishing wall control for dirty boxing, grinding, or takedown setup, a dominant position in MMA cage fighting.

Simplified: 5-second hold winno immediate takedownsprogressive striking

Wall control developed here transfers directly to cage fighting, "organized control" vs. "just pressing" is identical in competition.

Readiness to progress

  • Establishes functional pin consistently
  • Maintains pin under active resistance
  • Can articulate: "They couldn't rotate because…"
  • Respects DNS (maintains good posture)
  • 5-second holds achieved reliably

Warning signs

Just pressing, not controlling Gets DNS'd frequently Pin breaks immediately Exhausted quickly, using strength

Safety & related games

🧱 Use mats and a controlled wall surface 🤐 DNS threats stay controlled, no cranking 🔄 Reset on significant space or disengage

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Pressure to Clinch · TKO Pin Follow-on→Wall Escape · Wall to Ground Related→Wall Grinding

docs.title: Wall Defensive Submission
docs.text:
Wall · GrapplingWall Defensive Submission WallGrapplingDefensiveAdvancedCounter

Turn a defensive wall position into a threat, submission as weapon and escape tool.

StartDefender pinned against the wall; controller has established wall control. → The GoalDefender threatens or finishes a submission; controller holds control safely. → FinishSubmission or threat-to-escape → defender wins, switch · Control held & subs defended → controller wins, reset.

Pinned isn't beaten, make their control dangerous.

Guillotine, Kimura, front headlock from the bottom of the pin. The threat creates the escape even when it doesn't finish.

Key Positions

🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

What to Read

Attune to the felt resistance to rotation through your grips and the controller's posture, where the controller's head and arms sit against your frames, and the moment their control loosens. That changing load specifies which submission is available (head low → guillotine, arm exposed → Kimura) and when the threat opens an escape, not a memorized sequence.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, one pinned defender, one controller. PositionDefender pinned against the wall; controller has wall control. BoundaryA wall or cage within a marked perimeter, on mats. RolesDefender threatens submissions; controller holds control while staying safe. Start & resetBegin from the pin; reset when a submission lands, is defended, or the controller disengages.

The Matchup

🛡️ Defender (pinned)

Trying to threaten or complete a submission from defensive position, not just escape.

Identify what's available from position, guillotine, front headlock/DNS, Kimura, standing arm triangle. Use the threat to create escape even without finishing, and finish if the controller gives the opening. VS 🧱 Controller

Trying to maintain wall control while avoiding submission danger.

Keep the intent to control, not just defend. Protect neck and arms, recognize submission setups, keep head position safe, and choose to disengage or defend in place.

The Rules

🎯 Threats requiredThe defender must threaten submissions, not just escape. This constraint develops the submission as a defensive weapon, counter-offense from defense. 🧱 Controller keeps control intentThe controller maintains the intent to control, not just survive the submissions, this keeps the control situation realistic. ✅ Position-available submissions onlyPrescriptive. Submissions must be genuinely available from the position, no fantasy techniques. This keeps technique selection reality-based. 🤝 Controlled submissionsApply submissions with control and tap early, control before squeeze. This keeps the game safe during learning. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens against a wall or cage within a marked perimeter, any shape. The wall position remains the environment throughout.

How to Win

Defender Complete a submission or use the threat to escape → defender wins, switch.A finish or a threat-created escape both count, the submission threat opens space even when it doesn't finish. Controller Hold control & defend subs for the duration → controller wins, reset.Hold position and stay out of danger for the full set duration, control intact, neck and arms safe; same roles.

The Levels

1Guillotine onlyOne threat to read.Defender can only threaten the guillotine. Focus: recognizing head-position vulnerability. 2Add arm attacksGuillotine + Kimura.Multiple submission threats available. Focus: reading which submission the position offers. 3Submission to escapeThreat as escape tool.Defender uses threats to create escape; score for escape OR submission. Focus: submission as an escape tool. 4Full MMAController strikes.Controller can strike during wall control; defender must time submissions around the strikes. Focus: defensive submissions under MMA pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q Pinned against the wall, what tells you which submission is available? The felt resistance to rotation and the controller's posture: head low specifies a guillotine, an exposed arm specifies a Kimura. You read the changing load, not a memorized sequence. Q Pinned isn't beaten. What is the threat for? To make their control dangerous. The threat creates the escape even when it doesn't finish, so it works as both a weapon and an escape tool. Q What actually defines a wall pin you are attacking from? At least two points of pinning control, hips and upper body held flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin. Q What is being measured to know the skill has formed? The press to pin-or-escape phase transition: whether the controller keeps shoulders and hips pinned flat vs. free to rotate. The skill has formed when threats reliably loosen control and open escapes.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🛡️Defender

Identify what's available from position, threaten guillotine / front headlock / Kimura, use the threat to create escape, finish if the opening is given.

🧱Controller

Maintain position while protecting neck and arms, recognize setups, keep head position safe, choose to disengage or defend in place.

Coaching cues

👁️What was available?

Ask defenders: "What submission was available there?" This develops recognition of head and arm position.

🚪Threat to escape

Ask: "Did you use the threat to escape?" Submission threats create escape opportunities even when they don't finish.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Must threaten subs (not just escape)→Develops submission as a defensive weapon Controller keeps control intent→Creates a realistic control situation Position-available subs only→Reality-based technique selection Progressive submission options→Scaffolds from one sub to many to escape-use Wall position maintained→Defensive submissions specific to the environment

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), develops defensive-submission perception: recognizing when submissions exist from disadvantaged positions, turning defense into counter-offense.

What the defender reads

👁️Visual

Controller's head position → guillotine / DNS; controller's arm position → Kimura.

✋Haptic

Controller's posture → what submission is available; control loosening → escape timing from the threat.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own grip and hook position → submission setup.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the controller keeps the defender's shoulders and hips pinned flat vs. free to rotate while staying clear of submission danger, track control time held against submission threats landed. The press→pin-or-escape phase transition is the order parameter; the skill has formed when threats reliably loosen control and open escapes.

Representativeness

Models: using submission threats from a defensive wall position, "their head is down, guillotine is available", to make control dangerous, identical to competition.

Simplified: progressive subsclear threat requirementcontrolled submissions

Builds on Wall Escape; an end state, completing a submission resolves the game.

Readiness to progress

  • Recognizes submission opportunities from position
  • Threatens effectively, controller feels danger
  • Finishes when the controller gives an opening
  • Uses threats to create escape
  • Can describe: "Their head was down so I…"

Warning signs

Only tries escape, no submissions Controller doesn't feel danger Cranks without control Only one submission works

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled submissions, tap early 🛑 Stop on cranking submissions or slamming 🔁 Reset if stalling without progress

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Wall Escape, understand wall defense first Follow-on→End state, submission resolves the game Related→DNS · Defensive Solutions

docs.title: Wall Escape - Anti-Pin
docs.text:
Wall · WrestlingWall Escape, Anti-Pin WallWrestlingDefensiveIntermediateCounter

Start pinned, shoulders flat to the wall, break free by actively escaping or reversing, never by waiting it out.

StartDefender pinned, shoulders flat, rotation compromised. → The GoalDefender breaks the pin to escape or reverse; attacker holds it. → FinishEscape or reverse → defender wins · Pin held 10s → attacker wins · Reset on full disengage.

Pinned isn't pinned for good, break it before it sets.

Survival doesn't count here. Only an active escape or reversal wins.

Key Positions

🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

What to Read

Attune to the felt resistance to rotation through your frames and grips, the load the attacker's weight places on your shoulders and hips, and especially the moment it slackens. That changing resistance specifies which way to rotate and when the pin can be broken, not the attacker's face or a memorized step.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, pinned defender (focus), attacker holding the pin. PositionWall pin already established, shoulders flat. BoundaryWall or cage within a marked perimeter. RolesDefender escapes or reverses; attacker holds the pin, no takedowns. Start & resetBegin from the pin; reset on lost control or full disengage.

The Matchup

🛡️ Defender (focus)

Trying to break the pin and return to space facing the attacker, or reverse the position.

Escape or reversal, never passive survival. Rotate shoulders, build frames, move hips, turn to face, then disengage. A dropping attacker head opens a guillotine or front-headlock threat that buys the escape. VS 🧱 Attacker

Trying to maintain the pin and deny escape for the full hold.

Hold the pin, no takedowns. Re-pin against rotation attempts, deny frames, and protect head position so the defender can't use a neck threat to escape.

The Rules

🧱 Wall stays dominantThe wall remains the controlling constraint of the position. The whole problem is solved against it, not in open space. 🏃 Active escape, no passive survivalThe defender must actively attempt to break the pin. Simply riding out the clock or stalling under control does not count. 🚫 No takedownsThe attacker's only job is holding the pin, no takedowns yet. The focus stays on the press-vs-break of the wall pin itself. 🔄 Rotate before disengagingEscapes follow rotate → frame → move hips → turn to face → disengage. Trying to disengage before rotating typically fails. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens against a wall or cage within a marked perimeter, any shape. Both players stay inside it.

How to Win

Defender Break free facing the attacker, or reverse → defender wins.Shoulders no longer flat, defender facing the attacker with space, or a clean reversal. The escape is complete, not merely attempted. Attacker Pin held 10 seconds → attacker wins.The longer 10-second hold reflects the disadvantaged starting position the defender begins in. Reset Attacker loses control or defender fully disengages → reset to the pin.Begin again from the established pin so the escape skill keeps getting reps.

The Levels

1EscapeBreak the pin, create space.No strikes. Success is shoulders no longer flat and facing the attacker, pure pin-break mechanics. 2Escape + disengageReturn to striking range.Create meaningful space and return to striking range with defensive posture. The attacker may follow and attempt to re-clinch. 3Escape + reverseTake the dominant position.Defender now puts the attacker on the wall. Highest difficulty, requires reading the attacker's balance. 4Full MMALight strikes both ways.Attacker uses strikes to maintain pin pressure; defender uses strikes to create escapes. Escape becomes urgent under realistic striking pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to know which way to rotate and when the pin can be broken? The felt resistance to rotation through your frames and grips, the load the attacker's weight places on your shoulders and hips, and especially the moment it slackens. Not their face or a memorized step. Q Why does surviving the hold not count as a win here? Only an active escape or reversal wins: shoulders no longer flat and facing the attacker with space, or turning the position. Passive survival just lets the pin consolidate. Q What is the escape sequence, and why does order matter? Rotate, frame, move hips, turn to face, then disengage. Trying to disengage before rotating typically fails, you expose the back and the pin re-sets. Q Why escape early rather than late, and how does the DNS help? Early escapes, before the pin consolidates, take far less effort and expose you to less damage. A dropping attacker head opens a guillotine or front-headlock threat that creates decision pressure and buys the escape, it need not finish.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🛡️Defender

Rotate shoulders, build frames, move hips, turn to face, then disengage or reverse. Escape early, before the pin consolidates.

🧱Attacker

Re-pin against rotation, deny frame creation, anticipate hip movement, and protect head position from neck threats.

Coaching cues

⏱️Early vs late

Ask: "Did you escape early or late?" Early escapes, before the pin consolidates, require far less effort and expose you to less damage.

🧠DNS pressure

A dropping attacker head opens guillotine and front-headlock threats. DNS need not finish, it creates decision pressure that enables escape.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Start from established pin→Forces escape-skill development from disadvantage Active escape required→Develops proactive, not passive, defense 10-second hold to win→Longer duration reflects the defender's disadvantage DNS allowed as catalyst→Creates decision pressure on the attacker Wall constraint→Forces a structured rotate → frame → hip → face sequence

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), the rotate → frame → hip → face sequence emerges through exploration, not instruction, and DNS shows how a defensive tool creates offensive opportunity.

What the defender reads

✋Haptic

Attacker's control points and weight distribution → where frames are possible and which way to rotate; pin-consolidation level → early vs. late timing.

👁️Visual

Attacker's head position → guillotine / DNS opportunity.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own shoulder position → rotation availability.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the attacker keeps the defender's shoulders and hips pinned flat vs. free to rotate, track control time held and whether the defender escapes early (before consolidation) or late. The press→pin-or-break phase transition is the order parameter; when early rotation stabilizes, the escape skill has formed.

Representativeness

Models: escaping cage immobilization, recognizing consolidation timing and the rotation sequence, identical to competition.

Simplified: start from pinno strikes at L1clear win conditions

Builds on Wall Control and feeds Wall Defensive Submission.

Readiness to progress

  • Escapes consistently from various pin types
  • Uses early escape timing
  • Rotate → frame → hip sequence is automatic
  • DNS threats are effective when available
  • Can describe: "I rotated when they shifted weight…"

Warning signs

Only survives, doesn't escape Always escapes late Escapes but doesn't disengage Panics under control

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact 🛑 Stop on lost composure or unsafe scramble 🔁 Reset if the defender only survives passively

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Wall Control, understand a good pin first Follow-on→Wall Defensive Submission Related→Full MMA Expression

docs.title: Wall Grinding
docs.text:
Wall · WrestlingWall Grinding WallWrestlingOffensiveIntermediateExploit

Deal damage from wall control without losing the control, dirty boxing, knees and grinding while the pin holds.

StartAttacker has wall control; defender pinned, ready to resist. → The GoalAttacker damages while holding the pin; defender escapes or nullifies it. → FinishSustained damage → attacker wins, switch · Escape or nullify → defender wins, reset.

Holding isn't the win, make the pin hurt.

Just holding doesn't count, but neither does winging shots that cost you position. Control AND damage at once.

Key Positions

🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

What to Read

Attune to the felt resistance to rotation through your grips and frames, when the pin feels solid enough to free a hand for a knee or short shot, versus when striking would loosen control. That balance of load at the shoulders and hips specifies when damage is safe, not a fixed combination or a memorized rhythm.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, attacker grinding from control, defender resisting. PositionEstablished wall control, shoulders flattened, hips controlled. BoundaryWall or cage within a marked perimeter, no ground transition. RolesAttacker controls and damages; defender escapes or nullifies the damage. Start & resetBegin from control; reset on escape, sufficient damage, or lost control.

The Matchup

🥊 Attacker

Trying to maintain wall control AND deal significant, sustained damage.

Dirty boxing, knees and short elbows while the pin holds. Manage energy, grinding is work. Find openings without over-committing; if damage drops, control is too tight, if control drops, you over-committed to strikes. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to escape the wall or nullify the damage output.

Active resistance, not passive. Create frames to block strikes, work rotation escapes, nullify knee opportunities, and look for a reversal or escape.

The Rules

💥 Damage requiredThe attacker must actually deal damage, just holding the pin doesn't count. The win is sustained output, multiple clean strikes, not a single shot. 🧱 Stay on the wallNo transition to the ground. Staying on the wall is what distinguishes this game from Wall to Ground and keeps the focus on grinding. 🏃 Defender resists activelyThe defender works real escapes and damage nullification, not passive shelling. Position resets if the attacker loses wall control completely. 🤝 Controlled contactLight to moderate contact, knees especially controlled. This keeps the grinding safe during learning. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens against a wall or cage within a marked perimeter, any shape. Both players stay inside it.

How to Win

Attacker Sustained damage while holding control → attacker wins, switch.Sustained output, multiple clean strikes while holding control, not a single lucky shot. Controlling AND damaging at once is the skill being rewarded. Defender Escape to space or nullify the damage → defender wins, reset.Either break free into open space, or shut the damage down long enough that grinding fails.

The Levels

1Control + strikesShort shots while holding.Attacker maintains control and works short strikes; defender defends and works escape. Focus: finding openings while controlling. 2Add kneesKnees from the clinch.Knees from clinch position, more damaging but they require space creation. Focus: knee setups from the wall. 3Defender strikes backTwo-way exchanges.Defender can strike from wall position; attacker must control while managing return fire. Focus: two-way wall exchanges. 4Full MMAGround option opens.Attacker may transition to ground if advantageous; defender may shoot to escape. Focus: wall fighting under full MMA options.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to know when it is safe to free a hand and strike? The felt resistance to rotation through your grips and frames: when the pin feels solid enough to free a hand for a knee or short shot, versus when striking would loosen control. Not a fixed combination or memorized rhythm. Q Holding isn't the win here, so what is, and what is the failure on either side of it? Control AND damage at once: sustained output, multiple clean strikes, while the wall pin holds. If damage drops, control is too tight; if control drops, you over-committed to strikes. Q Why must you grind economically? Grinding is work. Spend energy on openings that actually exist, not on muscling shots that cost you position and gas you out. Q What distinguishes this game from Wall to Ground? You stay on the wall: no transition to the ground. The whole focus is dirty boxing, knees, and grinding from the standing pin.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🥊Attacker

Maintain control (shoulders flat, hips controlled), find openings for knees and short punches, manage energy, deny escape while creating damage.

🛡️Defender

Create frames to block strikes, work rotation escapes, nullify knee opportunities, find a reversal or escape.

Coaching cues

⚖️Control AND damage

Ask: "Are you controlling AND damaging?" Many do one or the other. If damage drops, control is likely too tight; if control drops, they're over-committing to strikes.

🔋Economical grinding

Grinding is work. Spend energy on openings that exist, not on muscling shots that cost position.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Must deal damage (not just hold)→Develops the control-to-damage skill Stay on the wall→Distinguishes grinding from a ground transition Multiple clean strikes to win→Rewards sustained output, not single shots Progressive defender options→Scaffolds resistance from escape to striking back Wall position with striking→Creates a true dirty-boxing environment

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), degeneracy emerges as multiple damage solutions develop for the same control situation.

What the attacker reads

✋Haptic

Control stability → when a strike is safe; defender's frame position → where openings exist.

👁️Visual

Defender's guard → knee vs. punch opportunity; escape attempts → when to reinforce control.

🧭Proprioceptive

Own balance and position → maintain structure while striking.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the attacker keeps the defender's shoulders and hips pinned flat vs. free to rotate while still landing strikes, track control time held against damage output. The press→pin-or-escape phase transition is the order parameter; the skill has formed when damage rises without the pin slackening.

Representativeness

Models: dirty boxing and grinding against the cage, the pressure-fighter's accumulation of damage while holding wall position.

Simplified: stay on wallprogressive defender optionscontrolled knee contact

An end-state game built on Wall Control; ground equivalent is TKO Pin.

Readiness to progress

  • Deals damage while maintaining control
  • Finds openings consistently
  • Knees land cleanly from position
  • Manages energy effectively (doesn't gas)
  • Can describe: "I found the opening when they…"

Warning signs

Just holds, no damage Loses position when striking Gasses quickly Knees miss consistently

Safety & related games

🤝 Light-to-moderate contact, knees especially controlled 🛑 Stop on excessive force or lost composure 🔁 Reset if grinding becomes stalling

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Wall Control · Pressure to Wall Follow-on→End-state game, no follow-on Related→TKO Pin · Wall to Ground

docs.title: Wall Pin to Ground
docs.text:
Wall → Ground · WrestlingWall Pin → Ground Control Wall → GroundWrestlingOffensiveIntermediateStabilize → Exploit

Convert vertical control into horizontal, bring the opponent down from the wall pin and land in hip-and-shoulder control.

StartEstablished wall pin, attacker in control, defender's escapes limited. → The GoalAttacker brings them down with control intact; defender scrambles, stands, or exploits a sloppy descent. → FinishHip + shoulder control for 2–3s → attacker wins · Scramble, stand, or reverse → defender wins.

The takedown isn't the point, the controlled descent is.

Off-balance first, then descend with connection intact. Control precedes gravity, never dump bodyweight.

Key Positions

🧱 Wall pinAt least two points of pinning control, the hips and upper body held immobile and flat to the wall. If the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin.

What to Read

Attune to the felt resistance to rotation through your grips, and the moment the opponent's base goes, the load through the clinch that tells you their weight is committed and a descent is available, versus when it must be forced. That changing resistance specifies when to go and where the weight wants to fall, not a memorized takedown entry.

The Starting Position

PlayersTwo, attacker transitioning to ground, defender contesting the descent. PositionEstablished wall pin, defender's escapes already limited. BoundaryWall or cage within a marked perimeter, transitioning onto mats. RolesAttacker brings them down and establishes ground control; defender scrambles, stands off the wall, or exploits a sloppy transition. Start & resetAttacker initiates from the pin; reset on full stand-up escape or lost control.

The Matchup

🤼 Attacker

Trying to bring the opponent to ground safely and establish hip and shoulder control.

Off-balance first, then descend, control precedes gravity, no dumping bodyweight. Shorten the scramble window and land in control, not on top of a scramble. VS 🛡️ Defender

Trying to scramble during the descent, use the wall to stand, or exploit sloppy transitions.

The transition opens a scramble window, exploit it. Use the wall to stand, attack a dropping head with DNS during the descent, or beat the attacker to control.

The Rules

🛑 No dumping bodyweightSafety-critical, prescriptive. Control precedes gravity, the attacker may not force the descent by dropping bodyweight or momentum. Connection stays maintained all the way down. ⚖️ Off-balance before descentPrescriptive. The takedown is created, not forced, the base must be compromised before the descent begins. 🌀 Scrambles allowedScrambles are expected during the transition. The scramble window is a contested period where both athletes have opportunities. 🎚️ GnP dial-up, by permissionAt Levels 3 and 4, when strikes are on, the coach explicitly grants a meaner dial on ground-and-pound: mid-grapple, strength is already compromised, so firmer strikes stay safe. Use striking as a disincentivization tool, the cost that punishes stalling and lazy structure. Ground games train smashing on the ground, not grappling for its own sake. ⏱️ 2–3 second ground controlGround control means hips and shoulders controlled, stable for 2–3 seconds, a control threshold, lower than a TKO pin's dominance. ⬛ Stay inside the perimeterPlay happens against a wall or cage within a marked perimeter, any shape, transitioning onto mats. Both players stay inside it.

How to Win

Attacker Hip + shoulder control held 2–3s → attacker wins.The defender can't easily stand or reverse, and the position is stable. This is control, not yet dominance. Defender Scramble to neutral, stand off the wall, or reverse → defender wins.Any of the three exits the position before the attacker can consolidate control.

The Levels

1Controlled descentBring them down safely.No strikes. Success is a descent with maintained connection, the controlled-descent mechanic itself. 2Deny scrambleFollow and control.Defender actively scrambles during descent; attacker must follow and establish control. Success is control despite the scramble attempt. 3TKO pin threatAdd the exploit layer.After ground control, attacker works toward TKO pin access; strike threats become available. Introduces the exploitation layer. 4Full MMAStrikes throughout.Light strikes throughout the transition, attacker to set up and maintain control, defender during the scramble window. Wall-to-ground under realistic striking pressure.

Recall Check

Test yourself before moving on. Answer out loud, then reveal what good looks like.

Q What are you reading to know when to descend and where the weight wants to fall? The felt resistance to rotation through your grips and the moment the opponent's base goes: the load through the clinch that tells you their weight is committed and a descent is available, versus when it must be forced. Not a memorized takedown entry. Q The takedown isn't the point. What is, and what is the safety-critical rule? The controlled descent is. Control precedes gravity: off-balance first, then descend with connection intact. Never dump bodyweight or momentum to force it. Q What is the scramble window, and what does each player do with it? The contested period during the transition where both have opportunities. The attacker shortens it and lands in control; the defender exploits it, using the wall to stand, attacking a dropping head with a DNS, or beating the attacker to control. Q What counts as the attacker's win, and how is it different from a TKO pin? Hip and shoulder control held 2 to 3 seconds: the defender can't easily stand or reverse. This is control, not yet dominance, a lower threshold than a TKO pin.

Go Deeper

Task focus & coaching cues

Each role's job

🤼Attacker

Off-balance, then descend with connection maintained; shorten the scramble window; establish hip and shoulder control immediately on landing.

🛡️Defender

Exploit the scramble window, use the wall to stand, attack a dropping head, or beat the attacker to control.

Coaching cues

⚖️Base compromised?

Ask attackers: "Was their base compromised before you went?" Controlled descent requires reading balance, not forcing through strength.

🌀Scramble window

The transition is a contested window, posture and balance decide it, and DNS threats are most dangerous here. Attackers shorten it; defenders exploit it.

Constraints-Led analysis

Constraints → Affordances

Control precedes gravity (no dumping)→Develops controlled descent, not forcing Off-balance before descent→Creates earned, not forced, takedowns Scrambles allowed and expected→Realistic transition dynamics 2–3s hip + shoulder control→Clear success definition Wall → ground transition→First environment-change game

Implements Constrain to Afford (Renshaw et al., 2019), develops transition-affordance perception: reading when descent is available versus when it must be forced. The scramble window becomes a shared learning arena.

What the players read

✋Haptic

Attacker: defender's base stability → when descent is available; connection quality → where to follow. Defender: descent initiation → scramble timing.

👁️Visual

Attacker: scramble direction → anticipate and counter. Defender: wall position → stand-up; attacker head → DNS during scramble.

🧭Proprioceptive

Attacker: own balance during transition → controlled vs. falling.

What we measure (order parameter)

Whether the attacker keeps the opponent's shoulders and hips pinned flat vs. free to rotate through the descent and into the landing, track whether control survives the scramble window. The press→pin-or-scramble phase transition is the order parameter; the skill has formed when controlled descents consistently end in immediate control.

Representativeness

Models: taking an opponent from cage to ground, controlled descent, scramble-window management, and DNS threats, identical to competition.

Simplified: start from pinno strikes at L12–3s control criteria

First environment-transition game; builds on Wall Control and feeds Stand-Up Loop.

Readiness to progress

  • Descents are controlled, not dumped
  • Connection maintained through the transition
  • Scrambles won consistently
  • Ground control established in 2–3 seconds
  • Can describe: "I felt their base go so I…"

Warning signs

Dumps opponents Loses position in the scramble Gets DNS'd during descent Controlled but not controlling

Safety & related games

🤝 Controlled landings, no dumping bodyweight 🛑 Stop on uncontrolled descent or slam 🔁 Reset if the attacker forces through strength

Where it sits

Prerequisite→Wall Control Follow-on→Stand-Up Loop · TKO Pin emerges Related→TKO Pin · Full MMA Expression

docs.title: Win Ladder
docs.text:
Format · Self-Scaling DifficultyWin Ladder

Provisional (WIP): derived from class recording 2026-06-06

Captured live from class and pending review on the platform. Name and structure may change. Remove the WIP status once confirmed.

FormatAny GameSelf-ScalingWin = Harder Start

The Win Ladder is a format, not a standalone game. Take any asymmetric game and define a sequence of starting positions, from most favorable to least. Every time the attacker wins, their next round starts one rung lower. The game scales itself to the player, with zero coach intervention.

Winning costs you your head start, so every round finds your edge.

Success demotes you to a harder start; failure keeps you where you are. The player always plays at the rung they've earned. Self-scaling, no coach required.

The Rules of the Format

🪜 Define the rungs firstBefore playing, state the ladder: an ordered list of starting positions from most to least favorable for the attacker. Three to five rungs works; each must be a real, definable position. ⬇️ Win = demote yourselfWhen the attacker wins, their next round starts one rung lower. The win condition and the defender's rules never change, only the attacker's starting advantage shrinks. ⏸️ Lose = stay putNo demotion on a loss. The player sits at the hardest rung they can still win from, which is exactly the amber-zone difficulty the session wants. 🎯 Same win, every rungThe objective never changes between rungs, only the start does. If the win condition needs to change, that's a new game, not a ladder rung.

Worked Example: Wall Single-Leg (as played 2026-06-06)

Start shoulder to shoulder on the wall. Attacker wins by putting one or both of the defender's hips on the ground for a meaningful period (knees and shoulders don't count). Defender wins by separating, reversing to top, or threatening a top neck strangle (guillotines, ninjas; finish the threat, win the round; let it go, lose it). Strikes on, GnP dial-up by permission. Each attacker win moves the next start down a rung:

1High singleLeg up, grip under the knee.The leg is hiked as high as the attacker wants, gripped under the knee, not past it (no high-crotch). The most favorable start on the ladder. 2Body lockDouble unders, hands locked.The attacker gets connected double underhooks; the defender sets up however they want, typically a whizzer plus head control. 3Regular singleHands connected, leg down.Connected hands on the leg, but the defender's foot stays on the floor. Their base is back; the finish must be earned. 4Low singleKnees on the ground.The attacker starts kneeling on the mat with a low single. The hardest rung, earned by winning everything above it.

Run It On These Games

🧱Wall to Ground

The worked example above: takedown entries at the wall, from high single down to low single.The ladder gives Wall to Ground a self-scaling start without touching its win conditions or safety rules.

🚪Escape games

Demote the escaper: each win starts them deeper in trouble (side control → mount → back).Any of the per-position escape games can chain into a ladder along the position hierarchy, harder starts for proven escapers.

🤝Clinch entries

Each win starts the entry from a worse tie: inside ties → collar tie → no ties at range.Same logic standing: the proven clinch-enterer earns progressively less initial connection.

What to Read

The format trains calibration on top of the base game's information. Each rung removes some of the attacker's pre-made connection, so the attacker must perceive what the lost grip used to give them and rebuild it mid-action, while the defender attunes to the felt resistance through the remaining contact to find the escape the new rung affords.

Why this is a format, not a game

The ladder doesn't define a problem of its own: the base game supplies the start type, the win condition, and the information to read. What the ladder supplies is difficulty regulation: success demotes the winner's start, so each player self-selects into productive instability (the amber zone) without the coach adjusting anything. It is a control-parameter schedule you can lay over any asymmetric game, the same way Positional Sparring lays a live competitive layer over a defined position. See Session Design for the two dials this format turns automatically.

docs.title: Learning Paths
docs.text:
Guided, ordered journeys through the games. Each path stacks games in dependency-correct order so every step builds on skills you already have. New to the system? Start with Foundations First, it's free.

  • Foundations First ⭐

    The free starter path, build the defensive shell and range awareness every other path assumes.

  • Striking Offense

    Isolate each weapon, then learn to land combinations and sustain pressure toward a finish.

  • Closing the Distance

    Win the circle and convert open-space control into clinch and takedown entries.

  • The Wall Game

    Establish, exploit, escape, and cycle the wall pin, the vertical control battle.

  • The Ground Game

    Access, control, and finish from top; escape, recover, and stand up from bottom.

  • The Complete Fight

    The capstone, thread every phase from first touch to a finish.

docs.title: Closing the Distance
docs.text:
Who this is for

Players comfortable in open space who want to stop trading at range and start dictating where the fight happens, the clinch, the wall, or the mat.

The journey: Win the circle, convert spatial control into a clinch, and chain that pressure into wall pins and takedowns.

docs.title: The Path
docs.text:
