1. Touch Game, Manage initiative and range/timing, the foundation before space, pressure, and clinch.
  2. Pressure to Clinch, Win the circle: manage distance and convert spatial control into the clinch.
  3. Pressure to Wall, Chain strikes into a controlling, damaging wall position for wall dominance.
  4. Pressure to Takedown, Chain strikes to a takedown while managing all three threats at once on defense.
  5. Open Space Takedown, Land controlled takedowns where escape routes and DNS threats are at their most dangerous.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Complete Foundations First, Touch Game starts this path, and Pressure to Takedown also requires Close-Range Defense. On the defensive side, pair this path with Takedown Defense and Counter-Wrestling, the counters to the entries you're drilling here.

docs.title: Where this leads
docs.text: Wall entries open into The Wall Game; takedowns open into The Ground Game. Both converge in The Complete Fight.

docs.title: The Complete Fight
docs.text:
Who this is for

Advanced players who have worked the individual paths and want to thread every phase together, from first touch at range to a finish on the mat.

The journey: Run one continuous arc through all four phases, striking range, clinch, wall, and ground, chaining each transition toward a TKO or submission.

docs.title: The Path
docs.text:
1. Touch Game, Manage initiative and range/timing, the opening phase, controlling distance.
  2. Pressure to Clinch, Win the circle: convert spatial control into the clinch.
  3. Pressure to Takedown, Chain strikes to a takedown while managing all three threats on defense.
  4. Wall Control, Use the wall as the dominant constraint to organize control.
  5. Wall to Ground, Make the controlled transition from wall to ground, control precedes gravity.
  6. Ground Control, Train the Stabilize stage: control that holds while creating openings to finish.
  7. The finish, Convert control into the end state: a TKO from accumulated damage or a submission from a stabilized position.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: This is the capstone, complete every prior path first: Foundations First, Striking Offense, Closing the Distance, The Wall Game, and The Ground Game. In particular, Ground Control sits at the end of the Ground Game's own chain (Ground Escape → Positional Sparring → Ground Access → Ground Control), so that path must be complete before the finish here makes sense.

docs.title: Where this leads
docs.text: This is the end of the journey, the point where the isolated skills become one fight. From here, raise the level inside any game toward full MMA expression.

docs.title: Foundations First
docs.text:
Who this is for

Brand-new players, or anyone building the defensive shell and range sense the rest of the system assumes. This is the free starter path.

The journey: Build a reliable defense one solution at a time, integrate those solutions under pressure, then learn to manage range and initiative, the on-ramp to everything else.

docs.title: The Path
docs.text:
1. Tight Block, Build the blocking shell and composure under fire, absorbing punches behind a tight guard.
  2. Parry the Straight, Develop the parry as a dedicated skill: deflecting straights through pattern recognition and timing.
  3. Slip the Straight, Develop the slip as a dedicated evasion skill: moving the head offline to make straights miss.
  4. Evade the Punch, Integrate evasions by matching the right head movement to the punch type, slip straights, duck hooks.
  5. Close-Range Defense, Build defensive adaptability: choosing when to block, parry, or dodge as solutions scale up.
  6. Touch Game, Manage initiative and range/timing, the foundation before space, pressure, and clinch.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: None, this is where you start.

docs.title: Where this leads
docs.text: Once your defense and range feel automatic, branch into Striking Offense to build your attack, or Closing the Distance to turn range control into clinch and takedown entries.

docs.title: The Ground Game
docs.text:
Who this is for

Players who get the fight to the mat, or get taken there, and need both halves of ground fighting: surviving and recovering from bottom, then accessing, controlling, and finishing from top.

The journey: Start with survival from the bottom, build the escape and recovery hierarchy, win the positional scramble, then access and stabilize top control to finish.

docs.title: The Path
docs.text:
1. Ground Escape, Build the escape hierarchy: read weight and pressure to get out of bad spots.
  2. Leg Reclaim, Recover position incrementally, creating separation with the legs.
  3. Ground to Standing, Return to standing, the ultimate ground escape in MMA.
  4. Ground Defensive Submission, Use submission as a defensive weapon from bottom position.
  5. Positional Sparring, Build positional-value awareness and the ability to win scrambles and consolidate position.
  6. Ground Access, Develop guard passing and position advancement, the Access stage before control.
  7. Ground Control, Train the Stabilize stage: control that holds while creating openings to finish.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
All ground games assume the Decision States concept. Ground Escape itself has no game prerequisites, so this path is enterable on its own; to reach the ground in a live fight, complete Closing the Distance (Open Space Takedown) or The Wall Game (Wall to Ground / Stand-Up Loop) first.

Ordering note

The documented prerequisites form a cycle (Ground Access ↔ Positional Sparring), so a strict order is impossible. This path leads with the bottom/defensive games (which only require Ground Escape), then seats Positional Sparring before Ground Access, see the report for detail.

docs.title: Where this leads
docs.text: Ground Control sets up the TKO and submission finishes that close out The Complete Fight.

docs.title: Striking Offense
docs.text:
Who this is for

Players who can defend and manage range, and now want to build an attack, from isolating single weapons to sustaining pressure toward a finish.

The journey: Sharpen each striking weapon in isolation, then combine them into combinations that land, and finally sustain offense through resistance.

docs.title: The Path
docs.text:
1. Lead Hand Offense, Develop the lead hand as a weapon, finding variety and timing within a tight constraint.
  2. Rear Hand Offense, Develop the rear (power) hand, finding timing, entries, and setups for power shots.
  3. Lead Leg Offense, Develop the lead leg, finding range, timing, and variety within a tight constraint.
  4. Rear Leg Offense, Develop the rear (power) leg, finding timing, setups, and entries for power kicks.
  5. Land the Target, Build target selection, combination construction, and misdirection without a counter-threat.
  6. Sustained Offense, Develop sustained pressure: maintaining the attack through resistance rather than single-shot landing.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Complete Foundations First to defend and manage range before you commit to attacking. Land the Target and Sustained Offense also assume the Three Zones and Confidence Rating concepts.

docs.title: Where this leads
docs.text: Sustained Offense feeds the KO/TKO finish in The Complete Fight. To turn forward pressure into grappling, move to Closing the Distance.

docs.title: The Wall Game
docs.text:
Who this is for

Players who can pressure an opponent to the cage and want to own the vertical battle, pinning, grinding, escaping, and cycling the wall.

The journey: Arrive at the wall, establish a dominant pin, exploit it for damage, defend it, and learn how wall positions cycle rather than resolve.

docs.title: The Path
docs.text:
1. Pressure to Wall, Chain strikes into a controlling, damaging wall position for wall dominance.
  2. Wall Control, Use the wall as the dominant constraint to organize vertical stabilization and control.
  3. Wall Grinding, Deal damage from the wall while keeping control, distinct from transitioning to ground.
  4. Wall Escape, Escape and reverse off the wall, the defensive answer to wall control.
  5. Wall to Ground, Make the controlled transition from wall to ground, where control precedes gravity.
  6. Stand-Up Loop, Manage the loop: understand how wall positions cycle rather than resolve in one action.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Complete Closing the Distance, both Pressure to Wall and Wall Control require Pressure to Clinch, and Wall Control gates every later step here.

docs.title: Where this leads
docs.text: Wall to Ground and the Stand-Up Loop both spill into The Ground Game, and the full wall sequence is a core thread of The Complete Fight.

docs.title: Training Mindset
docs.text:
System PrincipleApplies to Every GameLearning Over Winning

The games are academic exercises, not competitions. The goal is learning, not winning. Points, wins, and role switches are measurement tools, they tell you whether you're grasping the concept. They are not trophies to collect.

When you play these games, you are studying, experimenting, testing hypotheses, gathering feedback, and building understanding.

Be Your Own Harshest Critic

Only claim wins you genuinely earned. This is the fundamental training mindset for the ecological method. If a game has an honor system, claiming a false win doesn't help you, it actively hurts your development by giving you false feedback.

Three questions to keep yourself honest:

  • Did I actually accomplish what the game asks?
  • Would I be confident saying this counted if someone was watching closely?
  • Am I learning, or am I gaming the system?

If you're not sure whether something counted, it probably didn't. Reset and try again.

Games Are Vehicles, Not Destinations

The games exist to create specific learning conditions. They constrain the environment so you can focus on particular skills. The game itself is not the point, the skill development is.

"I need to win this game"→"I need to understand this skill" "I scored more points"→"I successfully applied the concept" "I beat my partner"→"I learned something about how this works"

Your training partner is not your opponent. They're your collaborator in creating the conditions for both of you to learn.

The Cost of Ego

If you prioritize winning over learning, the price shows up fast:

🎭False confidence

You'll claim wins you didn't earn.The result is false confidence and no real improvement, you've taught yourself the wrong lesson.

🙈Avoided challenge

You'll avoid situations where you might "lose."You stop challenging yourself, and challenge is exactly where growth happens.

🎲Gaming the rules

You'll game the rules instead of developing skills.You end up with technique without understanding, hollow at the moment you need it.

📉Plateau

You'll stall because you're optimizing for the wrong thing.The athletes who improve fastest are honest about what they can and can't do, seek out challenges, and treat every rep as a chance to learn.

Applying This Mindset

🎯Skill isolation games

The win condition is a clear target, not a trophy.When you parry, slip, or evade cleanly, that's feedback you're understanding the skill. When you get hit, that's feedback something needs work. A clean success beats ten sloppy "wins" that teach nothing.

🤝Honor system games

Games like Land the Target only work if you're honest.If you set out to land a left hook to the body and land it cleanly, claim it. If you were going for the head and got lucky with a body shot, don't, that's not what the game is training.

♻️All games

Reset when you should. Give real resistance.Don't bend the rules to avoid switching roles. Give your partner real resistance, not token opposition. Accept getting hit or taken down as information, not failure.

For Coaches

Watch for the ego signals, and address the mindset directly when you see them:

Claims wins too quickly or frequentlyA rush to claim is usually a rush past the learning. Slow it down and ask what actually happened. Avoids the "losing" positionSteering away from disadvantage means steering away from the exact reps that build the skill. Gets frustrated when the game exposes weaknessExposure is the point. Frustration is fine when managed, reframe it as information. Prioritizes looking good over getting betterPerformance for the room is the opposite of training for real skill.

"The goal isn't to win. The goal is to learn. If you're winning every time, we need to make it harder. If you're struggling, that's where the growth happens."

Create a training culture where struggle is valued, honesty is expected, and improvement matters more than performance.

The Mindset in Four Lines

  • Games are academic exercises, treat them that way.
  • Be your harshest critic, only claim what you earned.
  • Prioritize learning over winning, points are feedback, not trophies.
  • Your partner is your collaborator, you're both here to improve.

The athletes who adopt this mindset improve faster, develop deeper understanding, and ultimately become better fighters, because they're training for real skill, not imaginary points.

System evolution notice

This principle applies across all games and may be referenced in game-specific documentation.

docs.title: Constraints-Led Approach (CLA)
docs.text:
Knowledge BaseTheoretical FoundationMMA Training System

This is the theoretical foundation for our ecological MMA training system. Every game, every constraint, every design decision traces back to the principles documented here.

Primary Source

Renshaw, I., Davids, K., Newcombe, D., & Roberts, W. (2019). The Constraints-Led Approach: Principles for Sports Coaching and Practice Design. Routledge.

For the complete bibliography including foundational theorists (Gibson, Bernstein, Kelso, Newell, Turvey), see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

Where to Start

New to CLA?→Theory then Key Concepts Designing a session?→Environment Design + Session Design Building MMA games?→MMA Applications Coaching?→Implementation + Pitfalls Quick lookup?→Glossary + Quick Reference

Knowledge Base Contents

🧠Theory

Ecological dynamics, theoretical foundations, key influences.

🔑Key Concepts

Constraints, affordances, perception-action coupling, skill acquisition.

🏗️Environment Design

The 4 EDP principles, the core methodology.

📋Session Design

GROW model, session planning frameworks.

🥋MMA Applications

Combat-sports-specific implementations.

🎓Implementation

Coaching guidelines, feedback, assessment.

⚠️Pitfalls

Common mistakes and solutions.

📖Glossary

Complete A–Z terminology reference.

⚡Quick Reference

Checklists, cards, quick guides.

What Is the Constraints-Led Approach?

CLA is a coaching methodology predicated on Ecological Dynamics Theory, it reframes what athletes, skill, learning, and the coach actually are.

🧬Athletes

Are complex adaptive systems.Networks of integrated physical, cognitive, emotional, and tactical subsystems, not a fixed set of techniques to be programmed.

🌱Skill

Is successful adaptation to the environment.Not the acquisition of fixed techniques, skill lives in the relationship between performer and environment.

🔀Learning

Emerges from the interaction of constraints.Not from instruction-following, behavior reorganizes as constraints change.

🏛️The Coach

Is an environment architect.A learning designer, not a technique prescriber, they build the conditions and let solutions emerge.

Key Relationship Hierarchy

Ecological Dynamics→The Theory Nonlinear Pedagogy→The Pedagogical Framework Constraints-Led Approach→The Methodology

Fundamental premise: "The individual–environment relationship is the appropriate scale of analysis for understanding human behavior." For MMA, the fighter–environment relationship (opponent, cage/ring, rules, surface) is the fundamental unit, not the fighter in isolation.

The Four Environment Design Principles

These principles operationalize CLA into actionable coaching methodology, see Environment Design for full details.

1️⃣Session Intention

What is the overriding goal that guides all decisions?The intention acts as an overriding, organizational constraint, every other decision answers to it.

2️⃣Constrain to Afford

What constraints will invite the behaviors we want?Design-in constraints that offer, invite, and encourage, without forcing one specific solution.

3️⃣Representative Learning Design

Does practice look AND feel like competition?Sample the critical informational constraints of the performance environment so solutions transfer.

4️⃣Repetition Without Repetition

Are we repeating problem-solving, not movements?Design-in the right amount of variability so athletes repeat the problem, not identical reps.

How CLA Informs Our Game Design

Every game in this system embodies CLA principles:

Constraints shape behavior→Rules create the learning environment Affordances invite action→Scoring systems reward target behaviors Self-organization→Athletes discover solutions, not prescribed techniques Representative design→Games include opponent, pressure, context Perception-action coupling→Reading and reacting stay connected Variability→Multiple levels, opponent types, conditions

Source · The Constraints-Led Approach (Renshaw, Davids, Newcombe & Roberts, 2019). Document version 1.0.

docs.title: Environment Design Principles (EDP)
docs.text:
CLA Knowledge BaseThe Core MethodologyFour Principles

Navigation: ← Key Concepts | CLA Index | Session Design →

Sources

The four Environment Design Principles are the core methodology from Renshaw et al. (2019). Representative Learning Design draws on Pinder et al. (2011). Repetition Without Repetition originates with Bernstein (1967). For the complete bibliography, see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

The EDP framework operationalizes CLA into four actionable principles that guide every coaching decision in the ecological approach.

1️⃣Session Intention

The overriding, organizational constraint.

2️⃣Constrain to Afford

Invite the behaviors you want.

3️⃣Representative Learning Design

Practice looks AND feels like competition.

4️⃣Repetition Without Repetition

Repeat the problem, not the movement.

Principle 1 · Session Intention

"The intentions of the session act as an overriding and organizational constraint." , Renshaw et al., 2019

  • Intentions shape cognitions, perceptions, and actions.
  • Coach and athlete intentions must be aligned.
  • Intentions determine constraint-manipulation decisions.
  • Intentions vary with training phase (pre-season vs. competition week).

Session intention categories

🌱Learning

Develop new skills/patterns.Higher challenge, acceptance of failure.

🎚️Adapting

Refine existing patterns.Varied conditions, problem-solving.

🔥Performing

Execute under pressure.Representative conditions, full intensity.

🎯Attuning

Calibrate to the environment.Familiarization, preparation.

Matching intentions to learning stage

Coordination Stage→Exploring, searching for solutions Adaptability Stage→Exploiting, refining, optimizing

For MMA: A fighter learning takedown defense (coordination stage) needs sessions exploring defensive options. A fighter refining it (adaptability stage) needs varied attackers and conditions to stress-test their solutions.

Principle 2 · Constrain to Afford

"Design-in constraints to offer / invite / encourage learners to explore opportunities for action related to the session intention." , Renshaw et al., 2019

Offer→Key affordances related to session intention Invite→Engagement through exaggeration/emphasis Avoid→Over-constraining (forcing vs. inviting)

The outlet analogy: a good constraint affords, it does not prescribe

Ecological psychology gives the cleanest test for a good constraint (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 271). Compare two ways to wire a building:

📍"Put an outlet exactly here"

Other-organization. One acceptable outcome, dictated from outside.

📐"Outlets every 12 feet"

A local rule. Infinite valid layouts, "the solution will emerge."

A good constraint is always the second kind. "Defend the takedown, points for staying standing" is "outlets every 12 feet"; "when they shoot you must sprawl" is "outlet exactly here." If a constraint forces one specific solution, it is over-constraining. See the Ecological Psychology section.

Constraining strategies

🔍Exaggeration

Amplify affordances to make them obvious.Larger targets, slower speeds.

📣Emphasis

Draw attention to specific affordances.Reward systems, verbal cues.

🚧Restriction

Limit options to channel exploration.Reduce techniques available.

➕Addition

Add elements to create affordances.Include opponents, obstacles.

Over-constraining vs. effective constraining

"Two-touch only" rules→Reward systems for quick passing Forced technique prescription→Environmental setup that invites technique Removing all decision-making→Channeling decisions within options Static, unopposed drilling→Dynamic, contextual practice

Co-adaptation principle: Constrain the opposition to facilitate behavior change in your athletes. To develop counter-striking, constrain the attacker to initiate first, the defender (primary learner) must then read and respond. The constraint on one creates the learning opportunity for the other.

Principle 3 · Representative Learning Design (RLD)

"Ensure that what learners are seeing, hearing, and feeling in the practice environment is similar to the performance environment." , Renshaw et al., 2019; RLD framework by Pinder, Davids, Renshaw & Araújo (2011)

🎯Action fidelity

The core concept."The movement solutions performers develop in practice must be effective when transferred to the performance environment."

❓The core question

Does practice sample what matters?"Does the practice environment sample the critical informational constraints present in the performance environment?"

RLD assessment questions

  • Does practice contain relevant informational constraints from performance?
  • Is perception-action coupling maintained (not decomposed)?
  • Are the emotional demands represented (Affective Learning Design)?
  • Will learned behaviors transfer to competition?

The RLD continuum

Low representativenessmodified conditions, simplified context↔High representativenessfull competition simulation

Neither extreme is always appropriate, match RLD to athlete needs.

RLD assessment checklist

  • Information sources, are relevant visual, haptic, acoustic cues present?
  • Perception-action coupling, is perceiving and acting integrated (not separated)?
  • Decision-making, are the decisions required similar to competition?
  • Opponents/partners, are human interactions included (not static objects)?
  • Emotional intensity, is appropriate pressure/consequence present?
  • Time constraints, is temporal pressure representative?
  • Space constraints, is the spatial setup representative?

Task simplification vs. task decomposition

✅Simplification: preferred

Scale difficulty while keeping the task whole.Maintains perception-action coupling, preserves essential information flows. Example: smaller cage, slower pace.

⚠️Decomposition: problematic

Break the task into isolated parts.Decouples perception from action, removes context and information. Example: shadow boxing without a target.

Key distinction: Simplification keeps the task intact but adjusts difficulty. Decomposition breaks the task apart, destroying the perception-action relationship.

What reduces RLD (avoid these)

Dribbling around conesNo adaptive opponent information. Hitting pads without partner reactionDecoupled perception-action. Unopposed drillingNo defensive information to read. Ball machines / predictable feedsMissing anticipatory cues. Static positional workNo dynamic decision-making.

What enhances RLD (implement these)

Live training partners→Real affordances, adaptation Varied opposition styles→Multiple solutions required Consequence systems→Emotional representativeness Competition simulation→Full pressure experience Contextual drilling→Maintains perception-action coupling

Affective Learning Design (ALD): "Does the practice look AND feel like the real thing?" Built on Purpose (clear, meaningful goals), Consequence (real outcomes for success/failure), and Emotion (representative intensity and pressure). Sparring looks like fighting, but adding scoring, consequences, or competition formats makes it feel like it.

Principle 4 · Repetition Without Repetition

"Practice is a particular type of repetition without repetition." , Bernstein, 1967. Repeat the process of solving problems, not identical movements. Design-in the appropriate amount of variability and (in)stability.

Variability is the feature, not the bug: the infant-walking robot tournament

The strongest evidence that variable practice beats regular practice comes from a simulation tournament (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 169–170). Robots trained on highly variable infant-walking paths were pitted against robots trained on regular, repetitive paths in a 4,000-game tournament. The variable-trained robots won 2,888 to 75. Variability does not degrade the skill, it builds the adaptable, degenerate system that copes with novelty. See the Ecological Psychology section.

Types of practice variability

🎯Task variability

Varying the task performed.Different techniques for the same goal.

🌍Environmental variability

Varying conditions.Different opponents, surfaces.

🧍Individual variability

Varying performer state.Fatigued, fresh, stressed.

Sources of variability: how to implement

Task goals, vary objectives→Submit vs. survive vs. control Rules, modify regulations→No takedowns, no striking Opponents, different partners→Size, style, skill level Environment, change conditions→Cage vs. mats, fresh vs. fatigued Equipment, alter gear→Different glove weights Timing, adjust duration→Round length, rest periods Intensity, vary effort→Light flow to hard sparring

Variability zones

🟢Green · Optimal challenge

High success likelihood.Promotes exploration, enhances adaptability.

🟡Amber · Productive instability

Requires search for new solutions.Promotes reorganization, the edge of chaos, optimal for learning.

🔴Red · Excessive chaos

Too much variability.Unmanageable / overwhelming, information overload.

Matching variability to skill level

Beginner / Coordination→Low variability, systematic (controlled), guided toward solutions Intermediate→Moderate variability, mixed Advanced / Adaptability→High variability, unsystematic (random), challenge to explore

Systematic manipulation (controlled, progressive constraint changes) targets specific adaptations. Unsystematic manipulation (random, varied changes) develops general adaptability.

Why variability matters

🤹Develops dexterity

Multiple solutions to problems.

🔁Builds degeneracy

Functional redundancy.

🔄Enhances adaptability

Coping with novel situations.

🔓Prevents system capture

Avoiding locked-in patterns.

Managing instability

Deliberately move performers to less stable areas to promote reorganization. Instability is necessary for learning, not a failure state.

📊Instability indicators

How to recognize productive instability.Increased performance variability, temporary performance decrement, search behaviors (trying new solutions), frustration (productive when managed).

🧑‍🏫Coach response

How to support the transition.Recognize instability as necessary for learning, support through the transition, allow time for reorganization, celebrate exploration, not just success.

Next · Session Design, the GROW model and planning frameworks.

docs.title: Glossary of Terms
docs.text:
CLA MethodologyA–V ReferenceHover for Definition

← Pitfalls · CLA Index · Quick Reference →

The shared vocabulary of the system. Every term traces to ecological dynamics, affordances, synergies, constraints, attractors. Hover or tap any term for its full definition.

Sources

Definitions adapted from Renshaw et al. (2019), Gibson (1979), Bernstein (1967), Newell (1986), and Kelso (1995). For complete bibliography, see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

A

📐 Action FidelityThe degree to which movement solutions developed in practice will be effective in the performance environment. 🔄 AdaptationThe process by which behavior becomes better suited to a specific environment. 💓 Affective Learning Design (ALD)Practice design that represents the emotional demands of competition, ensuring practice not only looks like but feels like the real thing. 🎁 AffordanceAn opportunity for action that the environment offers relative to an individual's capabilities. Affordances are relational (between person and environment), action-relevant, and can be perceived directly. 🗺️ Affordance LandscapeThe range of opportunities for action available in a practice or performance environment, ranging from narrow (few options) to rich (many options). 🟠 Amber ZoneA variability zone representing productive instability at the "edge of chaos" where new solutions must be searched for, reorganization is promoted, and optimal learning occurs. 🎯 AttunementThe process of becoming increasingly sensitive to relevant information in the environment.

C

🎛️ CalibrationAdjusting the relationship between perceived information and action execution to achieve desired outcomes. 🤝 Co-AdaptationThe continuous mutual adjustment between interacting individuals, such as attacker and defender in combat sports. 🕸️ Complex Adaptive SystemA system composed of many interacting components that can adapt and self-organize. Athletes and teams are viewed as complex adaptive systems in CLA. 🚧 ConstraintsInformation sources that shape the emergence of behavior; boundaries that guide organization. Constraints are not negative limitations but neutral features that reduce possible configurations to functional ones. 🧭 Constraints-Led Approach (CLA)A coaching methodology based on manipulating task, individual, and environmental constraints to guide skill development through self-organization rather than prescription. 🔗 Coordinative StructureSee Synergy.

D

♻️ DegeneracyThe ability of structurally different elements to achieve the same functional outcome. In MMA, having multiple techniques that can accomplish the same goal (e.g., various ways to achieve a takedown). 🔢 Degrees of FreedomThe independent components (muscles, joints, limb segments) that must be organized for coordinated action. The "degrees of freedom problem" refers to the challenge of organizing these many components. 🤹 DexterityThe ability to find motor solutions that are correct, quick, rational, and resourceful. Reflects the ability to solve novel movement problems in the moment. 👥 Dyadic SystemA two-person interaction unit (e.g., attacker-defender pair) that forms the basic unit of analysis in combat sports.

E

🌐 Ecological DynamicsThe theoretical framework integrating ecological psychology, dynamical systems theory, and complexity science that underlies CLA. 💪 EffectivityAn individual's capability to act on environmental affordances. The complement to affordance (environment offers, individual can use). ✨ EmergenceNovel behaviors arising from the interaction of system components, not prescribed or pre-planned. In CLA, technique emerges from constraints rather than being taught directly. 📐 Environment Design Principles (EDP)Framework of four principles for CLA session design: Session Intention, Constrain to Afford, Representative Learning Design, and Repetition Without Repetition. 🌍 Environmental ConstraintsGlobal features external to the task that shape behavior, including physical conditions (temperature, surface), social factors (culture, crowd), and historical factors (traditions, rule evolution).

F

📍 Fixed PrinciplesSignature positions or anchors that functional movements tend to pass through, representing stable states in coordination. 🏛️ Form of LifeThe behaviors, skills, attitudes, and practices that shape culture in a society, organization, or training environment. 🧊 Freezing Degrees of FreedomA strategy in early learning where the performer locks out movement options to reduce complexity, resulting in stiff or rigid movement patterns.

G

🟢 Green ZoneA variability zone representing optimal challenge where success likelihood is high, exploration is promoted, and adaptability can develop without overwhelming the learner. 📋 GROW ModelGoal-Reality-Options-Way Forward planning framework adapted for CLA session design.

I

👤 Individual ConstraintsPersonal characteristics unique to each performer that shape behavior, including physical (height, weight), psychological (motivation, confidence), experiential (skill level, history), and biological (fatigue, injury) factors. Also called Organismic Constraints. 🔗 Individual-Environment MutualityThe inseparable relationship between performer and performance context. The fundamental premise of ecological dynamics. 〰️ InstabilityTemporary perturbation of existing patterns, often necessary for reorganization and learning. Distinguished from stability (consistent patterns) and chaos (excessive disruption). 🎯 IntentionA goal-directed cognitive state that shapes perception-action coupling. Session intention is the overriding goal that guides constraint manipulation. 🧬 Intrinsic DynamicsExisting coordination tendencies an individual brings to a learning situation, which interact with task demands to produce behavior.

K

📖 Knowledge ABOUTDeclarative, verbalizable understanding of the environment, gained through instruction, video, and discussion. 🤸 Knowledge OFProcedural, action-supporting understanding developed only through direct experience in the environment. Cannot be taught verbally.

N

🌀 Nonlinear PedagogyThe pedagogical framework derived from ecological dynamics that serves as the bridge between theory and CLA methodology. 📈 NonlinearityDisproportionate relationship between inputs and outputs; small changes can have large effects, and large changes can have small effects. Learning is nonlinear.

O

🤲 Optimal GripHaving functional control over the field of affordances in a given context. The hallmark of skilled performance. 👤 Organismic ConstraintsSee Individual Constraints. 🔒 Over-ConstrainingManipulating constraints to force rather than invite behavior, removing decision-making and exploration.

P

🔁 Perception-Action CouplingThe tight integration of information pickup and movement execution. Information regulates action; action guides further information pickup. A continuous loop, not sequential processes.

R

⏳ Rate LimiterA constraint that currently limits the emergence of higher-level performance. Identifying the rate limiter helps target training. 🔴 Red ZoneA variability zone representing excessive chaos where variability is unmanageable, overwhelm occurs, and learning is disrupted. 🔄 Repetition Without RepetitionBernstein's principle that practice should repeat the process of problem-solving rather than identical movements. Variation in execution while repeating the goal. 📡 Representative Learning Design (RLD)Practice design that samples critical informational features of the performance environment, ensuring what athletes see, hear, and feel in practice is similar to competition.

S

🌱 Self-OrganizationSpontaneous emergence of coordinated patterns without external micromanagement. Behavior organizes itself under constraints rather than being prescribed. 🎯 Session IntentionThe overriding goal that guides constraint manipulation in a training session. One of the four EDP principles. 🤝 Shared AffordancesOpportunities for action that exist between multiple individuals, as in partner or team training. ⚡ SkillIn ecological dynamics, the ability to successfully adapt to a performance environment. Defined as relationship between performer and environment, not as internal acquisition. 🧩 Soft AssemblyThe temporary, context-dependent formation of coordinative structures. Synergies are soft assembled (not hardwired) and can rapidly form and dissolve. 🎼 SynergyA temporarily assembled grouping of body segments, muscles, and joints that function as a single unit to achieve a task goal. Also called Coordinative Structure.

T

📋 Task ConstraintsFeatures specific to the activity being performed that shape behavior, including goals, rules, equipment, spatial boundaries, and time limits. ✂️ Task DecompositionBreaking a task into isolated parts for separate practice. Problematic in CLA because it separates perception from action. 🎚️ Task SimplificationScaling difficulty while keeping the task whole. Preferred over decomposition because it maintains perception-action coupling. ➡️ TransferThe application of learning from one context (training) to another (competition). The ultimate measure of training effectiveness.

V

🎲 VariabilityDifferences in movement patterns or practice conditions. In CLA, variability is deliberately designed into practice to build adaptability. 🚦 Variability ZonesCategories (Green/Amber/Red) describing the challenge level of practice environments, from stable and predictable to chaotic and overwhelming.

Next · Quick Reference, checklists, cards, and quick guides.

docs.title: Coaching Implementation Guidelines
docs.text:
CLA MethodologyInstructionFeedbackAssessment

← MMA Applications · CLA Index · Pitfalls →

The coach is an environment architect, not a technique prescriber. You supply local rules and direct attention to information, then let the fighting style self-organize within the constraints you set.

Sources

Coaching guidelines and feedback principles from Renshaw et al. (2019). External focus research from Wulf (2013). For complete bibliography, see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

The Role Shift

Technique prescriber→Environment designer Tells athletes what to do→Creates conditions for emergence Corrects movements→Manipulates constraints Source of solutions→Facilitator of discovery The house-building philosophy: give rules, not a blueprint

The single best one-line articulation of the coach's role comes from the self-organizing house-building robots (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 284): give the contractors a list of rules and let them adapt to local circumstances, rather than a single rigid blueprint, "the houses will be far more adapted to their circumstances… and you (the divine architect) will have to do a lot less work." The coach supplies local rules (constraints); the fighting style self-organizes. (See the Ecological Psychology section.)

Five Questions for Constraint Manipulation

📦WHAT

Selection from Task, Individual, Environment categories.

🎚️HOW

Amount of change, direction.

⏱️WHEN

Timing within and between sessions.

🎯WHY

Connection to session intention.

👤WHOM

Individual differences.

Instruction Guidelines

✅Use instructions to

Direct attention to affordances (not prescribe actions) · frame intentions and goals · provide knowledge ABOUT (to complement knowledge OF) · guide exploration (not replace it).

🚫Avoid using them to

Prescribe exact movements · remove decision-making · direct internal focus of attention · replace experiential learning.

External vs. Internal Focus

Effect on environmentvsBody part movement Target / outcomevsMuscle activation Opponent / partnervsJoint angles Equipment contactvsLimb positions

Example, Internal (avoid): "Rotate your hip when you punch." External (use): "Drive through the target with your fist." External focus promotes automatic, fluid movement; internal focus disrupts natural coordination.

Attention-Directing Cues

🎯Target-focused ✅

"Hit the chin", "Control the wrist".Good cue, external, outcome-focused. Points attention at what to affect in the world.

💥Effect-focused ✅

"Make them feel your weight", "Move them".Good cue, names the effect on the opponent, not your body.

↔️Distance-focused ✅

"Close the space", "Create distance".Good cue, directs attention to the spatial relationship.

🏁Outcome-focused ✅

"Score the takedown", "Land clean".Good cue, frames the goal, leaves the solution open.

Body part, "Rotate your hips", "Turn your shoulder"Poor cue, internal, mechanical. Directs attention inward and disrupts coordination. Muscle, "Squeeze your glutes", "Engage your core"Poor cue, internal, mechanical. Joint, "Bend your elbow to 90 degrees"Poor cue, internal, mechanical. Position, "Keep your hands at chin level"Poor cue, internal, mechanical.

Feedback: Types

📋Knowledge of Results (KR)

After attempts, outcome information: "That landed", "You escaped".Tells the athlete what happened as an outcome.

🔍Knowledge of Performance (KP)

After attempts, process information: "You read the level change early".Tells the athlete about the process that produced the outcome.

⚡Concurrent

During performance, real-time cues: "Now!", "There!".Delivered in the moment to couple attention to information.

❓Questions

After exploration, promote reflection: "What did you notice?".Builds the athlete's own perception rather than handing them the answer.

Feedback serves calibration

What feedback (KR/KP, questioning, and especially fading feedback over time) is actually tuning is calibration, the learner's mapping between the detected information and the scaled response (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 206). Calibration is how to use a perceived invariant correctly (how hard to commit given this τ, how much to redirect given this felt resistance). Reducing feedback over time forces the athlete to own that mapping. Crucially, calibration is action-mode-specific and does not transfer (see Transfer Assessment below). (See the Ecological Psychology section.)

Feedback: Frequency

Beginner→More frequent, need guidance, can't self-assess well Intermediate→Moderate, building self-assessment capability Advanced→Less frequent, can self-assess; too much feedback disrupts

Key principle: reduce feedback over time to develop self-regulation.

The Questioning Approach

💬Instead of telling, ask

"What did you notice about their weight distribution?" · "What options did you see there?" · "What would happen if you…?" · "What made that one work?"

🌱Why it works

Develops the athlete's perception · builds self-assessment · promotes ownership of solutions · creates knowledge OF through reflection.

Assessment: What to Measure

🏁Outcome

Success rate, win/loss.Methods: competition results, drill completion.

🎞️Process

Movement quality, decisions.Methods: video analysis, observation.

🔄Adaptation

Response to variability.Methods: novel-situation testing.

➡️Transfer

Competition performance.Methods: match analysis vs. training performance.

Observation Framework: What to Watch For

  1. Search behaviors, is the athlete exploring options?
  2. Solution variety, are multiple techniques emerging?
  3. Reading ability, is the athlete perceiving affordances?
  4. Response timing, are reactions becoming more coupled?
  5. Stability under pressure, do skills hold when challenged?

Transfer Assessment

Key question: does training performance transfer to competition? Do not assume, test it.

Transfer is mode-specific and fragile: do not assume it

The deepest reason to test transfer rather than assume it: ecological psychology shows that calibration and affordance-perception do not transfer across action modes (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 160, 206). Infants who learned which slopes afforded safe crawling started over "from zero" when they began walking, the information is entirely different (different limbs, optic flow, base of support). Subjects calibrated to an optic-flow drift while walking did not carry that calibration to throwing or kicking. Implication for our library: a fighter who has learned to read τ for striking defense has not thereby learned to read τ for takedown defense. Build separate learning paths per action mode and verify transfer empirically, this is exactly why phase-scaffolding (striking → clinch → takedown) works: it re-educates attention at each new mode rather than assuming carry-over. (See the Ecological Psychology section.)

1 · Compare→Training behaviors to competition behaviors 2 · Assess→Decision-making quality under pressure 3 · Evaluate→Adaptability to novel situations 4 · Track→Competition outcomes over time 5 · Test across modes→Verify a skill built in one mode appears in another; never assume carry-over Skills appear in training but not competition Athlete reverts to old patterns under pressure Decision-making degrades in live settings Techniques work only against familiar partners

Managing the Learning Environment

🛟Psychological safety

Failure without judgment, exploration without ridicule, questions without shame, mistakes as information.Coach behaviors: celebrate exploration not just success · frame failures as data · model curiosity about problems · avoid punitive responses to errors.

🎯Individualization

Know each athlete's learning stage, constraints, optimal challenge, feedback response, and history.Adjust: constraint selection · variability amount · feedback frequency & type · session intensity.

Managing Frustration

Productive frustration→AMBER zone, precedes breakthrough; support it, don't eliminate it. Encourage persistence. Unproductive frustration→RED zone, leads to shutdown or regression; tighten constraints. Verbally acknowledge the challenge.

The Five Ws: Quick Reference

📦WHAT

Task / Individual / Environment.

🎯WHY

Session-intention connection.

⏱️WHEN

Timing within and between sessions.

🎚️HOW

Degree and direction of change.

👤WHOM

Individual athlete considerations.

Next · Pitfalls, common mistakes and how to avoid them.

docs.title: Key Concepts & Definitions
docs.text:
CLA Knowledge BaseCore VocabularyConstraints · Affordances · Coupling

Navigation: ← Theory | CLA Index | Environment Design →

Sources

Core definitions adapted from Renshaw et al. (2019) and foundational works by Gibson (1979), Bernstein (1967), and Newell (1986). For the complete bibliography, see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

Constraints

Constraints are NOT negative limitations. They are "boundaries or features that shape the form of a complex adaptive system searching for functional states of organization." , Newell, 1986

📡Information sources

They shape behavior rather than block it.

🧭Neutral boundaries

They guide organization, not punish.

🎚️Configuration reducers

They narrow possibilities to functional ones.

The three categories of constraints (Newell, 1986)

🧍Individual (Organismic)

Personal characteristics unique to each performer.Vary between AND within each person over different timescales (Renshaw et al., 2019).

🎯Task

Features specific to the activity being performed.Goals, rules, equipment, spatial and temporal features, the layer the coach manipulates most directly.

🌍Environmental

Global features external to the task.Physical, social, and historical conditions, the broad context the task sits inside.

Individual constraints: examples & MMA specifics

Physical, height, weight, limb length, flexibility→Reach advantage, weight class, body type Psychological, cognitions, emotions, motivation, intentions→Fight IQ, aggression management, mental toughness Experiential, skill level, training & competition history→Years training, fight record, martial-arts background Biological, genes, fatigue, injury status→Recovery capacity, injury history

Task constraints: examples & MMA specifics

Task goals, score, win, learn technique→Win by KO, survive round, work takedown defense Rules, competition regulations→Unified rules, organization-specific rules Equipment, tools, protective gear→Glove size, mouthguard, cup, cage vs. ring Spatial, playing area, boundaries→Octagon size, ring dimensions, fence Temporal, time limits, round duration→Round length, rest periods

Environmental constraints: examples & MMA specifics

Physical, temperature, altitude, surface, lighting→Arena conditions, mat texture, humidity Social, cultural expectations, team dynamics→Gym culture, corner communication Historical, traditional practices, sport evolution→Rule changes over time, stylistic trends

Affordances

"What the environment offers, provides, or furnishes for an organism", opportunities for action relative to an individual's capabilities. , Gibson, 1979

🔗 RelationalBetween individual capabilities and environmental properties, never purely in one or the other. ⚡ Action-relevantThey specify what can be done, and they solicit behavior, affordances invite action. 👁️ Perceived directlyNo complex cognitive processing required, perception detects them, it does not compute them. ⚖️ Not all functionalSome affordances lead to poor outcomes, "either for good or ill" (Gibson, 1979). Not every available action is a good one.

Types of affordances in combat sports (applied from Hristovski et al., 2006)

👊Strikeability

When/where to strike.Open chin, dropped hands, forward lean.

🤼Takedown-ability

A takedown opportunity.Overextension, single-leg position, clinch access.

🔒Submission-ability

A submission becoming available.Extended limb, back exposure, neck access.

🚪Escape-ability

An escape route.Hip space, posture breaks, frame creation.

↩️Counter-ability

A counter opportunity.Telegraphed attack, recovery position.

The affordance landscape

Narrow landscapefew options, highly structured, prescription-based, directive↔Rich landscapemany options, unstructured, discovery-based, exploratory

Coaching implication: move learners across this landscape based on their needs and development stage (Renshaw et al., 2019).

Shared affordances: "The richest affordances are provided by the behaviors of other people." , Renshaw et al., 2019. Training partners provide dynamic, responsive affordances, requiring intimate knowledge of your own action possibilities, your partner's, and how the two interact moment-to-moment.

Perception-Action Coupling

"Information regulates action, and action guides the pickup of information for further behavioral adaptations." , Gibson, 1979. Perception and action are not separate sequential processes, they form a continuous loop mediated by the performer's intentions.

Information sources in combat sports

👁️Visual

Opponent position, distance, movement.Reading feints, seeing openings.

✋Haptic

Contact pressure, grip tension.Feeling submission opportunities through grips and frames.

🧭Proprioceptive

Body position, balance, orientation.Base awareness during grappling.

🔊Acoustic

Corner instructions, crowd, impacts.Adjusting strategy mid-fight.

The specific invariants: what actually specifies the affordance

Modalities are the channels; the information they carry is a set of higher-order invariants. Ecological psychology shows perception detects these directly, there is nothing to "compute, " only something to attune to (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 82). Three named invariants matter most for combat sports:

⏱️τ (tau / time-to-contact)

The relative rate of optical expansion of an object's texture, specifies time-to-arrival, assuming current conditions continue. On a closing strike, shot, or level change the defender attunes to the rate of expansion, not absolute distance or speed. (p. 74, 165–166)

🌊Global optic flow

Self-motion and how to control it, rate of flow = movement speed. The fighter's own closing pressure and cage-edge approach. (p. 147–148)

✋Inertial array (inertia tensor)

For dynamic/effortful touch, resistance to rotation at a grasp point specifies length, orientation, and "off-balance-able-ness" of what is held. Grips, frames, clinch: what the hands feel through contact. (p. 123–127)

Coaching consequence: "Reading" should mean attuning to a named invariant (τ on a closing punch, the inertial array through a grip), not vaguely "seeing openings." See the Ecological Psychology section for the full treatment.

Implications for training (Renshaw et al., 2019)

  • Keep perception and action coupled during practice.
  • Avoid task decomposition that separates perception from action.
  • Use task simplification (scaling) instead of decomposition.
  • Include relevant information sources in all practice tasks.

Example: Shadow boxing separates perception from action (no opponent to read). Pad work with a reactive holder maintains the coupling.

Self-Organization & Coordination

Self-organization = spontaneous tendencies for system components to form coordinated patterns without external micromanagement (Kelso, 1995). "Spontaneous emergence" is shorthand, the full definition has four load-bearing requirements, all of which must hold.

  • Not other-organized, no external leader, blueprint, or rigid hierarchy directing the parts.
  • Minimally-intelligent components, parts follow simple local rules; no "loan of intelligence" required.
  • Local rules + constant mutual interaction, rules use only local information; remove the interaction and the pattern stops (metronomes on a non-transmitting surface never synchronize, p. 271–272).
  • Emergence at a larger scale, a nonrandom pattern comprehensible only at a larger scale than the parts, and not predictable from them.

For MMA: the "components" are the fighter's synergies and the two-fighter dyad; the "local rules" are the game's constraints; the emergent pattern is the fighting style. The coach supplies rules and lets the pattern emerge, they do not choreograph it. (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 270–272)

Murmuration of starlings·Schooling of fish Neural pattern formation→Technique combinations & defensive reactions emerge from constraint satisfaction

Coordination dynamics (Turvey, 1990)

🔄Intra-individual

Organizing functional relations between body parts to achieve task goals.

🤼Inter-individual

Organizing body position relative to opponents, surfaces, equipment.

The degrees-of-freedom problem (Bernstein, 1967)

The human movement system has enormous degrees of freedom that must be organized. Solutions develop in three stages:

1FreezingLock out degrees of freedom.Initial stage, a rigid stance, limited hip rotation. Reducing complexity to get the pattern off the ground. 2ReleasingGradually free more components.Intermediate stage, adding hip torque to punches as control develops. 3ExploitingUse reactive/passive forces.Advanced stage, flowing between positions with economy of motion, capitalizing on free forces.

Synergy formation (Turvey, 1990)

A synergy is a temporarily assembled grouping of body segments, muscles, and joints that function as a single unit to achieve a task goal.

🧷 Soft-assembled 🚫 Not permanently hardwired 🎛️ Emerge under constraints 🧩 Context-dependent ⚡ Rapidly formed & dissolved

For MMA: A takedown-defense synergy (sprawl) is assembled when needed and dissolves immediately after, allowing transition to other positions.

Dexterity

"The ability to find a motor solution for any external situation", to solve any emerging motor problem correctly, quickly, rationally, and resourcefully. , Bernstein, 1967

✅Correctly

Adequately and accurately.

⏩Quickly

In both decision-making and execution.

💡Rationally

Expediently and economically.

🎒Resourcefully

Quick-wittedly and with initiative.

For MMA: A dexterous fighter doesn't just know techniques, they find solutions to novel problems in the moment.

Degeneracy

"The ability of structurally different elements to perform the same function or yield the same output." , Edelman & Gally, 2001; applied to motor learning by Davids et al., 2008. In MMA: multiple ways to achieve a takedown (single leg, double leg, body lock, trips) from the same start.

🛡️Builds robustness

If one solution is blocked, others exist.

🔄Enables adaptability

Copes with changing conditions.

🔓Prevents technique capture

Never stuck with a single solution.

Degeneracy ↔ Multirealizability

Degeneracy is the same phenomenon ecological psychology calls multirealizability, the property of self-organizing systems whereby the same parts reorganize for many goals, and many different organizations achieve the same goal (your legs can walk, kick, skate, or arabesque; many takedown entries reach the same finish) (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 274). Building degeneracy in training is building a multirealizable system. See the Ecological Psychology section.

Skill Acquisition Framework

Skill redefined: "Skill acquisition is better framed as skill adaptability, forming more functional relationships with a performance environment." , Renshaw et al., 2019

Skill as an acquired entity/status→Skill as successful adaptation to environment Technique-focused→Relationship-focused Internal to the performer→Between performer and environment

Characteristics of skilled performance

🪨 StableConsistent under similar conditions. 🌊 FlexibleAdaptable to changing conditions. ⚙️ EconomicalEfficient use of energy. 🎯 Context-sensitiveAppropriate to specific situations. 🤹 DexterousQuick, accurate, rational, resourceful.

Optimal grip on affordances: "Skilled performance is predicated on having an optimal grip over the field of affordances." , Renshaw et al., 2019. That means perceiving the relevant affordances, knowing which to use and when, executing the appropriate actions, and continuously adapting to dynamic conditions.

Next · Environment Design Principles, the 4 principles that operationalize CLA.

docs.title: Combat Sports Applications
docs.text:
CLA MethodologyMMA-SpecificDyadic SystemsGame Design

← Session Design · CLA Index · Implementation →

The attacker–defender dyad is the basic unit of combat. Every game must include an adaptive opponent, keep reading coupled to reacting, and reward the fighter for perceiving what the opponent's position affords, not just executing their own offense.

Sources

Combat sports applications draw on Renshaw et al. (2019) and boxing-specific research by Hristovski et al. (2006). For complete bibliography, see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

MMA-Specific Constraints

📋Task constraints

Spatial, temporal, rules, scoring, equipment, goals.Spatial: cage size, fence proximity, corner access. Temporal: round duration, rest, clock management. Rules: unified rules, org variations, illegal techniques. Scoring: judging criteria, finish priorities. Equipment: glove size/type, attire. Goals: win method (KO, sub, decision), survive, damage.

🌍Environmental constraints

Physical, social, cultural, competitive.Physical: temperature, altitude, surface texture. Social: crowd noise, corner communication, media. Cultural: gym culture, stylistic traditions. Competitive: opponent style, ranking implications.

👤Individual constraints

Physical, technical, tactical, psychological, experiential.Physical: reach, weight, flexibility, cardio. Technical: grappling, striking, defensive ability. Tactical: fight IQ, game planning, adaptability. Psychological: confidence, aggression, composure. Experiential: fight experience, training background.

Position-Specific Affordances

🧍Standing

Strike-ability, clinch-ability, level-change opportunities.

🤼Clinch

Takedown angles, dirty boxing, break possibilities.

🛡️Guard

Sweep-ability, submission availability, stand-up options.

📐Side Control

Transition options, submission paths, pin maintenance.

⬆️Mount

Finish opportunities, position maintenance, submission setups.

🔙Back

Choke availability, position retention, opponent escape paths.

Combat Sports Dyadic Systems

The attacker–defender dyad is the basic unit of analysis in combat sports. It shows continuous co-adaptation, locally created information, constraint by distance/timing/position, and self-organization under competitive conditions.

Never train one side of the dyad in isolationEven when focusing on developing the attacker, include a responding defender, the information lives in the interaction.

Perceiving Affordances for the Opponent

The most MMA-relevant finding in ecological psychology is that the same lawful principles that govern an animal–environment system govern an animal–animal–environment system (Blau & Wagman, 2022, Ch. 11, p. 172–191). A fighter does not just perceive what they can do, they perceive, directly, what the opponent can do, because the same properties of the opponent that structure the energy they encounter also structure the energy you encounter. Combat is the book's own worked example.

👁️Affordances for self

"What can I do from here?", the lines, levels, and grips available to you right now.

🎯Affordances for the opponent

"What does their position afford them?", recognizing when the opponent is set to shoot, counter, or off-balance you (p. 175).

Win conditions should reward reading the opponent's affordances, e.g., recognizing the instant the opponent's stance affords them a shot, not just executing your own offense.

Two Preferred Distances: and the Dead Middle

The combat-sport gem: in kendo, competitors settle into two preferred interpersonal distances and avoid the middle that affords neither (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 188–189). A near range affords offense; a far range affords defense; the neutral middle affords nothing useful and is a trap. A fighter's choice to step in or out is driven by perceived affordances for striking that opponent. More-experienced competitors spend more time in the near (offensive) range because they can enter striking position faster.

Near range→Affords offense, live in it; experienced fighters spend more time here Dead middle→Affords neither, a trap; move through it, don't dwell Far range→Affords defense, reset and read from here

This is the primary-theory grounding for distance management as "the substrate underneath striking output." Build distance games on two preferred ranges + a dead middle: reward fighters for living in their advantageous range and avoiding the neutral zone. Make "more time in the offensive range" a progression marker.

Reading Movement that Shares Force Dynamics

Perceiving an opponent's affordances is learned by watching them move, but only movement that shares force dynamics with the threat is informative (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 180–181). Watching someone walk informs their jump-and-reach ability (both need vertical leg forces); watching them twist does not. Domain-specific: basketball players read jump-and-reach affordances for others better than non-players, but are no better at sitting/standing-reach affordances, experience tunes perception of the specific affordances of the sport.

⬇️Level change ↔ shot

A weight drop shares force dynamics with the takedown, it specifies "shoot-able." Tape study and live reading should focus here.

⚖️Weight shift ↔ strike

Loading a side specifies the strike it powers. Read the load, not the hand.

🚫Irrelevant motion

Feints that don't share force dynamics with a real threat carry no affordance information, train athletes to discount them.

Social Synergies: Break the Rhythm

The two-fighter dyad self-organizes into a synergy that spans both people, the same in-phase/anti-phase dynamics that govern a single body (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 186–188). People rocking in chairs synchronize unintentionally when they can see each other; people walking together fall into a shared gait. Fighters fall into rhythm with an opponent the same way.

A game can exploit this: breaking the opponent's rhythm = perturbing the social synergy. This grounds pace, "the cadence at which one fighter forces the engagement." Reward the fighter who imposes their cadence and disrupts the opponent's.

No mirror neurons required

Opponent-reading is information detection, not mental simulation (Blau & Wagman, 2022, Box 11.1, p. 177–178). Coach it as "read the information the opponent's movement gives off, " never "imagine yourself in their position." See the Ecological Psychology section.

Designing MMA Training with CLA

Developing Takedown Defense

Traditional ✗→Drill sprawl repeatedly → static partner → moving partner → hope it transfers CLA ✓→Create environment with takedown threat → allow self-organization of defense → vary timing/angles/setups → include striking context → adjust threat level (variability zones) ⚠️Why traditional fails

Separates perception from action · no variability in timing/angle/setup · defender executes on cue rather than reads · low transfer to live situations.

✅Why CLA works

Perception-action coupling maintained · multiple defensive solutions emerge · attacker variability builds adaptability · striking context ensures transfer to MMA, not just wrestling.

Developing Counter-Striking

Session intention: improve counter-striking off opponent attacks.

🎯Constrain to afford

Partner must initiate attacks · reward system for successful counters · limit fighter's initiating options.

📡Representative design

Realistic attack setups · maintain emotional intensity · keep perception-action coupled.

🔄Repetition without repetition

Vary partner attack selection · change timing and distance · alter angles and combinations.

Developing Submission Defense

🔒Constraint setup

Attacker starts with submission partially locked · defender must escape before completion · reset on escape OR submission.

✅Why it works

Defender perceives a real submission threat (not simulated) · time pressure creates representative stress · multiple escape solutions emerge · attacker learns finishing, defender learns surviving.

Progressions

150% lockedEasier escape. 275% lockedHarder escape. 3Add positionEscape from position + submission threat. 4Full sparringPositional sparring with submission awareness.

MMA Game Design Principles

🥊1 · Include the opponent

Every game has an adaptive opponent, not static targets.Less effective → More effective: heavy bag → partner with pads who reacts · solo drilling → positional sparring · choreographed sequences → constrained live rounds.

🔗2 · Maintain coupling

Always reading real information and responding, never just executing.Decoupled → Coupled: shadow boxing w/o target → with reactive partner · mitts with called combos → mitts with visual cues · submission on compliant partner → with resistance.

🌡️3 · Right challenge level

Match the variability zone to the learning goal.Green: new skill, confidence, limited-technique sparring, light pace. Amber: refinement, adaptation, full-technique sparring, moderate pace. Red (sparingly): testing limits, high-intensity sparring near competition.

🧩4 · Allow multiple solutions

Don't prescribe a single "correct" technique.Bad: "When they shoot, you must sprawl." Good: "Defend the takedown, points for staying standing." The second allows sprawl, underhook, lateral movement, or any functional solution.

♻️5 · Build degeneracy

Multiple ways to achieve goals, so fighters aren't stuck when one option is gone.Takedown game: train with wrestlers (counter-wrestling emerges), judoka (anti-clinch emerges), strikers (level-change emerges). Varied opponents → varied solutions → robust skill.

Priority Principles for MMA Training

  1. Maintain perception-action coupling, never separate reading from reacting
  2. Include live opponents, pads and bags have limited transfer
  3. Vary opponents, different sizes, styles, skill levels
  4. Create representative pressure, emotional intensity matters
  5. Allow self-organization, don't over-prescribe technique
  6. Embrace productive instability, struggle leads to growth
  7. Know your fighters, individual constraints matter most

Data Points to Track

🎯Intention achievement

Did the session accomplish its goal?

📦Constraint effectiveness

Which constraints worked, which didn't.

➡️Transfer

Do trained skills appear in sparring/competition?

👤Individual responses

How each fighter responds to training.

📈Stage progression

Movement through coordination → adaptability.

🌡️Variability tolerance

How much instability each athlete can handle.

Next · Implementation, coaching guidelines, instruction, feedback.

docs.title: Common Pitfalls & Solutions
docs.text:
CLA Methodology10 PitfallsSigns · Cause · Fix

← Implementation · CLA Index · Glossary →

Most CLA failures are calibration errors at the extremes. Too tight or too loose, too safe or too chaotic, too much instruction or none at all. Each pitfall below names the warning signs, why it fails, and the fix.

Sources

Common implementation challenges identified in Renshaw et al. (2019). For complete bibliography, see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

The Ten Pitfalls

1 · Over-ConstrainingForcing behavior rather than inviting it. Signs: no decisions to make, only one "correct" solution, creativity stifled, training feels like following orders. Fix: design constraints that channel exploration while allowing choice. 2 · Under-ConstrainingEnvironment too vague, lacks direction. Signs: athletes don't know what to focus on, randomness without purpose, no learning direction, sessions feel aimless. Fix: establish clear session intentions, design specific affordances. 3 · Low Representative DesignPractice doesn't transfer to competition. Signs: techniques work in training but fail in fights, athletes can't read opponents, poor decision-making under pressure. Fix: include relevant information sources, maintain perception-action coupling. 4 · "Game is the Teacher" MisinterpretationCoach becomes too passive, lacks direction. Signs: vague practice, no targeted development, athletes under/overwhelmed, coach watches without adjusting. Fix: coach is an active environment architect, not a passive observer. 5 · Ignoring Individual DifferencesOne-size-fits-all approach. Signs: same drills for all, mismatched challenge, some progress while others stagnate. Fix: scale by critical π ratios, not absolutes, know each athlete's history, level, needs. 6 · Eliminating All InstructionThinking CLA means no verbal guidance. Signs: athletes have no context, waste time discovering basics, principles of play unknown, frustration. Fix: instruction supports exploration rather than replacing it. 7 · Abandoning StructureThinking CLA means chaotic, unplanned sessions. Signs: no session intention, random manipulation, no progression, athletes unsure what to learn. Fix: use a systematic framework (GROW, EDP) while remaining adaptive. 8 · Misunderstanding VariabilityAdding randomness without purpose. Signs: constant change with no rationale, athletes never develop stability, no skill consolidation. Fix: match variability to learning stage and intention. 9 · Confusing Simplification with DecompositionBreaking tasks apart instead of scaling them. Signs: isolated technical drilling, skills without context, perception separated from action. Fix: simplify by scaling the whole task, not by decomposing it. 10 · Neglecting Affective DesignPractice looks like competition but doesn't feel like it. Signs: perform well in training but freeze in competition, emotional regulation doesn't transfer. Fix: include emotional representativeness (Affective Learning Design).

Each Pitfall, In Depth

1 · Over-Constraining: forcing behavior rather than inviting it

Signs

Athletes have no decisions to make Only one "correct" solution exists Creativity is stifled Training feels like following orders

The trap vs. the fix

Over-constrained ✗→"When I throw, you must parry with your lead hand, then throw a jab-cross." Removes all decision-making, the athlete learns to follow instructions, not read situations. Better ✓→"Defend my attack, then counter. Points for clean counters." The athlete chooses the defense and counter based on what they perceive.

2 · Under-Constraining: environment too vague

Signs

Athletes don't know what to focus on Randomness without purpose No learning direction Sessions feel aimless

The trap vs. the fix

Under-constrained ✗→"Just spar and work on stuff." No session intention, athletes default to comfortable patterns, no targeted development. Better ✓→"Spar with a focus on creating angles after your opponent attacks. Points for landing while off the center line."

3 · Low Representative Design: practice doesn't transfer

Signs

Techniques work in training, fail in fights Athletes can't read opponents Decision-making poor under pressure Drill performance ≠ sparring performance

The trap vs. the fix

Low RLD ✗→Takedown defense against a partner who shoots on command without setups. No reading required, the defender knows when the attack is coming. Better ✓→Attacker can shoot anytime within a round; may or may not shoot. Defender must read the level change and react.

4 · 'Game is the Teacher' Misinterpretation: coach too passive

Signs

Vague practice without purpose No targeted development Athletes underchallenged or overwhelmed Coach watches without adjusting

The trap vs. the fix

The trap ✗→"The game teaches, so I just let them play." The game only teaches if it's well-designed, without manipulation, athletes repeat existing patterns.

The coach's job

  • Design the game with clear intention
  • Observe athlete responses
  • Adjust constraints based on what emerges
  • Create progressions that challenge and develop

5 · Ignoring Individual Differences: one-size-fits-all

Signs

Same drills for all athletes Frustration from mismatched challenge Some progress, others stagnate No consideration of physical/psych differences

The trap vs. the fix

The trap ✗→Running all athletes through identical sessions regardless of size, background, or skill. A 5'5" wrestler and a 6'2" kickboxer have different individual constraints.

How to individualize, scale by critical π ratios, not absolutes. Saying "individualize" is not enough; the mechanism is body-scaling. Warren's stair-climbing studies showed that whether a step is "step-on-able" depends not on its absolute height but on the ratio of step height to leg length, plotted against the ratio, tall and short people fall on the same curve, crossing a single critical π value (≈0.88) where the action becomes impossible, and people perceive that boundary accurately (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 79–81). The same logic governs crossing gaps (leg length), passing through apertures (shoulder width, critical ratio ≈1.16–1.3), and reaching (arm length).

Apply it: when a game sets a distance, target height, aperture, or range, define it as a ratio of the athlete's reach / leg length / shoulder width rather than a fixed number, so a 5'5" wrestler and a 6'2" kickboxer face the same relational problem (e.g. a "close range" start scaled to each defender's arm length, so blocking is the live affordance for both). (See the Ecological Psychology section.)

Considerations

📏Physical

Reach, weight, mobility, set constraints as critical ratios of these.

🥋Technical

Grappling vs. striking base.

🧠Psychological

Aggressive vs. reactive profile.

📈Learning stage

Coordination vs. adaptability.

6 · Eliminating All Instruction: misunderstanding CLA

Signs

Athletes have no context Wasted time discovering basics Principles of play unknown Frustrated by lack of direction

The trap

Refusing to explain anything because "discovery learning means they figure it out themselves." CLA doesn't mean no instruction, it means instruction supports exploration rather than replacing it.

Good vs. not-good instruction

✅Good

"Notice when their weight shifts forward, that's your cue." · "The goal is to create space; how you do it is up to you." · "Pay attention to the pressure through their grips."

🚫Not good

"When they do X, you must do Y." · "Your elbow needs to be at this angle." · "Execute the technique exactly like this."

7 · Abandoning Structure: chaos isn't CLA

Signs

No session intention Random constraint manipulation No progression between sessions Athletes unsure what they're learning

The trap

"CLA is all about emergence, so I don't plan sessions, I just see what happens." But emergence happens within constraints, without thoughtful design, nothing meaningful emerges.

CLA sessions are…

  • Planned, clear intention, designed constraints
  • Structured, progression through zones
  • Adaptive, adjustments based on what emerges
  • Connected, build on previous sessions

8 · Misunderstanding Variability: randomness without purpose

Signs

Constant change with no rationale Athletes never develop stability Sessions feel chaotic No skill consolidation

The trap

Changing the drill every 2 minutes "for variability." Variability serves adaptability, but athletes also need time to explore and stabilize solutions.

Match variability to stage

Coordination→Lower variability, allow exploration of solutions Adaptability→Higher variability, stress-test solutions

Remember: the goal is "repetition without repetition", repeating the problem-solving process, not identical movements. This still requires time with each problem.

9 · Confusing Simplification with Decomposition

Signs

Isolated technical drilling Skills practiced without context Perception separated from action "Parts" practiced independently

The trap

Decomposition ✗: practicing the "hand movement" of a jab without footwork, then footwork without hands, then "putting it together." The jab is a whole-body action where all parts inform each other, separating them breaks the synergy.

Simplify, don't decompose

🐢Slower tempo

…but the whole technique.

🪶Reduced threat

Lighter contact, but the whole exchange.

📐Smaller space

…but the whole movement.

🔢Limited options

…but the whole decision process.

10 · Neglecting Affective Design: looks like competition, doesn't feel like it

Signs

Perform well in training, freeze in competition Emotional regulation doesn't transfer Pressure causes breakdown "It's different when it counts"

The trap

Sparring that's technically similar to fighting but has no consequences, pressure, or emotional intensity. Competition includes emotional demands, if training doesn't represent these, athletes aren't prepared for them. The fix: Affective Learning Design.

Methods

🏅 Scoring with visible results ⚖️ Consequences for outcomes ⏱️ Time pressure 🥊 Competition simulation 👀 Audience / observation 🧹 Stakes (loser cleans the mats)

Quick Diagnostic

Athletes don't explore→Over-constraining, loosen constraints, allow more choice Training is aimless→Under-constraining, add clear intention and constraints Skills don't transfer→Low RLD, increase representativeness Coach feels useless→"Game teaches" misunderstanding, be active architect, not passive Some athletes stagnate→Ignoring individual differences, individualize constraints Athletes are lost→Eliminating instruction, add attention-directing cues Sessions feel random→Abandoning structure, use GROW/EDP frameworks No skill stability→Misunderstanding variability, allow consolidation time Parts don't integrate→Decomposition, use simplification instead Freeze under pressure→Neglecting affect, add emotional demands

Next · Glossary, complete A–Z terminology reference.

docs.title: Quick Reference Cards
docs.text:
CLA MethodologyCheat SheetChecklists · Dials · Diagnostics

← Glossary · CLA Index

Everything on one page. The plan, the dials, the diagnostics, at-a-glance cards to grab mid-session.

Session Planning Checklist

  • Session intention established and clear
  • Athlete skill level assessed (Coordination / Adaptability)
  • Affordances to design-in identified
  • Constraints selected (Task / Individual / Environment)
  • RLD level determined (Low / Medium / High)
  • Variability level determined (Green / Amber / Red)
  • Success criteria defined
  • Practice environment prepared
  • Athletes mentally prepared

The Five Ws of Constraint Manipulation

📦WHAT

Task / Individual / Environment.

🎯WHY

Connection to session intention.

⏱️WHEN

Timing within and between sessions.

🎚️HOW

Degree and direction of change.

👤WHOM

Individual athlete considerations.

Dialling Challenge Up / Down

🔺Increase challenge

Add opponents/partners · increase time pressure · reduce space · add consequences · increase variability · raise intensity · add cognitive load.

🔻Decrease challenge

Remove opponents/partners · relax time pressure · increase space · reduce consequences · decrease variability · lower intensity · simplify decisions.

The Four Environment Design Principles

1️⃣Session Intention
