What is the overriding goal?

2️⃣Constrain to Afford

What constraints will invite target behavior?

3️⃣Representative Learning Design

Does practice look AND feel like competition?

4️⃣Repetition Without Repetition

Are we repeating problem-solving, not movement?

GROW Model

G, Goal→What is the session intention? Define clear focus. R, Reality→Where is the athlete now? Assess current level. O, Options→What activities bridge the gap? Design constraints. W, Way Forward→How will you implement? Prepare environment.

Variability Zones

🟢Green Zone

High success likelihood · promotes exploration.Use for: beginners, warm-up, consolidation.

🟠Amber Zone

Productive instability · requires new solutions.Use for: main learning, adaptation.

🔴Red Zone

Excessive chaos · overwhelm/breakdown.Use: sparingly, for stress testing.

Session Flow Pattern

Green→Build confidence Amber→Challenge, adapt Green→Consolidate, end well

RLD Assessment: Quick Check

  • Are relevant information sources present?
  • Is perception coupled with action?
  • Are decisions similar to competition?
  • Are live partners included?
  • Is appropriate pressure present?
  • Is time representative?
  • Is space representative?

Simplification vs. Decomposition

Scale the whole task ✓vsBreak into parts ✗ Keep perception-action coupled ✓vsSeparate reading from reacting ✗ Adjust difficulty ✓vsRemove context ✗ Slower tempo, whole technique ✓vsIsolated technical drilling ✗

Feedback Types

📋Knowledge of Results

After attempt, "That landed clean".

🔍Knowledge of Performance

After attempt, "You read the setup early".

⚡Concurrent

During, "Now!", "There!".

❓Questions

After exploration, "What did you notice?".

External vs. Internal Focus

Effect on environment ✓vsBody part position ✗ Target / outcome ✓vsMuscle activation ✗ Opponent / partner ✓vsJoint angles ✗ "Hit the target" ✓vs"Rotate your hip" ✗ "Move them" ✓vs"Engage your core" ✗

Learning Stage Quick Guide

🔎Coordination

Searching, exploring, rigid.Coach focus: guide toward solutions, lower variability.

🌊Adaptability

Refining, flexible, stable.Coach focus: challenge with variability, stress-test.

Common Pitfalls: Quick Diagnostic

No exploration→Over-constraining · loosen constraints Aimless sessions→Under-constraining · add clear intention Skills don't transfer→Low RLD · increase representativeness Coach feels useless→Passive interpretation · be active architect Some athletes stagnate→Ignoring individuals · individualize Athletes are lost→No instruction · add attention cues Random sessions→No structure · use GROW/EDP No stability→Too much variability · allow consolidation Parts don't integrate→Decomposition · use simplification Freeze under pressure→No affective design · add emotional demands

The Three Constraint Categories

👤Individual

Physical (reach, etc.) · psychological · experiential · biological.

📋Task

Goals · rules · equipment · space/time.

🌍Environmental

Physical · social · cultural · historical.

All three categories interact to produce emergent behavior, no single category dictates the solution.

Position-Specific Affordances (MMA)

🧍Standing

Strike-ability, clinch-ability, level changes.

🤼Clinch

Takedown angles, dirty boxing, breaks.

🛡️Guard

Sweeps, submissions, stand-up.

📐Side Control

Transitions, submissions, pins.

⬆️Mount

Finishes, position retention, setups.

🔙Back

Chokes, retention, escape paths.

Periodization Quick Guide

1Off-SeasonLower RLD · higher variability · exploration. 2Pre-CampModerate RLD · moderate variability · building patterns. 3Fight CampHigher RLD · lower variability · specific refinement. 4Fight WeekHighest RLD · lowest variability · execution.

Session Debrief Questions

  1. What were the goals and expectations?
  2. What actually happened?
  3. What went well and why?
  4. What can be improved?
  5. Key takeaways: 3 to continue, 3 to change.

Return to · CLA Index.

docs.title: Session Design Framework
docs.text:
CLA MethodologyGROW ModelTwo DialsPeriodization

← Environment Design · CLA Index · MMA Applications →

A session is a designed environment, not a list of drills. You set a goal, read where the athlete is, choose constraints to bridge the gap, then prepare to deliver it, and you scale difficulty with two independent dials, not one.

Sources

The GROW model and session planning frameworks are adapted from Renshaw et al. (2019). For complete bibliography, see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

The GROW Model

G: Goal→What is the intention for this session? Establish primary focus. R: Reality→Where is the athlete now? Assess current skill level. O: Options→What activities will bridge the gap? Select practice environments. W: Way Forward→How will you implement? Plan delivery and preparation.

GROW: The Detail

🎯G: Goal

The intention for the session and how it links to the overall goal.Ask: What is the goal/intention for this session? How does it link to the overall goal? What is the focus for learning?Consider: align coach & athlete intentions · connect to long-term development · identify the "rate limiter."

📍R: Reality

Where the athlete is now and what affordances to design in.Ask: What is the current skill level (Coordination or Adaptability)? What affordances need to be designed-in? Which? Why? When?Assess via observation, video, performance data, across physical, technical, tactical, psychological factors.

🧩O: Options

The activities and constraints that bridge the gap.Ask: What practice activities bridge the gap? Which practice environment? What constraints, and how will you manipulate them? How will you measure performance?

🚀W: Way Forward

How you prepare and deliver.Ask: How will you prepare the practice environment? How will you prepare the performers? Anything else needed to be ready?Prepare: physical setup · mental framing · cultural environment (values, beliefs, climate).

Success Criteria (under Options)

We will know we have been successful if…

👁️We SEE

Observable behaviors.

📊The DATA SHOWS

Measurable outcomes.

🗣️The COACH SAYS

Qualitative assessment.

💭The PERFORMER FEELS

Subjective experience.

The Session Planner

Session GoalsIntention · skill level (Coordination/Adaptability) · method (Explore/Exploit/Execute). Representativeness DialA 1–10 slider, Low → High. ConstraintsTask · Individual · Environment. Variability DialGreen Zone → Amber Zone → Red Zone (low → high instability). Task Descriptions1 · Pre-test / warm-up · 2 · Main activity · 3 · Progression / variation · 4 · Post-test / cool-down.

Session Flow Pattern

Green Zone→Confidence, build, assess baseline; athletes arrive in varied states, green calibrates them Amber Zone→Challenge, the productive instability needed for learning Green Zone→Consolidation, lock in the pattern, end the session positively Why this pattern: functional noise, and hysteresis

The "productive instability" of the amber zone is what ecological psychology calls functional noise (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 242–243). Noise is not error: it constantly perturbs the system and is precisely what lets the "ball" escape a shallow, local attractor (a comfortable-but-suboptimal habit) and settle into a deeper, better one. In one study, added noise made participants more likely to discover the efficient strategy (p. 243). The amber zone is therefore deliberate, useful perturbation, not chaos to be avoided.

The GREEN → AMBER → GREEN flow also respects hysteresis (p. 250): a system's current state depends on its history/trajectory, not just present conditions. A pattern entered under amber-zone pressure persists, so the order in which you pass through the zones matters, you cannot treat a session as path-independent. (See the Ecological Psychology section.)

The 30-Second Game Plan

A standing ritual before every game: after the rules are stated, athletes take 30 seconds to set a plan. The prompt: "What are the things I can genuinely test to solve this problem?" Not a script to execute, a hypothesis to run. When it fails, that's data: try something different on purpose, and cycle through options until one works.

⏱️Before the game

30 seconds, one plan, stated to yourself.The plan makes the game a deliberate experiment instead of a brawl. It also exposes shark eyes early: if the plan is "land strikes" and the win condition is the wall, the plan is wrong.

🔬During the game

Treat failure as a result, not a verdict.The faster you realize the thing you did didn't work and try something different on purpose, the faster you cycle through options and find one that works. Frustration repeats; experiments iterate.

🔍Test the stated rules

Only the spoken constraints bind."Nobody said the elbows go on the ground. All I said was turtle." Athletes who probe the legal space of a rule set find solutions the coach didn't prescribe, exactly the point.

Captured from class, 2026-06-06. Pairs with the Session Debrief below: the plan opens the loop, the debrief closes it.

Scaling Difficulty: The Two Dials

📡Representativeness Dial (RLD)

1 → 10. Low = modified, simplified. High = full competition.How closely practice samples the information of the competition environment. Turn it up to demand more competition-like reading and pressure.

🌡️Variability Dial

Green (stable/predictable) → Amber (productive instability) → Red (too much chaos).How much instability is in the environment. Amber is the target for learning; red overwhelms.

Use them together: High RLD + Amber Zone = maximum learning transfer. Low RLD + Green Zone = safe exploration for beginners.

The two dials are control parameters

In dynamical-systems terms, the representativeness dial and the variability dial are control parameters, variables that, when turned, reorganize the fighter's attractor layout (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 250). Turning them does not dictate the new behavior; it changes which behaviors are stable. Other control parameters you turn in a session include speed, pressure, range, fatigue, and number of opponents. Expect that pushing one past a critical value produces an abrupt, nonlinear reorganization (a stance or rhythm suddenly switches) rather than a smooth change, design for that switch rather than being surprised by it. (See Theory and the Ecological Psychology section.)

Constraint Manipulation During Sessions

🔒When to tighten

Channel and contain.Athletes lost or frustrated (too much chaos) · need to channel exploration in a specific direction · building confidence after failure · introducing a new concept.

🔓When to loosen

Open and test.Athletes succeeding too easily · patterns becoming rigid · need to test adaptability · building toward competition demands.

Adjustment Signals

High success, low effort→Too easy, increase challenge (loosen / add constraints) Frustration, repeated failure→Too hard, decrease challenge (tighten / remove constraints) Trying new solutions→Productive instability, maintain current level Rigid, repetitive patterns→System capture, increase variability

The Four Session Types

🌱Learning

Developing new coordination patterns.Lower RLD (modified) · Green→Amber zones · higher acceptance of failure · exploration encouraged · coach more active in design.

🔧Adapting

Refining and stress-testing patterns.Moderate→High RLD · amber-zone emphasis · varied conditions · problem-solving required · coach observes and adjusts.

🏆Performing

Executing under representative pressure.High RLD (competition simulation) · Green→Amber zones · success orientation · full intensity · coach minimal intervention.

🎚️Attuning

Calibrating to a specific environment/opponent.Very high RLD (specific simulation) · green zone · familiarization · confidence building · used pre-competition.

Session Debrief: Five Questions

  1. What were the goals and expectations? Intended affordances · success criteria.
  2. What actually happened? Design effectiveness · coaching delivery · learning observed.
  3. What went well and why? Learning-design successes · instruction/constraint effectiveness.
  4. What can be improved? Design modifications · constraint adjustments.
  5. What are the key takeaways? 3 things to continue · 3 things to change.

Integration with Periodization

1Off-SeasonLower RLD · higher variability · exploration, new skills.Attractor op: Learning, birth of new attractors. Expect exploration and temporary instability. 2Pre-CampModerate RLD · moderate variability · building specific patterns.Attractor op: Learning → adaptation transition. 3Fight CampHigher RLD · lower variability · refining for the opponent.Attractor op: Adaptation, moving an existing attractor to this opponent's context. 4Fight WeekHighest RLD · lowest variability · execution, confidence.Attractor op: Adaptation, stabilize the moved attractor. Learning vs. adaptation across the cycle

Periodization maps cleanly onto the dynamical distinction (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 241). Off-season is learning, the birth of a new attractor, so expect exploration and temporary instability. Fight camp is adaptation, the movement of an existing attractor to fit a specific opponent, not the acquisition of something new. Reserve the words accordingly: do not call fight-camp refinement "learning, " and do not expect off-season instability during fight week. (See Theory.)

Next · MMA Applications, combat-sports-specific implementations.

docs.title: Theoretical Foundations
docs.text:
CLA Knowledge BaseEcological DynamicsFive Key Influences

Navigation: CLA Index | Key Concepts →

Sources

This section synthesizes foundational research in motor learning and ecological psychology. For the complete bibliography, see Sources & Theoretical Foundations.

Ecological Dynamics

An integrated framework for understanding the acquisition of movement skills and the development of expert performance, woven from four scientific strands.

👁️Ecological Psychology

Perception-action coupling, affordances. (Gibson, 1979)

🌀Dynamical Systems Theory

Coordination dynamics, self-organization. (Kelso, 1995)

🧬Evolutionary Biology

Adaptation, form of life.

✨Complexity Sciences

Emergence, nonlinearity.

"Ecological dynamics provides an integrated framework for understanding the acquisition of movement skills and the development of expert performance." , Davids, Button, & Bennett, 2008

Core Theoretical Principles

🔗Individual–Environment Mutuality

Performer and environment are inseparable.Fighter technique cannot be understood apart from opponent/context. (Gibson, 1979)

🧩Complex Adaptive Systems

Athletes are networks of integrated subsystems.Fighter = physical + cognitive + emotional + tactical subsystems. (Davids et al., 2008)

🌱Self-Organization

Spontaneous emergence of coordinated patterns.Combinations emerge from constraints, not memorization. (Kelso, 1995)

⚡Nonlinearity

A control parameter past a critical value triggers a phase transition, the behavior reorganizes abruptly."Minor rule changes revolutionize fighting styles" because a rule shifts a control parameter past its critical threshold, not vague butterfly-effect nonlinearity. (HKB; Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 250)

🌟Emergence

Novel behaviors arise from component interactions.New techniques emerge from training environments rather than being installed by instruction.

Key Theoretical Influences

👁️Gibson

Affordances & perception-action coupling.

🤸Bernstein

Degrees of freedom, repetition without repetition.

🌀Kelso

Coordination dynamics.

⚙️Turvey

Action systems.

📐Newell

The constraints model.

James Gibson (1904–1979): Affordances & perception-action coupling

Key Work

Gibson, J.J. (1979). The Ecological Approach to Visual Perception. Houghton Mifflin.

  • The environment offers opportunities for action (affordances).
  • Perception and action are tightly coupled, not separate processes.
  • Information in the environment directly specifies possibilities for action.
  • No need for complex internal representations or cognitive processing.

"Affordances are what the environment offers, provides, or furnishes for an organism." , Gibson, 1979

For MMA: Fighters directly perceive opportunities to strike, take down, or submit based on opponent positioning, they don't calculate these opportunities, they see them.

Nikolai Bernstein (1896–1966): Degrees of freedom & dexterity

Key Work

Bernstein, N.A. (1967). The Co-ordination and Regulation of Movements. Pergamon Press.

  • The human body has enormous degrees of freedom (muscles, joints, segments).
  • Coordination is the process of mastering these redundant degrees of freedom.
  • Learning involves freezing → releasing → exploiting degrees of freedom.
  • Dexterity: motor solutions that are correct, quick, rational, resourceful.

"Practice is a particular type of repetition without repetition." , Bernstein, 1967

For MMA: A beginner freezes their hips when punching (reducing complexity). An expert exploits hip rotation for power (using the freed degrees of freedom).

Scott Kelso (b. 1947): Coordination dynamics

Key Work

Kelso, J.A.S. (1995). Dynamic Patterns: The Self-Organization of Brain and Behavior. MIT Press.

  • Coordination patterns emerge from the interaction of constraints.
  • Systems exist in stable states (attractors) but can transition between them.
  • Phase transitions occur when control parameters change.
  • Variability increases at transition points (instability precedes change).

For MMA: A fighter's stance is a stable attractor; under pressure (control parameter), they may transition to a different stance or position.

Dynamical-systems precision (Blau & Wagman, 2022)

The vocabulary above is loose without these distinctions. Treat a skill as an attractor layout, a landscape of stable behaviors that the coach reshapes (p. 234–242):

🗺️Skill = an attractor layout

Habitual patterns are attractors (basins that "attract nearby behaviors the way a magnet attracts iron"); the deepest is most stable and usually most efficient, the default under pressure. The coach reshapes the layout: deepen a desired attractor, destabilize an unwanted one. (p. 237)

🔀Learning vs. adaptation

Learning = birth of a new attractor (a genuinely new pattern, expect exploration and instability). Adaptation = movement of an existing attractor (adjusting a skill to a new context/opponent, fight-camp work). Don't use "adaptation" for both. (p. 241)

🎛️Control vs. order parameter

A control parameter, when changed, reorganizes the layout (speed, pressure, range, fatigue, # opponents, increasing speed makes the anti-phase attractor vanish). It doesn't dictate the new behavior, only which behaviors are stable. The order parameter is the macro variable you measure (e.g., relative phase / timing between fighters). (p. 250)

Why this matters for the nonlinearity claim: "minor rule changes revolutionize styles" is precise once framed as a control-parameter-driven phase transition past a critical value (HKB, p. 250). A rule change shifts a control parameter; past its critical threshold the attractor layout reorganizes abruptly, falsifiable and designable rather than mystical. See the Ecological Psychology section.

Michael Turvey (b. 1942): Action systems

Key Work

Turvey, M.T. (1990). Coordination. American Psychologist, 45(8), 938–953.

🔄Intra-individual

Organizing body parts relative to each other.

🤼Inter-individual

Organizing body relative to environment/opponents.

Action systems are temporarily assembled, task-specific organizations, coordination emerges from constraints, not from pre-programmed motor programs.

For MMA: A takedown defense involves both intra-individual coordination (sprawl mechanics) and inter-individual coordination (timing relative to the opponent's shot).

Karl Newell (b. 1945): The constraints model

Key Work

Newell, K.M. (1986). Constraints on the development of coordination. In M.G. Wade & H.T.A. Whiting (Eds.), Motor Development in Children (pp. 341–360). Martinus Nijhoff.

Three categories of constraints shape behavior, and behavior emerges from their interaction:

🧍Individual (Organismic)

Personal characteristics.

🎯Task

Features of the activity.

🌍Environmental

External conditions.

Manipulation of constraints guides learning without prescribing solutions.

For MMA: A game's rules (task), an athlete's reach (individual), and the cage (environmental) together shape what techniques emerge.

The Constraint Interaction Model

Behavior is not determined by any single constraint, it emerges from the interaction of all three. Change any constraint, and behavior reorganizes.

Individualphysical · psychological · experiential · biological↘Emergent behavior Taskgoals · rules · equipment · space→Emergent behavior Environmentalphysical · social · historical · cultural↗Emergent behavior

"Constraints do not prescribe or dictate specific patterns of coordination; rather, they act to reduce the number of configurations available, channeling behavior toward functional states." , Newell, 1986

Two-Stage Learning Model

Adapted from Newell (1985) by Renshaw & Chow (2018), different stages need different coaching approaches.

🔎Stage 1 · Learning to Coordinate

Assembling basic coordination patterns.Searching/exploring to develop intra- or inter-individual-environment patterns. Coach role: provide opportunities to explore solutions. Early learners need guidance toward functional solutions.

🎚️Stage 2 · Learning to Adapt

Optimizing and stabilizing patterns.Developing stable yet flexible coordinative structures; exploiting the individual-environment system. Coach role: provide variability and challenge to refine adaptability.

"Stage 1 involves learners searching and exploring to find a coordination solution. Stage 2 involves learners adapting their coordination patterns to achieve optimized and stable yet flexible performance." , Renshaw et al., 2019

Knowledge Types

Knowledge OF the environment (supporting action) can only be developed through doing, it cannot be verbally transmitted. (Distinction from Gibson, 1979)

💬Knowledge ABOUT

Declarative, verbalizable.Learned through instruction, video, discussion. You can explain submission defense this way.

🤲Knowledge OF

Procedural, action-supporting.Learned only through direct experience in the environment. The feel for when to escape comes only through live grappling.

Implications for Training Design

These theoretical foundations lead to specific design principles (Renshaw et al., 2019):

🔗The relationship is the unit

Don't train fighters in isolation, always include opponent, pressure, space.

🌱Skill is adaptation

Don't prescribe "correct" technique, create environments where functional solutions emerge.

📈Learning is nonlinear

Expect plateaus, breakthroughs, regressions, embrace instability as necessary for change.

🎛️Constraints shape behavior

Manipulate constraints to guide learning, the environment teaches; the coach designs it.

🔄Perception & action are coupled

Never separate reading from reacting, avoid task decomposition that breaks this coupling.

Next · Key Concepts, constraints, affordances, perception-action coupling.

docs.title: Ecological Psychology
docs.text:
Theory LayerThe Science UnderneathBlau & Wagman (2022)

The science our whole training system rests on. Where the Constraints-Led Approach is the method we coach with, ecological psychology is the science underneath it, the lawful account of how animals perceive, act, and learn.

The One Idea

The animal and its environment are one system. You cannot understand the fighter without the cage and the opponent, they are part of the system from the start, not "factors" added on. And the fighter does not compute the world from blurry input; the environment lawfully structures energy (light, sound, force) and the fighter directly detects it. Nothing is processed, only picked up. (Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 56–57, 82)

These two moves, animal–environment mutuality and direct perception, are the foundation. Everything else (affordances, synergies, attractors, self-organization, learning) follows from them.

Two Pillars

🔗Mutuality

The unit of analysis is the fighter–opponent–cage system, never the fighter alone. Fred's fear on a rickety bridge lives in the Fred-bridge system because he cannot fly, his action capabilities define the meaning of the event. (p. 57)

👁️Direct Perception

No central executive "fixes" ambiguous input. The animal detects lawfully structured information directly. Perception through a tool (a grip, a glove) is still direct, directness is about not being mediated by anything mental. (p. 56, 82, 219)

Science vs. Method

Two layers, two sources, both true, working at different grain.

Ecological Psychology (science)→Why behavior works the way it does, Gibson, Bernstein, Kelso, Turvey, Warren Constraints-Led Approach (method)→How we coach it, a methodology derived from the science (Renshaw et al., 2019)

Where the two agree, we stand on solid ground. Where our CLA docs simplify, this section gives the deeper source, with printed-page citations to the primary text.

The 14 Principles

The core ideas, and what each means in the cage. Each links to its full write-up on The Principles.

🔗Mutuality & Scale

The fighter–opponent–cage system is the unit. Describe behavior at the macroscopic scale, never the joint-angle scale.

👁️Direct Perception

Detect, don't compute. Train attention to information, never an internal calculation.

🌊Information & Invariants

Attune to higher-order invariants, τ, optic flow, the inertial array, not raw data.

🎯Affordances

Opportunities for action in the animal–world relationship. Reward the taken opportunity.

📏Body-Scaling (π)

Affordances scale as a ratio. Set range/height by reach and leg length, not absolutes.

♾️Perception–Action Loop

One continuous loop, never two stages. Never decompose it.

🎼Synergies & DoF

The body soft-assembles "choirs of muscles" that preserve the goal, not fixed parts.

🪶Forces for Free

Cooperate with gravity and reactive forces, "walking is controlled falling."

🔁Repetition w/o Repetition

Repeat the problem, not the movement. Variability is the feature.

🏔️Dynamical Systems

Skill is an attractor layout the coach reshapes by turning control parameters.

🐜Self-Organization

Order emerges from local rules + interaction. The coach is a rule-giver, not a choreographer.

📚Learning = Attunement

Educate intention → educate attention → calibrate the response.

🚧No Transfer Across Modes

Reading τ for striking ≠ for takedowns. Build separate per-mode paths.

🤝Perceiving With Others

Perceive affordances for self AND opponent. Kendo's two preferred distances.

✋Dynamic Touch

Feel structure and off-balance-ability through the inertial array at the grip.

Explore the Section

The Principles→All 14 expanded, science, evidence, and the application in the cage The Evidence→The landmark studies, what they did, what they prove, why they matter Design Rules→The 20 working rules every game and constraint must obey Glossary→The key terms, crisply defined

Source · Blau & Wagman (2022), Introduction to Ecological Psychology: A Lawful Approach to Perceiving, Acting, and Cognizing, Routledge. Page numbers are printed pages. Full working audit in ECOLOGICAL_PSYCHOLOGY_AUDIT.md.

docs.title: Design Rules
docs.text:
Theory Layer20 Working RulesWhat Every Game Obeys

The practical rule set, the takeaways from the principles and the evidence that every game, constraint, and content decision must obey. Grouped into four families. Each rule carries its printed-page citation to Blau & Wagman (2022).

Constraints & Scaling

Build the problem from the environment. Constrain the option space so functional behavior emerges, and scale the problem to the body, not to absolute numbers.

  • 1. The unit is the fighter–opponent–cage system, never the fighter alone. Every game includes an adaptive opponent and live environmental constraints (boundary, range). (p. 57)
  • 2. Constrain to afford; never prescribe. A constraint reduces the option space so behavior emerges, "outlets every 12 feet, " not "outlet here." If a rule forces one solution, it is over-constraining. (p. 146, 271)
  • 3. Scale constraints by critical ratios, not absolutes. Distances, heights, apertures, and ranges are set as ratios of the athlete's reach / leg length / shoulder width, so different bodies face the same relational problem. (Warren, p. 79–86)
  • 18. Train with the gear, in motion, with consequence. Affordances are for the animal-plus-object system and change with gloves, fatigue, and weapons; a moving fighter occupies more space; emotion is nested in the system. (p. 57, 86–87)
  • 19. Design affordances and information into the environment; prefer information over instruction. Make desired behavior perceptually obvious (live boundary, clear range markers); manipulate information to compel behavior (Chicago's optic-flow lines → 36% fewer crashes). (p. 211–216)

Information & Perception

Name what the athlete attunes to, and reward the opportunity actually taken, not isolated cues, and never an internal computation.

  • 4. Name the information the athlete must attune to, per game. Specify the invariant, τ (time-to-contact) for closing strikes/shots, global optic flow for self-motion/cage approach, the inertial array (resistance-to-rotation) for grips/clinch. "Reading" = attuning to a named invariant. (p. 82, 123–127, 165–166)
  • 5. Reward the taken opportunity (affordance), not isolated cues. Score the realized behavior, a clean counter, a completed off-balance, because an affordance is a simultaneous multi-property fit perceived in itself, not a single property. (p. 77, 87–88)
  • 6. Reward economical / preferred solutions, and let athletes find their own transition points. Perception is action-scaled to effort; skilled actors transition at a comfortable preferred boundary before their max. Phase-advance triggers are the athlete's perceived readiness, not a coach's command. (p. 81, 88)
  • 7. Never decompose the perception–action loop. Simplify by scaling the whole task (slower, smaller, lighter, fewer options); never break perception off from action. Static unopposed drilling removes the information the skill needs. (p. 192, 207)
  • 15. Design opponent-reading explicitly: perceive affordances for self AND opponent. Reward recognizing what the opponent's position affords them. (p. 175)
  • 16. Build distance games on two preferred ranges + a dead middle. Near affords offense, far affords defense, the middle affords neither, reward living in the advantageous range; experienced fighters spend more time in the offensive range. (kendo, p. 188–189)

Learning & Transfer

Let solutions self-assemble, repeat the problem rather than the movement, treat skill as an attractor layout, and never assume a skill carries across action modes.

  • 8. Let synergies soft-assemble to the goal; cue cooperation with free forces. "Points for staying standing, " not "you must sprawl." Cue base, suspension, redirecting directional force, capitalize on gravity and reactive forces ("controlled falling"). (p. 143–146)
  • 9. Repeat the problem, not the movement. Coordination stage: repeat the same problem to assemble the synergy. Mastery stage: repeat the goal across varied opponents/contexts. Variability is the feature. (p. 145, 169–170)
  • 10. Treat skill as an attractor layout the coach reshapes, and distinguish learning from adaptation. Learning = birth of a new attractor (exploration, expect instability). Adaptation = moving an existing attractor to a new context. Name your control parameters (speed, pressure, range, fatigue, # opponents) and order parameter (what you measure). (p. 234–251)
  • 11. Use noise / instability on purpose. The "amber zone" is functional noise that lets a fighter escape a shallow habit and find a deeper pattern. Perturb working patterns and reward re-organization. (p. 242–243, 272–273)
  • 12. Expect nonlinear switches under a control parameter, design for them. Stance, rhythm, and gait reorganize abruptly past a critical value; minor rule changes revolutionize styles because they cross a threshold. (p. 152–155, 250)
  • 13. Build learning paths as: educate intention → educate attention → calibrate. Choose the affordance, attune to the invariant that specifies it, then scale the response with feedback (reduce feedback over time). (p. 204–206)
  • 14. Assume NO transfer across action modes; verify transfer empirically. Reading τ for striking ≠ reading τ for takedowns; build separate per-mode paths and test. Phase-scaffold (striking → clinch → takedown) precisely because it re-educates attention at each new mode. (p. 160–161, 206)

Coaching Stance

The coach is an environment architect and rule-giver, not a choreographer. Keep the vocabulary disciplined and grounded.

  • 17. Coach by giving local rules and letting athletes adapt, not a blueprint. "Give the contractors a list of rules… the houses will be far more adapted to their circumstances, and you will have to do a lot less work." (p. 284–285)
  • 20. Keep the vocabulary disciplined and grounded. Maintain controlled vocabulary; add a sixth narration input to game capture, the information the athlete must attune to. Reserve "adaptation" for moved-attractor work and "learning" for new-attractor work. (p. 241)

Rules numbered as in the audit; grouped here for scanning. See The Principles for the science and The Evidence for the studies. Source · Blau & Wagman (2022), Introduction to Ecological Psychology, Routledge. Page numbers are printed pages.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
Theory LayerKey TermsCrisp Definitions

The vocabulary of ecological psychology, defined crisply. For the full treatment of each, see The Principles; for the studies, The Evidence. This complements the methodology-focused CLA glossary.

🎯Affordance

An opportunity for action that emerges from the relationship between animal and environment, neither in the world nor in the animal, but between them. Often phrased with "-able." (p. 73–78)

🌊Invariant

A higher-order pattern in structured energy that stays constant across transformations and lawfully specifies an affordance. (p. 82)

📡Specification

The lawful, unambiguous relationship by which an energy pattern carries information about an affordance, so it can be detected, not inferred. (p. 82)

👁️Direct perception

Detecting lawfully structured information without a mental intermediary (no representation, no inference, no central executive). Tools may mediate physically; perception stays direct. (p. 56, 82, 219)

⏱️Tau (τ)

The relative rate of optical expansion of a texture; specifies time-to-contact / time-to-arrival assuming current conditions continue. The information for timing. (p. 74, 165–166)

🎼Synergy

A coordinative structure, a temporary "choir" of potentially independent components soft-assembled to a task; goal-preserving and self-correcting. Bernstein's solution to the degrees-of-freedom problem. (p. 144–146)

🏔️Attractor

A stable behavior the system falls into and that attracts nearby behaviors "like a magnet attracts iron." The deepest attractor is the most stable and usually the most efficient. (p. 234–238)

🎛️Control parameter

A variable that, when changed, reorganizes the attractor layout (speed, pressure, range). It changes the layout, it does not directly dictate the new behavior. (p. 250)

📍Order parameter

The collective / macro variable that captures the coordination state, e.g., relative phase between two oscillators. What you measure. (p. 251)

🐜Self-organization

Pattern emerging from minimally intelligent components following local rules with constant mutual interaction, no leader, no blueprint, comprehensible only at a larger scale. (p. 270–272)

🔎Education of attention

Learning which components of a structured energy array carry the most useful information for an intended affordance, and shifting toward them. (p. 204–205)

🎚️Calibration

Learning how to scale perception/action to the detected information, the information-to-action mapping. Specific to the action mode; does not transfer across modes. (p. 206)

📏Critical π ratio

The body-scaled value at which an affordance appears or disappears (e.g., step ÷ leg = 0.88 for stepping). How "individualize" becomes precise. (Warren, p. 79–81)

♻️Degeneracy / multirealizability

Many different solutions reaching the same goal (and the same parts reorganizing for many goals). Robustness through variety. (p. 274)

Source · Blau & Wagman (2022), Introduction to Ecological Psychology, Routledge. Page numbers are printed pages. See also the methodology-side CLA glossary.

docs.title: The Evidence
docs.text:
Theory LayerLandmark StudiesThe Proof

The experiments behind the principles. Each card is the short version; the write-ups below run what they did → what it proves → why it matters to us. Citations are printed pages in Blau & Wagman (2022).

🎵The Metronomes

Metronomes on a free board self-synchronize. Change the constraint, not the components. (p. 61–62)

🪜Warren's Stairs

"Climb-able" is a ratio (step ÷ leg, critical π = 0.88), not a height. (p. 78–81)

🔨Bernstein's Blacksmiths

Joint paths vary every strike; the hammer-head path stays constant. (p. 145)

🗣️The Jaw Tug

Lips compensate for "b, " tongue for "z." The synergy protects the goal. (p. 146)

🤖Robot Tournament

Variable-trained robots beat regular-trained ones 2,888–75. (p. 169–170)

👶Crawling ↛ Walking

Slope-safe crawlers walk right off the edge. Skill is mode-specific. (p. 160–161)

⚔️Kendo Distance

Two preferred ranges + a dead middle; experts live in the offensive one. (p. 188–189)

🛣️Chicago's Lines

Optic-flow lines slow drivers, 36% fewer crashes. Information beats instruction. (p. 211)

🏠House-Building Robots

Whole houses from simple local rules, self-organization, no blueprint. (p. 285)

🏠The Moving Room

A swaying room makes people sway in sync, posture is fed by optic flow. (p. 147–148)

👓Prism Adaptation

Throwing through prism glasses moves an existing attractor, adaptation. (p. 241)

docs.title: The Metronomes on a Board
docs.text:
What they did. Several metronomes set ticking out of phase, placed on a board that rests on free-rolling cans. Left alone, they drift into synchrony. To break the synchrony, you don't adjust any metronome, you flip the cans so the board can no longer move and transmit vibration.

What it proves. The body is a system, not a machine. You change a system's behavior by changing the constraint, not its components, and the coordinating interaction (the moving board) is load-bearing; remove it and the pattern stops.

Why it matters to us: The whole constraints-led method in one image. To change a fighter's behavior, change the environment's constraints, don't try to re-wire the parts.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 61–62)

docs.title: Warren's Stairs (π = 0.88)
docs.text:
What they did. Tall and short participants judged whether stairs of varying riser heights were climbable, and then climbed. Researchers plotted the boundary against absolute step height, then against the ratio of step height to leg length.

What it proves. Against absolute height the curves differ by body; against the ratio they collapse onto one critical value, π = 0.88, above which stepping is impossible, and the perceived boundary matched the biomechanical one. A second experiment found the optimal (least-effortful) step at a ratio of 0.26, and people could perceive which stairs were most efficient. Affordances are body-scaled and action-scaled.

Why it matters to us: This is HOW we individualize constraints, by ratio of reach / leg length / shoulder width, not absolute numbers, so every body faces the same relational problem. And scoring should reward the economical solution, not just the possible one.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, Warren, 1984, p. 78–81)

docs.title: Bernstein's Blacksmiths
docs.text:
What they did. Bernstein filmed expert blacksmiths striking the same target repeatedly and tracked both their joint trajectories and the trajectory of the hammer head.

What it proves. The joint trajectories varied from strike to strike, but the hammer-head trajectory stayed consistent. A synergy produces micro-level variability with macro-level consistency, the system preserves the goal-relevant outcome, not a fixed set of movements.

Why it matters to us: Repetition without repetition is real and measurable. Don't chase identical reps; chase a consistent outcome assembled from varying movements.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 145)

docs.title: The Jaw Tug
docs.text:
What they did. As a speaker produced a syllable, researchers mechanically perturbed the jaw mid-utterance and measured which articulators compensated.

What it proves. The compensation was goal-specific: for "b" the lips adjusted to still close the sound; for "z" the tongue adjusted. The synergy is self-correcting and recruits whatever parts preserve the goal, it is not a fixed muscle set.

Why it matters to us: "Points for staying standing, " not "you must sprawl." Constrain to the goal and let the synergy assemble and self-correct, the body will find a way you didn't prescribe.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, Kelso, p. 146)

docs.title: The Infant-Walking Robot Tournament
docs.text:
What they did. Two populations of walking robots were trained, one on highly variable infant walking paths, one on regular paths, then matched in a 4,000-game tournament.

What it proves. The variable-trained robots won 2,888 to 75. Practicing across variable contexts produced far more adaptable, robust control than practicing a regular, repeated path.

Why it matters to us: The hardest evidence for variability. Once a pattern exists, vary opponents and contexts aggressively, that is what builds mastery, not clean repetition.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, Box 10.2, p. 169–170)

docs.title: Crawling ↛ Walking (Non-Transfer)
docs.text:
What they did. Infants who had learned to avoid slopes too steep to descend safely while crawling were tested again on the same slopes once they became walkers.

What it proves. They did not transfer the learning, they walked right off slopes they had safely refused to crawl down, "starting over from zero." The information for the affordance is entirely different across modes (different limbs, optic flow, base of support). Experience doesn't make babies "afraid of heights"; it makes them better at perceiving affordances in context.

Why it matters to us: Reading τ for striking ≠ reading τ for takedowns. Assume no transfer across action modes; build separate per-mode paths and verify transfer empirically.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 160–161)

docs.title: Kendo's Two Distances
docs.text:
What they did. Researchers tracked interpersonal distance between kendo competitors over the course of bouts, comparing more- and less-experienced fighters.

What it proves. Competitors occupy two preferred distances, a near one that affords offense and a far one that affords defense, and avoid the middle that affords neither. More-experienced competitors spend more time at the near distance because they can move into striking position faster. Stepping toward or away is governed by perceived affordances for striking the opponent.

Why it matters to us: The gold-standard finding for distance management. Build distance games on two ranges + a dead middle; make "time in the offensive range" a progression marker.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, Box 11.2, p. 188–189)

docs.title: Chicago's Optic-Flow Lines
docs.text:
What they did. On a dangerous curve on Lake Shore Drive, transverse lines were painted at progressively decreasing spacing approaching the curve, increasing the rate of global optic flow as drivers advanced.

What it proves. The rising flow rate made drivers feel they were speeding up, so they slowed down, with no conscious thought and no instruction. Crashes fell by 36%. Manipulating information compels behavior more reliably than telling people what to do.

Why it matters to us: Information design beats instruction. Make desired behavior perceptually obvious, a live boundary, clear range markers, and let the information do the coaching.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 211)

docs.title: House-Building Robots
docs.text:
What they did. Wyss Institute robots were given simple local rules (and local information about neighboring blocks) rather than a master blueprint, then set loose to build structures.

What it proves. Whole houses emerged from local rules and constant interaction, self-organization with no central plan. The designer's lesson: give contractors a list of rules and let them adapt to local circumstances, and "the houses will be far more adapted… and you will have to do a lot less work."

Why it matters to us: The clearest articulation of the coach's role, a rule-giver, not a choreographer. Set the constraints; let the fighting style build itself.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, Box 16.2, p. 285)

docs.title: The Moving Room
docs.text:
What they did. Participants stood inside a room whose walls could be moved subtly while the floor stayed fixed.

What it proves. People unconsciously swayed in sync with the room, even being told to "stand still." Standing is "the prevention of falling, " a balancing task fed by global optic flow, not a static posture. Sway also decreases in directions that would disrupt a task and increases where it wouldn't, posture serves the suprapostural goal.

Why it matters to us: Base and posture are perceptually controlled and cooperative with offense, "base down so strike force doesn't rob your counters." Posture supports the behavior it carries; they're not in competition.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 147–151)

docs.title: Prism Adaptation
docs.text:
What they did. Participants threw at a target while wearing prism glasses that optically shift the world sideways; their throws were tracked as they adjusted and then again after the glasses came off.

What it proves. They re-aimed to hit the target (and over-corrected the other way once the prisms were removed), an existing skill's attractor was moved to a new context. This is adaptation (movement of an attractor), distinct from learning (birth of a new attractor).

Why it matters to us: Name the difference. Teaching a genuinely new pattern is learning, expect instability, allow exploration. Tuning an existing skill to a new opponent is adaptation, that's fight-camp work.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 241)

Next · Design Rules, turning the evidence into practice · The Principles · Glossary. Source · Blau & Wagman (2022), Introduction to Ecological Psychology, Routledge. Page numbers are printed pages.

docs.title: The Principles
docs.text:
Theory Layer14 Core Principles+ Dynamic Touch

The full set, each expanded: the science, the key evidence, the application in the cage, and a printed-page citation to Blau & Wagman (2022). Return to the Overview for the one-glance map.

docs.title: Mutuality & Ecological Scale
docs.text:
The foundational assumption: the animal–environment system is the fundamental unit of analysis. You cannot understand the animal without its environment, nor the environment without the animal that inhabits it, the environment is not a "factor" added to an equation, it is part of the system from the start. Fred's fear on a rickety bridge is nested in the Fred-bridge system because he cannot fly; his particular action capabilities constrain the meaning of the event.

The second move is scale discipline. Ecological psychology rejects reductionism, the whole is different from the sum of its parts, and studies macroscopic, ecological variables, not the lower-order variables of Newtonian physics. "If the answer is still ambiguous, you are probably still standing too close." Causality is treated as nonlocal and nonlinear; the body is a system, not a machine, and you change a system's behavior by changing constraints, not components (flip the cans so the board can't move, and the metronomes sync).

In the cage: Every game includes the opponent and the live environmental constraints (cage edge, range, surface). Describe behavior at the macroscopic scale, reads, distance, pressure, off-balancing, never at the joint-angle scale. The stylistic matchup is the unit, not the fighter.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 57–64)

docs.title: Direct Perception
docs.text:
Traditional theories posit a central executive that receives ambiguous stimulation, "fixes" it, and infers the world. Blau & Wagman banish it: it requires a loan of intelligence (it must already be smart to do its job), suffers the outness and feedback problems, and, fatally, lacks explanatory power (positing it just pushes the question back a step).

Perception is instead direct: the animal detects lawfully structured information; nothing needs to be processed, only detected. Direct does not mean unmediated by tools, perception through an interface, a hand-held rod, or a grip is still direct, because directness is about whether perception is mediated by something mental (a representation or inference), not something physical.

In the cage: Cues direct attention to information, never prescribe a computation or an internal body-part movement. Tight Block's "see center mass, the eyes lie" is exactly this: it points the eyes at the informative variable. Drop any "read → decide → react" sequential framing.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 56, 82, 87–88, 219)

docs.title: Information & Invariants
docs.text:
Animals are immersed in an ocean of energy, patterns of light, sound, and physical force. This energy is not uniform; surface and substance properties lawfully structure it. At any point of observation the animal encounters a unique distribution of structured energy (the ambient optic array for light). Because the structuring is lawful and unambiguous, the energy pattern specifies, carries information about, the affordances of surrounding surfaces. The relevant variables are higher-order invariants, not lower-order properties.

⏱️Tau (τ)

The relative rate of optical expansion of an object's texture; specifies time-to-contact, assuming current conditions continue. Used for braking, catching, defensive blinking. (p. 74, 165–166)

🌊Global optic flow

Specifies self-motion and how to control it; rate of flow = movement speed. The moving-room and Chicago-lines paradigms. (p. 147–148, 211)

✋Inertial array (I)

For effortful touch, resistance to rotation at a grasp point specifies length, orientation, and "hit-with-able-ness." (p. 123–127)

Lawfulness implies the same information is available across energy forms (multiple modalities can specify the same affordance) and across nervous systems (the brain's details are "potentially irrelevant", this is a psychology for all animals).

In the cage: Name the invariant per game. "Reading" means attuning to τ on a closing strike or shot, to global optic flow for self-motion and cage approach, and to the inertial array through grips and frames, not vaguely "seeing openings."

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 82, 91–93, 123–127, 165–166, 218)

docs.title: Affordances
docs.text:
Affordances are opportunities for behavior that emerge from the relationship between animal and environment. They are neither purely objective (a property of the environment) nor purely subjective (in the animal), like friction, they emerge from a relationship. "Hide-behind-able" exists for the cat but not the dog, and only when there is both an organism and a place to hide. Affordances are the activity-specific meanings of the environment, and because meaning is in the relationship, meanings can be perceived directly.

🔗Relational

Animal-relative and contextual: a puddle affords jumping when running, not when standing still. (p. 76)

🔮The (im)possible future

Catching a ball requires perceiving where it will be; you perceive whether a behavior could be performed before attempting it. (p. 74)

🧩Multiple fit

A surface affords standing only if horizontal AND flat AND rigid AND extended, miss one and the affordance is absent. (p. 77)

🎒Animal-plus-object

Affordances are for the system including gloves, pads, fatigue, a moving body. (p. 86–87)

A subtle point: perceiving an affordance does not require perceiving its component properties. You perceive the opportunity in and of itself, not the measurements, improving perception of leg length does not improve perception of the jump-and-reach affordance.

In the cage: Keep the "-able" vocabulary, strikeable, takedown-able, escape-able. Score the taken opportunity (a clean counter, a completed off-balance), not an isolated cue like "hands dropped." An affordance is a simultaneous multi-property fit, not a single property.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 73–89)

docs.title: Body-Scaling (Critical π Ratios)
docs.text:
Whether a step is "step-on-able" depends not on absolute step height but on the ratio of step height to leg length. Warren's (1984) stair-climbing studies are the paradigm: plotted against absolute height, tall and short people give different curves; plotted against the ratio, the curves overlap onto a single critical π value of 0.88 (above which stepping becomes impossible), and the perceived boundary matched the biomechanical one.

The same logic governs crossing gaps (leg length), fitting through apertures (shoulder width, critical ratio ≈ 1.16–1.3), and reaching (arm length). A related finding: people transition to a new mode, one-hand to two-hand grasp, reach to lean, at a comfortable preferred boundary before their absolute limit.

Absolute height→Different curves per body, ambiguous Ratio (step ÷ leg)→One critical value, 0.88, the same problem for every body

In the cage: This is HOW we individualize. When a game sets a distance, target height, or aperture, scale it to the athlete's reach / leg-length / shoulder-width ratio, so a 5'5" wrestler and a 6'2" kickboxer face the same relational problem. Let athletes find their own preferred transition point rather than forcing the absolute-limit solution.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, Warren, 1984, p. 78–81, 84–88)

docs.title: Perception–Action Loop
docs.text:
Perception's purpose is to guide doing, Turvey's reframing is "getting light into the muscles." Perceiving and acting are continuous, not separate sequential stages, a Möbius-strip relationship. Perceiving is an activity that unfolds over time: information (τ, or the inertial array) "can be detected only as the animal or an object moves." Action generates the information that guides further action. This loop cannot be decomposed without destroying the information.

In the cage: Maintain the loop in every task. Simplify by scaling the whole task, slower, smaller, lighter, fewer options, never by breaking perception off from action. A static, unopposed drill removes the very information the skill needs.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 90, 139, 192, 207)

docs.title: Synergies & Degrees of Freedom
docs.text:
Controlling the body via individual anatomical units is a nonstarter: one arm alone involves 7 joint degrees of freedom and ~26 muscles → ~4×10²⁶ combinations; the whole body has 600+ muscles. This is Bernstein's degrees-of-freedom problem.

The solution is the synergy (coordinative structure): "a grouping of potentially independent anatomical components that work together as a functional unit", a "choir of muscles that harmonize." Synergies are soft-assembled (temporary, task-specific), goal-preserving and self-correcting, and produce micro-level variability with macro-level consistency.

🗣️The jaw tug

Perturb the jaw mid-syllable: the lips compensate for "b, " the tongue for "z." The synergy preserves the goal, recruiting whatever parts it needs. (p. 146)

🔨Bernstein's blacksmiths

Joint trajectories vary strike to strike, but the hammer-head trajectory stays consistent. (p. 145)

In the cage: Let synergies soft-assemble to the goal. "Points for staying standing" lets a sprawl / underhook / lateral solution assemble; "when they shoot you must sprawl" wrongly prescribes anatomy. Score the goal, not the movement.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 142–146)

docs.title: Forces for Free
docs.text:
Movements occur in a world and with a body that both obey physics, so many forces are provided gratis, gravity, reactive forces, the body's connectivity. "Walking is merely controlled falling." Coordinated movement is about capitalizing on and cooperating with free forces, it is "more like sculpting than like building."

In the cage: Cue cooperation with physics. Tight Block's "base under fire, create suspension like an ATV over bumps" and The Square's "redirect directional force rather than absorb it" are textbook forces-for-free cues. Make this a standard cue category.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 143–144)

docs.title: Repetition Without Repetition
docs.text:
"No two instances of a given behavior are exactly the same. They can't be. The context will always differ." Therefore learning a skill may require practicing the same movement in the same context, but mastering it requires achieving the goal using different behaviors across different contexts.

The strongest evidence: in a 4,000-game tournament, robots trained on highly variable infant-walking paths beat robots trained on regular paths 2,888 to 75. Variability is what builds adaptability.

In the cage: Repeat the problem, not the movement. Coordination stage, repeat the same problem to assemble the synergy. Mastery stage, repeat the goal across varied opponents and contexts. Variability is the feature, not the bug.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 145, 169–170)

docs.title: Dynamical Systems & Attractors
docs.text:
Behavior is modeled as a ball on an attractor layout. Locations on the sheet are possible behaviors; the ball is current behavior; basins are attractors, stable behaviors that attract nearby behaviors "the way a magnet attracts iron." The deepest attractor is the most stable and usually the most efficient.

📍Order parameter

The macro variable that captures the coordination state, e.g., relative phase between two oscillators. What you measure. (p. 251)

🎛️Control parameter

A variable that, when changed, reorganizes the layout, e.g., movement speed makes the anti-phase attractor vanish. (p. 250)

🌑Death of an attractor

A stable pattern destabilizes and disappears, breaking a habit; anti-phase at speed. (p. 238)

✨Birth of an attractor

Learning a genuinely new pattern, the juggling study. This is learning. (p. 240)

↔️Movement of an attractor

Adjusting an existing skill to a new context, prism-glasses throwing. This is adaptation. (p. 241)

〰️Functional noise

Perturbation lets the ball escape a shallow attractor and find a deeper one, not error. (p. 242–243)

The distinction between learning (new attractor) and adaptation (moved attractor) matters. So do hysteresis (current state depends on history), equifinality (different starts, same end), and pluripotentiality (same start, different ends).

In the cage: Treat a fighter's habitual patterns as an attractor layout the coach reshapes, deepen a desired pattern, destabilize an unwanted one. Name your control parameters (speed, pressure, range, fatigue, # opponents) and your order parameter (what you measure). Expect abrupt reorganization past a critical value, that's why a minor rule change can revolutionize a style.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 234–251)

docs.title: Self-Organization
docs.text:
Self-organization is the alternative to other-organization (a central executive, a blueprint, a conductor), and it is the rule, not the exception, in biological systems. Its definition has four load-bearing parts:

  • Not other-organized, no external leader, no blueprint, no rigid hierarchy.
  • Components are minimally intelligent, they follow simple rules; no loan of intelligence.
  • Rules are local, followed using only local information, requiring constant mutual interaction (remove the interaction and the pattern stops).
  • Patterns emerge, a nonrandom configuration comprehensible only at a larger scale than the parts, and not predictable from them.

Self-organized systems are dynamic, recover from perturbations (the orchestra player who loses their place re-synchronizes with neighbors), are multistable (convection rolls, bathtub vortex), and multirealizable (the same legs walk, kick, skate, arabesque). Ants find the shortest path via pheromone local rules; robots build whole houses from simple local rules. The deep lesson: a wise designer gives contractors a list of rules and lets them adapt to local circumstances, "the houses will be far more adapted… and you will have to do a lot less work."

In the cage: The coach is a rule-giver, not a choreographer. Give local rules (the game's constraints) and let the fighting style emerge. Perturb a working pattern and reward re-organization, score the recovery, not just the pattern.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 270–285)

docs.title: Learning = Attunement
docs.text:
Learning is the fine-tuning of perceiving-and-acting abilities, not the accumulation of facts or the enrichment of representations. Four named processes:

Education of intention→Which behaviors to attempt, and which affordances to perceive Education of attention→Which components of the array carry the most useful information, shift toward them Calibration→How to scale the response to the detected information (the information–action mapping) Perceptual differentiation→Sensitivity to an informative variable develops slowly, over long timescales

In the cage: Sequence games as educate intention → educate attention → calibrate. Choose which affordance to go for, attune to the invariant that specifies it ("see center mass, " attune to τ), then scale the response with feedback that reduces over time. Don't expect beginners to perceive what experts perceive.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, Jacobs & Michaels, 2007, p. 204–206; differentiation p. 168)

docs.title: No-Transfer-Across-Modes
docs.text:
The single most important learning finding for us: animals learn specific affordances, not general facts. Infants who learned which slopes afford safe crawling did not transfer that to walking, they "started over from zero" after the new motor skill, because the information about affordances for crawling is entirely different from that for walking (different limbs, different optic flow, different base of support).

Calibration is the same: subjects calibrated to a visual drift while walking did not transfer it to throwing or kicking, what they learned was "a specific relationship between optic flow direction and walking, " not a general fact about the world.

In the cage: Assume no transfer across action modes and verify empirically. A fighter who reads τ for striking defense has not thereby learned to read τ for takedown defense, the information differs. Build separate per-mode learning paths. The Square's striking → clinch → takedown scaffold is right precisely because it re-educates attention at each new mode.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, crawling↛walking p. 160–161; walking↛throwing p. 206)

docs.title: Perceiving / Acting With Others
docs.text:
The same lawful principles that govern animal–environment systems govern animal–animal–environment systems. The book frames combat directly. Three pillars:

👀Affordances for others

The same properties that structure the energy you encounter structure what others encounter, so you perceive affordances for self AND for them. Watching someone walk informs their jump-and-reach (shared leg forces); watching them twist does not. Basketball players read others' jump-reach better. (p. 175, 180–182)

➕Emergent, not additive

Two people through a doorway turn shoulders at less than the sum of their widths, they share personal space. Pair affordances are emergent. (p. 184–185)

🪑Social synergies

Rocking chairs synchronize unintentionally when people can see each other. Driven by detecting information about each other, not mirror neurons. (p. 177–178, 186–191)

The combat-sport gem (kendo): competitors exhibit two preferred interpersonal distances, a near distance that affords offense and a far distance that affords defense, and they avoid the middle distance that affords neither. More-experienced competitors spend more time at the near (offensive) distance because they can move into striking position faster. The decision to step toward or away is based on perceived affordances for striking the opponent.

In the cage: Reward reading what the opponent's position affords them. Build distance games on two preferred ranges + a dead middle; make "spends more time in the offensive range" a progression marker. Study opponent movement that shares force dynamics with the threat (level change ↔ shot). Frame it as information detection, never "imagine yourself in their position."

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 172–191; kendo Box 11.2 p. 188–189)

docs.title: Dynamic Touch & Haptics
docs.text:
The information for touch is the inertia tensor (I), the resistance to rotation in different directions at a grasp point. Wielding an object specifies its length, orientation, and "hit-with-able-ness." Perceived length tracks the major principal moment of inertia (I_xx), not geometric length directly, Fred perceives a racket's length and hit-with-able-ness blindfolded, by feel alone.

A fighter perceives the opponent's structure, base, and "off-balance-able-ness" through what their grips and frames feel, the inertial array, exactly as Fred perceives the racket. Stable base is the postural synergy that supports offense: posture and the behavior it supports are cooperative, not competitive.

In the cage: Clinch and grappling games should make the win condition about perceiving and exploiting felt information, off-balancing by redirecting directional force (The Square's "redirect, don't just absorb"). Cue athletes to attune to pressure and resistance-to-rotation through the grips.

(Blau & Wagman, 2022, p. 122–127, 149–151)

Next · The Evidence, the studies behind these principles · Design Rules, how they become practice. Source · Blau & Wagman (2022), Introduction to Ecological Psychology, Routledge. Page numbers are printed pages.

docs.title: Change Log
docs.text:
ReferenceSystem Evolutionv0.1 → v0.5

A record of significant changes to the system's logic, structure, and design decisions. When we refine our thinking, change terminology, or restructure, we document it here.

Why This Log Exists

📜Historical record

A trace of design decisions over time.

🧭Reference

Understand why things are the way they are.

🔍Transparency

Open visibility into system evolution.

Version Timeline

Dec 2025 · v0.1.0→Initial System, core striking games & concepts Jan 2026 · v0.2.0→Environment Organization, wall environment, skill isolation Jan 2026 · v0.3.0→Full MMA Expression & weapon isolation, 31 games Jan 2026 · v0.4.0→System Restructure, Transition Zone, renames, end states Jan 2026 · v0.5.0→CLA Documentation Rollout, 33 games Jun 2026 · v0.6.0→Library expansion & Game-Plan engine (current), 57 games

v0.6.0: Library Expansion & Game-Plan Engine (Current · Jun 2026)

📈Library grown to 57 games

Per-position ground, wall, and clinch coverage plus the first submission-offense games.Built from class mining and structured gap-filling; every externally-sourced idea audited and re-expressed in the system's voice before becoming canonical.

🎯Get Your Game Plan engine

An intake tool that turns your goals and gear into an ordered, personal game plan.Reads each game's metadata; recommends only field-tested (non-WIP) games that fit your equipment and experience.

🛡️Ecological-principles QA

A three-tier check keeps every game true to the constraints-led method.Mechanical lint plus judgment review across the library; flags games missing a live perceptual constraint.

v0.5.0: CLA Documentation Rollout (Jan 2026)

📚CLA sections on all games

Standardized Constraints-Led documentation added to all 33 games.Each game now carries Constraints Analysis, Information Structure (haptic/visual/proprioceptive), Representativeness, Variability Guidelines, and Readiness Indicators, referencing Renshaw et al., 2019 throughout.

🔢Game count verified at 34

A consistency-check script validates game counts.All cross-references verified and updated.

🧹Navigation cleanup

Removed deleted Boundary Trigger references; fixed Winning the Circle → Pressure to Clinch nav links.

v0.4.0: System Restructure (Jan 2026)

🔀Transition Zone added

New environment between Striking and Wrestling.Added Clinch Denial, Counter-Striking, and Counter-Wrestling games.

🏷️Winning the Circle → Pressure to Clinch

Renamed to describe the activity, not the outcome."Winning the Circle" became a concept page; "Boundary Trigger" merged into Pressure to Clinch as higher levels.

🦵Guard Recovery → Leg Reclaim

Renamed to avoid technique-specific terminology.The new name describes the actual goal: getting legs between you and the opponent.

🔒Defensive Submissions added

Wall & Ground Defensive Submission.Defensive options were incomplete without submission threats, these add a deterrent from the wall pin and attacks from the bottom.

🎯End states made explicit

KO, TKO, Submission added as flowchart nodes.All pathways now visually lead to fight-ending outcomes.

✂️Ground Finish removed

Split into TKO (via Ground Control) and Submission.Clearer representation of the actual finishing paths.

🚲Evasive Movement added

New striking defensive game for "getting on your bike."Distinct from Close-Range Defense, avoiding vs. defending.

🅰️Combined game roles clarified

Combined games have A/B roles, not necessarily mirrored.Documentation updated to reflect distinct objectives per side.

v0.3.0: Full MMA Expression & Offensive Skill Isolation (Jan 2026)

🥋Full MMA Expression level

Added a top level to all games.The highest level adds cross-domain threats.

👊Offensive weapon isolation

Lead/Rear Hand and Lead/Rear Leg Offense.Constraint: a single weapon develops creativity with that tool.

📈Expanded to 31 games

Ground games and remaining wall games added.Ground Access/Control/Escape, Wall Grinding, Stand-Up Loop, and Sustained Offense.

v0.2.0: Environment Organization (Jan 2026)

🗂️Environment → Domain → Focus

Reorganized the entire system by environment first.Clearer navigation and game discovery.

🧱Wall environment completed

Wall Control, Wall Escape, Wall to Ground.Plus Stand-Up Loop for cyclical dynamics.

🥊Skill Isolation formalized

Parry, Block, Slip, Evade as the foundation.Defensive skill development before integration.

v0.1.0: Initial System (Dec 2025)

🥊Core striking games

Touch & Don't Get Touched, Pressure to Clinch, Land the Target, Close-Range Defense.

💡Core concepts

Confidence Rating, Three Zones of Attack, Defensive Solutions, TKO Pin.

🏗️Basic structure

Game template, level progression system, MkDocs site structure.

Design Decision Log

Why \"Leg Reclaim\" instead of \"Guard Recovery\"?

Decision: Rename Guard Recovery to Leg Reclaim.

  • "Guard" is technique-specific BJJ terminology
  • The actual goal is positional: get legs between you and the opponent
  • "Leg Reclaim" describes the action without prescribing technique
  • Maintains the ecological principle: environment teaches, not terminology

Why add the Transition Zone?

Decision: Create a new environment between Striking and Wrestling.

  • Clinch Denial exists at the seam between domains
  • Counter-Striking and Counter-Wrestling are defensive responses to pressure
  • Visual clarity: shows the critical moment striking becomes wrestling
  • Games in this zone handle domain transitions specifically

Why are Counter-Striking and Counter-Wrestling defensive?

Decision: Classify counters as defensive focus.

  • Counters respond to opponent initiation (reactive, not proactive)
  • The goal is deterrence: making initiation dangerous
  • You cannot counter without opponent action
  • Offense that serves a defensive purpose

Why make end states explicit in flowcharts?

Decision: Add KO, TKO, Submission as visible nodes.

  • The system should show where everything leads
  • Reinforces the Fight Philosophy: all paths lead to finish
  • A visual reminder of the goal during training
  • Prevents games from feeling disconnected from purpose

Future Considerations

Items under consideration for future versions:

🔄 Counter-counter games 🥊 Dirty boxing as distinct wall striking phase 🤝 Clinch-specific games (underhook battle, pummel) 🌀 Scramble-focused games (chaos transitions) 🦓 Referee intervention scenarios (stand-ups, breaks) Contributing to this log

When significant changes are made to the system, document them here with:

1→What changed 2→Why it changed 3→What the old way was (if applicable)

This maintains institutional knowledge as the system evolves.

docs.title: FAQ & Troubleshooting
docs.text:
ReferenceCoach & Athlete Q&ATroubleshooting

Common questions and fixes for coaches and athletes using this system. Tap any question to open the answer.

General Questions

What is the ecological approach?

The ecological approach to motor learning rests on four pillars:

🧩Constraints-led

Use rules and boundaries to shape behavior.

🎯Representative design

Training mirrors the demands of competition.

🌱Emergence

Skills emerge from practice, not just instruction.

🔍Exploration

Athletes discover solutions through guided play.

Games in this system are designed to create learning conditions, not to prescribe techniques.

How is this different from traditional drilling?

Coach prescribes technique→Athlete discovers solutions Repetition of fixed movements→Exploration of possibilities Compliance-focused→Problem-solving focused Isolated from context→Representative of real fighting

Do I need to teach technique separately?

Technique instruction can complement games, but:

  • Games come first, they create the "why"
  • Technique emerges from game play
  • Explicit instruction fills gaps, it doesn't replace discovery
  • Over-coaching can reduce learning

Full MMA Expression

What is Full MMA Expression?

Full MMA Expression is the highest level of any game, where cross-domain threats are added while keeping the game's core focus.

Striking games→Add grappling threats (shoot, clinch) Wrestling games→Add striking threats

See: Full MMA Expression Concept

When should athletes attempt Full MMA Expression levels?

Only after they're proficient at lower levels. Don't rush it, the skill must be solid before adding complications.

Signs of readiness:

  • Consistent success at levels 1–4
  • Primary skill feels automatic
  • Ready for additional cognitive load
  • Seeking realistic pressure

How do I scale Full MMA Expression appropriately?

Start conservative and build up:

1Low intensityIntroduce the threat gently. 2Announce it"I'm going to add a clinch option now." 3Increase realismGradually ramp the threat up. 4Pull back if neededRegress if the primary skill breaks down.

Do all games need Full MMA Expression?

Every game has a Full MMA Expression level documented, but not every session needs it. Most training happens at levels 1–4. Use it when:

✅ Athletes are proficient at lower levels 🧪 You want to stress-test skills 🏆 Preparing for competition

Game-Specific Troubleshooting

Athletes aren't switching roles in asymmetric games

Problem: One side is dominating; roles aren't switching.

1Check skill levelsMismatch may require an intensity adjustment. 2Review constraintsAre the invariants being followed? 3Adjust levelsDrop to an easier level. 4Add constraintsGive the struggling side an edge (e.g. attacker must pause between strikes).

Athletes are gaming the rules instead of developing skills

Problem: Athletes find loopholes that win the game without developing the target skill.

1Address mindsetReference the Training Mindset principle. 2Close the loopholeAdd or clarify invariants. 3Adjust the levelIt may be too easy or too hard. 4Discuss with the athleteThey may not realize what they're doing.

Athletes get frustrated when they can't 'win'

Problem: A competitive mindset is interfering with learning.

1Reframe successSuccess is learning, not winning. 2Remove scorekeepingSome games work better without tracking. 3Emphasize processCelebrate good attempts, not just results. 4Reference principlesThe Training Mindset addresses this directly.

Games feel chaotic or unstructured

Problem: Athletes don't know what they're supposed to be doing.

1Clarify objectivesEnsure everyone knows the goal. 2DemonstrateShow what good play looks like. 3Start simplerDrop to Level 1. 4Check prerequisitesAthletes may lack foundational skills.

Safety Questions

How hard should contact be?

Touch, contact made, no force→Skill isolation, beginners Light, force felt, not damaging→Most development games Moderate, significant force, controlled→Advanced games, integration

When in doubt, go lighter. Quality over intensity.

When should I stop a game?

Stop immediately if:

An athlete loses composure Contact becomes excessive Rules are being ignored An athlete appears hurt The learning environment breaks down

Reset, address the issue, then resume.

How do I manage aggression?

Some athletes escalate intensity inappropriately:

1Set expectationsClearly, before the game starts. 2Match intensityPair athletes of similar intensity preference. 3Call resetsInterrupt escalation early. 4Address privatelyDiscuss mindset after the session. 5Adjust pairingSome athletes shouldn't work together.

Planning Questions

How long should rounds be?

Skill isolation→2–4 minutes Development games→4–6 minutes Combined games→3–5 minutes Integration→5–10 minutes

Shorter rounds with more resets often beat long continuous play.

How many games per session?

Quality over quantity:

🐣Beginners

2–3 games, more time per game.

🧗Intermediate

3–4 games, balanced time.

🥇Advanced

Can handle more variety.

One well-developed skill beats five superficial exposures.

What if I don't have the right equipment?

Adapt as needed, the constraints matter more than the gear:

Wall / cage→Marked perimeter, padded wall, corner Mats→Grass, carpet, reduced ground work Gloves→Lighter contact, open-hand games Boundary markers→Tape, cones, imaginary lines

Athlete Questions

How do I know if I'm improving?

Look for these markers:

🔁 ConsistencyCan you repeat successful actions? 🎨 VarietyDo you have multiple solutions available? 🧘 ComposureAre you calmer under pressure? 👁️ RecognitionDo you see opportunities earlier? ↗️ TransferDoes the skill show up in sparring?

What if I keep losing at a game?

Losing is learning. Struggle is where growth happens.

1AnalyzeWhat specifically is going wrong? 2AskGet feedback from your partner or coach. 3SimplifyDrop to an easier level. 4FocusWork on one thing at a time. 5PersistKeep going, that's where it forms.

Reference: Training Mindset principle

Should I always play to win?

No. Games are academic exercises.

  • Learning matters more than winning
  • Honest play creates real feedback
  • Struggle is valuable
  • Your partner is your collaborator

See: Training Mindset principle

Quick Reference

Game Selection Cheat SheetLevel ProgressionContact LevelsStop Conditions

Striking defense→Parry the Straight · Slip the Straight Striking offense→Land the Target Range / timing→Touch Game Space control→Pressure to Clinch Full integration→Pressure to Takedown Wall work→Wall Control

1Single actionPredictable timing. 2Variable timingMore complexity (L2–3). 4Counters & combosAdvanced mechanics (L4–5). 5Full MMACross-domain threats (highest level).

Touch, contact made, no force→Skill isolation, beginners Light, force felt, not damaging→Most development games Moderate, significant force, controlled→Advanced, integration

Loss of composure Excessive contact Rules being ignored Injury concern Learning environment breaks down

Coaching Scenarios

How do I know when an athlete is ready for the next level?

Look for these signs:

✅Consistent success

They hit the game's objective most of the time.

😴Boredom
