The current level no longer challenges them.

⚙️Automatic execution

They don't think hard about the basics.

🙋Asking for more

They want additional challenge.

When in doubt, stay at the current level longer. Mastery beats rushing.

What if athletes in my group are at different levels?

1Pair by levelSimilar-level athletes work together. 2Handicap the advanced athleteAdd constraints (e.g. one hand only). 3Role assignmentAdvanced athletes play feeder/attacker roles. 4Split the groupRun different levels simultaneously. 5Use it as teachingAdvanced athletes coach beginners between rounds.

How do I introduce this system to a traditional gym?

Gradual integration works best:

1Start with one gamePick something fun like Touch Game. 2Frame it as a warm-upLess threatening to existing culture. 3Let results speakAthletes will notice skill transfer. 4Add games slowlyOne new game every few weeks. 5Don't criticize the old wayPresent games as "adding tools."

Can I mix games with regular sparring?

Yes, and you should. Games develop skills; sparring tests integration.

Game → Sparring→Touch Game warm-up, then free sparring Themed sparring→Spar with one game's focus ("only score with counters") Game → Integration→Skill isolation, then open rounds

What if an athlete has physical limitations?

Adapt the game, not the athlete. The ecological approach embraces individual solutions.

1Modify rangeCloser or farther based on mobility. 2Reduce intensityLower contact levels. 3Change objectivesFocus on what they CAN do. 4Adjust timingMore time to execute. 5Skip levelsNot every level suits every athlete.

How do I handle a big skill gap between partners?

🥇 Advanced partner

Provide honest resistance, work weak areas, focus on timing & reading, be a good training partner.

Don't sandbag completely; use the off-hand or limit tools to stay challenged while creating learning opportunities. VS 🐣 Less experienced

Focus on survival first, ask questions, celebrate small wins.

Don't overreach, progress, not dominance, is the measure. Learn from the experience gap.

What about athletes who are too passive?

Passive athletes need encouragement to engage:

1Simplify objectives"Just touch their shoulder once." 2Remove consequencesPlay without scoring temporarily. 3Pair with patient partnersAggressive opponents make it worse. 4Celebrate attemptsReward action regardless of outcome. 5Check for fearPassivity often masks uncertainty or anxiety.

Do I need to follow prerequisite chains strictly?

Guidelines, not rules. Use judgment, you know your athletes.

  • Prerequisites indicate logical progression, Skill A helps with Skill B
  • Experienced athletes can skip ahead, a wrestler doesn't need Takedown Defense basics
  • Return to prerequisites when stuck, regression often means foundation gaps

See: Prerequisite Map

Can I create my own game modifications?

Absolutely.

When modifying:

1Keep the core problemDon't lose what the game teaches. 2Maintain invariantsRules exist for reasons. 3Test it firstTry it yourself before deploying. 4Document changesTrack what works. 5Share itOthers may benefit.

How do I track athlete progress across games?

Simple methods, but avoid over-measuring; learning isn't always linear or visible:

🪜Level tracking

Record highest level achieved per game.

📊Confidence rating

Athlete self-rates comfort (1–5).

🎥Video comparison

Record early vs. later performance.

↗️Sparring transfer

Note when game skills appear in sparring.

📝Coach notes

Qualitative observations.

Resources

Find a game→Game Finder Check prerequisites→Prerequisite Map Understand a term→Glossary See all games→Games Library Plan a session→Session Planning Development notice

More FAQs will be added based on common coaching questions.

docs.title: Game Finder
docs.text:
ReferenceFind Your GameBy Goal · Time · Gear · Partner

Not sure which game to play? Tell the finder what you want, it points you to the game that builds it. Start with the decision tree, then narrow by goal, time, equipment, or partner.

Quick Decision Tree

🥊Want striking

Defense (beginner): Parry, Slip, Block → Evade, Close-Range Defense.Offense: Land the Target.

🤼Want wrestling

Open space: Pressure to Clinch, Open Space Takedown, Pressure to Takedown.Wall: Wall Control, Wall Escape, Stand-Up Loop.

🥋Want both together

Start with Touch Game, then Pressure to Clinch.

Narrow it down by goal, time, equipment, or partner below.

Find by Training Goal: Defense

Against PunchesAgainst TakedownsAgainst Wall Pins

Beginner: start here

Deflect punches with the hands→Parry the Straight Evade with head movement→Slip the Straight Absorb on a tight guard→Tight Block

Intermediate: build on foundations

Match evasion to punch type→Evade the Punch Layer multiple solutions→Close-Range Defense

Avoid being cornered→Pressure to Clinch Scrambles & DNS defense→Open Space Takedown

Break pins, create space→Wall Escape Get up when taken down near the wall→Stand-Up Loop

Find by Training Goal: Offense

StrikingTakedownsWall Control

Read defense, set up, land targets→Land the Target

Chain strikes into takedowns→Pressure to Takedown Controlled takedowns from the clinch→Open Space Takedown

Establish & maintain pins→Wall Control Transition pin to ground control→Wall Pin to Ground

Find by Training Goal: Fundamentals & Integration

FundamentalsRealistic MMA Integration

🎯Touch & Don't Get Touched

Range, timing, composure under contact.The foundational engagement game, learn to manage distance before anything else.

🧭Pressure to Clinch

Space awareness, pressure vs. movement.Low contact, space-focused, teaches how to cut off the marked perimeter.

🥊Parry the Straight

Basic defensive mechanics.The simplest skill-isolation game, a clean first rep of defensive reading.

⛓️Pressure to Takedown

Full strike-to-takedown sequence.Chains striking and wrestling into one continuous problem.

🔀Pressure to Clinch (L3+)

Recognizing when domains change.At higher levels, the moment striking becomes wrestling becomes the focus.

🥋Any game at Full MMA

Adding cross-domain threats.The highest level of any game introduces threats from the other domain.

See: Full MMA Expression

Find by Time Available

⏱️Short: 15–20 min

Pick one game, work levels 1–3: a skill-isolation game (Parry, Slip, Block, Evade), Touch Game, or Pressure to Clinch.One focused problem, repeated cleanly, beats spreading thin across many games.

⏳Medium: 30–45 min

Pair two related games and let one feed the other.Skill Isolation → Close-Range Defense · Touch Game → Pressure to Clinch · Wall Control → Wall Escape.

🕐Full: 60+ min

Run a full pathway from isolation to integration.Skill Isolation → Close-Range Defense → Land the Target · Touch Game → Pressure to Clinch → Wall Control · Wall Control → Wall Escape → Wall to Ground.

Find by Equipment

No EquipmentGloves OnlyWall + Mats

Light contact only→Touch & Don't Get Touched Use tape/cones for the marked perimeter→Pressure to Clinch

🥊 All Skill Isolation games 🛡️ Close-Range Defense 🎯 Land the Target

🧱 Wall ControlPadded wall preferred for repeated pin work. ↩️ Wall Escape ⬇️ Wall Pin to GroundMats essential, play ends on the ground. 🔁 Stand-Up Loop

Find by Partner Skill Level

Partner Less ExperiencedPartner More ExperiencedEqual Partners

Clear roles, adjustable intensity→Skill Isolation games Defender doesn't counter→Land the Target Space-based, low contact→Pressure to Clinch

🎭Any asymmetric game

Put the less experienced player in the learning role.

🪜Use lower levels

The experienced partner moderates intensity.

🛡️Focus on defensive games

Less experienced defends and learns to read.

Any game works. Progress through the levels together, both sides feed and learn in turn.

Still Not Sure?

Brand new to the system→Touch & Don't Get Touched, the foundational game Want striking defense→Parry the Straight, the simplest isolation game Want wrestling→Pressure to Clinch, low contact, space-focused Want full MMA feel→Pressure to Takedown, chains everything together System evolution notice

More games will be added as the system develops. This finder will be updated accordingly.

docs.title: Prerequisite Map
docs.text:
ReferenceProgression Map57-Game System

How games connect. Every game has an entry point and a path forward, this map shows the prerequisites, the chains they form, and the concepts each game leans on.

Entry Points: No Prerequisites

Start here. These games ask nothing before you play them.

🥊Parry the Straight

Striking defense, simplest defensive mechanic.A single deflection skill, isolated and clean.

🛡️Tight Block

Striking defense, pure absorption skill.Block-only behind a tight shell, no parry or head movement.

↪️Slip the Straight

Head movement, a single evasion type.Isolates slipping before layering other solutions.

👊Lead Hand Offense

Striking offense, single weapon isolation.Creativity from one tool, the lead hand.

🤜Rear Hand Offense

Striking offense, single weapon isolation.Creativity from one tool, the rear hand.

🦵Lead Leg Offense

Kicking offense, single weapon isolation.Creativity from one tool, the lead leg.

🦶Rear Leg Offense

Kicking offense, single weapon isolation.Creativity from one tool, the rear leg.

🎯Touch Game

Range and timing, foundational engagement.Manage distance before adding strikes or grappling.

🤼Ground Escape

Ground defense, basic bottom position.The foundational ground game; no prerequisites.

Prerequisite Chains by Environment

Skill Isolation (9)Open Space, Striking (8)Transition Zone (2)Open Space, Wrestling (7)Wall (6)Ground (25)

Parry the Straight→Close-Range Defense Tight Block→Close-Range Defense Slip the Straight→Evade the Punch Evade the Punch (needs Slip)→Close-Range Defense Lead Hand · Rear Hand · Lead Leg · Rear Leg→Land the Target Close-Range Defense→Kick Defense Isolation (WIP)

Touch Game→Pressure to Clinch Close-Range Defense (needs Skill Isolation defense)→Counter-Striking · Evasive Movement Evasive Movement→Pressure to Clinch Counter-Striking (needs CRD + Land the Target)→Takedown Defense Land the Target (needs Three Zones)→Sustained Offense · Touch Game Sustained Offense→KO / TKO finish Pressure to Clinch→Wall Control · Clinch Denial · Open Space Takedown Counter-Striking · Land the Target→One Shot Discovery (WIP)

Clinch Denial (needs Pressure to Clinch + Hand Controls)→Wall Control Counter-Wrestling (needs CRD + Pressure to Clinch)→Open Space Takedown

Takedown Defense (needs Pressure to Clinch)→Open Space Takedown Pressure to Takedown (needs CRD + Pressure to Clinch)→Wall Control · Open Space Takedown Pressure to Wall (needs Pressure to Clinch)→Wall Control Open Space Takedown (needs Pressure to Clinch)→Ground games Open Space Takedown · Back Escape→Body Lock to Ground (WIP) Pressure to Clinch→50/50 Clinch (WIP) Tight Block · Close-Range Defense→The Square (WIP)

Wall Control (needs Pressure to Clinch)→Wall Escape · Wall to Ground · Wall Grinding Wall Escape→Stand-Up Loop Wall Defensive Submission (needs Wall Escape)→Submission finish Wall to Ground→Stand-Up Loop · Ground games Wall Grinding→TKO finish Stand-Up Loop (needs Wall to Ground)→Ground games

Ground Access (needs Open Space Takedown / Wall to Ground)→Ground Control Wall Pin to Ground→Win Ladder (WIP) Ground Control (needs Ground Access)→TKO / Submission finish Ground Escape→Leg Reclaim · Ground to Standing Leg Reclaim (needs Ground Escape)→Positional Sparring · Ground to Standing Ground to Standing (needs Ground Escape)→Return to standing Ground Defensive Submission (needs Ground Escape)→Submission finish Positional Sparring (live format)→Run live on Ground Access & Control Ground Escape→Back Escape Ground Control · Back Escape→Back Maintenance (WIP) Leg Reclaim · Seated Guard Retention (WIP)→Closed Guard Bottom (WIP) Ground Access→Closed Guard Pass (WIP) Ground Escape · Ground to Standing · Leg Reclaim→Ground-and-Pound Defense (WIP) Leg Reclaim→Half-Guard Bottom (WIP) Ground Access→Half-Guard Pass (WIP) Ground Control→Knee-on-Belly Control (WIP) Ground Escape→Mount Escape Ground Control · Mount Escape→Mount Maintenance (WIP) Mount Maintenance (WIP) · Mount Escape→Mount Submission Hunt (WIP) Ground Control→North-South Control (WIP) Ground Access→Open Guard Pass (WIP) Leg Reclaim→Seated Guard Retention (WIP) Ground Escape→Side-Control Escape Ground Control · Side-Control Escape→Side-Control Ride (WIP) Ground to Standing→Turtle Breakout (WIP)

The Macro Progression

The whole system flows through six stages to a finish. The per-game chains above fill in each stage; this is the spine they hang on.

① Skill Isolation→Single defenses and single weapons, built clean ② Open Space Striking→Integrate defense and offense at range ③ Transitions and Wrestling→Clinch entries, takedowns, and their denials ④ Wall→Pin, grind, escape, transition down ⑤ Ground→Access, control, escape, reclaim ⑥ Finish→KO · TKO · Submission

Pathway Guides

Striking DefenseStriking OffenseWrestlingGround

Parry / Block / Slip→Evade the Punch Evade the Punch→Close-Range Defense Close-Range Defense→Counter-Striking · Evasive Movement

Weapon Isolation→Land the Target Land the Target→Sustained Offense → KO/TKO Land the Target→Touch Game → Pressure to Clinch

Pressure to Clinch→Pressure to Takedown → Wall Control Pressure to Clinch→Takedown Defense → Open Space Takedown

Offensive→Open Space Takedown → Ground Access → Ground Control → TKO/Sub Defensive→Ground Escape → Leg Reclaim → Ground to Standing Combined→Positional Sparring (role reversal possible)

Concept Prerequisites

Some games require understanding a specific concept first.

Three Zones→Land the Target · Sustained Offense · Evasive Movement Confidence Rating→Land the Target · Sustained Offense · Counter-Striking TKO Pin→Wall Control · Wall to Ground · Wall Grinding · Pressure to Takedown Defensive Solutions→All Skill Isolation games Hand Controls→Close-Range Defense · Clinch Denial · Counter-Wrestling · Pressure games Decision States→All Ground games · Positional Sparring Full MMA Expression→Highest level of any game

Readiness Checklists

Before Close-Range Defense

  • Can parry straight punches consistently
  • Can slip with good timing
  • Can absorb on a tight guard
  • Understands defensive solution trade-offs

Before Land the Target

  • Understands the Three Zones concept
  • Can identify defensive patterns
  • Has weapon isolation experience

Before Pressure to Takedown

  • Competent at Close-Range Defense
  • Competent at Pressure to Clinch
  • Can chain strikes while moving forward

Before Ground Games

  • Completed Open Space Takedown or Wall to Ground
  • Understands Decision States
  • Comfortable with the positional hierarchy

Before Full MMA Expression

  • Proficient at lower levels of the game
  • Primary skill feels automatic
  • Ready for cross-domain pressure

System evolution notice

This map reflects the current 57-game system and will update as games are added.

docs.title: Sources & Theoretical Foundations
docs.text:
ReferenceTheoretical LineageMotor Learning Research

This system is built on established research in motor learning, ecological psychology, and sports coaching. This page documents the theoretical lineage and the key sources that inform our approach.

Theoretical Lineage

Ecological Psychology→Gibson, 1979 Coordination Dynamics→Bernstein, 1967 · Kelso, 1995 Constraints Model→Newell, 1986 Ecological Dynamics→Davids et al., 2008 Nonlinear Pedagogy→Chow et al., 2011 Constraints-Led Approach→Renshaw et al., 2019 This System→Ecological MMA Games

Primary Source

📖 Renshaw, I., Davids, K., Newcombe, D., & Roberts, W. (2019). The Constraints-Led Approach: Principles for Sports Coaching and Practice Design. Routledge.

This text provides the methodological framework for our entire system. Its four Environment Design Principles, Session Intention, Constrain to Afford, Representative Learning Design, Repetition Without Repetition, directly shape how every game is structured.

See: CLA Knowledge Base

Foundational Theorists

James J. Gibson (1904–1979): Ecological psychology, affordances, direct perception

Key work

Gibson, J.J. (1979). The Ecological Approach to Visual Perception. Houghton Mifflin.

Core ideas

🌍Affordances

The environment offers action opportunities relative to the organism's capabilities.

👁️Direct perception

Information specifies action possibilities without cognitive mediation.

🔁Perception-action coupling

Perceiving and acting form a continuous loop, not separate processes.

How we use it

  • Games are designed around affordances (what the environment invites)
  • Athletes learn to perceive opportunities, not execute memorized techniques
  • Training maintains the perception-action link (no isolated drilling)

Nikolai Bernstein (1896–1966): Motor coordination, degrees of freedom, dexterity

Key work

Bernstein, N.A. (1967). The Co-ordination and Regulation of Movements. Pergamon Press.

Core ideas

🎚️Degrees of freedom

The body's enormous movement possibilities must be organized.

🧊Freeze → Release → Exploit

Learners constrain movement, then progressively free it.

🤹Dexterity

Finding solutions that are correct, quick, rational, resourceful.

🔄Repetition without repetition

Repeat problem-solving, not identical movements.

How we use it

  • Games allow multiple solutions (dexterity, not technique compliance)
  • Levels progress from constrained to open (freezing → exploiting)
  • Variability is built into practice (repetition without repetition)

Scott Kelso (b. 1947): Coordination dynamics, self-organization, phase transitions

Key work

Kelso, J.A.S. (1995). Dynamic Patterns: The Self-Organization of Brain and Behavior. MIT Press.

Core ideas

🌀Self-organization

Coordinated patterns emerge spontaneously under constraints.

🧲Attractors

Stable coordination states behavior tends toward.

⚡Phase transitions

Shifts between patterns when control parameters change.

📉Instability precedes change

Variability increases before new patterns emerge.

How we use it

  • Techniques emerge from constraints, not instruction
  • Games create conditions for self-organization
  • Productive instability (amber zone) is necessary for learning

Karl Newell (b. 1945): Constraints model for motor development

Key work

Newell, K.M. (1986). Constraints on the development of coordination. In M.G. Wade & H.T.A. Whiting (Eds.), Motor Development in Children: Aspects of Coordination and Control (pp. 341–360). Martinus Nijhoff.

Core ideas

🧍Individual

Organismic constraints unique to the athlete.

🎯Task

The rules and goals of the activity.

🌳Environmental

The surrounding physical and social context.

Behavior emerges from the interaction of all three, not any single factor. Constraint manipulation guides learning without prescribing movement.

How we use it

  • Every game manipulates specific constraints
  • Rules (task constraints) shape what behaviors emerge
  • Individual differences are respected (not one-size-fits-all)

Michael Turvey (b. 1942): Action systems, ecological realism

Key works

Turvey, M.T. (1990). Coordination. American Psychologist, 45(8), 938–953.Turvey, M.T., & Shaw, R.E. (1999). Ecological foundations of cognition. In R. Núñez & W.J. Freeman (Eds.), Reclaiming Cognition (pp. 95–110). Imprint Academic.

Core ideas

🧩Intra-individual

Organizing body parts relative to each other.

🤝Inter-individual

Organizing the body relative to environment/opponents.

🛠️Action systems

Temporarily assembled, task-specific organizations.

How we use it

  • Games develop both types of coordination
  • Combat sports require continuous inter-individual coordination (reading opponent)
  • Synergies (movement solutions) are soft-assembled, not hardwired

Secondary Sources

Ecological DynamicsNonlinear PedagogyRepresentative DesignCombat Sports

📕Davids, Button & Bennett (2008)

Dynamics of Skill Acquisition: A Constraints-Led Approach. Human Kinetics.

📄Araújo, Davids & Hristovski (2006)

The ecological dynamics of decision making in sport. Psychology of Sport and Exercise, 7(6), 653–676.

📄Chow et al. (2007)

The role of nonlinear pedagogy in physical education. Review of Educational Research, 77(3), 251–278.

📄Chow et al. (2011)

Nonlinear pedagogy: Learning design for self-organizing neurobiological systems. New Ideas in Psychology, 29(2), 189–200.

📄Pinder et al. (2011)

Representative learning design and functionality of research and practice in sport. Journal of Sport and Exercise Psychology, 33(1), 146–155.

🥊Hristovski et al. (2006)

How boxers decide to punch a target: Emergent behaviour in nonlinear dynamical movement systems. Journal of Sports Science and Medicine, 5(CSSI), 60–73.

How This System Implements CLA

Constraints shape behavior→Game rules (task constraints) create the learning environment Affordances invite action→Scoring rewards target behaviors without prescribing technique Self-organization→Athletes discover solutions; coaches don't dictate movement Perception-action coupling→Games include opponents and context; no isolated drilling Representative design→Games model real MMA decision states Repetition without repetition→Levels and variability guidelines ensure varied practice Individual differences→Constraints can be adjusted for each athlete

Citation Format Used in This System

💬For CLA principles

"Quoted principle or concept", Renshaw et al., 2019

🔗For foundational concepts

This implements perception-action coupling (Gibson, 1979) through…

For full theoretical background on any concept, see the CLA Knowledge Base.

Game-Design Attributions

When a game in this library was inspired or directly shaped by an identifiable creator's design, we credit them here. Mechanics are re-expressed under our own names; this page is the authoritative attribution.

🎙️Alex Calienes

Original designs narrated in class recordings: One Shot Discovery (WIP), Ground-and-Pound Defense (WIP), Turtle Breakout (WIP), the Stalk and Corral variant of Pressure to Wall, and ongoing.Recorded 2026-05-30 and 2026-06-06 classes. Games are marked WIP pending live-platform review before going canonical.

🧱Leo (student)

Wall-pin principle: pin one part of the body to the wall, then remove whatever is below that point (pin the waist, pull the leg; pin the knee, pull the foot).Described to Alex before the 2026-06-06 class, captured on the recording. Not yet built into a game; credited here so the lineage survives if it is.

🥋Greg Souders / Standard Jiu-Jitsu

Many of our per-position ground games and submission ladders are shaped by Souders' ecological / constraints-led catalog.Public game library at Slime Mold Grappling Club (slimemoldgrappling.com). We re-express the mechanics under our own names with our threshold house rule applied.

🌍Rob Cole / BJJ Globetrotters

Scramble games (Ankle Wars, Spin Up, Creative Starts, No Knees) and the Cross Ashi control game.Via BJJ Globetrotters Camp and Slime Mold Grappling Club attributions. Cross-ashi safety constraints (no heel exposure) come directly from this source.

🦵Justin Mark / Impact Jiu-Jitsu

Seated-guard-vs-standing games (Create & Break Connections, Control A Leg, Destabilization).

👟Craig Jones

Shin-surfing leg ride (positional retention with shin on thigh).

📐Stephan Kesting (Grapplearts)

Turtle seatbelt game ("dislodge the sternum"), situational sparring framework, RNC / back-escape designs.

🎓John Danaher

Positional sparring formats (pass-or-sweep, submit-or-full-guard) and escape-to-dominance philosophy."New Wave Jiu Jitsu" and "Go Further Faster" are Danaher's branded product names; mechanics are generic, branded copy is not reused.

💬Discord harvest contributors

Game ideas mined from public Discord discussions and credited per game.Including Aaron, breanne, Spacecityliving, Vasja, Diviney, jan tan musi, dNice, Chase, hesostrong, binney, MrWild, gwj, mtwol, RobDurden, and others, as cited in docs/_drafts/discord-harvest-2026-05-26.md.

Working rule: capture the mechanic, re-express under our own names and conventions; credit the originator here. Never reuse branded names or verbatim proprietary text. If a game's origin is not identifiable (widely-taught generic mechanics), no forced attribution.

Further Reading

1Start hereRenshaw et al. (2019), the primary CLA text.The most direct, practice-oriented entry point into the approach. 2For theoryGibson (1979), dense but foundational.The origin of affordances and direct perception. 3For applicationDavids, Button & Bennett (2008), practical skill acquisition.Bridges theory and the practice floor. 4For combat sportsHristovski et al. (2006), boxing-specific research.Emergent striking behavior under constraints. Acknowledgement

This system stands on the shoulders of decades of motor learning research. We gratefully acknowledge the scientists whose work made this approach possible.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
Shared VocabularyTerms Used System-WideDefinitions & Cross-Links

The shared language of the Ecological MMA Games System. Every game, concept, and coaching cue draws on these terms, hover any card with a marker for the deeper note.

Control States

📌Pin

A control position where the defender's movement is significantly limited by the attacker's body position and pressure. Distinct from TKO Pin.

🧱Wall Pin

An opponent held to the wall/cage by at least two points of pinning control, hips and upper body rendered immobile and flat.Distinct from merely pressing them into the wall: if the shoulders can still rotate, it's pressure, not a pin. Two-plus pinning points flatten and immobilize both the hips and the upper body.

⬆️Top Control

Ground position where the attacker is above the defender, managing hips and shoulders.

🎯TKO Pin

A control state, not just a position, that compromises posture and mobility.Hips and shoulders meaningfully controlled · posture & mobility compromised · at least one limb free to strike · defender options are high-cost.

🔗Hand Connection

Hands locked around the waist or around a leg above the knee. Collecting below the knee doesn't count.The standard for any "connect the hands" win condition. A grip below the knee (low-single style) is a real attack but not a connection: collect above the knee for it to count. Keeps connection wins meaning controlling attachment, not a dive at the ankle.

🌀Scramble

A transition state where control is contested and neither athlete has position.Outcome depends on hip mobility, base recovery, and positional awareness.

🚪Disengagement

Breaking contact and returning to a neutral position with defensive posture established.

Clinch Positions

🤼Clinch

A close-range position where fighters control each other with grips, too close to strike freely. Always name the type (50/50, body-lock, collar-tie…)."Clinch" is a category, not one position, the grip configuration decides who's dominant. So a clinch is never referenced generically; always specify which.

⚖️50/50 Clinch

Each fighter has one overhook and one underhook, diagonally matched (one per side), starting chest to chest. Symmetric and neutral, neither dominant.Also called the over-under clinch. It's up for grabs: whoever first wins inside control or the angle gains the advantage.

Decision States

🤝Access

The ability to connect or engage an opponent. First phase of any exchange.

🔒Stabilize

Preventing escape or reversal after connection is made. Control establishment phase.

💥Exploit

Executing damage, submissions, or positional advance from a stable position.

↩️Counter

Interrupting, escaping, or reversing the opponent's attack. Defensive response phase.

Submission Categories

🔗Defensive Neck Submissions (DNS)

Neck attacks used defensively to punish errors, allowed as a defensive tool, not a primary objective.Punishes poor posture, over-committed control attempts, and head-position errors.

Design Concepts

🌐Full MMA Expression

The highest level of any game, cross-domain threats added while keeping the core focus.Striking games add grappling threats (shoot, clinch); wrestling games add striking threats. Tests skills under realistic pressure without overwhelming beginners.

🌱Representative Design

Training that mimics the decision-making context of competition. The environment creates the learning, not verbal instruction.

📏Invariants

Fixed rules within a game that cannot be violated, they define the problem space.Example: "No clinching in open space" (Touch Game Level 1).

🎯Task Focus

The specific perceptual and motor challenges an athlete must solve within a game.

🧱Constraints

Environmental, task, or rule limitations that shape behavior. In ecological training, constraints replace instructions.

Environmental Terms

🥊Open Space

Training area without structural constraints (walls, cages). Movement is unrestricted.

⬛Boundary

Edge of the training area (wall, cage, marked perimeter) that limits retreat and triggers new behaviors.

🧱Wall Environment

Context where the wall / cage is the dominant constraint, changing available techniques.

🤼Ground Environment

Context where both athletes are on the ground; gravity and horizontal positioning dominate.

Game Structure Terms

🎮Game

A distinct learning problem.Stable environment · clear goal · defined invariants · clear win conditions.

🪜Level

A progression inside the same game.Same environment · same core problem · stricter or more complete success criteria.

🚦Entry Condition

The starting setup for a game, positions, distances, and reset rules.

🏁Win Condition

How success is determined for each role in a game.

Safety Terms

💡Informational Strikes

Light contact used to measure distance, create reactions, or test control, not to cause damage.

🪂Controlled Descent

Bringing an opponent down while maintaining connection and balance. Opposite of "dumping."

⚠️Dumping

Forcing an opponent down with bodyweight or momentum without proper control. Unsafe and penalized.

Grappling Positions

🛡️Guard

Bottom position with legs engaged between you and opponent. Defensive control and attacks.

🦿Half Guard

Bottom with one of opponent's legs trapped between yours. Transitional position.

🪑Mount

Top position sitting on the torso, knees on either side. Dominant striking / submission spot.

↔️Side Control

Top position perpendicular to opponent, chest-to-chest. Strong control.

🎒Back Control

Controlling from behind, typically with hooks and a seatbelt grip.

🐢Turtle

Defensive all-fours position, protecting neck and limbs. Temporary survival.

🧭North-South

Top position facing the opposite direction, head-to-head.

Striking Terms

👊Jab

Lead hand straight punch. Primary range-finder and setup tool.

🥊Cross

Rear hand straight punch. Primary power punch.

🪝Hook

Curved punch targeting the side of head or body.

⬆️Uppercut

Upward punch targeting chin or body.

🦶Teep

Push kick / front kick. Used for distance management.

🌀Round Kick

Circular kick using the shin as the striking surface.

🦵Low Kick

Kick targeting the opponent's legs (calf, thigh).

Defensive Terms

🤚Parry

Redirecting a strike with a small hand movement.

↪️Slip

Moving the head offline to avoid a straight punch.

🔄Roll

Rotating shoulders / head to deflect hooks.

⬇️Sprawl

Hip defense against takedowns, driving hips back and down.

💪Underhook

Arm positioned under opponent's armpit. Key clinch control.

🪢Overhook (Whizzer)

Arm positioned over opponent's arm. Counter to the underhook.

🦴Frame

Using skeletal structure (forearms, elbows) to create space. Not strength-dependent.

Training Formats

📍Positional Sparring

Starting from a specific position with defined reset conditions. High repetition.

🌊Flow Rolling

Cooperative, exploratory grappling at low intensity. Movement over winning.

🔥Live Sparring

Full resistance sparring with defined rules. Competitive intensity.

🔁Situational Drilling

Repeating specific scenarios with progressive resistance.

Role Terms

🅰️A Side

The athlete whose learning is prioritized. Usually the attacker / initiator.

🅱️B Side

The athlete providing resistance / reactions. Still learning, in support of A Side.

🍴Feeder

Partner providing controlled stimulus (strikes, pressure) for skill development.

System Terms

🟣Combined Game

Both athletes have simultaneous offensive and defensive objectives. Neither role is purely reactive.

🔀Transition

Movement between fight environments (Open Space → Wall, Open Space → Ground, etc.).

🚪Escape

Disengaging from a controlled position and returning to a neutral or advantageous state.

➡️Pressure

Forward movement that reduces opponent's space and options. Striking or wrestling-based.

✂️Cage Cut

Using footwork and positioning to limit opponent's lateral movement.

docs.title: System Map
docs.text:
Visual NavigationAll 57 GamesEnvironment → Finish

The fight is cyclical. You move between environments until someone finishes, and every path has a reverse. This map routes you from foundation skills through every game to a finish.

Fight States & Transitions

🥊 Open Space

Standing at distance. Every fight starts here.

KOTKOSUB Clinch / Takedown →← Escape 🧱 Wall

Clinch against the cage.

KOTKOSUB Takedown →← Stand Up 🤼 Ground

Horizontal grappling.

TKOSUB

How to Read the Map

Foundation→Open Space Open Space→Transitions (to Wall / Ground) Transitions→Positions (Wall / Ground) Positions→Finish (KO · TKO · SUB) 🟢 DefensiveSurvive, escape, prevent transitions. The B Side is developing skills. 🟠 OffensiveAttack, control, initiate transitions. The A Side is developing skills. 🟣 CombinedBoth roles active, scrambles and mixed situations where both sides have live objectives.

The Cycle in Four Beats

1️⃣Start standing

Every fight starts in Open Space, standing at distance.

2️⃣Transition

From Open Space, move to Wall (clinch) or Ground (grappling).

3️⃣Escape back

From Wall or Ground, you can escape to Open Space.

4️⃣Finish

Any position can lead to a finish, KO, TKO, or Submission.

The Three End States

💥Knockout (KO)

A single strike ends the fight.Primary path: a clean strike landed with power and precision.

🥊TKO

Accumulated damage overwhelms the defense.Primary paths: sustained offense, wall grinding, ground control.

🔒Submission

A choke or joint lock forces the tap.Primary paths: ground control, defensive submissions.

🏗️ Foundation: Skill Isolation

Skills developed in isolation before integration into live fighting.

Defensive 🟢

🤚Parry the Straight

Hand deflection timing.

🛡️Tight Block

Guard absorption.

↪️Slip the Straight

Head movement.

💨Evade the Punch

Full evasion vocabulary.

Offensive 🟠

👊Lead Hand Offense

Jab mechanics and setups.

🥊Rear Hand Offense

Cross / power hand timing.

🦵Lead Leg Offense

Lead kick variety.

🦿Rear Leg Offense

Power kick delivery.

🥊 Open Space

Standing at distance. This is where every fight starts.

Striking Defense 🟢

🛡️Close-Range Defense

Layered defensive response.

💨Evasive Movement

Creating distance under fire.

⚡Counter-Striking

Punishing initiation.

Striking Offense 🟠

🎯Land the Target

Reading defense, landing clean.

🔥Sustained Offense

Maintaining pressure to finish.

Control 🟣

✋Touch and Don't Get Touched

Range awareness and timing.

🧱 Open Space → Wall

Transitions from standing at distance to the cage / clinch.

Prevent 🟢

🚫Clinch Denial

Breaking clinch attempts.

Initiate 🟠

➡️Pressure to Clinch

Space control and clinch entry.

🧱Pressure to Wall

Driving opponent to the cage.

🤼 Open Space → Ground

Transitions from standing to the ground (takedowns / knockdowns).

Prevent 🟢

🛑Takedown Defense

Sprawl, underhooks, recovery.

⚡Counter-Wrestling

Punishing shot attempts.

Initiate 🟠

⬇️Pressure to Takedown

Chaining strikes to takedown.

🤸Open Space Takedown

Takedowns without the wall.

🤼Body Lock to Ground

Ground the hips from body lock or back.

🧱 Wall

Standing against the cage. You arrive via clinch or pressure.

Escape & Defend 🟢

🚪Wall Escape

Breaking the pin, returning to open space.

🔒Wall Defensive Submission

Submissions to deter / punish control.

Control & Grind 🟠

📌Wall Control

Maintaining the pin.

⚙️Wall Grinding

Accumulating damage toward TKO.

Wall → Ground 🟠

⬇️Wall to Ground

Taking the fight to ground from the wall.

Live Format 🟣

🔁Stand-Up Loop

Managing repeated stand-up / re-pin cycles.

🤼 Ground

Horizontal grappling. You arrive via takedown or knockdown.

Escape & Defend 🟢

🚪Ground Escape

Basic escapes from bottom.

⛰️Mount Escape

Frame and bridge from under mount.

📐Side-Control Escape

Recover before the position advances.

🎒Back Escape

Defend the neck, turn to face.

🦵Leg Reclaim

Guard recovery.

🧍Ground to Standing

Technical stand-up.

🔗Turtle Breakout (WIP)

Stand and face before the hands connect.

🔒Ground Defensive Submission

Submissions from bottom.

Control & Finish 🟠

🗝️Ground Access

Passing guard.

📌Ground Control

Maintaining dominant position.

⛰️Mount Maintenance

Keep mount, turn it into a TKO pin.

🥋Side-Control Ride

Hold side, take an arm or mount.

🎒Back Maintenance

Keep the back, close on the chin.

🦵Knee-on-Belly Control

Ride light, attack or step to mount.

🧭North-South Control

Pin, transition, trap the kimura.

Live Format 🟣

⚔️Positional Sparring

The live layer: both compete for position from any ground start.

🪜Win Ladder (format, WIP)

Self-scaling overlay: every win demotes your next start a rung.

The Complete Cycle: In & Out

Every position has a way IN and a way OUT.

Open Space → Wall⇄In: Pressure to Clinch / Wall · Out: Wall Escape Open Space → Ground⇄In: Pressure to Takedown / Open Space Takedown / Knockdown · Out: Ground to Standing Wall → Ground⇄In: Wall to Ground · Out: Stand-Up Loop (scramble) Ground → Wall←Out: Scramble (Stand-Up Loop)

System Statistics

9Skill Isolation 15Open Space 2Transition 6Wall 25Ground 57Total Games

Concept Integration

Where the system's core concepts show up across the games.

🎯Three Zones

Offensive target selection.Appears in: Land the Target, Sustained Offense.

📊Confidence Rating

Commitment timing.Appears in: Land the Target, Skill Isolation.

🛡️Defensive Solutions

Defense selection.Appears in: all skill isolation, Close-Range Defense.

🤝Hand Controls

Bridges striking and clinch.Appears in: Touch, Pressure to Clinch, Pressure games.

📌TKO Pin

Exploitation endpoint.Appears in: Wall Grinding, Ground Control, Mount Maintenance, Side-Control Ride, Back Maintenance, Knee-on-Belly Control.

🔄Decision States

Access → Stabilize → Exploit → Counter.Appears in: all games.

🌐Full MMA Expression

Cross-domain threat integration.Appears in: Level 4 of all games.

⚖️Fight Philosophy

Finish while taking minimal damage.Appears: system-wide.

System evolution notice

Games can be added infinitely, the fight states and transitions are the structure; games are the teaching tools. See the Change Log for version history.

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text:
The FrameworkProblems, Not TechniquesDecision-State Logic

The big idea: the Ecological MMA Games System organizes training around problems, not techniques. Every fight is a chain of repeating decision states, the system teaches you to solve them, and lets the environment do the teaching.

Core Premise

MMA interactions repeat predictable decision patterns. The system is designed around these decision states, not named techniques.

🤝Access

Connecting or engaging an opponent, closing distance, initiating clinch.

🔒Stabilize

Stabilizing position so the opponent can't escape or reverse.

💥Exploit

Exploiting with strikes, submissions, or positional advance.

↩️Counter

Countering escapes or reversals when the opponent interrupts.

The Universal Pattern

Every exchange cycles through the same four states, and every state loops back to the next access.

1AccessConnect or engage 2StabilizePrevent escape or reversal 3ExploitStrike, submit, or advance 4CounterEscape or reverse

Every state loops back to the next Access

🤝Access

Can I connect or engage?Example: closing distance, initiating the clinch.

🔒Stabilize

Can I prevent escape or reversal?Example: establishing a pin, controlling the hips.

💥Exploit

Can I strike, submit, or advance?Example: TKO pin, ground and pound.

↩️Counter

Can the opponent interrupt or escape?Example: escape, reversal, defensive neck submission.

Environmental Layers

The dominant environment matters more than technique. When the environment changes, the learning problem changes, this is the primary factor for deciding when to build a new game versus a new level.

🥊Open Space

No structural constraints, movement dominant.

🧱Wall

Vertical constraint, limits retreat.

🤼Ground

Horizontal constraint, gravity dominant.

System Structure

Four layers stack from principle down to practice, each one serves the layer below it.

Principles↓Philosophy, safety, design rules Vocabulary↓Glossary, taxonomy, concepts Games↓Documented training problems by environment Coach Tools↓Assessment, templates, safety rules

Key Principles

🌱The environment does the teaching

Representative Design, behavior emerges from constraints.Athletes learn by solving problems, not following instructions. The coach designs the environment, not the solution.

🛡️Control precedes damage

Control comes before striking; stability before exploitation.Strikes are allowed in most games, but they are informational and carry positional trade-offs.

⚖️Every offense needs a defense

The system is built symmetrically.Teach pinning → teach escaping. Teach takedowns → teach scrambles. No domination layer exists without a matching resistance layer.

Where to Go Next

🗺️System Map

Visual overview of all games.

📖Glossary

Key terminology.

🎯Games Library

All documented games.

🧰Coach Tools

Practical coaching resources.

docs.title: Tags & Taxonomy
docs.text:
MetadataFiltering & OrganizingEnvironment → Domain → Focus

Structured metadata organizes and filters every game in the system. Tags drive session planning, card-deck generation, and gap analysis, and they all hang off one three-level hierarchy.

Organizational Hierarchy

Environment→Domain Domain→Focus 📍Environment

Physical space / context.Skill Isolation · Open Space · Wall · Ground · Transitions

🥋Domain

Skill category.Striking · Wrestling · Transitions

🎯Focus

Tactical objective.Offensive · Defensive · Combined

Environment Tags

🎯 skill-isolationConstrained drills focusing on single skills. 🥊 open-spaceStanding position without structural constraints. 🧱 wallWall / cage as the dominant constraint. 🤼 groundGround as the dominant constraint. 🔀 transitionsMoving between environments.

Domain Tags

🥊 strikingPunches, kicks, and strike defense. 🤼 wrestlingClinch, takedowns, and positional control. 🔀 transitionsMovement between environments.

Focus Tags

🟠 offensiveA Side is developing skills; primary learning is attack-oriented. 🟢 defensiveB Side is developing skills; primary learning is defense-oriented. 🟣 combinedBoth sides have active learning objectives simultaneously.

Difficulty & Duration

🟩 beginnerFoundational games, minimal prerequisites. 🟨 intermediateRequires foundational skills, moderate complexity. 🟥 advancedRequires multiple prerequisites, high complexity. ⏱️ short1–3 minute rounds. ⏲️ medium3–5 minute rounds. ⏳ long5+ minute rounds.

Stage Tags (Combat Phase)

🤝access

Engagement and connection phase.

🔒stabilize

Control establishment phase.

💥exploit

Damage or submission phase.

↩️counter

Defensive response phase.

Technique Tags

🥊 Striking: Offense

jabLead hand straight punch. crossRear hand straight punch. hookCurved punch to side of head / body. uppercutUpward punch to chin / body. overhandLooping power punch. teepPush kick / front kick. round-kickCircular kick to leg / body / head. low-kickKick targeting the legs. body-kickKick targeting the torso. head-kickKick targeting the head. kneeKnee strike. elbowElbow strike.

🛡️ Striking: Defense

parryDeflecting strikes with the hands. blockAbsorbing strikes with the guard. slipHead movement to avoid straight punches. rollHead movement to avoid hooks. pullLeaning back to avoid strikes. checkBlocking kicks with the shin. catchCatching the opponent's kick.

🦶 Movement

footworkStance and movement patterns. angleCreating off-center positioning. distanceRange management. pressureForward movement and cage cutting. retreatBackward movement and escape. pivotRotational movement. level-changeChanging height / elevation.

🤼 Wrestling

underhookArm under opponent's armpit. overhookArm over opponent's arm (whizzer). collar-tieHand behind opponent's neck. wrist-controlControlling opponent's wrists. pummelFighting for underhook position. sprawlHip defense against takedowns. single-legSingle leg takedown. double-legDouble leg takedown. tripOff-balancing with the leg. throwHip or shoulder throw.

🧩 Grappling Positions

guardBottom position with legs engaged. half-guardBottom with one leg trapped. mountTop position sitting on torso. side-controlTop position perpendicular to opponent. back-controlControlling from behind. turtleDefensive all-fours position. north-southTop position head-to-head. crucifixControlling both arms from the side.

🔒 Submissions

guillotineFront headlock choke. rncRear naked choke. arm-triangleHead and arm choke. darceArm-in head and arm choke. armbarStraight arm lock. kimuraShoulder lock (double wristlock). americanaReverse shoulder lock. triangleLeg triangle choke. heel-hookRotational knee / ankle attack. ankle-lockStraight ankle lock.

Situational Tags

beginner-friendlySuitable for new practitioners. high-intensityRequires significant exertion. low-intensityTechnical focus, lower exertion. live-sparringFull resistance, competitive. positional-sparringSpecific start position, reset on escape / score. flowCooperative, exploratory pace. timingEmphasis on timing development. reactionEmphasis on reaction training.

Game Tag Matrix

↪️Slip the Straight

Open Space: Wrestling

⬇️Pressure to Takedown

Wall

Frontmatter Schema

Every game file includes standardized YAML frontmatter:

---
title: Game Name
environment: open-space          # skill-isolation, open-space, wall, ground
domain: striking                 # striking, wrestling, transitions
focus: defensive                 # offensive, defensive, combined
difficulty: beginner             # beginner, intermediate, advanced
duration: short                  # short, medium, long
min_players: 2

  concepts:
    - concept-slug

---

What This Metadata Enables

🔍Filter games

By environment, domain, focus, or any dimension.

🃏Generate card decks

With prerequisite awareness built in.

📈Track development

Tag Relationships

Skill Isolation→Striking Open Space→Striking + Wrestling Wall→Wrestling Ground→Wrestling

And every domain splits into the three focuses

Striking · Wrestling→Offensive · Defensive · Combined System evolution notice

Tags and taxonomy may expand as the game library grows.

docs.title: Assessment Rubrics
docs.text:
Coaching ToolEvaluationSystem Integrity

Criteria for evaluating athlete performance, and for checking that the games themselves are still doing their job. Each rubric runs from ⭐ emerging to ⭐⭐⭐⭐ mastery.

Offensive Rubrics

Stabilize Before Exploit

⭐→Attempts exploitation without establishing control ⭐⭐→Establishes control but rushes to exploit ⭐⭐⭐→Consistent stabilize → exploit sequence ⭐⭐⭐⭐→Dynamic stabilization with immediate exploit recognition

Control Before Strike

⭐→Strikes without positional advantage ⭐⭐→Establishes position, loses it when striking ⭐⭐⭐→Maintains control while threatening strikes ⭐⭐⭐⭐→Oscillates fluidly between control and strike access

Respect Counters

⭐→Ignores DNS and escape threats ⭐⭐→Reacts to counters after they're applied ⭐⭐⭐→Anticipates and prevents counters ⭐⭐⭐⭐→Uses counter-threat awareness to set up attacks

Defensive Rubrics

Act Early

⭐→Waits until fully controlled to act ⭐⭐→Attempts escape after position consolidates ⭐⭐⭐→Acts during transition / before consolidation ⭐⭐⭐⭐→Prevents control establishment entirely

Use Structure

⭐→Uses strength / desperation to escape ⭐⭐→Creates frames but doesn't use them effectively ⭐⭐⭐→Structural escapes with proper sequencing ⭐⭐⭐⭐→Frames and structure are pre-positioned

Disengage with Intent

⭐→Escapes in any direction without plan ⭐⭐→Creates space but remains vulnerable ⭐⭐⭐→Turns to face before disengaging ⭐⭐⭐⭐→Disengages to an advantageous position

System Integrity Checks

Read the game, not just the athlete

If you observe these patterns, the constraints are mis-set, not the athlete failing.

💪Brute force domination

Skill mismatch or intensity too high.Lower intensity or re-pair athletes so technique, not strength, decides the exchange.

🔁Memorized repetitions

The game is too predictable.Add variability so the athlete must read and adapt rather than run a script.

🔥Ego escalation

Competitive framing instead of learning.Reset the frame: the game is a problem to solve, not a fight to win.

❓Confusion about goals

Win conditions are unclear.Restate the observable win condition before restarting.

🚫No defensive success

Constraints favor offense too heavily.Rebalance so the defender has a real, winnable problem.

Diagnosis protocol when red flags appear

1Stop the gamePause play immediately. 2ReviewConstraints and invariants. 3CheckIntensity levels. 4ClarifyWin conditions. 5RestartAdjust and resume.

Game-Specific Assessments

👆Touch Game

Range honesty, knows when in range · Composure after being touched · Counter-touch immediacy · Initiative recovery, how fast they reset mentally.

🧱Wall Control

Rotation denial of shoulder turn · Balance maintenance of own structure · DNS respect, protects head position · Early escape, defender acts before consolidation.

🔻Transitions

Control through transition, connection maintained · Scramble anticipation · Base compromise, takedown created, not forced · Safety compliance, controlled descent.

Progress Tracking

Level progression criteria

Level 1 → 2→Consistent success at current level Level 2 → 3→Can handle increased resistance Level 3 → Open play→Integrates skill under realistic conditions

Ready for the next level

  • Consistent success (>70% of attempts)
  • Maintains composure under pressure
  • Can articulate what they're doing and why
  • No safety violations

Not ready yet

Success is inconsistent Relies on athleticism over technique Shows frustration or ego response Safety lapses occur System evolution notice

Assessment criteria may be refined as patterns emerge across the games. See the Change Log for revisions.

docs.title: Lesson Plan Generator
docs.text:
Coaching Tool~60 Minute SessionsSimple → Complex

Select your focus areas and the generator suggests a 5-game session plan (~60 minutes). Each plan follows a logical progression from simpler to more complex games.

Supervision Required

All sessions should be conducted under qualified coach supervision. See the Disclaimer for important safety information.

Quick Generator

### Step 1: Select Environment Focus Skill Isolation Open Space Striking Transition Zone Open Space Wrestling Wall Ground ### Step 2: Select Focus Type Defensive Offensive Combined ### Step 3: Select Difficulty Range Beginner Intermediate Advanced Generate Session Plan

Manual Session Templates

If you prefer structured sessions, use these pre-designed days. Each runs roughly 60 minutes, progressing from a warm-up game to live application.

Template A · Striking Defense Day

Goal: build defensive striking skills from isolation to integration.

1Parry the Straight8 min · warm-up, hand defense 2Slip the Straight8 min · head movement foundation 3Close-Range Defense12 min · layered defense 4Evasive Movement10 min · footwork, ring generalship 5Touch and Don't Get Touched15 min · integration

Template B · Pressure Wrestling Day

Goal: develop pressure-based wrestling from striking range.

1Pressure to Clinch10 min · space control warm-up 2Pressure to Clinch (L3)10 min · clinch to wall transition 3Clinch Denial10 min · defensive transition 4Pressure to Takedown15 min · offensive chain 5Open Space Takedown15 min · full wrestling exchange

Template C · Wall Work Day

Goal: the complete wall game from entry through resolution.

1Pressure to Wall10 min · getting to the wall 2Wall Control12 min · establishing control 3Wall Escape12 min · defensive response 4Wall Grinding12 min · damage from the wall 5Stand-Up Loop14 min · full wall exchange

Template D · Ground Survival Day

Goal: defensive ground skills for MMA survival.

1Ground Escape12 min · basic escape patterns 2Leg Reclaim12 min · guard recovery mechanics 3Ground to Standing10 min · technical standup 4Ground Defensive Submission12 min · bottom submissions 5Positional Sparring14 min · full ground exchange

Template E · Offensive Ground Day

Goal: the offensive ground path from takedown to finish.

1Open Space Takedown12 min · entry to ground 2Ground Access12 min · passing, advancing 3Ground Control12 min · maintaining position 4Positional Sparring12 min · live positional work 5Ground Control → Finish12 min · control to TKO / submission

Template F · Counter Fighting Day

Goal: develop a reactive, counter-based fighting style.

1Evade the Punch10 min · defensive reading 2Close-Range Defense10 min · handling pressure 3Counter-Striking12 min · punishing initiation 4Counter-Wrestling12 min · wrestling as counter 5Takedown Defense16 min · full defensive wrestling

Template G · Full MMA Integration

Goal: practice all games at Full MMA Expression level.

1Touch and Don't Get Touched (L4)10 min · striking with TD threat 2Pressure to Clinch (L4)10 min · space control, full MMA 3Pressure to Clinch (L5)12 min · Full MMA Expression 4Open Space Takedown (L4)14 min · wrestling with strikes 5Stand-Up Loop (L4)14 min · complete MMA exchange

Building Custom Sessions

The five-beat session shape

1Warm-up game8–10 min · simpler, lower intensity 2Skill builder #110–12 min · isolated skill 3Skill builder #210–12 min · related skill or progression 4Integration game12–15 min · combine skills 5Live application15–20 min · combined or free play

Pairing principles

Defensive striking→Offensive striking (for combined) Wall offense→Wall escape (understand both sides) Ground access→Ground escape (attacker needs a defender) Counter-striking→Land the Target (need an initiator) Takedown offense→Takedown defense (balance the session)

Progression within a session

🧩 Simpler → ComplexStart with fewer variables, add them as athletes succeed. ⏩ Slower → FasterBuild up intensity across the session. 🔗 Isolated → IntegratedMove from single skills to combined expression. 🔓 Constrained → OpenMore rules early, fewer rules as the skill holds.

Session Notes Template

Document each session so the next one builds on it.

## Session: [Date]

### Focus: [Primary skill/environment]

### Games Played:
1. [Game], [Duration], Notes:
2. [Game], [Duration], Notes:
3. [Game], [Duration], Notes:
4. [Game], [Duration], Notes:
5. [Game], [Duration], Notes:

### Observations:
- What worked:
- What to adjust:
- Player standouts:
- Safety notes:

### Next Session:
- Follow up on:
- Progress to:

Game Reference Quick List

Skill IsolationOpen Space StrikingTransition ZoneOpen Space WrestlingWallGround

Defensive: Parry the Straight, Tight Block, Slip the Straight, Evade the Punch

Offensive: Lead Hand Offense, Rear Hand Offense, Lead Leg Offense, Rear Leg Offense

Defensive: Close-Range Defense, Evasive Movement

Offensive: Land the Target, Sustained Offense

Combined: Touch and Don't Get Touched, Pressure to Clinch

Defensive: Clinch Denial, Counter-Striking, Counter-Wrestling

Combined: Pressure to Clinch (L3+)

Defensive: Takedown Defense

Offensive: Pressure to Takedown, Pressure to Wall

Combined: Open Space Takedown

Defensive: Wall Escape, Wall Defensive Submission

Offensive: Wall Control, Wall to Ground, Wall Grinding

Combined: Stand-Up Loop

Defensive: Ground Escape, Leg Reclaim, Ground to Standing, Ground Defensive Submission

Offensive: Ground Access, Ground Control

Combined: Positional Sparring

Session planning philosophy

The best sessions balance challenge and success. Players should succeed enough to build confidence while being pushed enough to grow. Adjust games and levels based on what you observe during play. For the reasoning behind game selection, see the Session Planning Guide.

docs.title: Safety & Intensity Rules
docs.text:
Coaching ToolNon-NegotiableApplies to Every Game

Safety principles in this system are non-negotiable. They cannot be overridden for competitive pressure, athlete preference, or training efficiency. The game serves learning, never the other way around.

Five Non-Negotiable Principles

🦵No falling bodyweight onto legs

Takedowns and transitions never drop bodyweight onto an opponent's legs or knees.This is the most common source of serious training injury, protect the knee at all costs.

⚖️Control precedes gravity

All takedowns are created via off-balancing, not dumping. The base must be compromised before descent begins.If you have to force someone down, you haven't earned the takedown, and you're inviting a scramble injury.

👆Strikes are informational

Strikes in games are light and informational. They measure distance, create reactions, and test control, not cause damage.A strike's job in a game is to specify information, not to hurt a training partner.

🔒Submissions are emergent

Submissions (especially DNS) are defensive tools that emerge from constraints, not primary objectives to be hunted.No jumping guard, no sitting to finish, no chasing submissions as the main goal.

🛑Coach intervention is mandatory

The coach must stop play the moment the game stops serving its purpose.Intervention isn't optional supervision, it's a structural part of every game.

FLAGGED FOR REVIEW

The Submissions are emergent principle contains errors and needs revision. Content is provisional until corrected.

When the Coach Must Stop Play

  • Structure degrades into scrambling chaos
  • Intensity escalates beyond appropriate levels
  • Any safety violation occurs
  • Learning value has disappeared

Intensity Zones

Match effort to development. These are learning environments, not competition simulations, quality reading beats maximal output at every level.

🟢Beginner · 30–40%

Focus on pattern recognition.Low intensity keeps the problem visible, the athlete is learning what to look for before learning to do it under fire.

🟡Intermediate · 40–60%

Add resistance and timing pressure.Enough live pressure to force adaptation, still controlled enough to keep structure intact.

🟠Advanced · 60–75%

Realistic problem-solving.Close to fight realism, but still inside the cap, the skill must hold under genuine resistance.

🔴Maximum Cap · 80%

Never exceed in training games.Even advanced athletes stay under 80% in ecological games, beyond it, learning gives way to survival and risk.

Red Flags: Stop Immediately

Brute force without techniqueIndicates the constraints are mis-set, the game is rewarding strength over skill. Memorized repetitions without adaptationThe game is too predictable, there's no live problem left to solve. Ego-driven intensity escalationA competitive mindset has taken over, the learning frame is gone. Panic responsesThe athlete is overwhelmed, the problem is too hard for their current level. Static grinding without progressNo learning is occurring, the exchange has stalled out.

When red flags appear, stop the game and reassess:

  • Are the constraints appropriate?
  • Is intensity appropriate?
  • Does the athlete understand the problem?

Safety Rules by Environment

🟦Open Space

No chasing collisions · no forward crashes · reset if exchanges become static.

🧱Wall

No grinding into the wall · no neck cranks · light strikes only.

🔻Transitions (Wall → Ground)

Control precedes gravity · no dumping bodyweight · no slamming · stop if descent becomes uncontrolled.

🟫Ground

No falling onto legs · DNS recognized and stopped before completion · no sustained suffocation.

FLAGGED FOR REVIEW

The Wall environment rules need revision.

DNS in Training

Defensive Neck Submissions (guillotines, front headlocks) occur when the attacker's head drops into a vulnerable position. DNS is not a safety concern, it's a natural consequence of poor head position, and the defender may capitalize with a choke.

Playing to submission→DNS goes to finish DNS leads to ground, submission maintained→Continue to finish Any submission→Standard tap rules apply

Intervention Language

Coaches use clear, consistent commands so play stops the instant it needs to.

🔁 "Reset"Return to the starting position. ❄️ "Freeze"Stop all movement immediately. 📉 "Check intensity"Reduce effort level. ⏱️ "Time"Round complete. 🚪 "Out"Exit the game, a safety concern has been raised.

Athlete Responsibility

  • Respect tap signals immediately
  • Self-regulate intensity
  • Report any discomfort or injury
  • Prioritize partner safety over "winning"

Culture of Safety

The safest gyms are those where athletes actively protect each other, not just themselves.

System evolution notice

Safety rules may be expanded as new games or contexts are added. They expand, they never relax.

docs.title: Session Planning Guide
docs.text:
Coaching ToolGame-BasedNo Fixed Template

Sessions are game-based from start to finish, there is no prescribed structure. What you run depends entirely on the training goal in front of you.

Core Philosophy

No universal session template

Session structure is entirely dependent on the training goal. There is no "typical session" pattern to follow.

🎯The problem

The specific skill or problem being addressed.

👥The athletes

Who's present and their current development.

🥊The context

General training vs. fight-specific preparation.

⏱️The constraints

Time available and equipment on hand.

Warm-Up Expectations

Athletes should arrive ready to play. Games start immediately, they are the training, not something that happens after a warm-up.

Situational judgment, not a phase

A coach who knows athletes haven't warmed up can start the first game at lower intensity, choose a less physically demanding game to open, or let athletes ease into full engagement. The system doesn't prescribe "warm-up games" as a structural phase.

Planning Considerations

1Goal-driven selectionPick games that serve one clear objective.Ask: what problem are we solving today? What skill are we developing? What does success look like? Don't run games just to fill time. 2Prerequisite awarenessConfirm the foundations are in place.Check the game's frontmatter for prerequisite concepts and games. If athletes lack them, address those first, simpler games prepare athletes for complex ones. 3Role balanceMake sure athletes experience both sides.In asymmetric games, switch roles regularly and track total time in each. Some athletes need more time in a specific role for their development. 4Intensity managementMatch effort to goal and readiness.High intensity → shorter rounds, more rest. Skill development → moderate intensity, quality over volume. Technical work → lower intensity, more reps and feedback.

Common Mistakes

Too much varietyBetter to develop one skill deeply than touch many superficially. Skipping prerequisitesComplex games fail without the foundational skills underneath them. Unbalanced rolesAthletes need time on both sides of asymmetric games. Ignoring fatigueQuality degrades when athletes are exhausted, adjust or end the session. Rigid adherence to a planIf something isn't working, adapt. The plan serves the learning, not the reverse.

Adapting to Context

General class vs. fight camp

General class→Broader skill development, more variety across sessions Fight camp→Focused on the specific problems this athlete needs to solve

By athlete level

🟢Beginners

Simpler games, more instruction, build vocabulary.

🟡Intermediate

Progress through game levels, introduce combined games.

🟠Advanced

Nuanced work, game modifications, more autonomy.

By time available

Short session→Focus on one game, work through its levels Long session→Address related skills, more games in sequence

Using Game Metadata

Each game's frontmatter carries the information you need to choose well.

🔑 PrerequisitesWhat must athletes already know before this game works? 📊 DifficultyIs this appropriate for the group in front of you? ⏱️ DurationHow long are typical rounds? 🧰 EquipmentWhat do you need on hand to run it?

Ready to assemble a session? See the Lesson Plan Generator for templates and a randomized builder.

Development notice

This guide may be refined based on coaching feedback and experience.

docs.title: Game Template
docs.text:
Authoring ToolPage StructureCopy & Adapt

Use this template when documenting new games. Every game page follows the same skeleton so coaches always know where to look.

Anatomy of a Game Page

A complete game page moves top-to-bottom through eight sections, from identity to connections.

1Game IdentityProblem · environment · stageThe identity box at the top: the core problem the game solves, where it takes place, and its decision stage. 2Roles & ObjectivesWhat each side is trying to doUses neutral A Side / B Side labels with a one-line objective each. 3SetupStarting position · reset triggerPositions, distances, and the conditions that send play back to neutral. 4RulesAllowed · not allowedThe invariants that shape behavior toward the learning goal. 5Win ConditionsObservable outcomes per sideSpecific, observable conditions, no vague "controls the opponent". 6LevelsProgressive difficultySame problem, escalating constraints, from core skill to full expression. 7SafetyContact limits · stop conditionsEvery game declares how hard contact may be and when play pauses. 8ConnectionsPrerequisites · leads toHow the game sits in the curriculum, what comes before and after.

Copy These Blocks

FrontmatterPage Structure 
---
title: Game Name
environment: open-space
domain: striking
focus: defensive
difficulty: beginner
duration: short
min_players: 2
equipment: none
prerequisites:
  concepts: []
  games: []
tags:
  - tag1
  - tag2
---

# Game Name

!!! info "Game Identity"
    - **Problem:** What this game solves
    - **Environment:** Where it takes place
    - **Stage:** Decision state (Access/Stabilize/Exploit/Counter)

Brief description.

## Roles & Objectives
| Role | Objective |
|------|-----------|
| **A Side** | What they're trying to do |
| **B Side** | What they're trying to do |

## Setup
**Starting Position:** Positions and distances
**Reset When:** Conditions that trigger reset

## Rules
**Allowed:** ...   **Not Allowed:** ...

## Win Conditions
| Role | Wins By |
|------|---------|
| A Side | Specific, observable condition |
| B Side | Specific, observable condition |

## Levels
| Level | Focus | Constraints |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Core skill | Most restricted |

## Safety
- Contact limits
- Stop conditions

## Connections
**Prerequisites:** Games/concepts needed first
**Leads To:** Follow-on games

Section Guide

Game Identity

The identity box appears at the top of every game page.

🎯Problem

The core learning challenge in one sentence, e.g. "Defending strikes at close range."

📍Environment

The physical context, Open Space, Wall, or Ground.

🧭Stage

The decision state, Access, Stabilize, Exploit, or Counter.

Roles & Objectives

Games use A Side and B Side as neutral role labels, mapped per game type.

Offense vs Defense→Attacker · Defender Positional→Top Player · Bottom Player Skill Isolation→Feeder · Practitioner Mirrored→Player 1 · Player 2 (same goal)

Rules (Invariants)

Rules define the problem space. Good rules:

  • Are clear and unambiguous
  • Shape behavior toward the learning goal
  • Remove distracting options

Example

"No clinching" removes grappling so athletes focus on striking range.

Win Conditions

Win conditions must be specific and observable.

Bad "A Side wins by controlling B Side", vague and unmeasurable. Good "A Side wins by holding pin (flat shoulders, controlled hips) for 5 seconds."

Levels

Progressive difficulty within the same problem.

1Core skillIn isolation, most restricted 2Added resistanceMore options introduced 3Full expressionRealistic pressure with counters 4Full MMACross-domain threats

Safety

Every game declares three things.

🤝 Contact limitsHow hard can strikes be? 🛑 Stop conditionsWhen does play pause automatically? 👁️ Intervention criteriaWhen must the coach step in?

See the Safety & Intensity Rules for the system-wide non-negotiables.

Pre-Publish Checklist

  • Problem is clearly defined
  • Environment and stage are tagged
  • Win conditions are observable
  • Safety section is complete
  • Prerequisites are mapped
  • At least 3 levels defined
  • Tags added for search
Template evolution

This template updates as patterns emerge. See the Change Log for history.
